name: Sergey Levine
title: Associate Professor / Co-founder
lab: Robotic AI and Learning Lab (RAIL), UC Berkeley; Berkeley AI Research (BAIR)
research_focus: Foundation models for robotics — Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models that generalize across robot platforms and tasks; offline reinforcement learning; learning from human video data; real-world policy optimization with RL fine-tuning
direction_of_travel: Model-free deep RL (SAC) → offline RL (IQL, CQL) → robot learning from internet-scale data (RT-2, OpenVLA) → robot foundation models / physical intelligence (π0, π0.5, π0.6*). The through-line: can robot learning be as data-scalable as language model training? Now operationalizing that bet commercially at Physical Intelligence.
key_contributions: π0 / π0.5 / π0.6* — generalist robot foundation model (Physical Intelligence); flow matching on top of pretrained VLM
RT-2 — Robotic Transformer 2, VLA integrating internet-scale VLM knowledge into robot control
Offline RL / IQL (Implicit Q-Learning) — solved distributional shift in offline RL without OOD queries
SAC (Soft Actor-Critic) — entropy-maximizing off-policy deep RL; among most widely used algorithms (10000+ citations)
OpenVLA — open-source 7B-parameter VLA outperforming RT-2-X (55B) on 29 tasks
FAST tokenizer — DCT-based action tokenization enabling 5x faster VLA training on 10k hours of robot data
MEM (Multi-Scale Embodied Memory) — long+short-term memory for VLAs enabling tasks >10 minutes
Human-to-robot transfer emergence — co-training VLAs on human video + robot data at scale nearly doubles performance

top_papers.title: π0: A Vision-Language-Action Flow Model for General Robot Control
top_papers.one_liner: First generalist robot foundation model using flow matching on top of a pretrained VLM, trained across 8 robot platforms on tasks like laundry folding and table cleaning

top_papers.title: Offline Reinforcement Learning with Implicit Q-Learning (IQL)
top_papers.one_liner: Solved offline RL by never querying OOD actions — uses expectile regression on value function; became the go-to offline RL algorithm

top_papers.title: OpenVLA: An Open-Source Vision-Language-Action Model
top_papers.one_liner: Open 7B-parameter VLA trained on 970k real-robot demonstrations, outperforms closed RT-2-X (55B) by 16.5% with 7x fewer parameters

top_papers.title: Soft Actor-Critic (SAC)
top_papers.one_liner: Maximum-entropy off-policy deep RL algorithm; dominant baseline for continuous control and robot learning for half a decade

top_papers.title: FAST: Efficient Action Tokenization for VLAs
top_papers.one_liner: DCT-based action tokenizer enabling training on dexterous high-frequency tasks; released with universal pretrained tokenizer on 1M robot trajectories

recent_arxiv.title: MEM: Multi-Scale Embodied Memory for VLAs
recent_arxiv.summary: Combines video-based short-term memory with text-based long-term memory in a VLA; enables tasks spanning up to 15 minutes (kitchen cleanup, grilled cheese). Authors include Torne, Pertsch, Walke, Levine, Finn, Driess.

recent_arxiv.title: AsyncVLA: Asynchronous Vision-Language-Action Models
recent_arxiv.summary: Decouples semantic reasoning (large remote model) from reactive execution (lightweight edge adapter); 40% higher success vs baselines, handles 6-second communication delays.

recent_arxiv.title: Steerable Vision-Language-Action Policies
recent_arxiv.summary: Trains VLAs on synthetic commands at multiple abstraction levels; VLMs steer robot behavior at inference time. Outperforms prior embodied reasoning VLAs.

recent_arxiv.title: Q-learning with Adjoint Matching (QAM)
recent_arxiv.summary: Novel TD-RL algorithm for optimizing diffusion/flow-matching policies using adjoint matching — avoids unstable backprop through multi-step denoising. SOTA on sparse reward offline RL.

recent_arxiv.title: Emergence of Human to Robot Transfer in VLAs
recent_arxiv.summary: Human video co-training doubles VLA performance on generalization tasks once robot pretraining is sufficiently diverse — embodiment-agnostic representations emerge at scale.

recent_arxiv.title: FAST: Efficient Robot Action Tokenization
recent_arxiv.summary: DCT-based tokenization enabling training on 10k hours of robot data, 5x faster than diffusion VLAs while matching performance.

current_projects: Physical Intelligence (pi.website) — generalist robot foundation models. Products: π0 (Oct 2024, open-sourced Feb 2025), π0.5 (open-world generalization, Apr 2025), π0.6* (RL fine-tuned, Nov 2025), FAST tokenizer, MEM (15-min tasks, Mar 2026). Backed by Sequoia, Khosla, Bond, Lux, Redpoint, CapitalG, Thrive, Jeff Bezos, OpenAI. Team of 60+. ~$2.4B valuation on $400M Series B (Nov 2024).
personal_site_summary: Levine's Berkeley page positions him as RAIL Lab director focused on 'algorithms that enable autonomous agents to acquire complex behaviors through learning.' Teaches CS 285 (Deep RL) and CS 182 (Deep Learning). Operationally split between Berkeley and Physical Intelligence.
notable_quotes: Robot learning holds tremendous promise to unlock the full potential of flexible, general, and dexterous robot systems. [from π0 abstract]
Human-to-robot transfer emerges once the VLA is pre-trained on sufficient scenes, tasks, and embodiments — diverse pretraining produces embodiment-agnostic representations. [from emergence paper]
The Physical Intelligence Layer [blog]: vision for PI as infrastructure layer enabling partner applications across robotics use cases
industry_connections: Co-founder and research lead at Physical Intelligence (pi.website); former Google Brain researcher; collaborates closely with Chelsea Finn (also PI co-founder), Karl Pertsch, Brian Ichter, Danny Driess, Pieter Abbeel. PI investors: Sequoia, Khosla, Bond, Lux, Redpoint, CapitalG, Thrive Capital, Jeff Bezos, OpenAI.
commercialization_signal: EXTREMELY STRONG — co-founder of Physical Intelligence; $400M Series B at ~$2.4B valuation; active product roadmap (π0 → π0.5 → π0.6* → MEM) with clear deployment partners; open-sourced π0 weights creating ecosystem.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Levine is building the ChatGPT moment for robots — a single foundation model that can be trained once on massive diverse robot data and then fine-tuned to do any physical task. Physical Intelligence is the company executing that bet with real hardware and $400M in the bank.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: With π0.6* you're adding RL fine-tuning on top of the VLA pretraining — how are you thinking about the data flywheel here: is real-world deployment data the key moat, or is the architecture insight (flow matching + VLM backbone) the defensible piece?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: PI is the canonical embodied world model company — the robot foundation model thesis made flesh. If you believe physical AI follows the same scaling laws as language AI, this is the primary bet.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Enterprise robotics deployment partnerships, manufacturing/warehouse customers for π0 pilots, supply chain hardware intros (robot OEMs), co-investment alongside Sequoia/Khosla in future rounds, distribution in verticals (food service, logistics, healthcare).
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Generic 'when will robots replace workers' without having read PI's actual safety documentation first.

controversial_take: Levine's implicit bet is that real-world robot data — not simulation — is the path to generalization, directly countering the sim-to-real camp (DeepMind, NVIDIA Isaac). Also betting that imitation learning + RL fine-tuning at scale beats pure RL from scratch for physical tasks — contradicting the AlphaZero/self-play philosophy.
open_problem: Closing the data efficiency gap: enabling a robot trained on 10k hours of data to generalize to a novel kitchen without any fine-tuning. π0.5 made progress but true zero-shot open-world manipulation remains unsolved.

name: Jiajun Wu
title: Assistant Professor / Co-founder
lab: Stanford Vision and Learning Lab (SVL); Stanford AI Lab (SAIL)
research_focus: Physical scene understanding — building AI that perceives, reasons about, and interacts with the 3D physical world. Key areas: 4D world generation (spatial + temporal), neuro-symbolic concept grounding, multi-modal perception, embodied AI and robotic manipulation, 3D scene representation.
direction_of_travel: Neuro-symbolic program induction for physics (MIT PhD) → 3D object/scene understanding → generative 3D worlds and spatial intelligence → co-founding World Labs to productize spatial world models at scale. Consistent question: 'how does an agent build an internal model of the physical world?' Moving from analysis to synthesis to embodiment.
key_contributions: World Labs — co-founded with Fei-Fei Li, Justin Johnson, Ben Mildenhall (NeRF inventor); building spatial intelligence / 3D world generation AI
WonderWorld (CVPR 2025 Highlight) — interactive 3D scene generation from a single image
Birth and Death of a Rose (CVPR 2025 Oral) — 4D temporal scene modeling with realistic life-cycle dynamics
The Scene Language (CVPR 2025 Highlight) — hybrid representation combining symbolic programs with neural embeddings for 3D scenes
VAGEN (NeurIPS 2025) — reinforcing world model reasoning in VLMs
Embodied Agent Interface (NeurIPS 2024 Oral) — benchmarking LLMs for embodied decision-making

top_papers.title: WonderWorld: Interactive 3D Scene Generation from a Single Image
top_papers.one_liner: Generates navigable, persistent 3D scenes from a single image in real time — research foundation for World Labs' Marble product. CVPR 2025 Highlight.

top_papers.title: Birth and Death of a Rose
top_papers.one_liner: 4D generative modeling of temporal scene dynamics — pushing toward physically grounded world models with life-cycle priors. CVPR 2025 Oral.

top_papers.title: The Scene Language: Representing Scenes with Programs, Words, and Embeddings
top_papers.one_liner: Unified representational language for 3D scenes combining symbolic programs with neural embeddings. CVPR 2025 Highlight.

top_papers.title: Embodied Agent Interface: Benchmarking LLMs for Embodied Decision-Making
top_papers.one_liner: Comprehensive benchmark for evaluating how well LLMs reason about physical tasks in embodied settings. NeurIPS 2024 Oral.

recent_arxiv.title: Building Intelligent Agents with Neuro-Symbolic Concepts
recent_arxiv.summary: CACM 2026 — capstone synthesis of Wu's neuro-symbolic research program for embodied AI agents.

recent_arxiv.title: VAGEN: Reinforcing World Model Reasoning in VLMs
recent_arxiv.summary: NeurIPS 2025. RL-based approach to improving VLMs' causal physical world reasoning capabilities.

recent_arxiv.title: DexSkin: High-Coverage Conformable Robotic Skin
recent_arxiv.summary: CoRL 2025 Oral. Dense tactile sensing for robot manipulation — bridges perception and world model grounding.

current_projects: World Labs (worldlabs.ai) — co-founded with Fei-Fei Li, Justin Johnson, Ben Mildenhall. Products: (1) Marble — flagship multimodal world model generating persistent navigable 3D worlds (Nov 2025); (2) World API — public developer API for 3D world generation (Jan 2026); (3) RTFM — real-time frame model for interactive world generation (Oct 2025). Use cases: VFX/film, gaming/AR/VR, robotics simulation, architecture. Investors: a16z, Adobe, AMD, Cisco, Databricks, Intel, NEA, NVIDIA, Salesforce. Feb 2026 additional funding announced. Blog: '3D as code' manifesto (Mar 2026) — '3D is becoming the universal interface for space the way text became the universal interface for software.'
personal_site_summary: Wu's Stanford page emphasizes physical scene understanding as core research mission. 12 PhD students, 3 postdocs, 5 research affiliates. World Labs is the commercial embodiment of his research. Spans SAIL, SVL, Graphics Lab, Robotics Center, and HAI — extremely broad institutional footprint.
notable_quotes: Text became the universal interface for software; 3D is becoming the universal interface for space. [World Labs '3D as code' blog, Mar 2026]
Spatial intelligence is the ability to process visual data, make predictions and act upon those predictions — seeing is for doing and learning.
industry_connections: Co-founder of World Labs (with Fei-Fei Li, Justin Johnson, Ben Mildenhall); collaborations with Josh Tenenbaum (MIT), Bill Freeman (MIT/Google). World Labs investors: a16z, NVIDIA, Adobe, NEA, Databricks, Salesforce.
commercialization_signal: VERY STRONG — co-founder of World Labs; launched Marble product, World API, RTFM; Feb 2026 new funding; targets VFX/film, gaming, robotics simulation verticals. Research and commercial work tightly coupled.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Wu is building the AI that turns a single photo or text description into a full interactive 3D world you can walk around in. His company World Labs (with Fei-Fei Li) is productizing this as the 'spatial intelligence' layer for film, gaming, robotics, and design.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: With the World API now live — are you seeing unexpected use cases pulling toward real-time interactive simulation for robotics training, or is film/VFX demand dominating early traction? How does the robotics simulation thesis fit into the Marble roadmap?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: World Labs is the world model company for 3D spatial intelligence. If physical AI requires world models to pre-train robots in simulation, World Labs could become foundational infrastructure — the sim layer under Physical Intelligence, Figure, 1X, etc.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to robotics companies needing simulation environments, game studio partnerships, architecture/design firm pilots, co-investment in next round alongside a16z/NVIDIA.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Do not ask him to compare World Labs to Luma/Runway/Sora unless you have deep technical opinions — he'll be asked this constantly.

controversial_take: Wu's implicit thesis is that 3D spatial representation — not language or flat video — is the correct substrate for general intelligence. This puts him in direct opposition to researchers who argue 2D video + enough tokens will suffice. His neuro-symbolic background also keeps him skeptical of pure neural approaches.
open_problem: Persistent, physically-consistent 4D world generation at room/building/city scale with physical consistency — Marble makes progress but true persistent world simulation is unsolved.

name: Chelsea Finn
title: Associate Professor, CS & EE, Stanford; Co-Founder, Physical Intelligence
lab: IRIS Lab (Intelligent Robotic Interactive Structures), Stanford SAIL
research_focus: Intelligence through robotic interaction at scale. VLA (vision-language-action) models as robot foundation models, cross-embodiment transfer, memory architectures for long-horizon tasks, test-time verification for robotic policies, open-world generalization of generalist robot policies.
direction_of_travel: From meta-learning theory (MAML) → robot learning at scale → foundation models for physical intelligence. Currently building the infrastructure layer for general-purpose robotics at Physical Intelligence, with 'PI Layer' API product announced Feb 2026.
key_contributions: MAML — Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (ICML 2017): most cited papers in modern ML, ~25k citations
π0 — First generalist robot foundation model combining VLM pre-training + flow matching (Physical Intelligence, 2024)
π0.5 — Open-world generalization: robot that can clean an entirely new kitchen it has never seen (2025)
Physical Intelligence (π) co-founder with Sergey Levine — $400M raised, ~$2.4B valuation
MEM — Multi-Scale Embodied Memory enabling robot tasks > 10 minutes (March 2026)
CoVer-VLA — test-time verification pipeline: 45% real-world improvement without retraining

top_papers.title: Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning for Fast Adaptation of Deep Networks (MAML)
top_papers.one_liner: Train a model such that a few gradient steps on a new task yields strong generalization — works for any gradient-descent model across classification, regression, and RL

top_papers.title: π0: A Vision-Language-Action Flow Model for General Robot Control
top_papers.one_liner: First generalist robot policy combining 3B-parameter VLM with flow matching for high-frequency motor control across 8 robot platforms

top_papers.title: π0.5: a Vision-Language-Action Model with Open-World Generalization
top_papers.one_liner: Co-training on heterogeneous robot/web data enables mobile manipulators to clean entirely new kitchens and bedrooms without environment-specific training

recent_arxiv.title: MEM: Multi-Scale Embodied Memory for Vision Language Action Models
recent_arxiv.summary: Combines video-based short-horizon memory with text-based long-horizon memory. Enables robots to perform tasks >10 minutes (kitchen cleanup, sandwich prep) with in-context adaptation.

recent_arxiv.title: Scaling Verification Can Be More Effective than Scaling Policy Learning for VLA Alignment
recent_arxiv.summary: CoVer-VLA: test-time verification pipeline. 22% in-distribution, 45% real-world improvement — without retraining. Challenges assumption that scale is the only path.

recent_arxiv.title: Data Analogies Enable Efficient Cross-Embodiment Transfer
recent_arxiv.summary: Paired demonstrations across different robot embodiments outperform large unstructured diversity datasets. 22.5% improvement in cross-embodiment transfer.

recent_arxiv.title: VLAW: Iterative Co-Improvement of Vision-Language-Action Policy and World Model
recent_arxiv.summary: Jointly trains a VLA policy and a world model in an iterative loop — each improves the other. Directly relevant to world models workshop.

current_projects: Scaling Physical Intelligence's VLA models (π0 → π0.5 → MEM line), building the 'Physical Intelligence Layer' API/platform for robotics companies. Stanford research: memory architectures for long-horizon robot tasks, test-time verification, cross-embodiment data efficiency.
personal_site_summary: Research mission: 'intelligence through robotic interaction at scale.' IRIS Lab, SAIL, ML Group affiliations. Awards: ACM 2018 Doctoral Dissertation Award, MIT TR35 Pioneer, Intel Rising Star, Microsoft Faculty Fellowship.
notable_quotes: Mission: 'intelligence through robotic interaction at scale' — robots developing broadly intelligent behavior through learning and interaction
On π0 open-sourcing: released model weights, code, and π0-FAST autoregressive model (Feb 2025) — unusual transparency for a well-funded startup
industry_connections: Physical Intelligence (co-founder with Sergey Levine) — 60+ person team. Backed by: Sequoia (Alfred Lin), Bond, Jeff Bezos, Khosla Ventures, Lux Capital, OpenAI, Redpoint, CapitalG, Thrive Capital. $400M raised as of Nov 2024. Stanford IRIS Lab feeds talent directly into PI.
commercialization_signal: VERY HIGH. Physical Intelligence is one of the best-funded robotics startups in history (~$400M raised, ~$2.4B valuation). 'Physical Intelligence Layer' product announcement (Feb 2026) signals API platform go-to-market. PI open-sourced π0 weights (Feb 2025) as developer acquisition strategy.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Chelsea Finn invented MAML — the algorithm that lets an AI model learn how to learn, adapting to new tasks from just a few examples. She co-founded Physical Intelligence, which is building the GPT for robots: a single model that controls any robot for any task. They've shown a robot cleaning an entirely new kitchen. They just announced a platform (the 'PI Layer') so robotics companies can plug into their models via API.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: The PI Layer positions you as the intelligence layer for hardware companies — but how do you think about the boundary between the policy model and robot-specific fine-tuning? Are you moving toward hardware partners doing zero fine-tuning, or is FAST + platform-managed fine-tuning the long-term architecture?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Physical Intelligence is building the robot intelligence stack analogous to what cloud providers built for compute. If you invest in robotics hardware, humanoid startups, or industrial automation, PI's model layer is becoming infrastructure. Chelsea's VLAW paper (world model + policy co-training) also positions PI as directly relevant to world models research.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to robotics hardware companies needing a generalist intelligence layer, industrial automation customers for pilot programs, co-investment signal in their next round.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Don't probe the Stanford dual-role tension (professor vs. startup founder). Don't ask about competition with Google DeepMind Robotics in a confrontational framing.

controversial_take: Physical Intelligence's open-source strategy (releasing π0 weights while commercializing the platform layer) is a direct bet that the model becomes a commodity and value accretes to the platform/data flywheel — analogous to Meta's LLaMA strategy. Risk: well-resourced competitors (Google DeepMind, Boston Dynamics) can fine-tune the open weights and undercut the platform.
open_problem: How do you scale robot data collection fast enough to match the internet-scale data that made GPT work? The FAST tokenizer and cross-embodiment data analogies are partial answers, but robots still learn from thousands of demonstrations while LLMs learned from trillions of tokens. The data bottleneck, not the model architecture, is the binding constraint.

name: Graham Neubig
affiliation: Carnegie Mellon University / All Hands AI
title: Associate Professor, Language Technologies Institute, CMU; Co-Founder, All Hands AI
lab: NeuLab (Neural Networks for NLP), CMU LTI
research_focus: AI software development agents, coding agent benchmarking and evaluation, multi-agent systems for software engineering, agent data protocols, agent training and generalization, multilingual NLP. Core product: OpenHands — open-source platform enabling AI agents to write code, run terminals, and browse the web.
direction_of_travel: From academic NLP researcher (multilingual MT) → LLM applications → coding agents and software engineering automation. OpenHands (formerly OpenDevin) launched ~2024, became 69k-star GitHub phenomenon, spawned All Hands AI startup. Now building full commercial stack: open-source community + enterprise cloud + SDK + benchmark standards.
key_contributions: OpenHands platform — open-source AI software development agent; 69.3k GitHub stars, 77.6 SWE-bench score (March 2026), ICLR 2025
All Hands AI — commercial entity building enterprise-grade AI coding agents; featured in NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang's GTC 2026 keynote
Agent Data Protocol (ADP) — standardized 'interlingua' for agent training datasets; unified 13 datasets, ~20% benchmark improvement
CUBE — universal agent benchmark standard built on MCP+Gym for cross-platform evaluation interoperability
OpenHands SDK — composable framework for production-ready software agents with sandboxed execution and multi-LLM routing
Hybrid-Gym — synthetic training environment for coding agents; 25.4% SWE-bench improvement

top_papers.title: OpenHands: An Open Platform for AI Software Developers as Generalist Agents
top_papers.one_liner: Platform for AI agents that write code, run terminals, and browse the web — MIT licensed, 69k stars, 77.6 SWE-bench, the 'Linux of coding agents'

top_papers.title: Agent Data Protocol: Unifying Datasets for Diverse, Effective Fine-tuning of LLM Agents
top_papers.one_liner: Interlingua standard for 13 agent training datasets achieving ~20% performance gains — addresses data fragmentation in agent fine-tuning

recent_arxiv.title: CUBE: A Standard for Unifying Agent Benchmarks
recent_arxiv.summary: Universal protocol built on MCP+Gym allowing benchmarks to be wrapped once and evaluated anywhere. Could become the ISO standard for AI agent benchmarking.

recent_arxiv.title: Hybrid-Gym: Training Coding Agents to Generalize Across Tasks
recent_arxiv.summary: Synthetic training environment for generalizable coding skills. 25.4% SWE-Bench improvement via generalizable auxiliary tasks.

recent_arxiv.title: How Well Does Agent Development Reflect Real-World Work?
recent_arxiv.summary: Empirical analysis of whether SWE-bench reflects real software engineering work. Important for understanding benchmark validity.

current_projects: Building All Hands AI commercial stack: enterprise cloud with Slack/Jira/Linear integrations, self-hosted Kubernetes enterprise edition, OpenHands Index (live SWE benchmark leaderboard), Critic model (March 2026 — improves accuracy from 57.9% to 73.8%). Featured in NVIDIA GTC 2026 Jensen Huang keynote.
personal_site_summary: phontron.com — minimal site. Research output tracked via arXiv and GitHub. 33-person All Hands AI team. Enterprise customers: TikTok, VMware, Roche, Amazon, Netflix, Mastercard.
notable_quotes: All Hands AI mission: 'We build AI software development agents for everyone, in the open'
On OpenHands: 'the foundation for secure, transparent, model-agnostic coding agents'
industry_connections: All Hands AI raised $5M seed (Menlo Ventures, Sept 2024). NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang named OpenHands at GTC 2026 as 'one of the AI Native leaders in AI for Software Development.' Enterprise customers in production: TikTok, VMware, Roche, Amazon, Netflix, Mastercard. Integration with NVIDIA Nemotron 3 Super model. YC S24 partnership via Laminar.
commercialization_signal: VERY HIGH. OpenHands is the leading open-source coding agent by GitHub stars (69.3k). Business model: MongoDB/Elastic-style open-source + paid enterprise cloud. Only $5M seed raised (Sept 2024) — significantly underfunded relative to traction. Fortune 500 customers already in production. CUBE benchmark standard initiative creates platform moat. Likely raising Series A in 2025/2026.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Graham Neubig is a CMU professor who co-founded All Hands AI to commercialize OpenHands — the most popular open-source AI coding agent in the world, with 69,000+ GitHub stars. It scores 77.6 on the industry's hardest software engineering benchmark. NVIDIA's CEO Jensen Huang called it out by name at GTC 2026. Business model is MongoDB-style: free open source, paid enterprise cloud. They've only raised $5M seed but are in production at Amazon, Netflix, and TikTok.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: The CUBE benchmark standard proposal could make All Hands AI the de-facto measurement authority for the coding agent space. Is that a deliberate platform strategy, or academic infrastructure? How do you think about the tension between open benchmarking (which benefits competitors) and the ecosystem lock-in it creates?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: If AI coding agents replace/augment 20-50% of software engineering in 5 years, All Hands AI is the open-source platform play — analogous to Red Hat for Linux. Enterprise revenue model is proven. Traction metrics (69k stars, NVIDIA keynote mention, Fortune 500 customers) are exceptional for a $5M seed company — appears significantly underfunded vs. Devin ($21M) or GitHub Copilot Workspace.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Lead or participate in their Series A. Introductions to enterprise software companies deploying coding agents at scale. Portfolio companies needing autonomous code review, bug fixing, or legacy refactoring pipelines.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Don't bring up Devin (Cognition AI) without knowing the open-source vs. closed framing cold. Don't confuse OpenHands with a code autocomplete tool — it runs full terminals and browses the web.

controversial_take: The CUBE benchmark standardization push is a power move disguised as academic service: if All Hands AI writes the standard for how coding agents are evaluated, they structurally advantage OpenHands. Similar to how Google's Chrome dominance shapes web standards. The academic framing gives it legitimacy that pure commercial benchmark capture wouldn't have.
open_problem: Long-horizon task completion with minimal human oversight. Current SOTA (77.6 SWE-bench) handles well-specified, bounded tasks. Real software engineering involves ambiguous requirements, multi-week projects, team coordination. TOM-SWE (theory-of-mind for user intent) and AMA-Bench (long-horizon memory) work suggests Neubig knows this is the next frontier.

name: Arash Vahdat
title: Research Director, Fundamental Generative AI
lab: NVIDIA GenAIR (Generative AI Research)
research_focus: Foundational generative AI: diffusion models, flow matching, latent-space generative modeling, video generation, and increasingly scientific applications (protein design, weather forecasting, drug discovery). Core expertise in hierarchical VAEs, score-based models in latent space, and efficient diffusion sampling.
direction_of_travel: Scaling diffusion/flow-matching to long-form video and scientific domains. Recent pivot toward protein structure generation (Proteina, La-Proteina, ProtComposer) and climate/weather emulation. Parallel track on inference efficiency (few-step generation, consistency models, distillation). Arc: foundational generative modeling → latent-space efficiency → multimodal/scientific scale.
key_contributions: NVAE (NeurIPS 2020 Spotlight): Deep hierarchical VAE, first VAE to scale to 256x256 high-res images
LSGM (NeurIPS 2021): Score-based generative modeling in latent space — unified VAEs and diffusion models
EDM (NeurIPS 2022): Elucidating the design space of diffusion models — canonical modular framework
Proteina (ICLR 2025 Oral): Scaling flow-based protein backbone generation to 800-residue proteins
ProtComposer (ICLR 2025 Oral): Compositional protein structure generation via 3D ellipsoid spatial layouts
StormCast (2024): Diffusion-based emulation of 3km NOAA atmospheric model
Climate in a Bottle (2025): Diffusion-based foundation model for 5km global climate simulation

top_papers.title: Elucidating the Design Space of Diffusion-Based Generative Models (EDM)
top_papers.one_liner: Modular framework separating noise schedules, architectures, and training objectives — became the canonical reference design for the field

top_papers.title: NVAE: A Deep Hierarchical Variational Autoencoder
top_papers.one_liner: First hierarchical VAE to scale to high-resolution images; combining depth-wise separable convolutions and spectral regularization

top_papers.title: Score-based Generative Modeling in Latent Space (LSGM)
top_papers.one_liner: Hybrid model unifying VAE encoders with score-based diffusion in latent space — faster sampling, better likelihoods

top_papers.title: Proteina: Scaling Flow-based Protein Structure Generative Models
top_papers.one_liner: Large-scale flow-matching backbone generator for proteins up to 800 residues; ICLR 2025 Oral

recent_arxiv.title: Mode Seeking meets Mean Seeking for Fast Long Video Generation
recent_arxiv.summary: Decoupled Diffusion Transformer separating local fidelity from long-range coherence to scale video generation from seconds to minutes

recent_arxiv.title: Demystifying Data-Driven Probabilistic Medium-Range Weather Forecasting
recent_arxiv.summary: SOTA probabilistic weather prediction requires neither specialized architectures nor complex training; simpler design space than assumed

recent_arxiv.title: La-Proteina: Atomistic Protein Generation via Partially Latent Flow Matching
recent_arxiv.summary: Jointly generates amino acid sequences and full-atom protein structures via partially latent flow matching

recent_arxiv.title: Climate in a Bottle: Generative Foundation Model for Kilometer-Scale Global Atmosphere
recent_arxiv.summary: Diffusion-based foundation model emulating global 5km climate simulations without auto-regressive rollout instability

current_projects: Long-form video generation; atomistic protein design (La-Proteina); probabilistic weather/climate forecasting; hybrid language models blending autoregressive and diffusion paradigms; few-step generative models (distillation, consistency models). Oversight of NVIDIA's core generative AI research feeding into Cosmos and video products.
personal_site_summary: Site (latentspace.cc) was unreachable during research. Vahdat leads NVIDIA's Fundamental Generative AI group (GenAIR), directing a team spanning diffusion, flow matching, video, protein design, and scientific emulation.
notable_quotes: NVIDIA's generative AI research directly feeds commercial products — Cosmos world foundation model, video generation, and NVIDIA's AI-for-science initiatives are downstream of his team's work
industry_connections: NVIDIA Research — industry research lab with direct pipeline to product. Research feeds into NVIDIA Cosmos (world foundation model), NVIDIA's video generation stack, and AI-for-science platforms. Collaborated with NOAA/weather forecasting groups. Key academic collaborators: Tero Karras, Miika Aittala (NVIDIA), Morteza Mardani (NVIDIA, co-organizer ReALM-GEN).
commercialization_signal: Very strong — NVIDIA's generative AI research has direct commercial translation into Cosmos (world foundation model for robotics/AV), video generation APIs, and AI drug discovery/climate partnerships. Vahdat's protein design work positions NVIDIA as a credible scientific AI platform player.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Arash runs NVIDIA's core generative AI research group — the team that figured out how diffusion models actually work and now applies that to proteins, video, and climate.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: With La-Proteina doing joint sequence-structure generation at the atomistic level — do you see that converging toward a single 'chemistry foundation model' that spans small molecules and biologics, or do you think the modalities stay separate?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: His group produces the algorithmic infrastructure (EDM, LSGM, flow matching advances) that underpins nearly all serious diffusion startups. Understanding his current research priorities reveals where NVIDIA is placing chips in scientific AI and where the next generation of spin-outs is likely to emerge.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Access to biotech/pharma LPs assessing AI-for-drug-discovery platforms; intelligence on which protein design startups are technically differentiated vs. riding NVIDIA's open-source work; climate AI companies using his emulation methods.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Generic questions about Stable Diffusion or image generation — he's moved well past that and it signals you haven't done homework.

controversial_take: The field over-invested in diffusion for video; his 'Mode Seeking meets Mean Seeking' paper suggests long-form video requires a fundamentally different architectural split between local and global coherence. The 'just scale it' hypothesis for video is wrong.
open_problem: How to build a unified generative model of chemistry spanning small-molecule drug design, protein backbone generation, and molecular dynamics — without collapsing into separate specialized models for each modality.

name: Karsten Kreis
title: Principal Research Scientist, Generative AI
lab: NVIDIA Fundamental Generative AI Research
research_focus: Foundational generative AI algorithms (diffusion models, flow matching, consistency models) and their application to molecular modeling, protein design, and drug discovery. Also active in 4D/video generation, diffusion for language, and few-step generation. Physicist by training — brings rigorous mathematical perspective to model design.
direction_of_travel: Strong pivot toward generative AI for structural biology and drug discovery. Since 2024, majority of output is protein design (Proteina, ProtComposer, La-Proteina), molecular generation (GenMol, ReaSyn), and molecular dynamics. Creative media work has given way to scientific AI. Also pushing on inference efficiency (Align Your Steps, Align Your Flow) — NVIDIA sees efficient generation as core product differentiator.
key_contributions: LSGM (NeurIPS 2021): Co-designed score-based generative modeling in latent space with Vahdat and Kautz
EDM (NeurIPS 2022): Co-author of Elucidating Diffusion Models — canonical design space framework for the field
Align Your Latents (CVPR 2023): Applied latent diffusion to high-resolution video generation — foundational for NVIDIA's video stack
Magic3D (CVPR 2023 Highlight): High-resolution text-to-3D via two-stage diffusion optimization
Align Your Steps (2024): First principled optimization of diffusion sampling schedules using stochastic calculus
Proteina (ICLR 2025 Oral): Co-designed large-scale flow-based protein backbone generator
GenMol (ICML 2025): Drug discovery generalist via discrete diffusion
Co-founder, Variational AI — generative AI drug discovery startup (Merck deal, September 2025)

top_papers.title: Elucidating the Design Space of Diffusion-Based Generative Models (EDM)
top_papers.one_liner: Canonical reference for diffusion model design — separates noise schedules, architectures, and training objectives into modular components

top_papers.title: Align Your Latents: High-Resolution Video Synthesis with Latent Diffusion Models
top_papers.one_liner: Extends latent diffusion to temporally consistent high-resolution video — influenced Sora-era video architectures

top_papers.title: Magic3D: High-Resolution Text-to-3D Content Creation
top_papers.one_liner: Two-stage coarse-to-fine 3D generation combining NeRF and mesh optimization — CVPR 2023 Highlight

top_papers.title: Align Your Steps: Optimizing Sampling Schedules in Diffusion Models
top_papers.one_liner: First principled approach to sampling schedule optimization via stochastic calculus — improves FID without retraining

top_papers.title: GenMol: A Drug Discovery Generalist with Discrete Diffusion
top_papers.one_liner: Unified discrete diffusion for drug generation, optimization, linker design via fragment-based decoding; ICML 2025

recent_arxiv.title: Align Your Flow: Scaling Continuous-Time Flow Map Distillation
recent_arxiv.summary: Continuous-time objectives for flow map distillation achieving SOTA few-step generation; NeurIPS 2025

recent_arxiv.title: La-Proteina: Atomistic Protein Generation via Partially Latent Flow Matching
recent_arxiv.summary: Joint sequence and full-atom structure generation via partially latent representation

recent_arxiv.title: ReaSyn: Exploring Synthesizable Chemical Space with Iterative Pathway Refinements
recent_arxiv.summary: Generates synthesizable drug analogs via bidirectional reaction pathway refinement — addresses the synthesizability gap in generative chemistry

recent_arxiv.title: Test-time scaling of diffusions with flow maps
recent_arxiv.summary: Flow Map Trajectory Tilting for reward optimization at inference time — extends test-time compute scaling to diffusion models

current_projects: Protein design at atomistic resolution (La-Proteina); drug discovery generalist models (GenMol, ReaSyn); flow map distillation for few-step generation; test-time scaling for diffusion reward optimization; molecular dynamics simulation. Maintains oversight of NVIDIA's generative modeling infrastructure. Variational AI (co-founded drug discovery company).
personal_site_summary: karstenkreis.github.io is active and well-maintained. Lists full publication record, highlights ICLR 2025 Oral papers (Proteina, ProtComposer), notes area chair roles at ICLR 2026 and ICML 2026, and references Variational AI co-founding. Bio emphasizes physicist-to-AI-researcher trajectory. Teaches diffusion model tutorials at SIGGRAPH, NeurIPS, CVPR.
notable_quotes: Research interests span both the development of foundational generative AI algorithms and their application across scientific and creative domains (from personal site)
industry_connections: 1) NVIDIA Research (principal scientist, direct commercial pipeline to Cosmos/video products). 2) Variational AI — co-founded generative AI drug discovery company; signed collaboration with Merck in September 2025; partnerships with Rakovina Therapeutics, AdMare Biotech, Oncocross. Variational AI's Enki foundation model optimizes across 50+ drug-likeness properties simultaneously.
commercialization_signal: Exceptional — has both NVIDIA's direct commercial pipeline AND a co-founded drug discovery company (Variational AI) already in commercial partnerships with Merck. Clearest commercialization signal of the group.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Karsten is a physicist who co-invented some of the most-cited diffusion model papers and co-founded a drug discovery AI company that just landed a Merck deal.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: With GenMol handling multiple drug discovery tasks through a single discrete diffusion model — do you see the generalist approach eventually displacing task-specialized models in hit-to-lead, or does the precision required in late-stage optimization push back toward specialization?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: He sits at the exact intersection of foundational diffusion model research and AI drug discovery commercialization. Variational AI's success or failure is a real-world test of whether generative models can deliver on pharma promises. His view on what's working is uniquely grounded.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Strategic context on where pharma is in its AI procurement cycle (build vs. buy vs. partner); introductions to biotech investors who can accelerate Variational AI's next round; LP relationships in pharma who could be Variational AI customers.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Whether NVIDIA is 'too big' to do good science — he clearly views NVIDIA as enabling research he couldn't do elsewhere.

controversial_take: The synthesizability gap (generating molecules that look good in silico but can't be made in the lab) has been the field's biggest embarrassment. ReaSyn suggests the solution is reaction-pathway-aware generation from the start, not post-hoc synthesizability filters. Most current generative chemistry approaches are building on a broken foundation.
open_problem: How to design a generative model that jointly optimizes across the full drug discovery cascade — target binding, ADME/Tox, synthetic accessibility, selectivity — without collapsing into reward hacking on individual metrics.

name: Aakanksha Chowdhery
title: Member of Technical Staff, Reflection AI
research_focus: Frontier LLM pretraining and post-training at scale; RL-based self-improving agents; distributed ML infrastructure; multimodal models; agentic software engineering systems
direction_of_travel: ML systems/inference efficiency (Pathways, MLSys) → large-scale LLM pretraining lead (PaLM 540B, Gemini) → now building frontier open AI company with RL-at-scale as core thesis; shifted from closed Google research to open frontier intelligence startup
key_contributions: Lead author of PaLM (540B parameter language model, Google, 2022) — established new scaling benchmarks and few-shot capabilities
Lead researcher on Gemini 1.0 and co-author on Gemini 1.5 — Google's flagship multimodal model family
Co-author of Pathways (MLSys 2022) — distributed ML training infrastructure enabling efficient multi-TPU training
Co-author of PaLM-E (ICML 2023) — embodied multimodal language model for robotics
Program Chair for MLSys 2026
Co-teaches CS329A at Stanford on self-improving AI agents
Co-founder of Reflection AI — raised $2B (October 2025) to build frontier open intelligence

top_papers.title: PaLM: Scaling Language Modeling with Pathways
top_papers.one_liner: 540B parameter LLM trained via Pathways on 6144 TPUs, achieving breakthrough few-shot and reasoning performance; Chowdhery was first/lead author

top_papers.title: Gemini: A Family of Highly Capable Multimodal Models
top_papers.one_liner: Google's flagship multimodal model family processing text, images, audio, video; Chowdhery was lead researcher

top_papers.title: PaLM-E: An Embodied Multimodal Language Model
top_papers.one_liner: Combined language and vision for robotic manipulation; demonstrated multimodal grounding for embodied AI

top_papers.title: Pathways: Asynchronous Distributed Dataflow for ML
top_papers.one_liner: Google's distributed ML system enabling efficient training across multiple TPU pods simultaneously

recent_arxiv.title: Gemma: Open Models Based on Gemini Research and Technology
recent_arxiv.summary: Open 2B and 7B models derived from Gemini research; outperforms similarly-sized open models on 11/18 text-based tasks. Last confirmed public paper before departing Google.

current_projects: Co-founding and building Reflection AI, which raised $2B (announced October 2025) to build frontier open intelligence. Mission: 'building super intelligent autonomous systems' with conviction that 'reinforcement learning at scale will unlock the next frontier of capability.' Near-term focus is autonomous coding AI. Team pedigree across PaLM, Gemini, AlphaGo, GPT-4, and AlphaCode 2. Full pretraining + post-training stack. Open software thesis: 'Open software is what gets forked, customized, and embedded into systems worldwide.'
personal_site_summary: Site describes Chowdhery as Adjunct Professor at Stanford co-teaching CS329A on self-improving AI agents, and researcher at Reflection AI focused on 'autonomous self-improving agents, especially in software engineering.' Lists leadership on PaLM (540B), Gemini pretraining/scaling/finetuning, PaLM-E, MedPaLM, and Pathways. Awards include Paul Baran Marconi Young Scholar Award (2012).
notable_quotes: Open software is what gets forked, customized, and embedded into systems worldwide.
The answer to AI safety is not concealed development but rigorous science conducted openly.
There is a window of opportunity today to build frontier open intelligence, but it is closing.
Real-world evaluations matter most for demonstrating AI breakthroughs.
[On Reflection AI mission]: Building super intelligent autonomous systems with conviction that reinforcement learning at scale will unlock the next frontier of capability.
industry_connections: Co-founder of Reflection AI (raised $2B, October 2025); formerly Google Brain/Google DeepMind (lead researcher on PaLM and Gemini); Adjunct Professor Stanford; Program Chair MLSys 2026
commercialization_signal: LEFT Google for Reflection AI (Dario/Daniela Amodei-backed startup). This is a major career move signal — she was one of the leads on PaLM and Gemini.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: She left Google after building their most powerful AI models to start a company that wants to be the open-source alternative to both OpenAI and DeepSeek, and just raised $2 billion to do it.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Given that your team includes people who built both GPT-4 and Gemini, how are you thinking about the pretraining vs. post-training resource allocation decision — and is your RL-at-scale thesis primarily about RLHF, GRPO-style process reward, or something architecturally new?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Reflection AI is the highest-profile bet on open frontier AI with RL post-training as the core unlock. Their $2B raise validates that institutional capital believes the open model frontier is fundable. This is the exact nexus of SPOT workshop themes: scaling post-training, RL for LLMs, and autonomous coding agents.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Enterprise customer introductions for autonomous coding tooling; co-investor relationships for Series B/C; talent pipeline from portfolio companies; strategic partnerships in AI infrastructure (compute, evaluation)
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: The 'Reflection 70B' controversy from mid-2024 (a separate company briefly claimed a fake model under a similar name, causing confusion with the real Reflection AI before Chowdhery's company was public)

controversial_take: Reflection AI's implicit claim that a fully open frontier model can beat closed labs is commercially bold — most of the ML community believes the compute and data moats are too large for an open approach to close the gap at the very frontier. The $2B raise suggests investors disagree.
open_problem: How to make RL-at-scale for autonomous software agents stable and compute-efficient enough to beat supervised approaches on production coding tasks without runaway reward hacking.

name: Ion Stoica
title: Professor of Computer Science, UC Berkeley; Director, Sky Computing Lab; Co-founder and Executive Chair, Anyscale
lab: Sky Computing Lab, UC Berkeley
research_focus: AI systems and infrastructure; LLM inference optimization; distributed computing for ML; cloud and heterogeneous computing; high-throughput serving systems; LLM evaluation (Chatbot Arena); AI workload scheduling
direction_of_travel: Distributed systems theory/networking (BGP, Chord DHT) → big data infrastructure (Apache Spark, Mesos, co-founded Databricks 2013) → distributed ML (Ray framework, co-founded Anyscale 2019) → now leading AI inference and evaluation infrastructure (vLLM, Chatbot Arena, SkyPilot, SGLang) via Berkeley's Sky Computing Lab. Produces the most widely deployed open-source AI serving infrastructure in the world.
key_contributions: Co-inventor of Apache Spark — most widely used big data processing framework; underpins Databricks ($43B+ valuation)
Co-creator of Ray — distributed execution framework for AI; nearly every open-source RL framework for post-training LLMs is now built on Ray; joined PyTorch Foundation (Oct 2025)
Co-author of vLLM/PagedAttention (SOSP 2023) — 2-4x throughput improvement for LLM serving; now industry standard for open-source LLM inference
Co-founder of Databricks (2013, $43B+ valuation)
Co-founder of Anyscale (2019) — commercializing Ray for enterprise AI workload orchestration
Chatbot Arena: community-driven LLM evaluation via human preference voting — became standard for ranking frontier models
SGLang: high-performance inference framework achieving 25x performance on NVIDIA GB300 NVL72
Recent systems papers (2024-2026): Qrita (2x GPU throughput), Jenga (4.9x serving throughput), Autellix (4-15x throughput for LLM agent programs)

top_papers.title: Efficient Memory Management for Large Language Model Serving with PagedAttention (vLLM)
top_papers.one_liner: PagedAttention applies OS virtual memory paging to KV cache — 2-4x throughput improvement; de facto standard for open-source LLM serving

top_papers.title: Apache Spark: A Unified Engine for Big Data Processing
top_papers.one_liner: Unified in-memory big data processing framework that displaced MapReduce; foundational to Databricks

top_papers.title: Ray: A Distributed Framework for Emerging AI Applications
top_papers.one_liner: General-purpose distributed execution framework for AI; now underpins nearly all open-source LLM RL post-training frameworks

top_papers.title: Chatbot Arena: An Open Platform for Evaluating LLMs by Human Preference
top_papers.one_liner: Community-driven LLM benchmarking via paired human preference votes; became most trusted public ranking of frontier models

recent_arxiv.title: Qrita: High-performance Top-k and Top-p Algorithm for GPUs using Pivot-based Truncation and Selection
recent_arxiv.summary: 2x throughput and half memory use for sampling operations in LLMs vs. vLLM; targets long tail of inference latency in production serving.

recent_arxiv.title: Jenga: Effective Memory Management for Serving LLM with Heterogeneity
recent_arxiv.summary: Addresses KV cache memory fragmentation in heterogeneous LLM architectures. 79.6% better GPU memory utilization and 4.92x serving throughput.

recent_arxiv.title: Autellix: An Efficient Serving Engine for LLM Agents as General Programs
recent_arxiv.summary: Optimizes agentic LLM workloads by treating agent programs as first-class scheduling citizens. 4-15x throughput improvement vs. state-of-the-art at same latency.

current_projects: Director of Sky Computing Lab at Berkeley producing: vLLM (NVIDIA, Google Cloud, AWS, AMD sponsors — standard for open-source LLM serving); SGLang (25x performance on NVIDIA GB300 NVL72); Chatbot Arena (standard frontier model benchmark); SkyPilot (cross-cloud cost-optimized AI deployment); Ray (PyTorch Foundation, 10x download growth YoY, underpins every major open-source RLHF framework). Executive Chair of Anyscale, commercializing Ray for enterprise.
personal_site_summary: people.eecs.berkeley.edu/~istoica lists Stoica as Professor and Director of Sky Computing Lab. Key active projects: Chatbot Arena, vLLM, SkyPilot, Ray, Apache Spark. Three successful company co-foundings: Conviva (2006), Databricks (2013), Anyscale (2019). Students and postdocs at Stanford, CMU, Michigan, Yale and leadership at OpenAI, Google, Microsoft Research.
notable_quotes: [On Ray's role]: Nearly every open-source RL framework for post-training LLMs is now built on Ray.
[On vLLM's impact]: PagedAttention achieves near-zero waste in KV cache memory — 2-4x throughput improvement at the same latency.
[On AI systems research agenda]: The key challenge is systems that can handle LLM agents as general programs — not just stateless inference requests.
industry_connections: Executive Chair, Anyscale (co-founder, 2019; commercializing Ray); Co-founder, Databricks (2013, $43B+ valuation); Director, Sky Computing Lab (UC Berkeley); NVIDIA sponsor partner via vLLM and SGLang; AWS, Google Cloud, Alibaba Cloud as vLLM sponsors; Ray in PyTorch Foundation; deep connections to OpenAI, Google, Microsoft via former students
commercialization_signal: EXCEPTIONAL — track record of co-founding two unicorns (Databricks at $43B+, Anyscale). Sky Computing Lab continues producing infrastructure enabling the AI stack. Pattern: publish influential open-source system → build commercial company → reach massive scale. Ray is now the substrate for LLM post-training RL. Best approached as a co-investor / LP / strategic relationship rather than a direct investment target.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: He co-created the software that runs most of the world's big data (Apache Spark, now inside Databricks at $43B), then created the framework that almost every AI training system runs on (Ray), and his lab at Berkeley is currently building the plumbing that makes LLMs fast and cheap to serve.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Ray now underpins nearly every open-source RL post-training framework — are you seeing convergence on a standard distributed RL programming model, or fragmentation, and does that shape how Anyscale is thinking about the post-training layer as a product area?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Stoica's lab produces the direct infrastructure enablers of the SPOT workshop's thesis. vLLM makes serving economical. Ray makes distributed RL training accessible. Chatbot Arena provides evaluation. Autellix optimizes agent serving. He has a proven playbook for turning Berkeley infrastructure research into dominant commercial platforms.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Portfolio company introductions for Ray/Anyscale adoption; LP network for future Databricks-style exits; co-investment in Berkeley spinouts; enterprise sales introductions for Anyscale
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Comparisons between Databricks and Snowflake or questions about the data warehouse market — too generic

controversial_take: Stoica's lab has essentially become the infrastructure department for open-source AI, with NVIDIA, Google Cloud, and AWS all sponsoring vLLM — which means the 'neutral academic infrastructure' story is complicated by the fact that his commercial entity (Anyscale) benefits directly from Ray's adoption in the same ecosystem. Feature not bug from an investment perspective.
open_problem: Whether LLM serving infrastructure can be abstracted enough to be cloud-agnostic (the SkyPilot thesis) or whether NVIDIA's CUDA moat and memory bandwidth requirements mean that optimal LLM inference will always be hardware-specific, limiting the cross-cloud arbitrage opportunity.

name: Arnaud Doucet
title: Senior Staff Research Scientist
research_focus: Principled computational methods for generative modeling and Bayesian inference: diffusion models, score matching, discrete masked diffusion, Sequential Monte Carlo, optimal transport, molecular simulation.
direction_of_travel: Sequential Monte Carlo/particle filtering (PhD) → Bayesian nonparametrics → score-based generative models (2020–2022) → discrete diffusion, Feynman-Kac guidance, speculative sampling acceleration, molecular dynamics. Full-time at DeepMind since 2024.
key_contributions: Sequential Monte Carlo / particle filtering (textbook-level foundational work)
Masked diffusion for discrete data — simplified framework (NeurIPS 2024), extended to self-speculative sampling (ICLR 2026)
Feynman-Kac Correctors for principled guided diffusion (ICML 2025 Spotlight)
Particle Denoising Diffusion Sampler (PDDS) for unnormalized densities (ICML 2024)
Score-Optimal Diffusion Schedules — adaptive discretization (NeurIPS 2024)
Schrödinger Bridge Flow for unpaired data translation (2024)
Speculative sampling for diffusion models — ~2x speedup (2025)
Griffin/RecurrentGemma — hybrid RNN-attention architecture deployed at Google (2024)

top_papers.title: Simplified and Generalized Masked Diffusion for Discrete Data
top_papers.one_liner: Unifies masked diffusion into a clean variational framework; beats GPT-2-scale autoregressive models on language and sets CIFAR-10/ImageNet64 records.

top_papers.title: Feynman-Kac Correctors in Diffusion: Annealing, Guidance, and Product of Experts
top_papers.one_liner: SMC resampling enables principled multi-objective and annealed sampling from pretrained diffusion models — ICML 2025 Spotlight.

top_papers.title: Self-Speculative Masked Diffusions
top_papers.one_liner: ~2x fewer forward passes in masked diffusion via causal attention switching — ICLR 2026.

top_papers.title: Accelerated Diffusion Models via Speculative Sampling
top_papers.one_liner: Extends LLM speculative decoding to continuous diffusion, halving NFEs while guaranteeing exact samples.

top_papers.title: Particle Denoising Diffusion Sampler
top_papers.one_liner: Adapts denoising diffusion to sample from unnormalized densities with asymptotic consistency guarantees — ICML 2024.

recent_arxiv.summary: PITA framework for molecular simulation via progressively annealed diffusion — NeurIPS 2025 Spotlight.

recent_arxiv.title: Feynman-Kac Correctors in Diffusion
recent_arxiv.summary: SMC-based framework for composed/annealed diffusion sampling — ICML 2025 Spotlight.

recent_arxiv.title: Accelerated Diffusion Models via Speculative Sampling
recent_arxiv.summary: Parameter-free speculative sampling: halves NFEs, exact sampling guarantee.

recent_arxiv.title: Schrödinger Bridge Flow for Unpaired Data Translation
recent_arxiv.summary: Stable fixed-point computation of Schrödinger bridges, eliminating repeated DDM training.

current_projects: Molecular simulation (Boltzmann sampling, parallel tempering with neural transports); discrete diffusion (masked language/protein models); inference acceleration (speculative sampling, learned solvers); LLM architecture at DeepMind (Griffin/RecurrentGemma).
personal_site_summary: Oxford stats page is minimal — confirms Senior Staff Research Scientist at DeepMind + Visiting Professor at Oxford. Full pivot to industry research.
industry_connections: Google DeepMind (Senior Staff Research Scientist, full-time since 2024). Co-authored Griffin/RecurrentGemma — production LLM architecture deployed within Google.
commercialization_signal: Strong. Griffin/RecurrentGemma already deployed in Google products. Molecular simulation work directly applicable to drug discovery pipelines. Inference acceleration has immediate COGS reduction value.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: He builds the mathematical scaffolding that makes AI image and drug-discovery generators work faster and more reliably — the person who figured out the rules of the engine, not just how to drive the car.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your speculative sampling work halves compute cost in diffusion models — do you think inference-time acceleration is mostly a solved problem now, or are there fundamental limits still to crack?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: His inference acceleration (speculative sampling, optimal schedules), guidance (Feynman-Kac correctors), and molecular simulation (PITA/Boltzmann sampling) directly underpin commercializable diffusion stacks — lower COGS for any diffusion API; Boltzmann sampling = drug discovery.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Portfolio company introductions in computational drug discovery; signal on enterprise diffusion model deployment bottlenecks; LP network with deep biotech exposure.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Asking whether diffusion models will be 'replaced' by flow matching or autoregressive models — he works across all of them and will find the framing naive.

controversial_take: The theoretical justification for classifier-free guidance is essentially wrong — guided diffusion doesn't sample from the intended tilted distribution, it boundary-seeks. The field has been building on a mathematical fiction.
open_problem: How to make the Feynman-Kac/SMC framework for composing diffusion models scalable to production text-to-image resolution and throughput, without the resampling overhead that currently limits it.

name: Kevin Murphy
title: Principal Scientist
lab: Google DeepMind Research
research_focus: Kevin Murphy works on probabilistic machine learning, Bayesian methods, and world models, combining deep learning with principled uncertainty quantification. Recent work includes transformer world models for data-efficient RL, code world models for game playing, diffusion model predictive control, and a comprehensive RL overview. He is also Editor-in-Chief of JMLR.
direction_of_travel: Senior industry researcher at the top of his field; three landmark MIT Press textbooks on probabilistic ML (2012, 2022, 2023) now the dominant reference works. Moving from theoretical foundations into active world model and RL research at DeepMind — ICLR 2026 paper on code world models for game playing shows continued technical depth.
key_contributions: Probabilistic Machine Learning textbook trilogy (MIT Press, 2012/2022/2023) — 130,000+ citations total
Improving Transformer World Models for Data-Efficient RL (2025, arXiv)
Code World Models for General Game Playing (ICLR 2026)
Reinforcement Learning: An Overview (arXiv 2024, comprehensive survey)

top_papers.title: Machine Learning: A Probabilistic Perspective
top_papers.one_liner: The definitive textbook on probabilistic approaches to machine learning — winner of the 2013 DeGroot Prize.

top_papers.title: Probabilistic Machine Learning: An Introduction
top_papers.one_liner: Modern update covering deep learning, Bayesian methods, and generative models from a probabilistic perspective.

top_papers.title: Improving Transformer World Models for Data-Efficient RL
top_papers.one_liner: Achieves new state-of-the-art on Craftax-classic with improved transformer-based world models.

top_papers.title: Code World Models for General Game Playing
top_papers.one_liner: Represents world dynamics as executable code for more generalizable game-playing agents, ICLR 2026.

recent_arxiv.title: Reinforcement Learning: An Overview
recent_arxiv.summary: Comprehensive 200+ page overview of modern RL including model-based RL and world models, actively updated through 2025.

recent_arxiv.title: Improving Transformer World Models for Data-Efficient RL
recent_arxiv.summary: Improves transformer world models achieving SotA on Craftax benchmark for model-based RL.

recent_arxiv.title: Code World Models for General Game Playing
recent_arxiv.summary: Represents game dynamics as executable code programs rather than neural networks, enabling more interpretable and generalizable game agents, ICLR 2026.

current_projects: Transformer world models for RL; code world models; diffusion model predictive control; hierarchical world model planning.
personal_site_summary: Google Research profile listing research interests in machine intelligence and machine perception, with links to books and selected publications.
industry_connections: Principal Scientist at Google DeepMind; Editor-in-Chief of JMLR; his textbooks are the standard reference for virtually every ML graduate program globally.
commercialization_signal: Google DeepMind is the commercial vehicle. Code world models and RL overview work directly informs Google's AI product development.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: He wrote the textbooks that trained an entire generation of AI scientists — now he's actively building the AI systems that can understand and reason about the world through internal simulation.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your code world models paper represents game dynamics as executable programs — do you think this symbolic representation will generalize beyond games to physical world models, or does continuous dynamics require a different approach?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: World models, model-based RL, and probabilistic AI are the exact center of the investment thesis — Murphy is arguably the most credentialed person at this workshop.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Connections to frontier AI companies seeking advisory board members with both theoretical depth and industry credibility; board-level relationships in the AI ecosystem.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Avoid asking about Google's internal competition with OpenAI or Anthropic — focus on the science.

controversial_take: Neural world models trained end-to-end will eventually be superseded by hybrid symbolic-neural systems where dynamics are represented as code — pure neural approaches lack the compositionality needed for general intelligence.
open_problem: Building world models that can simultaneously handle continuous physics, discrete events, and semantic abstractions within a single unified representation.

name: Sanjiv Kumar
title: Vice President, Google Fellow
lab: Google DeepMind, New York
research_focus: Sanjiv Kumar leads research on large foundation models, LLM efficiency, and Gemini development at Google DeepMind. His work spans training and inference efficiency for LLMs, optimization algorithms (with landmark contributions to adaptive methods like Adam), large-scale retrieval and similarity search, and rethinking compute paradigms for faster model training and deployment.
direction_of_travel: Reached the apex of academic-industry research leadership as a Google Fellow and VP; continues to shape the field through high-impact publications and by mentoring a generation of Google researchers, with Gemini development as his primary current focus.
key_contributions: Co-authored 'On the Convergence of Adam and Beyond' (ICLR 2018 Best Paper), the theoretical foundation for adaptive optimization
Developed ScaNN, Google's open-source billion-scale similarity search engine powering major Google products
Led research on speculative decoding for cascade inference (ICLR 2025 Honorable Mention), improving LLM serving efficiency

top_papers.one_liner: Identified convergence issues in Adam and proposed AMSGrad fix; ICLR 2018 Best Paper, foundational to modern optimizer design

top_papers.title: Faster Cascades via Speculative Decoding

current_projects: Co-organizing LIT workshop at ICLR 2026; leading Gemini model research and development; advancing LLM inference efficiency and optimization theory at Google DeepMind

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Sanjiv is essentially the head of machine learning research efficiency at Google DeepMind—he co-created the Adam optimizer theory, built Google's similarity search engine, and now leads efforts to make Gemini faster and cheaper to run.

controversial_take: Optimizer choice matters far more than most practitioners believe—the fact that Adam remains dominant after 10 years suggests the community has underinvested in optimizer research relative to architecture research.
open_problem: Is there a fundamental information-theoretic lower bound on inference cost for reasoning tasks, and how close are current speculative decoding approaches to that bound?

commercialization_signal: Very high—Matryoshka Representation Learning is deployed in major retrieval systems; MatFormer enables elastic serving of Gemini; directly involved in Gemini product development

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Prateek invented the idea that you can train one model to work at many different sizes simultaneously—Matryoshka embeddings let you search with less compute while keeping quality, and he's now applying the same idea to model quantization and post-training RL scaling.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: With Matryoshka Quantization enabling elastic precision, do you think the next step is dynamic per-token or per-layer precision selection at inference time, and does this require changes to the post-training recipe or just the serving stack?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Core to inference-time scaling and LLM post-training: the Matryoshka principle applied to quantization and inference is one of the most practically impactful approaches to efficient model serving; his ScaleRL work directly addresses post-training RL compute scaling.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to enterprise retrieval infrastructure companies using MRL; investor perspective on elastic serving startups; portfolio connections in India's AI ecosystem
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Avoid asking about his time at Microsoft Research India—he has fully moved on and focuses on Google work

controversial_take: The industry's obsession with model size as the primary scaling axis ignores that embedding dimensionality and quantization precision are equally important for real-world deployment—Matryoshka thinking should be applied everywhere, not just as a retrieval trick.
open_problem: Can Matryoshka-style nesting be applied to transformer depth (not just width), enabling elastic depth at inference time—and if so, what training objective achieves this without compromising quality at any depth?

His current and recent work spans elastic inference for LLMs (MatFormer), scalable RL post-training, extreme multi-label classification, and scalable nearest-neighbor search—combining rigorous theory with practical deployment at scale.

commercialization_signal: Extremely high—Google Fellow and VP with direct product impact; former Amazon VP; DiskANN deployed at production scale; MatFormer enables Gemini elastic serving; extensive track record of commercially impactful research

before_you_walk_up.informed_question: With DiskANN becoming the standard for vector search and MatFormer enabling elastic LLM serving, do you see a convergence between retrieval-augmented generation and elastic inference—essentially an elastic retrieval+generation stack?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Core to LLM post-training and inference-time scaling: his student's ScaleRL paper addresses RL scaling; MatFormer directly enables efficient serving; his network spans Google, Amazon, Microsoft, and UT Austin—invaluable for understanding industry direction.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to LP-level investors interested in academic-to-industry research bridges; co-investment in startups he's advising; partnership opportunities with UT Austin ML Lab
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Avoid asking about Amazon vs. Google competitive dynamics—he's navigated this carefully and won't engage

controversial_take: The trillion-parameter model race is wasteful—the real frontier is in algorithms that achieve the same capabilities with 10x fewer parameters through better optimization and architecture design, and this has been true since the 1990s.
open_problem: What is the right theoretical framework for understanding when elastic inference (MatFormer-style) preserves capability across compression levels, and can we predict the capability-compute frontier from training alone?

name: Samy Bengio
title: Senior Director, AI and Machine Learning Research
lab: Apple Machine Learning Research
research_focus: Fundamental limits of transformer reasoning, length generalization, the 'globality barrier' in autoregressive models, RL for reasoning, curriculum learning for sequence generation
direction_of_travel: Moved from speech/audio ML and deep learning fundamentals → understanding transformer reasoning limits → now focused on why and when LLMs fail at reasoning, and how RL/curriculum methods can extend reasoning capabilities
key_contributions: GSM-Symbolic: exposed mathematical reasoning fragility in LLMs (symbol substitution breaks performance)
The Illusion of Thinking: identified three performance regimes and complete accuracy collapse in Large Reasoning Models at high complexity
How Far Can Transformers Reason? (NeurIPS 2024): formalized the Globality Barrier—a fundamental limit on transformer in-context reasoning
What Algorithms Can Transformers Learn? (ICLR 2024): characterized length generalization failures
AdaBack (RL for Reasoning): adaptive curriculum that reveals rationales incrementally to bridge SFT and RL
AbstRaL: reinforcing abstract thinking in LLMs via RL
Co-led TorchVision / early deep learning infrastructure; adjunct professor at EPFL
General Chair NeurIPS 2018

top_papers.title: The Illusion of Thinking: Understanding the Strengths and Limitations of Reasoning Models via the Lens of Problem Complexity
top_papers.one_liner: LRMs have a hard accuracy ceiling—reasoning effort peaks then collapses at high complexity, revealing fundamental algorithmic limits

top_papers.one_liner: Swapping variable names in math word problems degrades LLM accuracy, showing pattern-matching not genuine reasoning

top_papers.title: How Far Can Transformers Reason? The Globality Barrier and Inductive Scratchpad

current_projects: Apple MLR: investigating fundamental reasoning limits of transformer architectures, building RL-based methods to extend reasoning via curriculum learning, developing agentic evaluation benchmarks (ASTRA-bench), studying the globality barrier in inference-time compute scaling
personal_site_summary: Senior Director at Apple MLR; adjunct professor at EPFL. Site lists recent keynote talks on reasoning limits of LLMs (2025: Cornell, EEML, IJCNN, CMU; 2024: NeurIPS, Deep Learning Indaba). Mentored Ronan Collobert, David Grangier among others. Early contributor to Torch/TensorFlow; sponsors Apple's MLX framework for Apple Silicon.
notable_quotes: LRMs exhibit a counterintuitive scaling limit: their reasoning effort increases with problem complexity up to a point, then declines despite having remaining token budget.
LRMs face a complete accuracy collapse beyond certain complexities.
The Illusion of Thinking—extended reasoning traces may appear sophisticated while concealing actual capability limits.
industry_connections: Senior Director at Apple Machine Learning Research (industry role); adjunct professor at EPFL. Previously Google Brain. Sponsored MLX open-source framework for Apple Silicon ML. Strong ties to Apple's LLM and on-device inference teams.
commercialization_signal: MEDIUM — Deep inside Apple (non-founding role), but his work on reasoning limits directly informs Apple Intelligence product decisions. No known startup founding or advisory roles outside Apple. His reasoning benchmarks (GSM-Symbolic, ASTRA-bench) could inform evaluation tooling startups.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: He proves mathematically where AI reasoning models hit a wall—and then builds training tricks to push past it.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your Globality Barrier work suggests scratchpads are necessary but not sufficient—do you think looped/recurrent architectures can escape the barrier, or is it a training data problem?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Direct relevance to inference-time compute thesis: he is characterizing the hard limits of chain-of-thought and extended thinking, and building RL-based training (AdaBack, AbstRaL) that could define the next generation of reasoning models.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Portfolio company introductions for evaluation tooling; access to enterprise customers who need reliable reasoning guarantees; co-investment signals if he ever spins out from Apple.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Comparisons to his brother Yoshua Bengio—he has built a completely independent research identity.

controversial_take: Reasoning models exhibit an 'illusion of thinking'—extended token budgets do not translate to genuine algorithmic problem-solving, and current LRMs fundamentally cannot perform exact computation regardless of chain-of-thought length.
open_problem: Can RL-based curriculum methods (adaptive rationale revelation) fundamentally extend what transformers can compute, or is the Globality Barrier a hard architectural limit that requires recurrent/looped designs to escape?

name: Beidi Chen
title: Assistant Professor
lab: Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, CMU; Visiting Researcher at Meta FAIR
research_focus: Algorithm-hardware co-design for efficient ML; sparse attention and inference acceleration; Monarch structured matrices; efficient generative inference; RL training infrastructure for LLMs; real-time video generation
direction_of_travel: Moved from randomized algorithms and LSH (SLIDE, MONGOOSE) → sparse/structured matrices for training efficiency (Monarch, Pixelated Butterfly) → now focused on efficient inference at scale (H2O, Deja Vu, ThunderAgent) and real-time generation (MonarchRT)
key_contributions: Monarch matrices (ICML 2022 Outstanding Paper Runner-Up): structured matrices that replace dense layers with hardware-efficient butterfly products
H2O: Heavy-Hitter Oracle for efficient KV cache eviction during LLM inference (NeurIPS 2023)
Deja Vu: contextual sparsity for efficient LLM inference (ICML 2023 Oral)
FlexGen: high-throughput LLM inference on single GPU via offloading (ICML 2023 Oral)
MonarchRT: 95% attention sparsity for real-time video generation at 16 FPS (2026)
ThunderAgent: program-aware agentic inference with 1.5-3.6x throughput gains (2026)
Jackpot: optimal budgeted rejection sampling for decoupled RL training (2026)
SLIDE (2019): first hash-based neural network training that beats GPU dense layers
