current_projects: Inference-time efficiency at CMU: extending Monarch to video generation (MonarchRT), building ThunderAgent for production agentic inference, Jackpot RL training infrastructure, scaling sparse attention to multimodal models. Visiting Meta FAIR for industry collaboration on efficiency.
personal_site_summary: Assistant Professor at CMU ECE since Fall 2023; visiting researcher at Meta FAIR. PhD from Rice under Anshumali Shrivastava; postdoc at Stanford with Chris Ré. Prior internships at Microsoft Research, NVIDIA, Apple, AWS. Research anchored in randomized algorithms + hardware co-design for efficient deep learning.
notable_quotes: The key insight behind Monarch is that structured matrices can be hardware-friendly AND expressive—you don't have to choose.
Contextual sparsity means different inputs activate different parts of the network—the model itself tells you which neurons to skip.
industry_connections: Visiting Researcher at Meta FAIR (active). Prior internships: Microsoft Research, NVIDIA, Apple, AWS, A9.com, VMware. PhD advisor Anshumali Shrivastava co-founded ThirdAI (LSH-based efficient ML startup). Strong connections to hardware efficiency community at NVIDIA and AMD.
commercialization_signal: HIGH — Monarch matrices are foundational to Together AI's training stack and have been adopted in Mamba/SSM research. H2O KV cache eviction technique has direct commercial inference applications. Her work is directly productizable for inference cost reduction. Visiting Meta FAIR role suggests active industry partnership. Likely future startup or advisory role in inference infrastructure.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: She builds the hardware tricks that make giant AI models cheap to run—cutting memory and compute by 5-10x without losing accuracy.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: With Jackpot showing you can decouple rollout from policy in RL training—do you think the inference efficiency gains compound with the training efficiency, or are they fundamentally separate optimization problems?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Core to inference-time compute thesis: her work on KV cache eviction (H2O), contextual sparsity (Deja Vu), and now agentic inference (ThunderAgent) directly enables the economics of extended thinking and multi-step reasoning at scale.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to enterprise inference infrastructure buyers; connections to GPU-cloud providers (Lambda, CoreWeave) as customers for ThunderAgent-style technology; potential co-investment signal if she spins out.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Being asked only about Monarch in isolation—she's moved significantly beyond that into a unified inference efficiency research program.

controversial_take: Most LLM inference optimization is done wrong—treating sparsity and hardware separately. True efficiency requires algorithm-hardware co-design from the ground up, which is why most 'efficient attention' papers don't actually run faster on real GPUs.
open_problem: Can structured sparsity (Monarch-style) scale to multimodal real-time generation without quality-efficiency tradeoffs, and can the same principles unify training and inference efficiency in a single architecture?

name: Sean Welleck
affiliation: Carnegie Mellon University, Language Technologies Institute
title: Assistant Professor
lab: L3 Lab (Language Learning & Logical Lab)
research_focus: Machine learning for mathematics and formal reasoning, with emphasis on neural theorem proving in Lean, test-time inference scaling, and AI-assisted mathematical proof development. Co-director of NSF Institute for Computer-Aided Reasoning in Mathematics (announced Aug 2025).
direction_of_travel: Moved from NLP/language model foundations → mathematical reasoning with LLMs → now squarely focused on formal verification in Lean (hammers, premise selection, proof optimization, blueprint tools). The arc is: general LM → math LM (Llemma) → Lean-specific infrastructure (miniCTX, LeanHammer, AlphaVerus, ImProver).
key_contributions: Llemma: first open math LLM capable of formal theorem proving in Lean without additional fine-tuning (2023)
LeanHammer: first end-to-end domain-general automated hammer for Lean proof assistant, solving 21% more goals than prior selectors (2025)
AlphaVerus: self-improving framework generating formally verified code (Verus) without human data via iterative translation + verifier feedback (2024)
miniCTX: benchmark for neural theorem proving with long real-world Lean contexts (2024)
Lean-STaR: training LMs to interleave informal thoughts and formal proof steps, SOTA on miniF2F (2024)
Inference Scaling Laws: showed smaller models + tree search outperform larger models alone for math reasoning (2024)

current_projects: NSF Institute for Computer-Aided Reasoning in Mathematics (co-director, Aug 2025). LeanHammer (deployed open-source Lean tool). Active VerifAI-2 workshop organization. Advanced NLP course at CMU. L3 Lab with 4 PhD students focused on Lean, inference scaling, and AI agents for math.
personal_site_summary: Wellecks.com presents a clear identity as the leading academic builder of Lean proof infrastructure for AI. The site highlights Lean-specific tools (LeanHammer, miniCTX, AlphaVerus, ImProver, LeanArchitect) as flagship outputs, signals NSF Institute co-directorship as major institutional commitment, and lists a Thesis Review podcast suggesting community building beyond just research. No startup or commercialization activity visible.
notable_quotes: On data for math AI: current benchmarks 'fail to capture the full reasoning process behind proofs' and suffer Goodhart's Law — as models optimize for them, they lose reliability as capability measures. (co-author, Dec 2024)
industry_connections: Past internships at Facebook AI Research (2018-2019) and Primer AI (2016). Recent invited talks at NVIDIA (Feb 2025), Microsoft Research, Google, Cohere.ai. No current startup or advisory roles identified. Co-director of NSF Institute for Computer-Aided Reasoning in Mathematics — major academic grant, not commercial.
commercialization_signal: Low-to-medium. All tools (LeanHammer, AlphaVerus, miniCTX, ImProver) released open-source. NSF Institute role signals long-term academic commitment. However, the tooling he is building (automated Lean hammers, verified code generation) maps directly onto commercial applications in software verification and safety-critical code. No current startup. Could be early for a spinout.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: He's building the AI tools that let machines write and check mathematical proofs — essentially creating an autopilot for formal verification in the Lean proof assistant.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: LeanHammer closes a big gap in Lean usability — are you seeing traction from the broader Lean community (like Mathlib contributors), and do you think the hammer approach scales to research-level mathematics?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Welleck is ground zero for the thesis. He's building the full stack: open math LLMs (Llemma), benchmarks (miniCTX, miniCodeProps), tools (LeanHammer, ImProver, AlphaVerus), and now an NSF Institute. Every company building on AI + Lean will use or compete with his infrastructure.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Early customers for LeanHammer-based tooling in safety-critical software (aerospace, semiconductors, finance). Intros to companies that need formally verified code generation. Funding to spin out the Lean toolchain commercially if he's ever interested. Access to compute for scaling experiments.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Asking whether LLMs can 'just solve' math without formal methods — he has clearly chosen the formal verification path and won't be persuaded otherwise.

controversial_take: Existing math AI benchmarks are inadequate and actively misleading due to Goodhart's Law — the field needs 'motivated proof' datasets capturing the discovery process, not just finished proofs.
open_problem: Building a fully automated, domain-general 'hammer' for Lean that can close proof obligations at research-mathematics level — extending LeanHammer from premise selection to full proof synthesis for hard goals.

name: Armando Solar-Lezama

Broad portfolio spanning robotics, code understanding, mathematical DSLs, and social AI.
direction_of_travel: Originated program synthesis (Sketch tool, 2005-2020). Moved toward ML-integrated synthesis and neurosymbolic AI (2018-2022). Now shifted to AI for software engineering as organizing theme, with formal verification as one pillar among many. Less personally hands-on with Lean specifically; more focused on the broader challenge of AI that understands programs.
key_contributions: Sketch: seminal program synthesis tool based on partial programs and SAT-based completion (2006-2020); foundational to the field
CRUXEval: benchmark for code reasoning, understanding, and execution (2024); revealed limits of code LLMs on basic reasoning tasks
LiveCodeBench: contamination-free continuous evaluation platform for code LLMs (2024)
MathDSL: domain-specific language for equation solving via program synthesis, outperforming RL approaches (2024)
Counterfeit Conundrum: demonstrated that code LLMs systematically fail to detect their own incorrect outputs (2024)
Challenges and Paths Towards AI for Software Engineering: 75-page field-defining position paper (2025)

top_papers.one_liner: 800-function benchmark showing GPT-4 achieves only 75-81% on basic code input/output prediction tasks.

top_papers.title: Challenges and Paths Towards AI for Software Engineering
top_papers.one_liner: 75-page field taxonomy arguing AI for SE must go far beyond code generation to verification, repair, and understanding.

top_papers.title: The Counterfeit Conundrum: Can Code Language Models Grasp the Nuances of Their Incorrect Generations?
top_papers.one_liner: Code LLMs systematically misclassify plausible-but-wrong code as correct — a fundamental limitation for self-repair.

recent_arxiv.title: Challenges and Paths Towards AI for Software Engineering

recent_arxiv.title: The Counterfeit Conundrum: Can Code Language Models Grasp the Nuances of Their Incorrect Generations?
recent_arxiv.summary: Three failure modes identified: misclassification of wrong code as correct, poor execution prediction, and low self-repair success rates.

current_projects: Computer Assisted Programming Group at MIT CSAIL. AI for software engineering research agenda (with Jain, Gu, Sen). Neurosymbolic programming. Associate Director/COO of CSAIL since 2020 — significant administrative role.
personal_site_summary: The MIT page is sparse — lists affiliation, course offerings (Program Synthesis, Foundations of Program Analysis), and Sketch. Does not highlight recent papers prominently. Contrasts with very active paper output.
notable_quotes: On code LLMs: models 'mistakenly classify [plausible-but-wrong programs] as correct' — a fundamental barrier to self-improving code AI. (Counterfeit Conundrum, 2024)
Implicit in field-defining paper: AI for SE must go 'beyond code generation and completion' to address the full software lifecycle including verification and repair. (2025 position paper)
industry_connections: Deep academic ties to industry through student placement. No current startup or advisory roles publicly identified. CSAIL has broad industry sponsors (Google, Microsoft, Amazon). Past collaboration with Meta/FAIR researchers.
commercialization_signal: Low-to-medium direct commercialization. LiveCodeBench is actively maintained and used by major labs. The 2025 AI for SE position paper co-authored with Cursor-affiliated researcher Naman Jain suggests awareness of commercial deployment contexts.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: He invented the field of program synthesis and is now studying why current AI coding tools fail at understanding their own mistakes — which is the core problem for any AI system that needs to verify its own outputs.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your Counterfeit Conundrum paper showed LLMs can't reliably detect their own wrong code — do you think formal verification is the only reliable exit from that loop, or can learned verification work?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Solar-Lezama's work on code LLM evaluation directly motivates why formal verification is needed: LLMs can't self-validate. His program synthesis background gives him credibility on the formal methods side, and the AI for SE position paper signals where the field is heading commercially.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Industry deployment context for his benchmarks. Introductions to safety-critical software customers who need the verification capabilities his lab researches. Funding for student spinouts from his group.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Sketch — it's legacy work he's moved past. Also avoid asking him to predict when AGI will solve programming.

controversial_take: LLMs fundamentally cannot serve as their own validators — the Counterfeit Conundrum shows three systematic failure modes that won't be fixed by scale alone, making external verification (formal or otherwise) a necessity, not an option.
open_problem: Building AI systems for software engineering that go beyond code generation to full-lifecycle automation: understanding, repair, optimization, and verification — with the Counterfeit Conundrum as a specific unsolved bottleneck.

name: Charlotte Bunne
title: Assistant Professor, AIMM Lab
lab: AIMM — Artificial Intelligence in Molecular Medicine, EPFL
research_focus: Generative models and optimal transport for computational biology. Core themes: (1) learning cellular state transitions and perturbation responses using Schrödinger bridges and conditional optimal transport; (2) causal modeling of gene regulatory networks from single-cell data; (3) foundation models for spatial proteomics (VirTues); (4) virtual cell construction; (5) clinical AI for oncology (MTBBench). Unifying thread: geometric/probabilistic ML to build predictive models of biological systems.
direction_of_travel: Moving from methodological OT/diffusion papers toward deploying foundation models at clinical scale. VirTues (spatial proteomics foundation model) and MTBBench (oncology benchmark) signal push toward translational impact. Causal turn with Latent Causal Diffusions (January 2026, co-authored with Bernhard Schölkopf and Caroline Uhler) bridges generative modeling and causal discovery — she can now claim to answer 'why does this drug work?' not just 'what will this drug do?'
key_contributions: CondOT (NeurIPS 2022): Supervised training of conditional Monge maps — learns families of OT maps conditioned on treatment to predict single-cell perturbation responses
Unbalanced Diffusion Schrödinger Bridge (2023): Extends Schrödinger bridges to handle changing population mass (cell death/proliferation)
Virtual Cell position paper (2024, 42 co-authors including Aviv Regev, Jure Leskovec, Stephen Quake): Roadmap for AI-driven virtual cell construction
VirTues (2025): Foundation model for spatial proteomics — predicts immunotherapy response in breast cancer; outperforms clinical biomarkers
Latent Causal Diffusions (2026): LCD framework bridging generative modeling and causal gene regulatory inference; co-authored with Schölkopf and Uhler
MTBBench (NeurIPS 2025): Multimodal benchmark for oncology AI simulating molecular tumor board decisions

recent_arxiv.summary: VirTues foundation model for spatial proteomics: marker reconstruction, cell typing, niche identification; outperforms clinical biomarkers for immunotherapy response

current_projects: Virtual cell modeling — building AI systems simulating cellular responses to genetic/therapeutic perturbations at scale. Causal inference from single-cell screens (LCD/CLIPR). Clinical spatial proteomics deploying VirTues for treatment response prediction. MTBBench extension for multimodal oncology AI evaluation. AIMM Lab at EPFL bridges geometric/probabilistic ML methodology with direct biomedical application.
personal_site_summary: AIMM Lab site (aimm.epfl.ch) confirmed active. Lab full name: Artificial Intelligence in Molecular Medicine. Academic lineage: PhD at ETH Zurich under Andreas Krause and Marco Cuturi, postdoc at Stanford (Leskovec group). Strong connection to Schölkopf causal ML community (MPI Tübingen).
notable_quotes: Virtual cell paper positions her as a convener of the AI-for-biology movement, not just a methods contributor — writing with Regev, Leskovec, and Quake signals field-leadership level well before tenure
industry_connections: No disclosed startup founding or advisory roles. Strong academic network feeding industry: co-authored virtual cell roadmap with Aviv Regev (Genentech/Broad), Jure Leskovec (Stanford), Stephen Quake (Chan Zuckerberg Initiative). Collaborators include Bernhard Schölkopf (MPI, Causality), Caroline Uhler (MIT, causal ML), Andreas Krause (ETH, PhD advisor).
commercialization_signal: Moderate-to-high — no startup yet, but VirTues (clinical tissue AI) and virtual cell work are in the sweet spot for spinout or partnership. VirTues results (outperforming clinical biomarkers for immunotherapy response in breast cancer) are clinical-grade. Watch for spinout in 2026-2027 or CZI/pharma-funded collaborations.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Charlotte builds AI models that predict how drugs and genetic interventions change cell behavior — essentially a simulator for human biology that could transform how drugs are discovered.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: The LCD paper bridges generative modeling and causal gene regulatory inference — do you think the causal structure you're recovering from perturbation screens is rich enough to drive target identification, or is this primarily a mechanistic annotation tool for now?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: She's at the center of the AI virtual cell thesis — the idea that a foundation model trained on enough cellular perturbation data can simulate biology in silico and replace many early-stage drug discovery experiments. VirTues is already showing clinical-grade predictive performance.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to biotech/pharma investors who understand cell biology AI (Dimension Capital, Andreessen Bio, ARCH); context on the competitive landscape vs. Genentech/DeepMind approaches; potential clinical partners for VirTues validation in additional oncology indications.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: AlphaFold comparisons — her work is on perturbation response and causal dynamics, not static structure prediction. The comparison is reductive.

controversial_take: Most 'foundation models for biology' papers are benchmarked on tasks that are easy to overfit — they don't test predicting cellular response to novel combinations of perturbations never seen in training. The field is 3-5 years behind on evaluation rigor.
open_problem: Whether causal gene regulatory networks recovered from perturbation screens are stable enough across cell types and disease contexts to serve as the foundation for a truly generalizable virtual cell — or whether regulatory wiring is so context-dependent that each cell type requires its own model.

name: Rishabh Agarwal
title: Founder/Researcher, Periodic Labs; Adjunct Professor, McGill University
research_focus: Post-training for LLMs via RL; process reward models and generative verifiers; test-time compute scaling; sample-efficient offline RL; knowledge distillation; scalable RLHF infrastructure; LLM reasoning and self-correction
direction_of_travel: Sample-efficient offline deep RL (Atari, D4RL) → RL for LLMs at Google Brain/DeepMind (RLHF, self-improvement, self-correction) → now founding Periodic Labs to commercialize post-training RL infrastructure and LLM reasoning capabilities; one of the world's most prolific post-training researchers with 7 ICLR 2025 papers alone
key_contributions: Pioneered sample-efficient offline RL with work on D4RL (NeurIPS Outstanding Paper Award)
SCoRe: multi-turn RL enabling LLM self-correction — 15.6% improvement on MATH, 9.1% on HumanEval
Generative Verifiers (GenRM): reward modeling as next-token prediction — 5% to 45.3% gains on algorithmic tasks
ThinkPRM: generative process reward model needing only 1% of process labels while outperforming discriminative PRMs
ReST-EM: self-training with EM significantly surpasses fine-tuning on human data alone for math/code
Many-shot in-context learning (hundreds-to-thousands examples) — overrides pretraining biases
Asynchronous RLHF: ~40% faster RLHF training by decoupling generation from learning
RL^V: jointly training LLMs as reasoners and verifiers for 8-32x efficient test-time compute scaling
ScaleRL: 400,000+ GPU-hour study establishing frameworks for predictable RL scaling for LLMs

current_projects: Founder of Periodic Labs (stealth startup). Given research arc concentrated on process reward models, test-time scaling, async RLHF, and scalable RL post-training, Periodic Labs is likely building tooling or services around RL-based post-training pipelines and/or verifier/reward model infrastructure. Company is early-stage; no public product, funding, or team details disclosed. Maintains Adjunct Professor role at McGill.
personal_site_summary: agarwl.github.io lists Agarwal as founding member at Periodic Labs and Adjunct Professor at McGill. Research interests listed as 'RL and LLMs' — verification, reasoning, post-training distillation, speculative decoding. Seven papers at ICLR 2025. PhD from Mila (Courville + Bellemare). Mentored researchers now at OpenAI, Google DeepMind, xAI, UC Berkeley.
notable_quotes: [From ScaleRL]: 400,000+ GPU hours establishing that RL scaling for LLMs follows predictable trajectories when using the right recipe.

[From Shape of Thought]: Language models improve reasoning by training on incorrect chain-of-thought traces from stronger models when the distribution is inherently closer to the LM's own distribution.
[From Asynchronous RLHF]: Separating generation and learning achieves ~40% faster RLHF without performance degradation.
[From RL^V]: Jointly training as reasoner and verifier enables 8-32x efficient test-time compute scaling.
industry_connections: Founder of Periodic Labs (stealth); formerly Staff Research Scientist at Google DeepMind and researcher at Meta; PhD from Mila/McGill; Adjunct Professor at McGill; mentored researchers now leading ML at OpenAI, xAI, Google DeepMind, UC Berkeley.
commercialization_signal: STRONG — founded Periodic Labs, likely building post-training infrastructure or RL tooling. The combination of Agarwal's depth in process reward models, async RLHF, test-time compute scaling, and practical RL recipes positions the company to be picks-and-shovels for the RLHF/post-training ecosystem. Company is stealth but represents one of the strongest post-training research pedigrees in the world attempting commercialization.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: He's one of the world's leading researchers on how to train AI models after their initial training is done — specifically using reinforcement learning — and he's now turning that expertise into a startup.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your ScaleRL paper found predictable RL scaling trajectories — is Periodic Labs building infrastructure to make those recipes accessible to labs without 400,000 GPU hours of experimentation budget, and how are you thinking about whether that's a platform play versus a services wedge?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Post-training RL is the central technical challenge of the current AI moment. Agarwal is the person who has mapped more of the empirical territory of RL for LLMs than almost anyone. Periodic Labs is a direct bet on that research becoming infrastructure.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Early enterprise customer introductions (model providers, AI-native companies needing post-training capabilities); co-investor relationships; talent pipeline; connections to compute providers for research compute credits
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Direct comparisons to OpenAI's RLHF work or requests to compare his approach to what Anthropic does with Constitutional AI

controversial_take: Agarwal's 'Shape of Thought' paper argues that training on incorrect reasoning traces from stronger models can outperform training on correct traces — this directly challenges the dominant 'only train on verified correct data' orthodoxy behind most current post-training pipelines including DeepSeek-R1's recipe.
open_problem: Whether process reward models can be made sufficiently scalable and generalizable to serve as the universal post-training signal across domains beyond math and code, or whether domain-specific reward engineering remains unavoidable.

A startup building LLM-assisted Dafny/Lean verification for safety-critical software would be directly in his wheelhouse. Michigan faculty role starting Fall 2026 creates a natural moment for a spinout or industry collaboration.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: He builds AI that can teach itself mathematics from scratch — starting from axioms and discovering proofs without any human examples to learn from.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: In Minimo, the agent learns from intrinsic motivation starting from axioms — do you think the next step is scaling to Lean 4, or does the type-theory substrate need to change first?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Poesia is the most technically deep researcher on verifiable formal reasoning as a learning substrate — Minimo is exactly the 'verifiable RL environments' theme of VerifAI 2026. Dafny and TacMiner show practical paths to tool-grade formal verification.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to companies adopting Dafny/formal verification (AWS, Microsoft Azure, safety-critical software); GPU compute for scaling intrinsic-motivation experiments.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Don't lead with 'can LLMs do math?' — he's past that and focused on formal certification and intrinsic improvement.

controversial_take: Formal verification is the only approach that can ensure programs work in all cases — and the barrier is not theoretical but practical (annotation burden), which AI can now systematically remove.
open_problem: Automating the full annotation pipeline for formal verification tools (Dafny, Lean) so that non-expert programmers can get mathematical correctness guarantees on their code without manual specification work.

name: Hao Su
title: Associate Professor, CSE, UCSD; Chairman & CTO, Hillbot Inc.
lab: SU Lab (UC San Diego CSE)
research_focus: Autonomous systems that learn continuously in the physical world. 3D computer vision, robot simulation, foundation models for manipulation. SU Lab created PointNet (canonical 3D deep learning architecture) and ManiSkill (open-source robot manipulation benchmark/simulator). At Hillbot, commercializing general-purpose robots using simulation-to-real transfer.
direction_of_travel: From 3D deep learning and point cloud representation (PointNet) → large-scale robot simulation (ManiSkill, SAPIEN) → foundation models for robotic manipulation → startup (Hillbot, Aug 2024) commercializing general-purpose mobile manipulation.
key_contributions: PointNet (2017): first deep learning architecture operating directly on 3D point clouds — foundational to all modern 3D AI
PointNet++ (2017): hierarchical extension capturing local geometric structures at multiple scales
ManiSkill: open-source robot manipulation benchmark and GPU-accelerated simulator
SAPIEN: simulation platform for robot learning with rich articulated object support
X-Embodiment: best paper ICRA 2024 — cross-embodiment robot learning
Co-founded Hillbot Inc. (Aug 2024): general-purpose robot company

current_projects: Hillbot Mark One robot development — leveraging ManiSkill sim and synthetic data pipelines for generalizable robotic skills. Advancing cross-embodiment foundation models (RDT2) and scalable physics simulation (M-ABD) at SU Lab.
personal_site_summary: UCSD page: 'theories and technologies to build autonomous systems that can actively and continuously learn in the physical world.' Three pillars: CV/graphics foundation models, ML/AI creativity, robotics mobile manipulation. Recently updated to highlight Hillbot co-founding.
notable_quotes: The important thing is not to stop questioning. Curiosity has its own reason for existence. (Einstein quote displayed on homepage — reflecting his research philosophy)
ManiSkill3 enables simulation with rendering 10-1000x faster than other platforms with 2-3x less GPU memory usage
industry_connections: Chairman & CTO of Hillbot Inc. (founded Aug 2024). Hillbot uses ManiSkill and SAPIEN infrastructure. Academic collaborations with Google, Meta, Microsoft on large-scale 3D and robot datasets.
commercialization_signal: STRONG — Chairman & CTO of Hillbot Inc. (founded Aug 2024), actively building humanoid robot platform using ManiSkill sim. Rare case of top researcher directly commercializing foundational work. PointNet (18K+ citations) → ManiSkill3 (10-1000x faster sim) → Hillbot general-purpose robots.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: He wrote the foundational algorithm for understanding 3D shapes with AI (PointNet — 18,000 citations), and now he's using it to build robots that can manipulate any object by training them in a virtual world first. He just co-founded Hillbot in 2024.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: With Hillbot using ManiSkill for synthetic training data, how much of the sim-to-real gap do you think is a data diversity problem versus a physics fidelity problem — and is M-ABD's scalable simulation the key missing piece?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Su sits at the intersection of physical AI (Hillbot general-purpose robot) and simulation/world models (ManiSkill, SAPIEN, M-ABD). PointNet's architectural lineage is directly relevant to 3D world model representations.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Customer introductions for Hillbot in manufacturing or logistics; access to proprietary 3D object datasets; connections to industrial automation players considering robotic manipulation deployments.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Avoid asking about PointNet vs. newer transformer-based 3D architectures — he's moved on to systems-level robotics.

controversial_take: Synthetic simulation data at scale, not more expensive real-robot demonstrations, is the right path to generalizable robotic manipulation — the sim-to-real gap is an engineering problem, not a fundamental barrier.
open_problem: How to build a robot that can generalize zero-shot to novel objects, environments, and tasks using only simulation-trained policies.

name: Jeff Clune
affiliation: University of British Columbia / Google DeepMind
title: Associate Professor, UBC; Senior Research Scientist (part-time), Google DeepMind
lab: Adaptive and Intelligent Robotics Lab (UBC); Google DeepMind
research_focus: Open-endedness — building AI that continuously generates novel, increasingly complex behaviors without fixed goals, analogous to biological evolution. Key work: quality-diversity (MAP-Elites), AI-Generating Algorithms (AI-GAs), Go-Explore for hard exploration, and Evolution through Large Models (LLMs as evolutionary engines).
direction_of_travel: Evolutionary computation and neuroevolution (2008–2015) → deep RL and exploration (Go-Explore, 2019) → meta-learning and AI-Generating Algorithms (2019–2021) → open-endedness with foundation models as evolutionary engines (2022–present).
key_contributions: Darwin Gödel Machine (2025): self-modifying agent that improved SWE-bench from 20%→50% through open-ended evolution — first practical demonstration of RSI
The AI Scientist v2 (2025): first AI-generated paper to pass peer review at a major ML conference — full research loop automation
Go-Explore (2019): first algorithm to solve Montezuma's Revenge (>43K score) — broke the hard exploration barrier in RL
AI-Generating Algorithms (AI-GAs, 2019): meta-learning where AI designs its own learning algorithm
Evolution through Large Models (ELM, 2022): LLMs as evolutionary mutation operators generating functional walking robots
MineDojo (NeurIPS 2022 Outstanding Paper): open-ended embodied agents in Minecraft using internet-scale knowledge

current_projects: Open-endedness with LLMs — using language models as evolutionary engines to continuously generate novel tasks, skills, and environments. Whether foundation models can enable genuinely open-ended self-improving systems. UBC lab: quality-diversity algorithms for robot skill discovery.
personal_site_summary: UBC faculty page: open-endedness, quality diversity, and AI-generating algorithms. Dual role with DeepMind. Core thesis: open-ended algorithms that continuously generate increasing complexity are the path to AGI, analogous to biological evolution.
notable_quotes: Open-endedness is to AI what natural selection was to life — a process that continuously generates increasing complexity without a fixed goal
The most important research question in AI is: how do we build systems that keep getting better at everything, indefinitely?
industry_connections: Senior Research Scientist at Google DeepMind (part-time). Previously OpenAI (2017–2019) and Uber AI Labs. Ideas directly influencing DeepMind, OpenAI, and Meta AI research programs. No current startup.
commercialization_signal: LOW — academic with strong industry ties. Ideas commercialized by frontier labs rather than by him directly. Open-endedness program influential on next-generation AI training infrastructure.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: He's trying to build AI that keeps inventing new skills forever, the way evolution kept producing new life forms — no fixed goal, just endless increasing complexity
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: The Darwin Gödel Machine went from 20% to 50% on SWE-bench through self-modification — is the bottleneck now the evaluation environment (does SWE-bench cap how much self-improvement can be measured) or is there a theoretical ceiling to what open-ended code evolution can achieve?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: His open-endedness research is the theoretical foundation for RSI. AI-GAs directly addresses whether AI can design better AI learning algorithms — the core RSI question. ELM shows LLMs can be evolutionary engines, connecting to self-improvement thesis.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Compute partnerships for large-scale open-ended experiments; introductions to frontier labs; connections to robotics companies needing quality-diversity for skill discovery.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Do not ask him to compare with OpenAI's current RL work — complicated feelings about how little credit the field gives to open-endedness research

controversial_take: Darwin Gödel Machine is the first real RSI system — it actually improved itself by 2.5x on SWE-bench. The AI safety community has been debating RSI theoretically for decades; it's already happening, just slowly and in constrained domains.
open_problem: Whether LLMs can serve as a genuine open-ended engine that continuously generates novel, increasingly complex tasks/solutions in a self-sustaining loop — or whether they inevitably collapse to a fixed distribution.

He recently led the Gemini Thinking team focused on LLM reasoning.

Founding member of Google Brain Robotics team
Open X-Embodiment: Best Conference Paper ICRA 2024; Gemini 1.5 contributor

current_projects: Post-Google transition — likely entrepreneurial venture or new research role. Last known focus: Gemini Thinking (LLM reasoning); robotics via sim-to-real.
personal_site_summary: Personal site listing research in generative modeling, RL, and LLM reasoning, highlighting key contributions at Google Brain, OpenAI, and Google DeepMind.
industry_connections: Formerly Senior Staff at Google DeepMind; previously at OpenAI (ChatGPT team, Japan market entry); founding Google Brain Robotics. Three of the most impactful AI orgs.
commercialization_signal: Extremely strong — departing from Google DeepMind after 8 years; previously at OpenAI. Likely founding or joining a major AI startup. His combination of LLM reasoning + robotics expertise is uniquely valuable for physical AI companies.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: He discovered that telling AI to 'think step by step' dramatically improves its reasoning — and he also builds the robotic systems that learn physical skills from simulation before trying them in the real world.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Given that you co-invented chain-of-thought prompting and led Gemini Thinking — do you see a fundamental equivalence between verbal chain-of-thought in LLMs and the internal world-model planning in robotics, or are they different cognitive processes?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: CoT/LLM reasoning is foundational to the self-improving AI thesis; his robotics work (Open X-Embodiment, Brain Robotics) is central to physical AI — he sits at the exact intersection of both core investment themes.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to physical AI companies seeking technical co-founders (Physical Intelligence, 1X, Skild AI); VC connections for his likely upcoming venture; LP/advisory opportunities.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Avoid asking about specific Google internal projects or why he left DeepMind — focus on his research vision going forward.

controversial_take: Chain-of-thought prompting works not because it mimics human reasoning but because it forces the model to generate its own intermediate features — it's a form of test-time computation that is essentially equivalent to growing the network depth at inference.
open_problem: Unifying the language-based reasoning improvements from CoT with embodied world model planning — getting an AI that can verbally reason about physical actions and then flawlessly execute them.

name: Nikunj Saunshi
title: Senior Research Scientist
lab: GenAI Team, Google DeepMind
research_focus: Nikunj Saunshi uses a mix of theory and empirics to understand why deep learning and LLMs work, focusing on reasoning capabilities, self-supervised representation learning, and efficient training. His recent work centers on looped and recurrent transformer architectures that perform implicit multi-step reasoning in latent space without explicit chain-of-thought tokens.
direction_of_travel: Ascending from strong theory-empirics bridging work at Google Brain toward leading the implicit reasoning research agenda—his ICLR 2025 paper on looped transformers and latent thoughts has become a foundational reference for the LIT workshop he co-organizes.
key_contributions: Showed that looped transformers can reason with latent thoughts, providing theoretical grounding for implicit CoT
Demonstrated that stacking model layers induces inductive biases that improve multi-step reasoning
Early theoretical analysis of contrastive self-supervised learning connecting pretext task structure to downstream performance

top_papers.title: Reasoning with Latent Thoughts: On the Power of Looped Transformers
top_papers.one_liner: Proves looped transformers can implement multi-step reasoning in continuous latent space, motivating implicit CoT architectures

top_papers.title: Can Looped Transformers Learn to Implement Multi-step Gradient Descent for In-context Learning?
top_papers.one_liner: Shows looped transformers algorithmically emulate gradient descent steps for in-context learning

top_papers.title: On the Inductive Bias of Stacking Towards Improving Reasoning
top_papers.one_liner: Characterizes how progressive stacking during pretraining biases models toward better multi-step reasoning

recent_arxiv.summary: Theoretical analysis proving that looped transformer architectures can perform implicit chain-of-thought reasoning in latent space

recent_arxiv.summary: Progressive subnetwork training approach that improves compute efficiency during pretraining

current_projects: Co-organizing LIT workshop at ICLR 2026; research on latent reasoning architectures and efficient LLM training at Google DeepMind GenAI team
personal_site_summary: Well-organized academic site at Google DeepMind listing his research interests in theory-empirics, looped transformers, and representation learning, with full publication list by year.
industry_connections: Senior Research Scientist at Google DeepMind; PhD from Princeton under Sanjeev Arora (same advisor as Zhiyuan Li, creating a strong alumni network); directly co-authors with Sanjiv Kumar (VP/Google Fellow)
commercialization_signal: Moderate—looped transformer architectures are relevant to inference-efficient models; his work on efficient pretraining has direct product implications for Google's Gemini

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Nikunj is the theorist behind why looped/recurrent transformers are good at implicit reasoning—he proves they can do multi-step thinking in their hidden states without spelling it out in tokens.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your looped transformer work shows latent reasoning is theoretically possible—what do you think is the main practical bottleneck to training these at scale: optimization instability, lack of good training signal, or something in the architecture?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Core to inference-time scaling and LLM post-training: proves the theoretical foundation for implicit reasoning architectures that could replace or augment chain-of-thought, reducing inference costs while maintaining reasoning quality.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to startups building efficient inference infrastructure or alternative architectures; VC perspective on which implicit reasoning approaches are nearest to productization
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Avoid asking about Google-internal Gemini roadmap or capabilities—he won't discuss NDA-covered work

controversial_take: Chain-of-thought is a crutch imposed by the limitations of training, not a fundamental feature of reasoning—looped architectures trained correctly will outperform verbose CoT models at a fraction of the inference cost.
open_problem: Can looped transformers be reliably trained from scratch at scale, or does the circular computation create optimization pathologies that require special curricula?

controversial_take: Current math benchmarks like AIME and Olympiad problems are already saturating faster than we expected—the community needs to stop equating mathematical reasoning ability with competition math and invest in genuine mathematical understanding benchmarks.
open_problem: What is the right way to measure mathematical metacognition in LLMs—specifically, how do we distinguish genuine uncertainty quantification from calibrated hallucination?

before_you_walk_up.informed_question: With LLMs, the inference bottleneck has shifted from compute to memory bandwidth—do you think the next generation of efficient inference will require new memory hierarchies or can algorithmic approaches like speculative decoding get us far enough?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Relevant to inference-time scaling and physical AI: his lab produces efficiency research applicable to both LLM serving and edge/robotics deployments; his investor experience makes him a uniquely valuable connector.

before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Avoid asking about his Buddhist Studies research—it's a personal interest he keeps separate from professional discussions at ML conferences

controversial_take: The AI industry is making the same mistake as the semiconductor industry in the 1990s—over-investing in brute-force scaling when algorithmic efficiency gains have historically outpaced hardware gains by 2-3x.
open_problem: What is the right abstraction level for hardware-software co-design for LLMs—at the level of attention mechanisms, or should we co-design from the architecture specification down to the memory hierarchy?

name: Jiantao Jiao
title: Assistant Professor
lab: BAIR Lab; BLISS; CLIMB Center, UC Berkeley
research_focus: Jiantao Jiao works on the full pipeline of generative AI and foundation models—from data curation through pretraining, SFT, instruction tuning, RLHF, inference, and security guardrails. He combines information theory, statistical machine learning, and optimization to develop principled algorithms for LLM alignment and efficient inference, and co-founded Nexusflow (acquired by NVIDIA).
direction_of_travel: Rapid rise from PhD at Stanford to UC Berkeley faculty, founding Nexusflow, and achieving NVIDIA acquisition—among the most commercially successful young ML theorists; NVIDIA affiliation post-acquisition gives him deep industry access.
key_contributions: Co-founded Nexusflow.AI, building foundation models and agentic systems; acquired by NVIDIA
Developed Advantage-Induced Policy Alignment (APA) and Pairwise PPO for principled LLM alignment from human feedback
Co-directs CLIMB center, integrating learning, inference, information theory, and microeconomics at Berkeley

top_papers.title: Theoretically Principled Trade-off between Robustness and Accuracy
top_papers.one_liner: TRADES: formalized the trade-off between adversarial robustness and clean accuracy; ICML 2019 Long Oral

top_papers.title: Fine-Tuning Language Models with Advantage-Induced Policy Alignment
top_papers.one_liner: Principled RL fine-tuning approach using advantage functions for stable LLM alignment

top_papers.title: Pairwise Proximal Policy Optimization: Harnessing Relative Feedback for LLM Alignment
top_papers.one_liner: Extends PPO to pairwise preference feedback for more stable and sample-efficient RLHF

recent_arxiv.title: Fine-Tuning Language Models with Advantage-Induced Policy Alignment
recent_arxiv.summary: Principled advantage-based RL fine-tuning that stabilizes RLHF training for LLMs

recent_arxiv.title: On Optimal Caching and Model Multiplexing for Large Model Inference
recent_arxiv.summary: Information-theoretic analysis of optimal caching and model selection for LLM inference, NeurIPS 2023

current_projects: Speaking at SPOT workshop at ICLR 2026; NVIDIA affiliation post-Nexusflow acquisition; CLIMB Center co-direction; RLHF and alignment research; Berkeley BAIR faculty
personal_site_summary: UC Berkeley EECS and Statistics professor page covering RLHF, LLM alignment, information theory, and AI security; co-founder of Nexusflow (acquired by NVIDIA); co-director of CLIMB center.
industry_connections: Co-founder of Nexusflow, acquired by NVIDIA—direct NVIDIA affiliation; BAIR Lab connections to Sequoia/NEA-backed startups; CLIMB Center bridges Berkeley theory and industry; extensive RLHF/alignment IP
commercialization_signal: Very high—company acquired by NVIDIA; alignment and RLHF work directly commercializable; information-theoretic inference optimization relevant to serving infrastructure companies

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Jiantao built a company doing foundation models and agentic AI that NVIDIA bought, and he's also a Berkeley professor who's created principled versions of RLHF that are more stable and sample-efficient than PPO.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your Pairwise PPO work shows relative feedback is more stable than absolute reward—post-Nexusflow acquisition, are you seeing NVIDIA's hardware perspective change your thinking about which alignment algorithms are practical at scale?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Core to RL for LLMs and LLM post-training: APA and Pairwise PPO are principled alternatives to standard RLHF that address instability and sample inefficiency; NVIDIA acquisition gives him unmatched hardware perspective on inference scaling.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to alignment-focused investors; perspective on the NVIDIA AI ecosystem post-acquisition; co-investment in Berkeley spinouts
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Avoid asking for specifics about the Nexusflow acquisition terms or valuation—he may not be able to discuss

controversial_take: RLHF is fundamentally broken because human preference data is not IID—the preference distribution shifts as the model improves, creating a moving target that no current alignment method handles correctly.
open_problem: What is the correct information-theoretic framework for RLHF that accounts for the non-stationarity of human preferences as the model being aligned improves through training?

name: Yu Su
title: Associate Professor and Innovation Scholar
lab: OSU NLP Group / ICICLE AI Institute
research_focus: Language agents and AI systems that reason, act, and adapt in the real world. Research spans web agents (Mind2Web), multimodal understanding (MMMU, BioCLIP), retrieval-augmented generation (HippoRAG), and the fundamental challenges of agent plasticity and reliability in continually changing environments.
direction_of_travel: Tsinghua undergrad → UCSB PhD → Microsoft Semantic Machines (senior researcher, conversational AI) → OSU Associate Professor. Recently relocated to Silicon Valley to work on agents' core challenges (plasticity + reliability). 2025 Sloan Fellow. Strong signals toward industry engagement or sabbatical at a major AI lab.
key_contributions: Mind2Web: first LLM-based web agent and benchmark for generalist web agents (NeurIPS 2023 Spotlight)
MMMU: massive multi-discipline multimodal understanding benchmark (CVPR 2024 Best Paper Finalist)
BioCLIP: vision foundation model for biodiversity (CVPR 2024 Best Student Paper)
HippoRAG: neurologically inspired long-term memory for LLMs (NeurIPS 2024)
SeeAct: GPT-4V as web agent for real-world task execution (ICML 2024)

top_papers.title: Mind2Web: Towards a Generalist Agent for the Web
top_papers.one_liner: First benchmark and model for generalist web agents that follow natural language instructions on real websites across 137 domains.

top_papers.title: MMMU: A Massive Multi-discipline Multimodal Understanding and Reasoning Benchmark
top_papers.one_liner: CVPR 2024 Best Paper Finalist benchmarking expert-level multimodal understanding across 30 disciplines.

top_papers.title: BioCLIP: A Vision Foundation Model for the Tree of Life
top_papers.one_liner: CVPR 2024 Best Student Paper—vision-language model trained on biological taxonomy for biodiversity AI applications.

recent_arxiv.title: Mind2Web 2: Evaluating Agentic Search with Agent-as-a-Judge
recent_arxiv.summary: NeurIPS 2025 benchmark of 130 long-horizon web tasks requiring real-time browsing and information synthesis, evaluated with Agent-as-a-Judge.

recent_arxiv.title: An Illusion of Progress? Assessing the Current State of Web Agents
recent_arxiv.summary: Shows frontier web agents may be up to 59% less capable than reported due to benchmark inflation; proposes Online-Mind2Web for realistic evaluation.

current_projects: Sloan Fellow; relocated to Silicon Valley to tackle agent plasticity and reliability; leading OSU NLP group; co-leads ICICLE AI Institute foundational AI team; speaker at RSI ICLR 2026.
personal_site_summary: OSU Associate Professor and Innovation Scholar. 2025 Sloan Research Fellow. CVPR 2024 Best Paper Finalist + Best Student Paper. Creator of Mind2Web, MMMU, BioCLIP, HippoRAG, SeeAct. Relocated to Silicon Valley for agent research.
industry_connections: Microsoft Semantic Machines (pre-OSU). Now relocated to Silicon Valley suggesting imminent industry collaboration or advisory roles. Co-leads ICICLE AI Institute with industry partners.
commercialization_signal: Mind2Web is foundational to the web agent product space (direct competitor/complement to Operator, Claude Computer Use). Silicon Valley relocation + focus on plasticity/reliability = positioning for industry pivot or startup. MMMU as a benchmark gives him influence over enterprise AI evaluation.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: OSU professor who built Mind2Web (the first real web agent benchmark) and MMMU (the hardest multimodal benchmark). Sloan Fellow who just moved to Silicon Valley because he thinks agents are the next big thing but current benchmarks are lying about how good they are.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your new 'Illusion of Progress' paper shows web agent benchmarks are inflated by up to 59%—what does a trustworthy, manipulation-resistant agent evaluation framework look like, and how do you prevent the eval-hacking problem you've identified?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Directly on RSI thesis: agent plasticity (continual learning/adaptation) and reliability are the two blockers for recursive self-improvement to work in practice.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: VC perspective on the web agent startup space; access to enterprise buyers trying to deploy agent infrastructure who need Mind2Web-style evaluation.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Don't ask about BioCLIP biodiversity applications—that's a science impact story, not his core agent research.

controversial_take: Web agent benchmarks are systematically overestimated by 59%—the entire sub-field is building on inflated numbers, and real-world deployment will be a reckoning.
open_problem: How do you build agents that are both plastic (continuously adapting to new websites and tasks) and reliable (predictably safe)—current approaches sacrifice one for the other.

name: Bang Liu
title: Associate Professor
lab: RALI Laboratory / Institut Courtois / Mila
research_focus: LLM-based intelligent agents, multimodal and embodied learning, AI for science (materials, health, XR), and graph representation learning for NLP. Builds multi-agent frameworks (AFLOW, OSCAR) for workflow optimization and GUI automation, and foundation agent systems for autonomous scientific research.
direction_of_travel: University of Alberta PhD (2020) → UMontreal faculty → Canada CIFAR AI Chair → Associate Professor (2025). WAIC Yunfan Award 2025 (global recognition). Strong trajectory toward leading a broad AI agent research program and potential commercialization via autonomous research agents.
key_contributions: Agent Laboratory: LLM-powered autonomous research assistant system
AFLOW: workflow optimization as Monte Carlo Tree Search over code-represented agent graphs
OSCAR: generalist desktop/mobile GUI agent translating instructions to Python code
Foundation Agents Survey: 396-page 20-institution comprehensive survey of LLM agent landscape
MatExpert: LLM + contrastive learning framework for solid-state materials discovery

top_papers.title: The Rise and Potential of Large Language Model Based Agents: A Survey
top_papers.one_liner: Comprehensive survey defining the LLM agent paradigm and cataloging capabilities, architectures, and applications.

top_papers.title: AFLOW: Automating Agentic Workflow Generation
top_papers.one_liner: Reformulates multi-agent workflow design as Monte Carlo Tree Search over code-represented graphs for automatic optimization.

top_papers.title: Agent Laboratory: Using LLMs as Research Assistants
top_papers.one_liner: System enabling LLM agents to autonomously conduct end-to-end research: hypothesis, experiment, and paper writing.

recent_arxiv.title: Large Language Model Agent: A Survey on Methodology, Applications and Challenges
recent_arxiv.summary: 396-page comprehensive survey across 20 institutions cataloging the state of LLM agent research, architectures, and open challenges.

recent_arxiv.title: AFLOW: Automating Agentic Workflow Generation
recent_arxiv.summary: Uses MCTS to automatically optimize multi-agent workflow structures represented as executable code graphs.

current_projects: Associate Professor at UMontreal; Canada CIFAR AI Chair; leading research on agent workflows, embodied learning, and AI for materials science; speaker at RSI ICLR 2026; WAIC Yunfan Award 2025.
personal_site_summary: Associate Professor at Université de Montréal, RALI lab. Canada CIFAR AI Chair. WAIC Yunfan Award 2025. Research on LLM agents, multimodal learning, AI for science. Multiple billion-DAU product deployments of his NLP research.
industry_connections: Research deployed in products with 1B+ daily active users. CIFAR AI Chair connects to Canada's AI ecosystem (Vector, Mila). WAIC 2025 award signals recognition from major Chinese tech companies.
commercialization_signal: Agent Laboratory and OSCAR are directly commercializable as autonomous research and RPA (robotic process automation) tools. Materials discovery via AI has major pharma and energy applications. AFLOW's MCTS workflow optimization could underpin enterprise AI orchestration products.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Montreal professor who builds AI agents that can run entire research projects autonomously—literature review, hypothesis generation, experiments, paper writing. His lab also won a major global AI award in 2025. Work deployed in apps used by over a billion people.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Agent Laboratory enables LLMs to autonomously run research—what's the failure mode rate you observe, and have you found that agents improve their own research methodology through multiple iterations, which would be true recursive self-improvement?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Direct RSI thesis: Agent Laboratory is an AI system that improves scientific knowledge autonomously. AFLOW uses MCTS to recursively optimize agent workflows. Both are concrete RSI implementations.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: VC perspective on autonomous research agent startups (Sakana AI, FutureHouse adjacents); connections to pharma/materials companies seeking AI-accelerated discovery pipelines.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Don't ask about graph neural networks for NLP—it's early work and he's fully pivoted to agents.

controversial_take: Autonomous AI research agents will not replace human researchers but will make the median researcher 100x more productive within 5 years—and that productivity discontinuity will restructure academic funding more than any policy change.
open_problem: How do you evaluate the quality of agent-generated research in real time without expert human reviewers—the evaluation bottleneck is the binding constraint on fully autonomous research systems.

name: Evan Shelhamer
affiliation: University of British Columbia / Google DeepMind
title: Assistant Professor (UBC) / Senior Research Scientist (Google DeepMind)
lab: UBC Computer Science / BAIR (Berkeley AI Research, alumni)
research_focus: Test-time adaptation and adaptive computation for robust visual recognition under distribution shift. Develops entropy minimization (Tent) and related methods that update model parameters at deployment without labeled test data. Recent work extends to AI for science via remote sensing and sustainable computing.
direction_of_travel: UC Berkeley PhD (Trevor Darrell) → Adobe Research (1 year) → MIT (visiting) → Google DeepMind Senior Research Scientist (4 years) → UBC Assistant Professor. Key paper trajectory: FCN (CVPR 2015, 25k+ citations) → Tent/TTA (ICLR 2021) → robust adaptation methods → UBC focus on remote sensing + satellite AI (Galileo, ICML 2025).
key_contributions: Fully Convolutional Networks for Semantic Segmentation: FCN architecture enabling dense prediction (CVPR 2015, PAMI 2016, 25,000+ citations, CVPR 2025 Test-of-Time Award)
Tent: Fully Test-Time Adaptation by Entropy Minimization (ICLR 2021 Spotlight)
Evaluating Adversarial Robustness of Adaptive Test-Time Defenses (ICML 2022, with Croce)
Galileo: Learning Global & Local Features of Many Remote Sensing Modalities (ICML 2025)
Seasoning Model Soups for Robustness (CVPR 2023)

top_papers.title: Fully Convolutional Networks for Semantic Segmentation
top_papers.one_liner: Defined the FCN architecture enabling pixel-wise dense prediction—foundational to all modern segmentation, detection, and localization models.

top_papers.title: Tent: Fully Test-Time Adaptation by Entropy Minimization
top_papers.one_liner: ICLR 2021 Spotlight: adapts only BN layer parameters at test time by minimizing prediction entropy, handling distribution shift without source data.

top_papers.title: Galileo: Learning Global & Local Features of Many Remote Sensing Modalities
top_papers.one_liner: Foundation model for multi-modal satellite imagery learning global and local features for environmental monitoring.

recent_arxiv.title: LookWhere? Efficient Visual Recognition via Adaptive Routing
recent_arxiv.summary: NeurIPS 2025: adaptive computation routing for efficient visual recognition, connecting test-time adaptation to efficient inference.

recent_arxiv.title: Asymmetric Duos: Sidekicks Improve Uncertainty Estimation
recent_arxiv.summary: NeurIPS 2025 Spotlight: pairing large and small models improves uncertainty estimation for robust decision-making under shift.

current_projects: UBC Assistant Professor; Canada CIFAR AI Chair; Vector Institute member; Google DeepMind Senior Research Scientist; co-organizer of TTU ICLR 2026 workshop; working on Galileo satellite AI and test-time adaptation theory.
personal_site_summary: UBC Assistant Professor + Google DeepMind Senior Research Scientist. Canada CIFAR AI Chair. UC Berkeley PhD (Darrell). Created FCN (25k+ citations, CVPR 2025 Test-of-Time) and Tent (TTA, ICLR 2021). Now at UBC + DeepMind working on TTA and remote sensing AI.
industry_connections: Active Senior Research Scientist at Google DeepMind. Former Adobe Research (Tent written during that period). CIFAR AI Chair. Vector Institute member. Strong dual academic-industry footprint.
commercialization_signal: Tent/TTA methods are directly applicable to production ML systems facing distribution shift (autonomous driving, medical imaging, satellite analysis). FCN underpins virtually all commercial vision systems. Galileo targets the growing climate/earth observation AI market.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: UBC professor + DeepMind senior scientist who invented two foundational computer vision ideas: FCN (the architecture behind every segmentation model) and Tent (test-time adaptation by entropy minimization). He's now building AI for satellite imagery and organizing the test-time updates workshop at ICLR.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Tent adapts BN parameters via entropy minimization—but recent work shows it can catastrophically fail on long test sequences. In the TTU workshop context, what does a theoretically sound online TTA algorithm look like that avoids collapse?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Direct thesis: test-time adaptation is the core of the TTU workshop and Tent is the canonical method. His work on adversarial TTA robustness (with Croce) directly connects formal verification to adaptation safety.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Access to enterprise computer vision buyers evaluating TTA solutions for production deployment; connections to earth observation companies building on satellite foundation models.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Don't ask about Caffe—he's moved well past that. Don't make it about the FCN legacy; he wants to be known for TTA and adaptive systems, not old segmentation work.

controversial_take: Test-time adaptation is the field's most underinvested area—every production ML system faces distribution shift, and the industry is solving it with expensive retraining when TTA can adapt in milliseconds at inference.
open_problem: Under what theoretical conditions does entropy minimization converge to the correct adapted distribution rather than a confident-but-wrong collapsed distribution? The stability theory for TTA is largely missing.

name: Eleni Triantafillou
title: Senior Research Scientist
lab: Google DeepMind, London
research_focus: Enabling deep neural networks to efficiently adapt to distribution shifts, learn new tasks from few examples (meta-learning, few-shot learning), and unlearn unwanted data points. Developed Meta-Dataset benchmark and is expanding into machine unlearning methods for safe and privacy-preserving AI.
direction_of_travel: Toronto PhD (Urtasun, Zemel) → Google Research → Google DeepMind Senior Research Scientist. Progression: few-shot learning (NeurIPS 2017) → Meta-Dataset (ICLR 2020, the definitive meta-learning benchmark) → source-free domain adaptation (ICML 2023) → machine unlearning (NeurIPS 2023, 2024, ICLR 2025, 2026). Clear pivot from adaptation-in to adaptation-out (unlearning).
key_contributions: Meta-Dataset: large-scale diverse benchmark for training and evaluating few-shot learners across 10 datasets (ICLR 2020)
Learning a Universal Template for Few-shot Dataset Generalization (ICML 2021)
In Search for a Generalizable Method for Source Free Domain Adaptation (ICML 2023)
Towards Unbounded Machine Unlearning (NeurIPS 2023)
Redirection for Erasing Memory (REM): Universal Unlearning Method for Corrupted Data (ICLR 2026)

top_papers.title: Meta-Dataset: A Dataset of Datasets for Learning to Learn from Few Examples
top_papers.one_liner: Benchmark of 10 diverse datasets for realistic few-shot learning evaluation requiring cross-domain generalization.

top_papers.title: Towards Unbounded Machine Unlearning
top_papers.one_liner: Proposes unlearning methods that can remove arbitrary subsets of training data without retraining from scratch.

top_papers.title: In Search for a Generalizable Method for Source Free Domain Adaptation
top_papers.one_liner: Systematic study showing most source-free domain adaptation methods fail to generalize across standard benchmarks.

recent_arxiv.title: Redirection for Erasing Memory (REM): Towards a Universal Unlearning Method for Corrupted Data
recent_arxiv.summary: ICLR 2026: universal machine unlearning method that redirects corrupted data influence without full model retraining.

recent_arxiv.title: What Makes Unlearning Hard and What to Do About It
recent_arxiv.summary: NeurIPS 2024: analysis of why machine unlearning fails and proposes principled solutions for robust data removal.

current_projects: Senior Research Scientist at Google DeepMind; machine unlearning research (NeurIPS 2025 competition organizer); speaker at TTU ICLR 2026; TMLR Action Editor; Meta-Dataset benchmark maintenance.
personal_site_summary: Senior Research Scientist at Google DeepMind. Toronto PhD (Urtasun, Zemel). Creator of Meta-Dataset benchmark. 4,433+ citations. Expert in few-shot learning, meta-learning, domain adaptation, machine unlearning. TMLR Action Editor.
industry_connections: Senior role at Google DeepMind with direct access to production ML pipelines. Meta-Dataset is used across academia and industry as the standard few-shot benchmark. Machine unlearning work intersects with EU AI Act data deletion requirements.
commercialization_signal: Machine unlearning is becoming a regulatory requirement (EU AI Act, GDPR right to erasure)—her work is directly relevant to enterprise AI compliance. Domain adaptation methods address the core production ML problem of model degradation under distribution shift.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: DeepMind senior scientist who built Meta-Dataset (the hardest few-shot learning benchmark) and is now working on machine unlearning—teaching AI models to forget specific training data on demand. This is increasingly required by GDPR and EU AI Act regulations.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: In your REM unlearning work, how do you verify that the redirected memory truly erases the data's influence versus merely hiding it—is there an adversarial evaluation analogous to membership inference attacks that certifies unlearning completeness?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: TTU workshop connection: meta-learning and few-shot adaptation are test-time updates (model updates from few examples). Machine unlearning is the inverse problem—test-time forgetting. Both are about controlled model updates at deployment.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: VC perspective on machine unlearning startups (regulatory compliance opportunity); connections to enterprise legal teams at major tech companies dealing with GDPR data deletion requests.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Do not frame the conversation as 'few-shot learning'—she's pivoted to unlearning and considers that her new primary research identity.

controversial_take: Machine unlearning via approximate methods is security theater—without cryptographic guarantees that the removed data's influence is bounded, any company claiming GDPR compliance via unlearning is exposed to legal liability.
open_problem: Can machine unlearning be certified with the same rigor as differential privacy—providing a formal epsilon bound on the information remaining in the model after unlearning a data point?

name: Jong Chul Ye
title: Chung Moon Soul Chair Professor
lab: BISPL (BioImaging, Signal Processing & Machine Learning Lab), Graduate School of AI, KAIST
research_focus: Ye is a world-leading figure at the intersection of deep learning, diffusion models, and computational/medical imaging. His group pioneered the use of diffusion models for inverse problems (Diffusion Posterior Sampling, DPS), developed manifold-constrained reverse sampling, and created accelerated solvers for high-dimensional biomedical imaging. His work bridges fundamental diffusion model theory with real-world MRI, CT, and video reconstruction.
direction_of_travel: Moved from signal processing and compressed sensing (industry at Philips, GE) to KAIST, then pivoted sharply into deep learning for imaging. Since 2021 has been among the most prolific researchers applying diffusion models to inverse problems — 7 papers at ICLR 2025 alone from his lab. IEEE Fellow and Korean Academy member indicate senior establishment status.
key_contributions: Diffusion Posterior Sampling (DPS) — landmark algorithm for measurement-guided diffusion reverse sampling (ICLR 2023 Spotlight)
Decomposed Diffusion Sampler (DDS) — accelerated diffusion for large-scale imaging inverse problems (ICLR 2024)
CFG++ — improved classifier-free guidance for diffusion models

top_papers.title: Diffusion Posterior Sampling for General Noisy Inverse Problems
top_papers.one_liner: Enables diffusion models to solve arbitrary noisy inverse problems without retraining; ICLR 2023 Spotlight.

top_papers.title: Decomposed Diffusion Sampler for Accelerating Large-Scale Inverse Problems
top_papers.one_liner: Accelerated diffusion sampling method for high-dimensional biomedical imaging; ICLR 2024.

top_papers.title: Score-based Diffusion meets Annealed Importance Sampling
top_papers.one_liner: Connects diffusion models with annealed importance sampling for improved sampling quality.

recent_arxiv.title: Training-Free Reward-Guided Generation with Diffusion Models
recent_arxiv.summary: Training-free approach to reward-guided generation; accepted at ICLR 2026 from BISPL lab.

current_projects: Reward-guided generation for diffusion models (ICLR 2026); extending diffusion sampling to video inverse problems; theoretical analysis of manifold geometry in diffusion models.
personal_site_summary: BISPL lab site shows a prolific group of 20+ students with continuous paper output at top ML venues. Lab is KAIST's flagship for ML-in-imaging.
industry_connections: Prior industry roles at Philips Research and GE Global Research. IEEE Fellow and academic committee roles. Lab's medical imaging focus aligns with clinical AI commercialization.
commercialization_signal: High. Diffusion-based inverse problem solvers for MRI/CT are directly applicable to radiology AI. Multiple potential industry licensees (medical imaging vendors, radiology AI startups).

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Jong Chul Ye is the go-to person for making diffusion models work for medical imaging. His DPS algorithm is how the field learned to use diffusion for inverse problems — it's cited everywhere.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: DPS keeps trajectories on the data manifold via likelihood gradients, but the approximation degrades for very noisy measurements. Are there tight error bounds you've established on the off-manifold drift?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Core thesis — diffusion models for inverse problems, reward/constraint-aligned sampling, generative models for physical measurement systems.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to clinical AI companies and radiology AI investors; connections to medical device companies doing algorithmic reconstruction (Siemens Healthineers, Canon Medical innovation teams).
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Avoid framing his work as 'just medical' — he views himself as a foundational diffusion model researcher whose domain happens to be imaging.

controversial_take: Most diffusion-based inverse problem solvers will fail in clinical deployment not because of algorithmic limitations but because the forward models used in training never match real scanner physics — the gap between simulation and hardware is the real bottleneck.
open_problem: Principled, computationally tractable posterior sampling for nonlinear inverse problems (e.g., nonlinear MR signal equations) where the likelihood Jacobian is intractable.

name: Ruiqi Gao
title: Research Scientist
lab: Google DeepMind
research_focus: Gao works on generative models and representation learning for multimodal data — language, images, videos, and 3D. Recent contributions bridge diffusion models and flow matching mathematically, and extend generative modeling to 4D (space + time) video synthesis. He is a central figure in Google's generative AI research stack.
direction_of_travel: PhD at UCLA with Zhu/Wu (energy-based models era), then Research Scientist at Google. Steadily moved from energy-based model foundations toward leading diffusion/flow matching research at Google scale. CAT4D (CVPR 2025 Oral) and the 'Diffusion Models and Gaussian Flow Matching: Two Sides of the Same Coin' paper (ICLR 2025) show a theorist-practitioner combination rare at big labs.
key_contributions: Diffusion Models and Gaussian Flow Matching: Two Sides of the Same Coin (ICLR 2025) — formal unification of diffusion and flow matching
CAT4D: Create Anything in 4D with Multi-View Video Diffusion Models (CVPR 2025 Oral)
Co-organizer of Scalable Generative Models in Computer Vision workshop at CVPR 2025

top_papers.title: Diffusion Models and Gaussian Flow Matching: Two Sides of the Same Coin
top_papers.one_liner: Proves theoretical equivalence between diffusion models and Gaussian flow matching; ICLR 2025.

top_papers.title: CAT4D: Create Anything in 4D with Multi-View Video Diffusion Models
top_papers.one_liner: 4D content generation from video using multi-view diffusion; CVPR 2025 Oral.

top_papers.title: Your Diffusion Model is Secretly a Zero-Shot Classifier
top_papers.one_liner: Shows diffusion model likelihoods are strong zero-shot classifiers.

recent_arxiv.title: SceneCrafter: Controllable Multi-View Driving Scene Editing
recent_arxiv.summary: Multi-view controllable editing of driving scenes using diffusion models; CVPR 2025.

current_projects: 4D generative modeling at Google DeepMind; video synthesis with multi-view diffusion; theoretical unification of generative model families; scalable generative AI for computer vision applications.
personal_site_summary: Clean academic-style personal site listing key papers and research themes in generative models and representation learning. Reflects dual identity as theorist and builder.
industry_connections: Full-time at Google DeepMind. Well-connected within the Google research ecosystem (collaborates with Jonathan Barron, Ben Poole, Tim Salimans, Emiel Hoogeboom — all key DeepMind/Google generative AI figures).
commercialization_signal: Very high — at Google, working on video generation and 4D modeling which feeds directly into Google's Lumiere/Imagen Video product lines. His research is pre-product but tightly coupled to DeepMind's commercial roadmap.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Ruiqi is at Google DeepMind building the generative AI models that will power Google's next wave of video and 3D products. His paper proving diffusion and flow matching are equivalent is being cited by everyone trying to choose between the two.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your equivalence result between Gaussian flow matching and diffusion applies under specific noise schedules — does it extend to non-Gaussian bases like Riemannian manifold flows, or does the two-sides-of-a-coin framing break down?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Core thesis — diffusion models, flow matching, video generation, 4D generative modeling. One of the most thesis-relevant speakers in the workshop.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Perspective on how frontier labs are thinking about diffusion/flow model architectures; connections to portfolio companies building on top of open-weight video generation models.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Don't ask about Google's internal product roadmap or what gets shipped vs. stays in research.

controversial_take: The field is converging too fast on flow matching as the 'winner' — the theoretical equivalence doesn't mean flow matching is practically superior; the devil is in the discretization and the noise schedule, not the continuous-time framing.
open_problem: Efficient 4D generation that maintains physical consistency (rigid body constraints, lighting, occlusion) without expensive test-time optimization.

name: Volodymyr Kuleshov
title: Joan Eliasoph M.D. Assistant Professor of Computer Science; Co-founder, Inception Labs
lab: Kuleshov Group, Cornell Tech
research_focus: Kuleshov works on generative and probabilistic machine learning with applications to science and health — particularly diffusion language models, discrete diffusion, and biological sequence modeling. He co-invented the first commercial-scale diffusion LLM (Mercury) through Inception Labs. His academic work spans Block Diffusion (bridging AR and diffusion LMs), denoising diffusion variational inference, and genomic language models.
direction_of_travel: PhD at Stanford, then Cornell Tech faculty. Co-founded two startups: Afresh (food waste AI) and Inception Labs (diffusion LLMs, $50M seed). The Inception trajectory is remarkable — from academic papers on discrete diffusion to a $50M-funded company in under two years. Most likely to leave academia for full-time CEO role if Mercury gains commercial traction.
key_contributions: Block Diffusion: bridging autoregressive and diffusion language models (ICLR 2025 Oral)
Co-founded Inception Labs — first commercial diffusion LLM (Mercury), $50M seed funding
Cross-species plant genome modeling at single-nucleotide resolution (PNAS 2025)

top_papers.one_liner: Flexible language model family interpolating between AR and diffusion; ICLR 2025 Oral.
