current_projects: Building Mercury and Mercury 2 (reasoning diffusion LLM) at Inception Labs; academic research on discrete diffusion, inference-time scaling, and diffusion LLMs for genomics.
personal_site_summary: Cornell CS faculty page with full publication list and course materials including a publicly available Deep Generative Models course. Reflects both researcher and educator identity.
industry_connections: Co-founder of Inception Labs (diffusion LLMs, $50M seed from Menlo Ventures, Databricks Ventures, M12, Snowflake Ventures, Mayfield). Co-founder of Afresh (food-waste AI). NSF CAREER, NIH MIRA awards.
commercialization_signal: Extremely high. Already has a funded startup (Inception Labs) commercializing diffusion LLMs. Mercury is live and generating revenue. This is one of the clearest founder-researcher profiles in generative AI.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Volodymyr is the rare person who both runs a $50M-backed startup building diffusion language models AND publishes papers on their mathematical foundations. Mercury is his company's product — a diffusion LLM that runs 10x faster than GPT-4o class models.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Mercury uses a masked diffusion approach — how do you handle the tension between parallel decoding (which gives speed) and the sequential dependencies that make language hard to model diffusively?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Direct core thesis — diffusion models for language, discrete diffusion, flow matching for sequences. Also touches AI for science (genomics). Highest priority speaker from commercialization angle.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: LP introductions if Inception raises a Series A; enterprise deployment partnerships for diffusion LLMs in regulated industries; introductions to genomics companies needing sequence generation.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Don't ask about Afresh (his food-waste startup) — it's dormant relative to Inception and pivoting away from it might be a sensitive topic.

controversial_take: Autoregressive models will not be the dominant paradigm for language generation in five years — diffusion LLMs will win on cost and latency once inference-time scaling is fully understood, and Mercury is the proof of concept.
open_problem: Achieving strong reasoning performance in diffusion LLMs without sacrificing the parallel generation speed advantage — the Mercury 2 'reasoning diffusion' direction is still an open research problem.

name: Tali Dekel
affiliation: Weizmann Institute of Science / Google DeepMind
title: Associate Professor; Staff Research Scientist, Google DeepMind
lab: Faculty of Mathematics and Computer Science, Weizmann Institute of Science
research_focus: Dekel is a world leader in visual generative AI — working on video generation, image synthesis, video editing with diffusion models, and multi-view 3D understanding. Her lab has produced seminal work in text-to-video diffusion (Lumiere), consistent video editing (TokenFlow), and personalized image/video synthesis. She explores the hidden capabilities of large pretrained diffusion models for creative content generation.
direction_of_travel: PhD at Tel Aviv, MIT CSAIL postdoc (Bill Freeman), Google Research Boston (senior scientist), then Weizmann faculty (2021) + ongoing Google DeepMind staff scientist. Rare dual academic/Google position. ~11,500 citations. ICCV 2019 Best Paper (SinGAN) marks her arrival as a top-tier researcher. Consistently publishes at CVPR, ICCV, SIGGRAPH, ICLR. Generates 3-4 high-impact papers per year.
key_contributions: Lumiere: Text-to-Video Diffusion Model — generates temporally coherent full-length video sequences
SinGAN: Learning a Generative Model from a Single Natural Image (ICCV 2019 Best Paper/Marr Prize)
TokenFlow: Consistent Diffusion Features for Consistent Video Editing (ICLR 2024)

top_papers.title: SinGAN: Learning a Generative Model from a Single Natural Image
top_papers.one_liner: Trains a generative model from a single image; ICCV 2019 Best Paper (Marr Prize).

top_papers.title: Lumiere: A Space-Time Diffusion Model for Video Generation
top_papers.one_liner: Space-time U-Net diffusion model generating temporally coherent full video sequences.

top_papers.title: TokenFlow: Consistent Diffusion Features for Consistent Video Editing
top_papers.one_liner: Training-free video editing framework exploiting diffusion feature consistency; ICLR 2024.

recent_arxiv.summary: Generative model for decomposing video into semantic layers; CVPR 2025.

recent_arxiv.title: TokenVerse: Multi-Concept Personalization in Token Modulation Space
recent_arxiv.summary: Multi-concept personalization of text-to-image diffusion models from a single image.

current_projects: Video generation and editing at Google DeepMind; multi-concept personalization (TokenVerse); video layer decomposition (Generative Omnimatte); exploring spatial and temporal controllability in diffusion-based video models.
personal_site_summary: Well-maintained Weizmann faculty page with full publication list and news. Reflects strong research identity in visual AI with both academic rigor and applied impact.
industry_connections: Staff Research Scientist at Google DeepMind (ongoing, concurrent with Weizmann position). Work on Lumiere feeds directly into Google's video generation products. Prior Google Research Boston scientist for 4 years.
commercialization_signal: Very high. Lumiere-class technology underpins Google's video generation products. Video generation is a multi-billion dollar opportunity. Personalization methods (TokenVerse) are directly applicable to content creation and marketing tools.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Tali is simultaneously a Weizmann professor and a Google DeepMind staff scientist. She built Lumiere (Google's text-to-video model) and TokenFlow (widely used for video editing). She's at the center of visual generative AI.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Lumiere's space-time U-Net generates the full video in one pass — how does the model handle long-range temporal consistency for videos beyond ~5 seconds where global attention becomes prohibitively expensive?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Core thesis — diffusion models for video generation, controllable generation, visual generative AI. One of the most commercially relevant speakers.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Connections to content creation and media companies building on top of video generation; LP introductions if she were to start a company; Israel deep-tech investor network.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Don't ask about the Israel/Gaza situation — she is Israeli, it could lead to a difficult conversation.

controversial_take: The race to longer video generation is mostly marketing. The real unsolved problem is controllability — being able to specify precise motion, object interaction, and physics in a generated video. Length without control is not what creators actually need.
open_problem: Physically plausible video generation — ensuring generated videos obey Newtonian mechanics, lighting consistency, and rigid-body constraints without explicit physics simulation.

name: Michael Mahoney
affiliation: UC Berkeley / ICSI / Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory
title: Professor of Statistics; VP and Director, Big Data Group (ICSI); Group Lead, ML and Analytics (LBNL)
lab: ICSI Big Data Group; LBNL ML and Analytics Group; Department of Statistics, UC Berkeley
research_focus: Randomized numerical linear algebra, scalable stochastic optimization, scientific machine learning, and geometric network analysis. Bridging theoretical foundations (randomized algorithms, implicit regularization) with large-scale scientific applications including foundation models for physical systems.
direction_of_travel: Yale PhD (statistical mechanics) → Yahoo Research → Stanford Mathematics → UC Berkeley/ICSI/LBNL. Amazon Scholar appointment reflects industry stature. Co-founded FODA (NSF/TRIPODS) institute. Moving into 'foundation models for science' as the natural extension of scientific ML work.
key_contributions: 47,000+ Google Scholar citations—one of the most cited ML theorists
Amazon Scholar appointment; FODA Institute Director at UC Berkeley
Co-organized Simons Institute programs on Foundations of Data Science (2013, 2018)
Director, PCMI Summer Session on Mathematics of Data (2016)
Randomized SVD and NLA algorithms foundational to modern large-scale ML

top_papers.title: Randomized Algorithms for Matrices and Data
top_papers.one_liner: Foundations and Trends survey on randomized linear algebra—the standard reference for randomized SVD, etc.

top_papers.title: Revisiting the Train Loss: an Efficient Performance Estimator for Neural Architecture Search
top_papers.one_liner: Shows training loss alone can predict architecture quality—seminal for training-free NAS.

current_projects: Foundational methods for foundation models for scientific ML; learning physics dynamics with language models; ICSI Big Data Group projects; FM4Science ICLR 2026 speaker.
personal_site_summary: UC Berkeley Statistics page listing research areas, recent work on scientific ML and foundation models, extensive service record, and Amazon Scholar role.
industry_connections: Amazon Scholar; LBNL group lead; ICSI VP; co-organized National Academy of Engineering Frontiers of Engineering; Simons Institute collaboration.
commercialization_signal: Medium—randomized linear algebra algorithms are in production at every major ML company. Amazon Scholar connection indicates active industry engagement, but no direct startup.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: One of the most cited mathematicians in machine learning—his randomized algorithms power numerical computations inside every major AI system. Now leading the charge on foundation models for science at Berkeley/LBNL/ICSI. Amazon Scholar with vast network.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your work on randomized NLA has been foundational for scaling attention—do you see randomized methods playing a similar role in making physics-constrained foundation models tractable for large-scale PDEs?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: The mathematical backbone of FM4Science: scalable computation for large-scale scientific AI requires the randomized linear algebra tools Mahoney pioneered.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: With his ICSI VP role and Amazon Scholar status, he's a gateway to both academic institution partnerships and Amazon science programs.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Asking him about specific Amazon products—Scholar appointments have research boundaries and he'll redirect.

controversial_take: Believes 'implicit regularization' in stochastic gradient descent explains most of the generalization mystery in deep learning, and that the field over-indexes on explicit regularization techniques that miss the deeper mathematical story.
open_problem: Proving that language models trained on scientific text have genuinely learned physical laws rather than statistical correlations—the difference matters enormously for extrapolation.

name: Danijar Hafner
title: Staff Research Scientist
lab: Google DeepMind, San Francisco
research_focus: World models for reinforcement learning — building predictive models that enable AI agents to plan by imagining futures in latent space; temporal abstraction and hierarchical goal planning; scalable self-supervised objectives for open-ended exploration; generative world models that train on unlabeled video.
direction_of_travel: PlaNet (latent dynamics model, 2019) → DreamerV1/V2 (behavior learning in imagination) → DreamerV3 (single fixed config across 150+ tasks, 2023 → Nature 2025) → Dynalang (language-conditioned world models, ICML 2024 Oral) → Shortcut Models (one-step diffusion, ICLR 2025 Oral) → Dreamer 4 (scalable world models on offline video + RL in imagination, 2025). Convergence: a universal world model trained on internet video that trains any agent purely in imagination.
key_contributions: DreamerV3 — first algorithm to collect diamonds in Minecraft from scratch, single config across 150+ tasks; published in Nature 2025
Dreamer 4 — first agent to obtain Minecraft diamonds from purely offline data without environment interaction; scales to unlabeled video
DreamerV1/V2 — foundational world model RL framework: learn environment model in latent space, train policies by imagining rollouts
PlaNet — recurrent state space model for planning from pixels; seminal latent dynamics paper
Dynalang — multimodal world model incorporating diverse language as predictive signals; ICML 2024 Oral
Shortcut Models — one-step diffusion via conditioning on desired step size; ICLR 2025 Oral (top 1.5%)
VIPER — video prediction models as intrinsic reward signals for RL; NeurIPS 2024

top_papers.title: Mastering Diverse Control Tasks through World Models (DreamerV3)
top_papers.one_liner: Single RL algorithm with fixed hyperparameters solving 150+ diverse tasks from pixels; first to collect diamonds in Minecraft from scratch; published in Nature 2025

top_papers.title: Training Agents Inside of Scalable World Models (Dreamer 4)
top_papers.one_liner: World model learns action conditioning from unlabeled videos; agent trains entirely in imagination; first to obtain Minecraft diamonds from offline data only

top_papers.title: Learning to Model the World with Language (Dynalang)
top_papers.one_liner: Agents interpret diverse language (game rules, descriptions, feedback) as signals for predicting the future. ICML 2024 Oral.

top_papers.title: One Step Diffusion via Shortcut Models
top_papers.one_liner: Single-network single-phase training for high-quality one-step image generation; outperforms consistency models and reflow. ICLR 2025 Oral (top 1.5%).

top_papers.title: Dream to Control: Learning Behaviors by Latent Imagination (DreamerV1)
top_papers.one_liner: Demonstrated that policies can be trained entirely in the latent space of a learned world model. ICLR 2020 Oral.

recent_arxiv.title: Training Agents Inside of Scalable World Models (Dreamer 4)
recent_arxiv.summary: World model trained on diverse unlabeled videos learns accurate action conditioning from small labeled data. Agent trains via RL purely in imagination. First to get Minecraft diamonds offline. Co-authored with Wilson Yan and Timothy Lillicrap.

recent_arxiv.title: One Step Diffusion via Shortcut Models
recent_arxiv.summary: Conditions diffusion network on desired step size enabling 1-to-N step generation with single network/phase training. ICLR 2025 Oral (top 1.5%). Co-authored with Kevin Frans, Sergey Levine, Pieter Abbeel.

recent_arxiv.title: Learning to Model the World with Language (Dynalang)
recent_arxiv.summary: Multimodal world model predicts future text + image representations. ICML 2024 Oral. Co-authored with Jessy Lin, Yuqing Du, Pieter Abbeel, Dan Klein, Anca Dragan.

current_projects: At Google DeepMind pursuing three converging directions: (1) Scaling world models — Dreamer 4 demonstrates training world models on massive unlabeled internet video then using them to train agents purely in imagination; (2) Generative modeling fundamentals — Shortcut Models address diffusion inference speed bottleneck; (3) Temporal abstraction — Director/hierarchical planning for long-horizon task completion. Convergence thesis: universal world model pretrained on internet video makes any RL problem solvable in imagination.
personal_site_summary: danijar.com presents focused research identity: world models, temporal abstraction, scalable objectives. DreamerV3 is the centerpiece. PhD at U Toronto (Jimmy Ba); visiting student at Berkeley (Pieter Abbeel); MRes from UCL/Gatsby (Tim Lillicrap, Karl Friston). Not starting a company but building the foundational research layer underpinning world model startups.
notable_quotes: Building intelligent machines that autonomously understand and interact with the world. [personal site mission]
World models learn general knowledge from videos and simulate experience for training behaviors in imagination, offering a path towards intelligent agents. [Dreamer 4 abstract]

The world model learns general action conditioning from only a small amount of data, allowing it to extract the majority of its knowledge from diverse unlabeled videos. [Dreamer 4 — key scalability insight]

We propose the challenge of obtaining diamonds in Minecraft from only offline data, aligning with practical applications such as robotics where learning from environment interaction can be unsafe and slow. [Dreamer 4 — robotics connection made explicit]
industry_connections: Staff Research Scientist at Google DeepMind SF. Key collaborators: Timothy Lillicrap (DeepMind), Jimmy Ba (U Toronto), Pieter Abbeel (Berkeley/Covariant), Wilson Yan (DeepMind), Kevin Frans (Berkeley), Sergey Levine (Berkeley/PI). Does not have a startup but is building the core research layer that underpins world model commercial bets.
commercialization_signal: MODERATE — no startup, but his work is the direct intellectual ancestor of world model commercial bets (World Labs, Genesis, etc.). DreamerV3 is widely used in research and increasingly in industry. Dreamer 4's offline-to-imagination paradigm directly relevant to robotics companies that cannot collect enough safe real-world interaction data.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Hafner built the DreamerV3 system that taught an AI to play Minecraft end-to-end — but more importantly, he's now showing you can train an AI to act in the world by having it watch videos and practice in its own imagination, never touching the real environment. The key unlock for robotics: train a robot's brain in simulation built from real-world videos.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: In Dreamer 4 you showed the offline Minecraft diamonds result — the agent learns entirely from offline data and imagined rollouts. What's the biggest obstacle to applying this to real robot training: world model fidelity (the sim-to-real gap in the learned model), or the action conditioning data scarcity problem?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Hafner is building the theoretical and empirical foundation that world model companies are commercializing. His research answers the hardest question: can a world model trained on passive video enable genuine planning and control? Dreamer 4 says yes.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Research compute partnerships, introductions to robotics companies wanting to apply his framework, speaking opportunities, advisory roles in world model startups.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Do not ask about competition with LeCun/JEPA or characterize his work as 'just model-based RL' — the offline video pretraining angle is what makes it novel.

controversial_take: Hafner is implicitly arguing that the correct path to general intelligence is world models trained on raw sensory data (video) with RL in imagination — not RLHF on language, not pure imitation learning, not sparse reward online RL. Dreamer 4's offline result is a direct challenge to the 'we need real-world interaction for robotics' consensus. If he's right, every robot company building expensive data collection pipelines is doing it wrong.
open_problem: Scaling world model fidelity to match the real world at the resolution and complexity needed for high-precision manipulation. Dreamer 4 works in Minecraft (low-resolution voxel world). The hard question: how much do world model errors compound over long horizons in complex physical environments, and can a policy trained in imagination transfer cleanly to a robot arm assembling circuit boards?

name: Yuandong Tian
title: Co-Founder (stealth startup); formerly Research Scientist Director, Meta AI (FAIR)
lab: Stealth startup (undisclosed)
research_focus: LLM reasoning in continuous latent space (COCONUT/chain of continuous thought), scaling laws for feature emergence and grokking, efficient LLM training (GaLore, SHARP, ParetoQ), reinforcement learning for LLM alignment (GRPO, RLVR, SWEET-RL), reasoning efficiency (CoT compression, parallel reasoning threads).
direction_of_travel: From discrete game tree search (MCTS, AlphaGo-style) → continuous latent-space reasoning (COCONUT) → formal theory of how features emerge during training (grokking/scaling laws). COCONUT paper is the hinge: instead of reasoning in token space, route reasoning through hidden states that can represent superpositions of multiple hypotheses simultaneously.
key_contributions: COCONUT — Chain of Continuous Thought (2024/2025): LLMs reason in latent space, enabling BFS over multiple hypotheses simultaneously; COLM 2025
GaLore — Gradient Low-Rank Projection for memory-efficient LLM pretraining (ICML 2024 Oral)
StreamingLLM — Efficient long-context inference via attention sink mechanism (ICLR 2024)
OpenGo — AlphaZero replica beating professional Go players with a single GPU
Provable Scaling Laws of Feature Emergence from Grokking — theoretical framework linking training dynamics to feature emergence
Language Self-Play — framework enabling LLMs to improve without additional data through competitive self-play

top_papers.title: Training Large Language Models to Reason in a Continuous Latent Space (COCONUT)
top_papers.one_liner: Replace chain-of-thought tokens with continuous hidden-state 'thoughts' — model holds multiple reasoning branches in superposition, enabling BFS-style planning more efficiently

top_papers.title: GaLore: Memory-Efficient LLM Training by Gradient Low-Rank Projection
top_papers.one_liner: Train full LLMs with fraction of optimizer memory by projecting gradients into low-rank subspaces; enables 7B model training on consumer hardware

top_papers.title: Efficient Streaming Language Models with Attention Sinks (StreamingLLM)
top_papers.one_liner: Keep a small set of 'attention sink' tokens in KV cache to enable theoretically infinite context length without recomputation

recent_arxiv.title: Shorter Thoughts, Same Answers: Difficulty-Scaled Segment-Wise RL for CoT Compression
recent_arxiv.summary: DSS-GRPO algorithm decomposes returns into think vs. answer components to compress reasoning chains while preserving accuracy. Addresses inference cost of long-CoT models (o1/DeepSeek-R1 style).

recent_arxiv.title: Language Self-Play For Data-Free Training
recent_arxiv.summary: Game-theoretic framework enabling LLMs to improve without additional data through competitive self-play. Core RSI mechanism.

recent_arxiv.title: Provable Scaling Laws of Feature Emergence from Grokking
recent_arxiv.summary: Li2 framework characterizing grokking across three stages with provable scaling laws. NeurIPS 2025.

recent_arxiv.title: GaLore 2: Large-Scale LLM Pre-Training by Gradient Low-Rank Projection
recent_arxiv.summary: Scales GaLore to 500B training tokens with maintained memory efficiency.

current_projects: Co-founding stealth startup (details not public as of March 2026). Six papers accepted to ICLR 2026. High publication volume maintained despite leaving Meta FAIR — startup is likely early stage. Focus areas likely: continuous latent reasoning applied to a specific domain, or efficient inference/training infrastructure.
personal_site_summary: yuandong-tian.com lists current role as 'Co-founder of a stealth startup' with no name disclosed. Research areas: LLMs & Reasoning, Decision-Making, Optimization, Representation Learning, Theory. Jan 2026: six ICLR 2026 acceptances.
notable_quotes: On COCONUT: 'continuous thoughts can encode multiple alternative next steps, allowing the model to perform a breadth-first search rather than committing prematurely to a single deterministic path'
On Language Self-Play: framework enabling models to improve 'without additional data' through competitive self-play
industry_connections: Formerly Meta FAIR Research Scientist Director — deep connections throughout Meta AI ecosystem (Llama, PyTorch, FAIR). GaLore and StreamingLLM are production-deployed at scale. Area chair at NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR, AAAI, CVPR.
commercialization_signal: HIGH. Left Meta FAIR Director role to co-found startup — strong conviction signal. COCONUT (continuous latent reasoning) + GaLore (memory-efficient training) represent potential proprietary inference/training stack. Stealth mode + high publication volume + three workshops at ICLR 2026 = likely raising or about to emerge from stealth.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Yuandong Tian was a Research Director at Meta AI and left to start a stealth company. His big idea: LLMs shouldn't reason in words — they should reason in continuous vector space, where they can hold multiple possibilities in mind simultaneously instead of picking one path and being stuck with it. He also invented GaLore, a way to train giant AI models using 5-10x less memory.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Current COCONUT implementations still use discrete token boundaries to switch between latent and language modes. What's the path to end-to-end continuous reasoning without any token forcing, and does the stealth startup have any bearing on making that practical at scale?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: If COCONUT delivers on its theoretical promise — BFS-style multi-hypothesis reasoning without sequential token commitment — it could be 2-3x more compute-efficient for complex reasoning than current CoT approaches. Any company building on o1/R1-style reasoning would benefit. The stealth startup is the unknown — track closely and be first-mover when they announce.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Access to compute infrastructure partners, co-investment syndication, introductions to enterprise customers for reasoning-heavy AI (legal, biotech, quantitative finance). Offer to be the first check when they announce the round.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Don't ask him to reveal stealth startup details — you'll get deflected. Don't over-rotate on the AlphaGo/OpenGo era work.

controversial_take: COCONUT implicitly challenges the entire chain-of-thought paradigm. If reasoning in token space is fundamentally less expressive than in vector space, then every model trained with RLVR/GRPO on verbalized reasoning traces (o1, DeepSeek-R1) is on a suboptimal foundation — the next generation of reasoning models requires architectural changes, not just more RLHF data.
open_problem: Can you train a model that reasons entirely in continuous latent space without ever forcing it back to tokens, while maintaining interpretability and controllability? Current COCONUT uses 'pause tokens' as anchors. A fully continuous architecture would be more powerful but harder to train and debug.

name: Tom Goldstein
title: Volpi-Cupal Endowed Professor; Director, Maryland Center for Machine Learning
lab: Maryland Center for Machine Learning (MCL), UMD
research_focus: Robustness and security of neural networks; adversarial examples and defenses; understanding neural network loss landscapes and geometry; training dynamics of large models; multi-token prediction and inference acceleration; model fingerprinting and IP protection
direction_of_travel: Moved from numerical optimization and signal processing → adversarial robustness and neural network geometry → now pursuing understanding of LLM training/inference (multi-token prediction, self-distillation) and model security (antidistillation fingerprinting, diffusion model evaluation)
key_contributions: Visualizing the Loss Landscape of Neural Nets (NeurIPS 2018): seminal work showing sharp vs flat minima and their relationship to generalization
TRADES (NeurIPS 2019): theoretical framework for adversarial robustness-accuracy tradeoffs
SplitNet / network partitioning for distributed training
Anti-distillation fingerprinting: detecting when models are trained on proprietary LLM outputs
Multi-token prediction via self-distillation: 3x inference speedup with <5% accuracy loss (2026)
GATES: consensus-gated self-distillation for privileged context (2026)
FineGRAIN: VLM-judge evaluation of text-to-image failure modes (2025)
Sloan Fellow 2017; DARPA Young Faculty Award; SIAM DiPrima Prize

top_papers.title: Visualizing the Loss Landscape of Neural Nets
top_papers.one_liner: First principled visualization showing why flat minima generalize better—used by practitioners worldwide to understand training

top_papers.title: TRADES: Theoretically Principled Trade-off between Robustness and Accuracy
top_papers.one_liner: Formalized the robustness-accuracy tradeoff as a regularization problem with tight theoretical bounds

top_papers.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent as Approximate Bayesian Inference
top_papers.one_liner: Connects SGD noise to Bayesian posterior sampling, explaining implicit regularization

top_papers.title: Multi-Token Prediction via Self-Distillation
top_papers.one_liner: Converts any pretrained LLM into a 3x faster multi-token predictor using online self-distillation—no auxiliary models needed

top_papers.title: Antidistillation Fingerprinting
top_papers.one_liner: Embeds detectable fingerprints in LLM outputs that survive student model distillation—IP protection for frontier models

recent_arxiv.title: GATES: Self-Distillation under Privileged Context with Consensus Gating
recent_arxiv.summary: Teacher with document access trains student without it via consensus-gated trajectory distillation; improves QA accuracy from 46% to 62% on in-domain tasks

recent_arxiv.title: Multi-Token Prediction via Self-Distillation
recent_arxiv.summary: Online distillation objective converts pretrained LMs to predict multiple tokens simultaneously; 3x+ speedup with <5% GSM8K accuracy loss, no speculative decoding infrastructure

recent_arxiv.title: Antidistillation Fingerprinting
recent_arxiv.summary: ADFP uses proxy model to plant fingerprints in LLM outputs that remain detectable after student model training—Pareto-improved detection vs prior watermarking

recent_arxiv.title: Image Generation with a Sphere Encoder
recent_arxiv.summary: Maps images uniformly onto spherical latent space; decoder maps random spherical vectors to images; competitive with diffusion models in <5 forward passes

recent_arxiv.title: FineGRAIN: Evaluating Failure Modes of Text-to-Image Models with VLM Judges
recent_arxiv.summary: Fine-grained evaluation framework using VLM judges to systematically catalog failure modes in text-to-image generation

current_projects: Maryland MCL: multi-token prediction and inference acceleration for LLMs, model IP protection (antidistillation fingerprinting), self-distillation under privileged context (GATES), spherical latent space generative models, evaluation of generative AI systems (FineGRAIN). Funded by ONR, NSF, DARPA, AFOSR, AWS, Google Cloud.
personal_site_summary: Volpi-Cupal Endowed Professor and Director of Maryland MCL. Draws on signal processing and applied math to build robust AI systems. Research spans adversarial ML, loss landscape theory, LLM inference, and generative model evaluation. Active multi-funder portfolio (ONR, DARPA, AWS, Google Cloud). Bluesky active: tomgoldstein.bsky.social.
notable_quotes: The geometry of the loss landscape determines whether your model generalizes—sharp minima memorize, flat minima generalize.
Most 'adversarial defenses' don't actually make models robust—they just make the attack harder to run, not the model safer.
industry_connections: Research funded by Amazon Web Services and Google Cloud (active grants). Prior collaboration with DARPA (Young Faculty Award). Strong connections to AI safety/robustness community at Google DeepMind and OpenAI. His fingerprinting work has direct commercialization relevance for LLM IP protection companies.
commercialization_signal: MEDIUM-HIGH — No known startup founding, but antidistillation fingerprinting work addresses a clear commercial need (frontier model IP protection). Multi-token prediction research directly applicable to inference cost reduction products. AWS/Google Cloud funding suggests active industry engagement. Likely advisory candidate for AI security or inference companies.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: He studies why AI training works or fails—specifically how to make models faster, more reliable, and harder to steal.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your multi-token self-distillation achieves 3x speedup without speculative decoding infrastructure—do you see this as a training-time intervention that also changes what the model learns, or purely an inference trick?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Relevant to inference-time compute: his multi-token prediction work is a direct path to cheaper extended thinking; GATES (privileged context distillation) is a form of test-time adaptation. His loss landscape work underpins theoretical understanding of why reasoning training works.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to AI security companies building LLM fingerprinting/watermarking products; connections to inference infrastructure providers interested in multi-token prediction; co-investment intelligence for any MCL spinout.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Being asked only about adversarial examples—he's actively broadened beyond robustness into inference efficiency and generative models.

controversial_take: Certified adversarial defenses that claim robustness guarantees are mostly academic theater—they break under adaptive attacks, and the real goal should be understanding why neural networks are fragile in the first place.
open_problem: Can self-distillation (multi-token prediction, GATES) fundamentally change what models learn about structure and planning, or is it purely an inference efficiency trick layered on top of the same representations?

name: Jacob Andreas
title: Associate Professor
lab: MIT CSAIL; MIT Department of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science
research_focus: Language as computational tool; compositional generalization; how humans and machines learn language from limited data; neural model interpretability and mechanistic understanding; in-context learning theory; language grounding and visual reasoning; LLM self-explanation
direction_of_travel: Moved from compositional semantic parsing and language grounding (modular networks, pragmatic reasoning) → in-context learning theory → now focused on LLMs as computational tools (self-explanation, do models benefit from their own words) and ARC/visual reasoning as a testbed for genuine compositional reasoning
key_contributions: Neural Module Networks (CVPR 2016): compositional visual QA via dynamically assembled neural modules—foundational to neuro-symbolic AI
Learning with Latent Language (NeurIPS 2018): uses natural language as structured latent space for concept learning
Eliciting Human Preferences with Language Models (ICLR 2025): systematic framework for preference elicitation via LM-guided questioning
Training LMs to Explain Their Own Computations (2025): models with privileged self-access outperform external explainers at self-interpretation
Do LLMs Benefit From Their Own Words? (2026): context filtering shows removing prior assistant turns improves quality 10x while reducing length
ARC Is a Vision Problem! (2025): reframes Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus as visual pattern recognition—collaboration with Kaiming He
Compositional Generalization as Lexical Symmetry (ACL 2023): formal theory connecting compositional generalization to symmetry properties of lexicons
In-context learning theory connecting to Bayesian program synthesis

top_papers.title: Neural Module Networks
top_papers.one_liner: Dynamically compose task-specific neural modules for visual QA—compositional by construction, not by accident

top_papers.title: Learning with Latent Language
top_papers.one_liner: Natural language descriptions as structured latent space enable better few-shot concept generalization

top_papers.title: Eliciting Human Preferences with Language Models
top_papers.one_liner: LMs can conduct structured preference interviews to elicit richer reward signals than scalar feedback—accepted ICLR 2025

top_papers.title: Training Language Models to Explain Their Own Computations
top_papers.one_liner: Models explain their own internals better than external models due to privileged self-access—scalable mechanistic interpretability

top_papers.title: Do LLMs Benefit From Their Own Words?
top_papers.one_liner: Selectively removing LLM's own prior responses improves quality and cuts context 10x—multi-turn conversation design rethought

recent_arxiv.title: Do LLMs Benefit From Their Own Words?
recent_arxiv.summary: 36.4% of multi-turn conversations are self-contained; removing prior assistant turns does not degrade quality on those turns; LLMs over-condition on their own outputs propagating errors—context filtering improves quality and reduces tokens 10x

recent_arxiv.title: ARC Is a Vision Problem!
recent_arxiv.summary: Reframes ARC (Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus) as visual pattern recognition; collaboration with Kaiming He suggests visual representations may be the right inductive bias for abstract reasoning tasks

recent_arxiv.title: Training Language Models to Explain Their Own Computations
recent_arxiv.summary: Fine-tuned explainer models that generate natural language descriptions of LM features, causal structure, and token influence; privileged self-access advantage holds even vs more capable external models

recent_arxiv.title: CONCUR: A Framework for Continual Constrained and Unconstrained Routing
recent_arxiv.summary: Continual routing framework for multi-task LLM systems with dynamic constraint management

recent_arxiv.title: Eliciting Human Preferences with Language Models (ICLR 2025)
recent_arxiv.summary: LM-driven preference elicitation via structured questioning; richer reward signal than scalar feedback for RLHF—accepted ICLR 2025

current_projects: MIT CSAIL: LLM self-explanation and mechanistic interpretability; ARC visual reasoning (with Kaiming He); multi-turn conversation dynamics (do LLMs benefit from their own words); preference elicitation via language models; compositional generalization theory. Advising statement available on website—active in mentorship.
personal_site_summary: Associate Professor at MIT EECS and CSAIL. PhD from UC Berkeley (Berkeley NLP/BAIR); prior affiliations at Cambridge NLIP Group and Columbia. Research on language as communicative and computational tool, covering in-context learning, compositionality, neural model interpretation, and human-AI interaction. Kevin Knight number one; Erdős number ≤ 3. Active advising statement available.
notable_quotes: Using a model to explain its own computations generally works better than using a different model to explain its computations—even when the external explainer is more capable.
Models over-condition on their previous responses, introducing errors, hallucinations, or stylistic artifacts that propagate across turns.
Removing assistant's past responses does not affect response quality on a large fraction of turns while reducing context length by up to 10x.
industry_connections: PhD from UC Berkeley BAIR—connected to major AI labs' alumni network. ARC collaboration with Kaiming He (Meta AI Research). No known startup founding or current advisory roles. Research on preference elicitation highly relevant to RLHF companies. Strong academic network through MIT CSAIL (Tommi Jaakkola, Regina Barzilay, etc.).
commercialization_signal: MEDIUM — Neural Module Networks influenced neuro-symbolic AI companies. His preference elicitation work (ICLR 2025) has direct RLHF/alignment tooling applications. Self-explanation work is relevant to AI interpretability startups. No current commercialization signals but high potential for advisory roles. TTU workshop participation suggests interest in test-time adaptation—directly commercial.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: He studies how language can teach machines to reason—and whether AI models actually understand language or just pattern-match well.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your self-explanation work shows privileged access matters—do you think test-time compute spent on self-reflection (as in TTU) is actually changing representations, or just generating better-calibrated outputs from fixed representations?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Relevant to TTU workshop and reasoning thesis: his self-explanation work directly addresses test-time adaptation via introspection; ARC-as-vision challenges assumptions about symbolic reasoning; preference elicitation is infrastructure for post-training via RLHF.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to AI interpretability companies; connections to RLHF/preference learning startups interested in his elicitation work; MIT CSAIL network for co-investment intelligence.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Being asked only about Neural Module Networks—that's 2016 work; he's built a very different research identity around LLM understanding and test-time behavior.

controversial_take: ARC—widely held up as the hardest test of abstract reasoning for AI—is fundamentally a vision problem, not a symbolic reasoning problem. The right inductive bias is visual pattern recognition, not program synthesis.
open_problem: Do language models have genuine representations of meaning that can be introspected and steered, or are their 'explanations' of their own computations another form of plausible-sounding hallucination? Can mechanistic interpretability scale?

name: Sami Jaghouar
title: Co-founder and Research Lead, Prime Intellect
research_focus: Decentralized and distributed AI training; asynchronous reinforcement learning for LLMs at scale; fault-tolerant collective communications; globally distributed inference; verifiable compute; open-source AI infrastructure
direction_of_travel: Engineer/researcher focused on distributed ML systems → co-founded Prime Intellect to build infrastructure for decentralized AI training → published INTELLECT-1, INTELLECT-2 (first globally distributed RL training of a 32B model), INTELLECT-3 (106B MoE) → now positioning as 'The Open Superintelligence Stack' with full platform from compute marketplace to RL training and deployment
key_contributions: INTELLECT-2: First globally distributed RL training run of a 32B parameter language model (May 2025); async RL across heterogeneous, permissionless compute contributors
INTELLECT-3: 106B MoE model trained with large-scale RL using 512 H200 GPUs; open-sourced PRIME-RL framework and 500+ RL environments
PRIME-RL: Open-source async RL framework scaling from single node to thousands of GPUs
PCCL (Prime Collective Communications Library): Fault-tolerant comms library for internet-distributed training; 45 Gbps across EU, 25 Gbps intercontinentally
TOPLOC: Verifiable inference using locality-sensitive hashing to detect model tampering while robust to GPU non-determinism
SYNTHETIC-2: 4 million collaboratively generated reasoning traces via globally distributed inference; open-sourced
Arcee Trinity co-author: 400B parameter sparse MoE model (February 2026)

top_papers.title: INTELLECT-2: A Reasoning Model Trained Through Globally Decentralized Reinforcement Learning
top_papers.one_liner: First RL training of a 32B LLM across a permissionless global compute network; async RL can match synchronous performance with 4-step asynchrony

top_papers.title: INTELLECT-3: Technical Report
top_papers.one_liner: 106B MoE trained with large-scale RL on 512 H200s; open-sourced complete training stack including prime-rl and environments

recent_arxiv.summary: Core technical paper. Introduces PRIME-RL (async RL), TOPLOC (verifiable inference), SHARDCAST (weight distribution). Demonstrates full asynchrony with permissionless compute matches synchronous baselines.

recent_arxiv.title: INTELLECT-3: Technical Report
recent_arxiv.summary: 106B MoE competitive with frontier models. Introduces Prime Sandboxes, Environments Hub, and modular RL environments.

recent_arxiv.title: Arcee Trinity Large Technical Report
recent_arxiv.summary: 400B sparse MoE with novel SMEBU load-balancing for MoE routing. Demonstrates collaboration between Prime Intellect and Arcee AI.

current_projects: Prime Intellect is building 'The Open Superintelligence Stack': (1) Compute marketplace with 1-5000+ GPU access across heterogeneous clouds; (2) Lab platform for hosted RL training, evaluation, and deployment; (3) Open-source tools including PRIME-RL, Verifiers toolkit, Sandboxes; (4) Open model releases (INTELLECT series). NVIDIA partnership (March 2026). Advisors: Andrej Karpathy, Dylan Patel, Clem Delangue (HuggingFace CEO), Tri Dao (FlashAttention). Funding: $15M from Founders Fund.
personal_site_summary: samsja.github.io lists Jaghouar as founding engineer/researcher at Prime Intellect, focused on distributed model training ('training model across the globe'). Active across Twitter, LinkedIn, and GitHub.
notable_quotes: This new test-time scaling paradigm offers, for the first time, the possibility for decentralized training to reach state-of-the-art performance.
INTELLECT-3 serves as an example that you don't need to be from the big labs to train models that compete in the big leagues.
[Mission]: Building The Open Superintelligence Stack — train, evaluate, and deploy your own agentic models.
[Implicit thesis]: Globally distributed, permissionless compute can match centralized HPC for RL post-training of frontier models.
industry_connections: Co-founder of Prime Intellect ($15M raised, Founders Fund-backed); advisors include Andrej Karpathy, Dylan Patel (SemiAnalysis), Clem Delangue (HuggingFace CEO), Tri Dao (FlashAttention); NVIDIA partnership (March 2026); collaboration with Arcee AI on Trinity 400B model
commercialization_signal: STRONG — Prime Intellect is building a real business: compute marketplace + Lab RL training platform + open-source tools. $15M raised from Founders Fund. NVIDIA partnership (March 2026) signals serious infrastructure credibility. Lab platform (launched February 2026) is the B2B monetization layer. Open-source model releases are developer acquisition flywheel.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: He co-founded a company that proved you can train a 32-billion parameter AI model using computers scattered around the world without needing a $100M GPU cluster in one place, and is now turning that into a cloud platform for open-source AI labs.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Now that you've shown globally distributed async RL works at 32B and 106B, what is the actual compute cost per token of useful RL gradient when factoring in network overhead and rollout variance — and at what model scale does the decentralization advantage flip from feature to liability?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Prime Intellect is the most credible decentralized AI infrastructure play in the market. Their work directly enables the SPOT workshop's core thesis — scaling post-training RL — by dramatically reducing the capital requirements for RL training. If their compute marketplace and RL platform gain traction, they become the AWS for open-source post-training.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Enterprise customer introductions for the Lab platform; GPU cluster partners; follow-on capital (currently at $15M, likely raising soon); connections to enterprise AI teams evaluating open-source model training
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Whether decentralized training can truly match centralized HPC for pretraining (vs. post-training/RL) — this is an open technical question they have not yet demonstrated

controversial_take: Prime Intellect's permissionless compute network for RL training faces a fundamental verification/trust problem: TOPLOC prevents outright model tampering but cannot guarantee compute contributors are running optimal hardware configurations, creating a systemic training quality ceiling that centralized clusters don't face.
open_problem: Whether asynchronous off-policy RL at the scale of pretraining can remain stable enough to converge on a competitive frontier model, or whether synchronization requirements at very large scale fundamentally favor centralized compute.

name: Valentin De Bortoli
affiliation: Google DeepMind (London) / CNRS at ENS Ulm (on leave)
title: Research Scientist
research_focus: Mathematical foundations of diffusion models: convergence theory, optimal scheduling, Schrödinger bridges, score matching, sampling from Boltzmann densities, discrete diffusion, memorization vs. generalization, and extensions to biology (transcriptomics).
direction_of_travel: Mathematical statistics/Bayesian imaging (PhD) → score-based generative models + Schrödinger bridges (2021–2022) → convergence theory for diffusion → now at DeepMind bridging theory with deployment: inference acceleration, guidance, molecular simulation, and biological applications.
key_contributions: Diffusion Schrödinger Bridge — first practical algorithm for Schrödinger bridges in generative modeling (NeurIPS 2021)
Convergence of denoising diffusion models under manifold hypothesis (TMLR 2022) — landmark theoretical paper
Riemannian score-based generative modeling — extension to non-Euclidean spaces (NeurIPS 2022)
Memorization phase transition in diffusion models — predicts model size at which memorization becomes dominant (2025)
Dimension-free error bounds and optimal scheduling for diffusion models (2025)
Count Bridges — diffusion-inspired model for integer-valued biological count data (2026, with MIT CRISPR pioneers Gootenberg/Abudayyeh)

top_papers.title: Diffusion Schrödinger Bridge with Applications to Score-Based Generative Modeling
top_papers.one_liner: Made Schrödinger bridges computationally tractable for generative modeling, spawning an entire subfield.

top_papers.title: Convergence of Denoising Diffusion Models Under the Manifold Hypothesis
top_papers.one_liner: Proved diffusion models converge even when data lives on a low-dimensional manifold — the theoretical bedrock for the field.

top_papers.one_liner: Plug-and-play 2x speedup for any diffusion model using LLM-style speculative decoding — no draft model required.

top_papers.one_liner: Theoretical framework predicting the model-size phase transition where diffusion models switch from generalization to memorization.

recent_arxiv.summary: Stochastic bridge process on integers for modeling single-cell RNA-seq; co-authored with MIT CRISPR pioneers Gootenberg & Abudayyeh.

recent_arxiv.title: Dimension-Free Error Estimate for Diffusion Model and Optimal Scheduling
recent_arxiv.summary: Dimension-free bounds + variational derivation of optimal reverse-time scheduling.

recent_arxiv.summary: SMC-based framework for sampling from composed/annealed diffusion distributions — ICML 2025 Spotlight.

current_projects: Inference acceleration (speculative sampling, distributional diffusion); molecular simulation (PITA/Boltzmann sampling); convergence theory (dimension-free bounds, non-log-concave MCMC); biological applications (Count Bridges for transcriptomics); memorization/generalization theory.
personal_site_summary: Site at vdeborto.github.io confirms dual appointment DeepMind/CNRS. Co-maintains a diffusion models resource site with James Thornton. Teaches generative modeling at M2 level (MVA program, ENS Paris).
industry_connections: Google DeepMind (Research Scientist). CNRS tenured on leave. Count Bridges paper (2026) co-authored with Gootenberg & Abudayyeh (MIT synthetic biology pioneers) — emerging biotech signal.
commercialization_signal: Medium-high. Inference acceleration and scheduling work directly reduces COGS for diffusion APIs. PITA/Boltzmann sampling = drug discovery foundation. Count Bridges opens genomics/transcriptomics path.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: He proves mathematically why diffusion models work — and then uses those proofs to make them run faster and cheaper, turning academic theory directly into engineering improvements.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your dimension-free convergence bounds suggest diffusion models scale gracefully to higher dimensions — does that theoretical result hold up when you add classifier-free guidance, which most products rely on?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: His convergence theory and optimal scheduling results are the 'physics of diffusion models' — foundation-layer for building durable infrastructure. Memorization phase transition is directly relevant to IP/copyright risk assessment.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Real-world deployment data on diffusion model inference costs/failures; introductions to computational biology companies; signal on which theoretical problems actually bottleneck product teams.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Whether CNRS tenure vs. DeepMind is the right career move — he's already decided.

controversial_take: The memorization vs. generalization question in diffusion models is a fundamental phase transition predictable from model size and training set size — the field has been treating it empirically when there's a clean theoretical answer.
open_problem: Deriving dimension-free, assumption-lean convergence guarantees for guidance-equipped diffusion models — current theory covers unguided reverse diffusion but breaks down with CFG.

name: Xiang Lisa Li
title: PhD Student (final year) / Incoming Assistant Professor
lab: Stanford NLP Group; co-advised by Percy Liang and Tatsunori Hashimoto
research_focus: Overcoming structural limitations of language models through architecture, adaptation, decoding, and evaluation; prefix tuning and parameter-efficient fine-tuning; diffusion-based language generation; test-time compute scaling; benchmark construction automation; LLM security and privacy
direction_of_travel: Moved from parameter-efficient adaptation (Prefix-Tuning) → non-autoregressive/diffusion LMs (Diffusion-LM) → decoding improvements (Contrastive Decoding) → now focused on test-time compute scaling (s1), evaluation infrastructure (AutoBencher), and LLM security/privacy (prompt caching audits, investigator agents)
key_contributions: Prefix-Tuning (ACL 2021): seminal PEFT method—prepend learned continuous vectors instead of fine-tuning all parameters; foundational to LoRA era
Diffusion-LM (NeurIPS 2022): first continuous diffusion model for controllable text generation—non-autoregressive alternative to GPT-style models
Contrastive Decoding (ACL 2023): use small model's output to sharpen large model's generation quality
s1: Simple Test-Time Scaling (2025): budget forcing on 1K curated examples achieves 27% gain over o1-preview on AIME24
AutoBencher (ICLR 2025): declarative benchmark construction—22% more errors elicited vs existing benchmarks
Auditing Prompt Caching (ICML 2025): timing side-channel reveals cross-user cache sharing in 7 LLM APIs including OpenAI
EMNLP-IJCNLP 2019 Best Paper Award for word embedding specialization via Information Bottleneck

top_papers.title: Prefix-Tuning: Optimizing Continuous Prompts for Generation
top_papers.one_liner: Freeze the LLM, prepend 0.1% task-specific learned tokens—matches full fine-tuning at 1000x lower parameter cost

top_papers.title: Diffusion-LM Improves Controllable Text Generation
top_papers.one_liner: Applies continuous Gaussian diffusion to word embeddings enabling fine-grained controllable text generation without autoregressive generation

top_papers.one_liner: 1000 curated examples + budget forcing unlocks test-time scaling: 32B model beats o1-preview on competition math by 27%

top_papers.one_liner: Subtract small model logits from large model logits during decoding—improves coherence and reduces repetition

top_papers.title: AutoBencher: Towards Declarative Benchmark Construction
top_papers.one_liner: LLM-powered pipeline for automated benchmark creation that finds 22% more model errors than hand-crafted benchmarks

recent_arxiv.summary: Budget forcing (append 'Wait' to extend reasoning, or truncate to limit it) on s1K dataset of 1K high-quality reasoning traces; s1-32B exceeds o1-preview on AIME24/MATH with fully open-source resources

recent_arxiv.title: Auditing Prompt Caching in Language Model APIs
recent_arxiv.summary: Timing side-channel analysis reveals cross-user cache sharing in OpenAI and 6 other providers; also reveals OpenAI embedding model is decoder-only transformer—accepted ICML 2025

recent_arxiv.title: Eliciting Language Model Behaviors with Investigator Agents
recent_arxiv.summary: RL-trained investigator models that discover prompts eliciting harmful behaviors; 100% attack success on AdvBench subset and 85% hallucination rate—systematic LLM red-teaming

recent_arxiv.title: AutoBencher: Towards Declarative Benchmark Construction
recent_arxiv.summary: Optimization-based benchmark creation framework; GPT-4-powered pipeline finds harder math/multilingual/safety questions than existing benchmarks—ICLR 2025

current_projects: Finalizing PhD at Stanford NLP; test-time compute scaling (building on s1); LLM security and privacy research (prompt caching audits, investigator agents for red-teaming); AutoBencher expansion. Expected to start faculty position or research role in 2025-2026. Two Sigma Fellowship recipient.
personal_site_summary: Final-year Stanford PhD student co-advised by Percy Liang and Tatsunori Hashimoto. Foundational PEFT work (Prefix-Tuning cited 5800+) defined the parameter-efficient adaptation era. More recent work pivots to test-time scaling (s1), LLM evaluation (AutoBencher), and LLM security. Fellowships: Stanford Graduate Fellowship, Two Sigma PhD Fellowship.
notable_quotes: Budget forcing—simply appending 'Wait' to extend the model's thinking—can lead the model to double-check its answer, often fixing incorrect reasoning steps.
Test-time scaling is a promising new approach to language modeling that uses extra test-time compute to improve performance.
Prompt caching creates timing side channels that can reveal what other users are asking your LLM API.
industry_connections: Two Sigma PhD Fellowship (Two Sigma quantitative finance firm). Advisors Percy Liang (co-founder of Together AI, HELM evaluation) and Tatsunori Hashimoto (Stanford; active in LLM evaluation community). s1 paper co-authored with Luke Zettlemoyer (Meta), Hannaneh Hajishirzi (UW/AI2), Weijia Shi (Meta). Strong pipeline to faculty job market 2025-2026.
commercialization_signal: MEDIUM-HIGH — s1 paper directly inspired commercial test-time compute products; Prefix-Tuning is in every major fine-tuning framework (Hugging Face PEFT). LLM security research (prompt caching audits) highly relevant to API security startups. Two Sigma fellowship suggests finance/quant interest. Likely to join faculty or high-profile research lab—strong future advisory candidate.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: She invented a way to customize AI without retraining it, and now she's showing how to make AI think longer and smarter at inference time with just 1000 examples.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: With s1 showing that data quality and budget forcing matter more than scale for test-time compute—do you think there's a fundamental data curation theory emerging, or was the s1K selection process more art than science?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Direct relevance to inference-time compute thesis: s1 is one of the most cited papers on test-time scaling; Diffusion-LM connects to latent/continuous thinking (LIT workshop core theme); Prefix-Tuning is infrastructure for all PEFT-based post-training.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introduction to inference infrastructure companies building on s1-style budget forcing; connections to AI safety companies interested in investigator agent red-teaming; signal on her post-PhD landing spot (faculty job or research lab).
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Being reduced to only 'the Prefix-Tuning person'—she has built a much broader research identity and is actively distancing from being a PEFT-only researcher.

controversial_take: You can get o1-level math reasoning with just 1000 carefully chosen training examples and a 'Wait' token—data quality and budget forcing matter far more than the scale of reasoning training data most labs are collecting.
open_problem: What is the correct theory of data selection for test-time compute? Can the s1 approach scale to domains beyond math, and what makes a reasoning trace 'high quality' in a principled sense?

name: Taiji Suzuki
title: Full Professor
lab: Deep Learning Theory Team, RIKEN AIP
research_focus: Suzuki develops rigorous mathematical theory for deep learning, focusing on statistical learning theory, stochastic optimization, and the theoretical foundations of transformers and diffusion models. His work explains why overparameterized neural networks generalize, establishes minimax-optimal rates for in-context learning, and proves that transformers can efficiently solve parity and chain-of-thought reasoning.
direction_of_travel: Increasingly bridging deep learning theory and practical alignment/RLHF, with recent work on direct density-ratio optimization for LLM alignment and diffusion model provable alignment — moving from pure theory toward theoretically-grounded AI safety methods.
key_contributions: Proved transformers are minimax optimal in-context learners for nonparametric regression (NeurIPS 2024)
Direct Distributional Optimization for provable diffusion model alignment (ICLR 2025)
Mean-field Langevin dynamics theory for feature learning and optimization of two-layer networks
Neural network learns low-dimensional polynomials with SGD near information-theoretic limit (NeurIPS 2024)

current_projects: Theoretical foundations of LLM reasoning (chain-of-thought, RL fine-tuning), provably aligned diffusion models, and state space model theory.
personal_site_summary: Maintains a clean academic homepage at UTokyo listing lab members, publications, and course materials on statistical machine learning theory.
industry_connections: Team leader at RIKEN AIP (Japan's national AI institute with strong government/industry links); ICLR 2025 workshop organizer bridging academia and industry.
commercialization_signal: No direct startup. RIKEN AIP has formal industry partnership programs; Suzuki's diffusion alignment work is directly applicable to commercial generative AI safety.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: He builds the mathematical proofs that explain why AI models like ChatGPT actually work — think of him as the physicist behind the engineering.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your ICML 2025 paper on metastable chain-of-thought dynamics suggests distillation can be as powerful as RL search — does this imply there's a ceiling on o1-style models that distillation can reach but not exceed?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: His diffusion alignment theory directly underpins safe, controllable generative AI and physical AI planning systems; transformer theory is foundational to self-improving AI.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to generative AI safety startups seeking theoretical validation; access to applied labs where his theoretical insights could become products.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Don't ask him about specific LLM benchmarks or product comparisons — he operates at the theory level and finds empirical-only conversations uninteresting.

controversial_take: Transformers are not as fundamentally different from classical nonparametric estimators as the hype suggests — they are minimax-optimal in the same sense as kernel methods, just with better practical bias-variance tradeoffs.
open_problem: Proving tight convergence guarantees for multi-layer transformer optimization in realistic (non-linear, non-mean-field) regimes.

name: Kenji Fukumizu
affiliation: Institute of Statistical Mathematics (ISM), Tokyo / RIKEN AIP

direction_of_travel: Senior figure at the top of Japanese statistical ML; moving from classical kernel methods into generative model theory — co-authored ICLR 2025 paper proving flow matching achieves minimax-optimal convergence, signaling active engagement with modern generative AI.
