personal_site_summary: Comprehensive academic homepage at ISM listing research interests in kernel methods, deep learning, topological data analysis, and recent publications.
industry_connections: Affiliated with Preferred Networks (Japan's leading ML startup); RIKEN AIP industrial partnerships; Max Planck Institute collaborations.
commercialization_signal: Preferred Networks affiliation is a strong commercialization signal — PFN works on industrial robotics and autonomous driving. His kernel/RKHS work underlies many industrial anomaly detection systems.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: He built the mathematical toolkit that lets AI systems rigorously compare probability distributions — essential for training generative models and detecting data anomalies.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your ICLR 2025 proof that flow matching achieves minimax-optimal convergence — does this suggest that ODE-based flow matching is theoretically superior to SDE-based diffusion in the low-data regime, or is it data-distribution-dependent?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Flow matching theory is directly foundational to the diffusion/generative AI investment thesis; kernel methods and RKHS tools underpin anomaly detection in physical AI systems.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to Western generative AI companies that lack rigorous theoretical foundations; connections to autonomous systems companies needing distribution comparison tools.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Avoid asking about specific deep learning frameworks or hardware — he operates at the mathematical foundations level.

controversial_take: Flow matching will replace diffusion models as the dominant generative modeling paradigm because it is not just faster in inference but theoretically cleaner — the SDE formulation of diffusion is a historical accident.
open_problem: Extending kernel-based independence measures to work efficiently with modern high-dimensional neural network representations without losing statistical power.

name: René Vidal
title: Rachleff Penn Integrates Knowledge University Professor
lab: GRASP Lab / THEORINET (NSF-Simons Collaboration)
research_focus: Vidal works on the mathematical foundations of deep learning and trustworthy AI, developing theories for optimization landscapes, neural collapse, generalization, and robustness of deep networks. He leads THEORINET, an NSF-Simons collaboration on the mathematical foundations of deep learning, and applies these principles to computer vision and biomedical data science.
direction_of_travel: Transitioned from Johns Hopkins to Penn as an endowed Penn Integrates Knowledge professor — a prestigious appointment signaling institutional importance. His research has expanded from geometric vision and subspace methods to LLM safety and hallucination, with 5 NeurIPS 2025 papers.
key_contributions: Neural collapse theory: penultimate layer features converge to simplex ETF geometry during training
Sparse subspace clustering (SSC) — foundational algorithm for geometric data clustering
THEORINET: comprehensive mathematical framework for deep learning (NSF-Simons funded)
Semantically equivalent adversarial attacks for eliciting LLM hallucinations (NeurIPS 2025)

top_papers.title: Sparse Subspace Clustering: Algorithm, Theory, and Applications
top_papers.one_liner: Self-expressive sparse representation for clustering union-of-subspaces structured data — widely used in motion segmentation.

top_papers.title: Robust Recovery of Subspace Structures by Low-Rank Representation
top_papers.one_liner: Low-rank data recovery method for robustly finding subspace structure in noisy data.

top_papers.title: Neural Collapse under MSE Loss: Proximity to and Dynamics on the Central Path
top_papers.one_liner: Shows neural collapse (ETF convergence) occurs under MSE loss and describes the optimization path.

top_papers.title: Mathematics of Deep Learning
top_papers.one_liner: Survey of the mathematical questions underlying deep learning — a foundational reference for the field.

recent_arxiv.title: Semantically Equivalent Adversarial Attacks for Eliciting LLM Hallucinations
recent_arxiv.summary: Constructs adversarial prompts that are semantically equivalent but elicit hallucinations, revealing model brittleness, NeurIPS 2025.

current_projects: Trustworthy AI (adversarial robustness, LLM hallucination); continual learning theory; THEORINET mathematical foundations of deep learning.
personal_site_summary: Maintained primarily through the JHU vision lab site listing research projects, lab members, and publications spanning geometric vision, deep learning theory, and biomedical applications.
industry_connections: Amazon Scholar; Affiliated Chief Scientist at NORCE (Norwegian Research Centre); NSF-Simons THEORINET co-PI with strong government funding.
commercialization_signal: Amazon Scholar role gives direct industry exposure; NORCE affiliation connects to Norwegian industry. No startup but his neural collapse work is foundational to representation learning products.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: He proves the mathematical laws governing how AI neural networks organize information — think of him as discovering the periodic table for deep learning.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Neural collapse suggests the last layer always converges to an ETF geometry — have you found cases where this breaks down for large language models, and does the geometry differ for decoder-only vs encoder-decoder architectures?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Neural collapse theory underpins representation learning for both self-supervised world models and formal verification of neural networks — directly on the investment thesis.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to AI safety companies building on formal verification of neural networks; connections to representation learning startups leveraging neural collapse insights.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Avoid asking about specific product features or asking him to rank which LLM is best — he is a mathematical theorist.

controversial_take: Neural collapse is not just an interesting phenomenon — it is a fundamental geometric law of deep learning that, once understood, will allow us to design architectures with provable properties rather than empirically tuning them.
open_problem: Extending the neural collapse framework to multi-task and continual learning settings where the ETF geometry must be maintained across evolving task distributions.

name: Dima Damen
title: Professor of Computer Vision (Bristol) / Senior Research Scientist (Google DeepMind)
lab: Machine Learning and Computer Vision Group (MAVI), University of Bristol
research_focus: Dima Damen's research centers on egocentric (first-person) vision and video understanding, developing large-scale datasets and models for recognizing human actions, object interactions, and skills from wearable camera footage. Her work increasingly focuses on building world models from egocentric video — understanding the physical and semantic structure of activities in everyday environments.
direction_of_travel: Dual appointment at Bristol and Google DeepMind creates a unique bridge between academic dataset leadership and industrial AI deployment. Organizing the ICLR 2026 World Models workshop signals expanding role in world model research.
key_contributions: EPIC-KITCHENS: the world's largest egocentric video dataset (100+ hours, 90K actions) — the standard benchmark for egocentric vision
Ego4D and Ego-Exo4D: large-scale multimodal egocentric/exocentric video datasets in collaboration with Meta AI
AMEGO: Active Memory from Long Egocentric Videos (ECCV 2024)
Learning from Streaming Video with Orthogonal Gradients (CVPR 2025)

top_papers.title: Rescaling Egocentric Vision: Collection, Pipeline and Challenges for EPIC-KITCHENS-100
top_papers.one_liner: EPIC-KITCHENS-100: 100 hours, 90K actions, 700 videos — the defining benchmark for egocentric action recognition.

top_papers.title: Scaling Egocentric Vision: The EPIC-KITCHENS Dataset
top_papers.one_liner: Original EPIC-KITCHENS paper — launched egocentric video understanding as a major research field.

top_papers.title: Ego4D: Around the World in 3,000 Hours of Egocentric Video
top_papers.one_liner: World's largest egocentric video dataset covering diverse activities across 74 locations and 9 countries.

top_papers.title: AMEGO: Active Memory from Long Egocentric Videos
top_papers.one_liner: Active memory mechanism for comprehending very long egocentric video sequences, ECCV 2024.

recent_arxiv.title: Learning from Streaming Video with Orthogonal Gradients
recent_arxiv.summary: Continual learning method for video streams using orthogonal gradient updates to prevent forgetting, CVPR 2025.

recent_arxiv.title: An Outlook into the Future of Egocentric Vision
recent_arxiv.summary: Survey mapping current egocentric vision research to the future of wearable AR/VR computing, IJCV 2024.

current_projects: EPIC-KITCHENS ongoing challenges; N-Body problem (parallelizing activities from single-person video); streaming video learning; world models from egocentric data.
personal_site_summary: Detailed academic homepage at Bristol listing research on egocentric vision, datasets (EPIC-KITCHENS, Ego4D), lab members, and her dual role at Google DeepMind.
industry_connections: Senior Research Scientist at Google DeepMind (Andrew Zisserman's Vision Team); EPSRC Fellow; ELLIS Fellow; dataset work used by AR/VR companies including Meta.
commercialization_signal: Strong dual-appointment signals: Google DeepMind connection provides direct path to product. EPIC-KITCHENS is used by AR/VR and smart home companies. Embodied AI and wearable computing are active investment themes.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: She records people going about their daily lives with head-mounted cameras and trains AI to understand every action, object, and skill from that first-person perspective — essential for future AR glasses and home robots.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your EPIC-KITCHENS work covers kitchen activities — are there specific activity patterns you've observed in the data that suggest world models from egocentric video generalize differently than those trained on third-person video?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Egocentric video world models are directly relevant to physical AI (embodied agents, AR/VR, smart homes) and to world model investment thesis — EPIC-KITCHENS is foundational training data.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to AR/VR hardware companies (Meta Reality Labs, Apple Vision Pro team) and smart home AI startups building on egocentric understanding; VC connections in embodied AI.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Avoid asking about autonomous driving — her work is specifically first-person human-activity focused, not vehicle-centric.

controversial_take: World models for embodied AI should be trained primarily on egocentric human video, not third-person robot demonstrations — the first-person perspective contains richer semantic and physical information for interaction.
open_problem: Building world models that can predict not just the visual future but the semantic and causal consequences of actions across very long horizons (hours, not seconds) from egocentric video.

name: John Langford
title: Partner Research Manager
lab: Machine Learning Group, Microsoft Research New York City
research_focus: John Langford works on the theoretical and practical foundations of machine learning, with focus on interactive learning (contextual bandits, online RL), learning reductions, and real-world deployable ML systems. He created Vowpal Wabbit, a production-grade online learning system, and has recently worked on belief state transformers and RL for embodied agents.
direction_of_travel: Longstanding senior researcher at Microsoft; shifted focus toward practical RL systems deployment and LLM/agent architectures in recent years. Involvement in ICLR 2026 World Models workshop suggests growing interest in integrating world models into deployable systems.
key_contributions: Vowpal Wabbit: production-grade online learning and contextual bandit system used at scale by Microsoft
The Belief State Transformer (ICLR 2025) — learning goal achievement under partial observability
Learning reductions framework: meta-algorithms reducing complex ML problems to simpler ones
Contextual bandit algorithms foundational to modern recommender systems

top_papers.title: Contextual Bandits Approach to Personalized News Article Recommendation
top_papers.one_liner: Foundational contextual bandit paper for news recommendation — the basis for modern personalization systems.

top_papers.title: A Reduction from Apprenticeship Learning and Model Based RL to Classification
top_papers.one_liner: Shows that imitation learning and model-based RL can be reduced to standard classification problems.

top_papers.title: The Belief State Transformer
top_papers.one_liner: Transformer architecture that maintains a belief state over goals, enabling efficient planning under uncertainty, ICLR 2025.

recent_arxiv.title: The Belief State Transformer
recent_arxiv.summary: Transformer trained to maintain a belief distribution over possible goals, achieving better generalization in goal-conditioned tasks, ICLR 2025.

current_projects: Belief state transformers; real-world RL deployment; contextual bandits at scale; contributing to Phi-4 reasoning models.
personal_site_summary: Personal blog at hunch.net covering ML theory and practice; Microsoft Research profile lists publications and projects including Vowpal Wabbit and contextual bandits.
industry_connections: Partner Research Manager at Microsoft; Vowpal Wabbit is deployed in production at Microsoft scale; involved in Phi-4 reasoning model development.
commercialization_signal: Very strong: Vowpal Wabbit is a production system at Microsoft. Direct involvement in Phi-4 technical reports signals continued involvement in Microsoft's commercial AI models.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: He built the AI software that Microsoft uses to decide what to show you in personalized feeds — and now he's working on AI that can maintain a mental model of goals to make better decisions.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your Belief State Transformer maintains distributions over possible goals — do you see this as a path toward world models that can reason about agent intentions, or is it more limited to goal-conditioned planning?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Belief states under partial observability are fundamental to world models for physical AI; contextual bandit foundations are relevant to RL-based agent systems.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to applied RL startups building on the contextual bandit and online learning foundations he established; connections to enterprise AI companies.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Avoid asking about Microsoft's OpenAI relationship or Azure product roadmap.

controversial_take: Most modern deep RL research is not actually solving the hard problem — interactive learning with real-world feedback constraints. Vowpal Wabbit from 2007 solves real problems that 2024 deep RL methods still struggle with at production scale.
open_problem: Making RL agents that can reliably improve from real-world feedback without catastrophic failures — the safety and exploration problem under production deployment constraints.

name: Cho-Jui Hsieh
title: Associate Professor
lab: UCLA CS Department; Google Research
research_focus: Cho-Jui Hsieh develops algorithms and optimization techniques for large-scale machine learning, with current focus on LLM efficiency spanning training speed, prediction speed, robustness, and model compression. His work addresses adversarial robustness, efficient fine-tuning, and large-scale recommendation systems, bridging theory and practical deployment.
direction_of_travel: Established associate professor at UCLA with concurrent Google Research scientist role; pivoting from foundational efficient ML toward LLM-specific efficiency challenges including fine-tuning, serving, and reasoning acceleration.
key_contributions: ICLR 2021 Outstanding Paper Award; pioneered neural network verification competition methods
Adversarial Robustness for Machine Learning textbook co-authored with Pin-Yu Chen
Large-scale recommender system algorithms deployed in industry; efficient LLM fine-tuning and inference research

top_papers.title: Adversarial Robustness for Machine Learning (Book)
top_papers.one_liner: Comprehensive textbook on adversarial machine learning; standard reference in the field

top_papers.title: ELLE: Efficient Lifelong Pre-training for Emerging Data
top_papers.one_liner: Efficient continual pretraining approach that avoids catastrophic forgetting with minimal compute overhead

top_papers.title: Efficient Large-Scale Recommendation on the GPU
top_papers.one_liner: GPU-optimized recommendation system algorithms for web-scale deployment

recent_arxiv.title: QuantSpec: Self-Speculative Decoding with Hierarchical Quantized KV Cache
recent_arxiv.summary: Senior collaborator on efficient speculative decoding; overlapping with Rishabh Tiwari's group

current_projects: Speaking at SPOT workshop at ICLR 2026; Google Research concurrent appointment; efficient LLM fine-tuning and inference research; adversarial robustness and verification; UCLA teaching
personal_site_summary: UCLA CS associate professor site covering large-scale ML optimization, adversarial robustness, recommendation systems, and LLM efficiency; concurrent Google Research scientist appointment.
industry_connections: Google Research Scientist (concurrent); Google Research Scholar Award 2021; Okawa Foundation Research Award; Samsung AI Researcher of the Year connection; PhD from UT Austin under Inderjit Dhillon (strong alumni network with Prateek Jain, Devvrit Khatri); Facebook Research Award
commercialization_signal: Moderate-high—efficient LLM fine-tuning and inference research directly applicable to serving infrastructure; adversarial robustness has enterprise security applications; Google concurrent role signals ongoing tech transfer

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Cho-Jui is the efficiency expert who makes machine learning practical at scale—he's worked on making recommender systems run on GPUs, on making neural networks provably robust, and now on making LLM fine-tuning and inference faster.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: With your background in both adversarial robustness and efficiency—do you think RL post-training for reasoning makes models more or less adversarially robust, and is anyone rigorously studying this question?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Relevant to LLM post-training and inference-time scaling: his work on efficient fine-tuning and inference optimization directly informs practical post-training pipelines; adversarial robustness perspective adds safety dimension to the efficiency conversation.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to enterprise ML security startups; perspective on UCLA-Google technology transfer opportunities; co-investment in adversarial robustness or efficient inference companies
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Avoid asking about comparisons between UT Austin and UCLA research culture—he moved away from UT Austin and prefers forward-looking conversations

controversial_take: Neural network verification is currently theatrical—the gap between what we can verify and what we deploy in production is so large that most robustness certification provides false assurance rather than real safety guarantees.
open_problem: Can we design LLM fine-tuning methods that simultaneously improve reasoning capability, maintain adversarial robustness, and reduce serving cost—or are these three objectives fundamentally in tension?

name: Arman Cohan
affiliation: Yale University / Allen Institute for AI (AI2)
title: Assistant Professor (Yale) / Faculty Research Scientist (AI2)
lab: Yale NLP Lab
research_focus: Natural language processing with emphasis on large language models, scientific document understanding, long-document processing, and retrieval-augmented systems. Develops evaluation and interpretability methods for LLMs and reasoning systems for specialized domains including science and biomedicine.
direction_of_travel: Georgetown PhD (2018) → AI2 Research Scientist → Yale Assistant Professor (2023) + AI2 Faculty Research Scientist. Strong dual-track academic-industry positioning. 25,000+ citations show foundational impact (Longformer, SciBERT, SPECTER). Growing focus on LLM evaluation and reasoning.
key_contributions: Longformer: attention mechanism scaling linearly with sequence length for long documents (AI2, 2020, widely adopted)
SciBERT: pretrained BERT model for scientific text (EMNLP 2020, 20,000+ downloads/month in 2020)
SPECTER: citation-informed document embeddings for scientific papers
A Discourse-Aware Attention Model for Abstractive Summarization of Long Documents (NAACL 2018)
Flex: unified evaluation framework for few-shot NLP (NeurIPS 2021)

top_papers.title: Longformer: The Long-Document Transformer
top_papers.one_liner: Replaces quadratic self-attention with linear sliding window + task-motivated global attention, enabling efficient processing of documents up to 4096 tokens.

top_papers.title: SciBERT: A Pretrained Language Model for Scientific Text
top_papers.one_liner: BERT trained on 1.14M scientific papers from Semantic Scholar, achieving SOTA on multiple scientific NLP benchmarks.

top_papers.title: SPECTER: Document-level Representation Learning using Citation-informed Transformers
top_papers.one_liner: Scientific paper embeddings using citation graph as training signal, enabling strong paper recommendation and classification.

recent_arxiv.title: Yale NLP Recent Work on LLM Evaluation and Reasoning
recent_arxiv.summary: Lab research on evaluating and interpreting LLMs, reasoning for specialized domains, and retrieval-augmented generation systems.

current_projects: Leading Yale NLP Lab; dual role as AI2 Faculty Research Scientist; research on LLM evaluation, reasoning, and scientific AI; speaker at RSI ICLR 2026 on LLM self-improvement capabilities.
personal_site_summary: Assistant Professor at Yale CS. Faculty Research Scientist at AI2. 25,000+ citations. Creator of Longformer, SciBERT, SPECTER. Yale NLP Lab focused on LLMs, evaluation, and scientific AI. Georgetown PhD 2018.
industry_connections: Active at Allen Institute for AI (AI2)—major nonprofit AI research lab with strong industry relationships. Longformer/SciBERT are embedded in commercial NLP stacks globally.
commercialization_signal: SciBERT and SPECTER are deployed in Semantic Scholar (AI2 product). Longformer underpins enterprise long-document processing pipelines. Yale + AI2 dual role positions him as a bridge between academic research and applied AI products.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Yale professor and AI2 researcher who built two foundational NLP tools—Longformer (for reading long documents) and SciBERT (for understanding science papers)—that are now embedded in commercial products worldwide. He's speaking about whether LLMs can improve themselves.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Given your experience building Longformer and SPECTER, do you see the scientific literature itself as a self-improvement signal for LLMs—i.e., can models trained to navigate citation graphs bootstrap better scientific reasoning capabilities?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: RSI connection: LLMs that understand scientific literature are prerequisites for AI systems that can autonomously improve via reading and reasoning about prior work—the knowledge accumulation component of RSI.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Access to AI2's commercial deployment experience; connections to enterprise scientific AI buyers (pharma, biotech, legal) building on Longformer/SciBERT foundations.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Do not ask about citation intent classification—it's a niche sub-problem from his early career.

controversial_take: Scientific AI assistants built on LLMs will hit a ceiling because LLMs cannot reason about temporal knowledge evolution—a paper from 2018 and 2025 on the same topic require fundamentally different epistemic treatment that static embeddings cannot capture.
open_problem: How do you build retrieval systems that reason about the temporal dynamics of scientific knowledge—knowing not just what papers say but whether that knowledge is still valid, superseded, or contested.

name: Xiaoxiao Li
title: Associate Professor
lab: Trusted and Efficient AI (TEA) Group, UBC ECE
research_focus: Trustworthy and efficient AI at the intersection of federated learning, medical imaging, and foundation models. Research spans privacy-preserving distributed learning (FedBN), AI for healthcare, textual gradient federated learning, and building responsible AI systems that are both accurate and privacy-preserving.
direction_of_travel: Yale PhD (James Duncan) → Princeton postdoc (Kai Li, Olga Troyanskaya) → UBC Associate Professor. Canada Research Chair (Tier II) in Responsible AI + Canada CIFAR AI Chair. Yale adjunct appointment. Growing toward agentic AI systems and foundation model trustworthiness. Test-time updates workshop involvement reflects expansion from federated learning to deployment-time adaptation.
key_contributions: FedBN: federated learning with local batch normalization for non-IID feature shift (ICLR 2021, 8000+ citations)
BrainGNN: interpretable graph neural networks for fMRI brain network analysis (Medical Image Analysis 2021)
FL-NTK: neural tangent kernel framework for federated learning convergence analysis (ICML 2021)
FedTextGrad: textual gradient optimization in federated learning (ICLR 2025)
FairMedFM: fairness benchmark for medical imaging foundation models

top_papers.title: FedBN: Federated Learning on Non-IID Features via Local Batch Normalization
top_papers.one_liner: Excludes BN layer parameters from federated averaging to handle feature distribution shift across clients, with convergence guarantees.

top_papers.title: BrainGNN: Interpretable Brain Graph Neural Network for fMRI Analysis
top_papers.one_liner: Graph neural network for fMRI brain connectivity analysis providing interpretable ROI-level explanations.

top_papers.title: FedTextGrad: Can Textual Gradient Work in Federated Learning?
top_papers.one_liner: First exploration of LLM-based textual gradients (TextGrad) in federated settings, enabling prompt optimization without gradient sharing.

recent_arxiv.title: FedTextGrad: Can Textual Gradient Work in Federated Learning?
recent_arxiv.summary: ICLR 2025: proposes FedTextGrad combining LLM textual gradient optimization with federated learning for privacy-preserving prompt optimization.

recent_arxiv.title: GMValuator: Similarity-based Data Valuation for Generative Models
recent_arxiv.summary: ICLR 2025: data valuation framework for generative models enabling identification of high-value training data.

current_projects: TEA Group at UBC; Canada Research Chair in Responsible AI; Canada CIFAR AI Chair; Vector Institute faculty; adjunct at Yale Medical School; co-organizer TTU ICLR 2026; industry collaborations with NVIDIA, Google, Meta, Microsoft.
personal_site_summary: UBC Associate Professor, ECE. Canada CIFAR AI Chair + Canada Research Chair (Tier II) in Responsible AI. Vector Institute faculty. Yale Medical School adjunct. TEA Group (Trusted and Efficient AI). Industry partners: NVIDIA, Google, Meta, Microsoft.
industry_connections: Active research collaborations with NVIDIA, Google, Meta, and Microsoft. Vector Institute membership provides access to Canadian AI industry ecosystem. Yale Medical School affiliation opens healthcare AI commercialization paths.
commercialization_signal: FedBN addresses a core need in healthcare AI (multi-hospital federated learning without data sharing)—directly commercializable in clinical AI. Industry partners at NVIDIA/Google/Meta suggest active technology transfer. FedTextGrad could enable privacy-preserving enterprise LLM fine-tuning.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: UBC professor who figured out how to train AI models across multiple hospitals without anyone sharing their patient data—her FedBN paper is cited 1400 times and is the standard for federated learning with distribution shift. Now expanding into test-time adaptation and foundation model trustworthiness.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: FedTextGrad uses LLM textual gradients in federated learning—does this open a new privacy attack surface where the textual gradient itself leaks information about the client's private data, and how do you bound that leakage?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Test-time updates + federated learning intersection: how do you adapt a federated model at test time without centralizing data? This is the key open problem connecting her FL work to the TTU workshop.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to healthcare AI investors and hospital system buyers for federated learning products; connections to enterprise data privacy teams at NVIDIA/Google who are already her partners.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Don't ask about neuroscience or fMRI analysis—that's the medical application side of her work, not her methodological focus.

controversial_take: Federated learning is oversold as a privacy solution—without formal differential privacy guarantees, the gradient sharing in federated averaging still leaks substantial private information. FedBN is a step, not a solution.
open_problem: How do you perform test-time adaptation in a federated setting where each client's distribution shift may be different, and centralizing test data for adaptation is prohibited?

name: Virginia Smith
title: Leonardo Associate Professor of Machine Learning
lab: CMU Machine Learning Department
research_focus: Safety and efficiency in machine learning systems, with emphasis on federated and collaborative learning, efficient training and inference, data privacy, and AI safety. Foundational work on federated multi-task learning (MOCHA) and heterogeneous federated optimization (FedProx). Recent work on LLM fine-tuning efficiency and federated foundation models.
direction_of_travel: Virginia undergrad → UC Berkeley PhD → CMU faculty → Leonardo Associate Professor. Sloan Fellow (2024), Samsung AI Researcher of the Year (2023), NSF CAREER, MIT TR35 Innovators Under 35, AFOSR Young Investigator (2025). One of the most decorated early-career ML systems researchers. Currently expanding federated learning to foundation model era.
key_contributions: MOCHA: federated multi-task learning with systems-aware optimization handling stragglers and communication cost (NeurIPS 2017)
FedProx: proximal term framework for heterogeneous federated networks with convergence guarantees
Ditto: personalized federated learning via local model fine-tuning with fairness guarantees
FLASC: sparse LoRA communication in federated fine-tuning with 10x communication reduction
Secure and Efficient Federated Learning in the Foundation Model Era (AFOSR-funded, 2025)

top_papers.title: Federated Multi-Task Learning (MOCHA)
top_papers.one_liner: Proposes multi-task learning as the right statistical framework for federated settings, with systems-aware optimization handling fault tolerance.

top_papers.title: Federated Optimization for Heterogeneous Networks (FedProx)
top_papers.one_liner: Adds proximal regularization to local client objectives, enabling convergence in heterogeneous federated networks where FedAvg diverges.

top_papers.title: Ditto: Fair and Robust Federated Learning Through Personalization
top_papers.one_liner: Personalized FL via local model fine-tuning objective that achieves both accuracy and fairness guarantees.

recent_arxiv.title: Secure and Efficient Federated Learning in the Foundation Model Era
recent_arxiv.summary: AFOSR-funded research developing FL methods for communication-limited and adversarial environments adapted for foundation models.

recent_arxiv.title: FLASC: Federated Learning with Adaptive Sparse Communication
recent_arxiv.summary: Applies sparsity to LoRA during communication while allowing full local fine-tuning, matching dense LoRA with 10x less communication.

current_projects: CMU Leonardo Professor; AFOSR Young Investigator (2025) on secure federated learning; Sloan Fellow; co-organizer Simons Institute semester on federated/collaborative learning; speaker at TTU ICLR 2026; working on federated foundation model fine-tuning.
personal_site_summary: Leonardo Associate Professor, CMU ML Department. Berkeley PhD. Sloan Fellow 2024. Samsung AI Researcher of the Year 2023. NSF CAREER. MIT TR35 Innovators Under 35. AFOSR Young Investigator 2025. 32,000+ citations. Expert in federated learning, privacy-preserving ML, AI safety.
industry_connections: Intel Rising Star (2022), Samsung AI Researcher of the Year (2023)—both imply active industry relationships. AFOSR funding connects to defense applications. CMU ML Department has strong industry ties across all major tech companies.
commercialization_signal: FedProx is used in production by Google's federated learning infrastructure (Gboard). MOCHA and Ditto are deployed in enterprise healthcare and financial federated learning. FLASC's communication efficiency directly addresses cost barriers in large-scale federated LLM fine-tuning.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: CMU professor who is the leading academic voice on federated learning—how to train AI models across distributed devices without centralizing private data. Her FedProx paper is cited 4500 times and is deployed in Google's keyboard AI. Now she's extending this to make foundation model fine-tuning federated.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: FedProx's proximal term handles statistical heterogeneity but adds a hyperparameter that's hard to tune in practice—in your FLASC and foundation model work, do you see a principled approach to automatically setting the proximal strength based on client drift?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: TTU workshop connection: federated learning at test time—adapting a shared global model to local client distributions at deployment without communication is the intersection of TTA and federated learning.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: VC perspective on federated learning infrastructure startups; connections to enterprise buyers in healthcare, finance, and defense who need privacy-preserving ML at scale.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Don't focus only on federated learning's medical applications—she's explicitly expanding to defense (AFOSR) and LLM fine-tuning (FLASC), and wants her work seen as general ML systems research.

controversial_take: The federated learning field has been too focused on convergence proofs and not enough on real deployment—most FL algorithms that look good in theory fail catastrophically on heterogeneous real-world data without extensive hyperparameter tuning.
open_problem: How do you perform test-time adaptation in a federated system where each device has only a handful of test samples, communication is expensive, and privacy constraints prevent sharing those test samples for adaptation?

name: Yingzhen Li
title: Associate Professor in Machine Learning
lab: Statistical Machine Learning Group, Department of Computing, Imperial College London
research_focus: Li works on probabilistic machine learning with emphasis on scalable approximate inference, deep generative models, and reliable/robust ML systems. She connects Bayesian theory with modern diffusion and flow-based models, producing algorithms that are both principled and practically scalable. Recent work includes improving covariance estimation in diffusion models, discrete diffusion samplers, and flow matching for MRI reconstruction.
direction_of_travel: PhD at Cambridge with Turner, then Microsoft Research Cambridge (senior researcher), then Imperial College. Steady upward trajectory in probabilistic ML. Now bridging classical Bayesian methods with diffusion models — a timely intersection. Turing Fellowship (2024) marks growing national recognition. General Chair for AISTATS 2025 and 2026 indicates leadership ambitions.
key_contributions: Improving Probabilistic Diffusion Models with Optimal Diagonal Covariance Matching (ICLR 2025 Oral, top 1.5%)
Discrete Neural Flow Samplers (DNFS) — trainable CTMC-based discrete samplers
Extensive work on approximate inference implemented in TensorFlow Probability and Pyro

top_papers.title: Improving Probabilistic Diffusion Models With Optimal Diagonal Covariance Matching

current_projects: Co-organizing ReALM-GEN at ICLR 2026; General Chair for AISTATS 2025 and 2026; research on inference-time alignment of diffusion models, discrete diffusion samplers, and causal discovery in time series.
personal_site_summary: Well-maintained academic site with full publication list, CV, and guidance for prospective students. Highlights probabilistic ML research spanning approximate inference, generative models, and Bayesian deep learning.
industry_connections: Previously at Microsoft Research Cambridge (2.5 years as senior researcher); work implemented in TensorFlow Probability and Pyro. Turing Institute Fellowship provides government/policy connections.
commercialization_signal: Moderate. Probabilistic ML and robust AI have clear enterprise applications (medical imaging, finance, safety). Her SMC alignment work has direct relevance to production inference pipelines.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Yingzhen is one of the UK's leading probabilistic ML researchers. She's the person bringing rigorous Bayesian thinking to diffusion models—making them not just generative but reliably calibrated.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your SMC alignment paper treats discrete diffusion as a controlled CTMC — is there a tractable path to doing this for continuous-state models without the exponential blowup in resampling?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Strong — probabilistic foundations for diffusion and flow models, inference-time alignment, generative models for science (MRI). Directly on thesis.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to healthcare AI companies using probabilistic imaging models; connections to UK deep-tech investors interested in AI reliability and calibration.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Avoid asking about the 'AI hype' framing of her work — she's firmly on the rigorous/foundational side and is skeptical of premature deployment narratives.

controversial_take: Most diffusion model practitioners are leaving significant quality on the table by treating covariance as a scalar — diagonal covariance matching is just the beginning of what full probabilistic treatment can deliver.
open_problem: Principled and scalable posterior inference for the latent variables of large diffusion/flow models — current variational methods are too loose and MCMC too slow.

name: Stefanie Jegelka
title: Humboldt Professor (TU Munich); Visiting Professor (MIT EECS)
lab: Munich Center for Machine Learning (MCML), TU Munich
research_focus: Jegelka works on the theoretical foundations of machine learning with combinatorial, algebraic, and geometric structure — most prominently graph neural networks (GNNs). She has made fundamental contributions to GNN expressivity, generalization, and approximation theory, and studies learning with symmetries and invariances. Her work bridges the theory of optimization, submodularity, and graph algorithms with modern deep learning.
direction_of_travel: PhD at ETH Zurich/MPI, UC Berkeley postdoc, MIT faculty, then Humboldt Professorship at TU Munich (2024) — among the most prestigious AI appointments in Germany (€5M endowment). 32,000+ citations. Sloan Fellow, DARPA Young Faculty Award, NSF CAREER. Invited sectional lecture at ICM 2022. ICML 2022 Program Chair. One of the most decorated ML theorists of her generation.
key_contributions: Foundational GNN expressivity and generalization theory — explaining when and why GNNs work
Learning with Exact Invariances in Polynomial Time (ICML 2025 Spotlight)
G1: Teaching LLMs to Reason on Graphs (NeurIPS 2025, Best Workshop Paper)

top_papers.title: Theory of Graph Neural Networks: Representation and Learning (ICM 2022 Sectional)
top_papers.one_liner: Comprehensive survey of GNN theory connecting expressivity, Weisfeiler-Leman tests, and generalization.

top_papers.title: Learning with Exact Invariances in Polynomial Time
top_papers.one_liner: Polynomial-time algorithm for learning with exact symmetry invariances; ICML 2025 Spotlight.

top_papers.title: Invariant Graphon Networks: Approximation and Cut Distance
top_papers.one_liner: Graphon-based GNN theory with approximation guarantees; ICLR 2025 Spotlight.

recent_arxiv.title: A projection-based framework for gradient-free and parallel learning
recent_arxiv.summary: Gradient-free parallel learning framework; AISTATS 2026.

current_projects: GRaM Workshop at ICLR 2026; GNN theory for symmetry and geometry; LLM reasoning on graphs; MCML research group at TU Munich; Humboldt Professorship research agenda on foundations of deep neural networks.
personal_site_summary: MIT CSAIL personal site (being transitioned to TU Munich). Lists publications by year. Note: page is not actively maintained according to her own note.
industry_connections: Google Faculty Research Awards (2016, 2021). Two Sigma Faculty Research Award. Program Chair ICML 2022. MCML Munich affiliation connects to BMW, Siemens, and Munich's industrial AI ecosystem.
commercialization_signal: Moderate. Foundational GNN theory work underpins graph ML in drug discovery, materials, social networks, and supply chains. Increasingly applied work (LLMs + graphs) has direct enterprise relevance.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Stefanie is one of the world's top theorists on graph neural networks and geometric ML. She just moved to TU Munich with a €5M German government award. She explains mathematically when and why GNNs work.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your invariant graphon networks paper uses cut-distance topology — does the approximation theory extend to dynamic graphs or temporal GNNs where the graphon changes over time?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Strong — geometric deep learning foundations, symmetry/invariance in neural networks. The GNN theory work underpins physical AI applications. Not core to diffusion/flow matching directly.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Connections to German industrial AI partners (BMW AI, Siemens AI Lab); introductions to US and European academic-to-industry bridge programs; Munich tech ecosystem connections.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Don't ask about the logistics of moving from MIT to Munich — dual appointments and transatlantic academic moves are complicated and can be a sensitive topic.

controversial_take: GNN expressivity debates focused on the Weisfeiler-Leman hierarchy are missing the point — practical GNN limitations come from optimization landscape and generalization, not expressive power, and the field has been solving the wrong problem for years.
open_problem: Efficient learning with exact symmetry invariances at scale — current polynomial-time algorithms work for small symmetry groups but break down for the continuous groups (SE(3), E(3)) relevant to molecular and physical systems.

name: Jakub Tomczak
title: Senior Staff Research Scientist, Generative AI x Science
lab: Chan Zuckerberg Initiative (CZI), San Francisco
research_focus: Tomczak is a generative AI leader with 15+ years of experience across VAEs, GANs, flow-based models, score-based models, diffusion models, autoregressive models, and LLMs. At CZI, he applies generative AI to biological and life sciences problems, including single-cell gene expression modeling and biological reasoning LLMs. He authored the first comprehensive textbook on deep generative modeling (Springer, 2022; 2nd edition 2024).
direction_of_travel: Polish PhD, Marie Curie fellowship at UvA (Max Welling), Qualcomm AI Research, VU Amsterdam assistant professor, TU/e associate professor (head of Generative AI group), then Senior Staff at CZI (2024). Founder of Amsterdam AI Solutions. NeurIPS 2024 Program Chair. CZI role marks the definitive shift from academic to industry-at-the-frontier, applying generative AI to Zuckerberg's bet on biology.
key_contributions: 'Deep Generative Modeling' textbook (Springer 2022/2024) — the field's primary graduate reference
Single-cell gene expression generation via latent diffusion models (CZI 2025)
NeurIPS 2024 Program Chair — community service at the highest level

top_papers.title: Deep Generative Modeling (Textbook)
top_papers.one_liner: First comprehensive textbook covering all major generative model classes; Springer 2022.

current_projects: Generative AI for single-cell biology at CZI; biological reasoning LLMs (Rbio); GRaM Workshop at ICLR 2026; continued book revisions for 3rd edition.
personal_site_summary: Well-maintained academic site listing all papers, textbook, and courses. Now based in San Francisco at CZI. Strong community engagement (NeurIPS program chair, textbook author, workshop organizer).
industry_connections: Senior Staff at Chan Zuckerberg Initiative (Zuckerberg-funded $3B life sciences bet). Prior: Qualcomm AI Research. Founder of Amsterdam AI Solutions. 2 patents. CZI connections to major pharma and biotech through CZI Science partnerships.
commercialization_signal: Very high. CZI role is explicitly at the generative AI x biology frontier. Single-cell generation and biological reasoning LLMs are pre-competitive infrastructure for drug discovery. CZI's endowment and network create direct pharma/biotech commercialization paths.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Jakub wrote the textbook that everyone uses to learn generative AI. He's now at the Chan Zuckerberg Initiative applying diffusion models and biological LLMs to single-cell genomics — part of Zuckerberg's bet on curing all diseases by 2100.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: For single-cell generation, the gene expression space is extremely high-dimensional and sparse — how do you handle the zero-inflation problem in the latent diffusion model's decoder?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Strong — generative models for biology (science angle), diffusion models for structured data. CZI context adds unusual institutional angle to the investment conversation.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Connections to biotech VCs (a16z Bio, GV, ARCH Venture Partners); CZI ecosystem introductions; pharmaceutical partnership discussions.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Avoid asking about academic vs. industry career choice — his transition to CZI is deliberate and he's clearly committed to the mission-driven science model.

controversial_take: Single-cell foundation models are being built the wrong way — training on aggregated atlas data ignores patient-level batch effects that make the models useless for clinical applications. We need to solve the batch correction problem inside the generative model, not post-hoc.
open_problem: Generative models that can simultaneously model gene expression, epigenetics, and spatial organization from the same cell — current models collapse to single modalities and lose the cross-modal dependencies that drive biology.

name: Natalia Berloff
title: Professor of Applied Mathematics
lab: Quantum Fluids Laboratory; π-Computing Group, DAMTP
research_focus: Physics-inspired analog computing using quantum and nonlinear optical systems (polaritons, Bose-Einstein condensates) to solve NP-hard combinatorial optimization problems. Maps Ising and XY Hamiltonians onto physical systems to exploit natural energy minimization. Connecting to associative memory via energy landscape optimization.
direction_of_travel: From pure applied mathematics (quantum fluids, superfluidity) into physics-inspired computing and quantum simulators. Leading EU Pathfinder HEISINGBERG project to build a quantum spatial annealer. PI of π-Computing group—moving toward practical quantum computing hardware.
key_contributions: Founded π-Computing research group at Cambridge
Pioneered polariton-based Ising machine for solving NP-hard optimization
Led EU Pathfinder HEISINGBERG project on Spatial Quantum Optical Annealer (up to 10,000 spins)
Cambridge Pilkington Prize 2005 for teaching excellence
5,600+ Google Scholar citations across quantum fluids and physics-inspired computing

top_papers.title: Realizing the classical XY Hamiltonian in polariton simulators
top_papers.one_liner: Demonstrates polariton condensates as hardware for XY model simulation—NP-hard in general.

top_papers.title: Exciton-Polariton Condensates
top_papers.one_liner: Review of polariton condensate physics underpinning her quantum computing approach.

current_projects: HEISINGBERG EU Pathfinder Quantum Annealer; polariton-based optimization machines; connecting Ising model solvers to associative memory energy minimization.
personal_site_summary: Cambridge DAMTP page listing research on quantum fluids, polariton condensates, and analog computing for optimization. Lists π-Computing group and EU Pathfinder grant.
industry_connections: π-Computing (potential spinout trajectory); EU Pathfinder industry partnerships; Alan Turing Institute affiliate.
commercialization_signal: High—π-Computing has clear startup potential; EU Pathfinder explicitly aims at commercial quantum computing; solving NP-hard problems is directly monetizable.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: A Cambridge physicist using lasers and quantum condensates to build analog computers that solve problems regular computers can't. Her machines physically minimize the same energy function that Hopfield networks use—making her a unique bridge between quantum hardware and AI memory.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your polariton Ising machine minimizes XY Hamiltonians physically—is the energy landscape it explores qualitatively similar to the energy surface of a modern dense Hopfield network, or are there fundamental differences that limit the analogy?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Quantum hardware realization of associative memory energy minimization. If Hopfield networks are the algorithm, Berloff's polariton machines could be the hardware.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to deep tech investors (e.g., Amadeus Capital in Cambridge, IQT) interested in quantum computing hardware for optimization—π-Computing looks investable.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Comparing polariton computing to gate-based quantum computers (e.g., IBM, Google)—she'll point out they solve different problems and the comparison is often unfair.

controversial_take: Believes classical Ising machines using polaritons or lasers will outperform gate-based quantum computers for practical optimization problems within 5 years—and that the 'quantum advantage' narrative is overhyped for ML applications.
open_problem: Scaling polariton annealer from 10,000 to 1 million spins while maintaining quantum coherence—the engineering challenge for practical NP-hard problem solving.

name: Laurence Perreault-Levasseur
title: Associate Professor; Canada Research Chair in Computational Cosmology and AI
lab: Ciela Institute; Mila – Quebec AI Institute
research_focus: Machine learning methods for cosmological data analysis, especially strong gravitational lensing. Develops simulation-based inference, score-based diffusion priors, and neural posterior estimators for measuring cosmological parameters (Hubble constant, dark matter substructure) from next-generation surveys (LSST, Euclid).
direction_of_travel: Cambridge PhD → Flatiron/Stanford postdoc → UMontreal/Mila associate professor. Canada Research Chair signals long-term academic trajectory. $4M Schmidt Futures grant for gravitational lensing shows strong external funding. Increasingly focused on deployment-ready inference tools for LSST/Euclid.
key_contributions: $4M Schmidt Futures Foundation grant for ML-based Hubble constant measurement via gravitational lensing

Score-based diffusion models as priors for joint source-and-lens inference

Visiting scholar at Flatiron Institute and Perimeter Institute; Mila associate member

current_projects: Hubble constant measurement via lensing (Schmidt-funded); LSST/Euclid inference pipeline with 'caustics' package; distribution shift robustness in neural posterior estimators.
personal_site_summary: Ciela Institute page describing Canada Research Chair, Mila affiliation, Schmidt Futures grant, and research on ML for gravitational lensing and cosmological inference.
industry_connections: Schmidt Futures (Eric Schmidt's philanthropic arm); Mila Quebec AI Institute (strong industry partnerships); Flatiron Institute (Simons Foundation).
commercialization_signal: Medium—research is scientific rather than directly commercial, but gravitational lensing techniques for inference could transfer to medical imaging or satellite data analytics.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: A cosmologist using diffusion AI models to measure the expansion of the universe—with $4M from Eric Schmidt to do it. Her ML tools for gravitational lensing are becoming the standard for the next generation of telescopes.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your score-based prior approach resolves the source-lens degeneracy for single lenses—are you thinking about how to extend this to population-level inference across thousands of Euclid lenses simultaneously?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: FM4Science exemplar: foundation models for astrophysics that encode physical priors (gravitational optics) via score-based models—not just pattern matching but physics-respecting inference.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to climate risk/remote sensing AI companies that could adapt her lensing reconstruction techniques for satellite imagery.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Hubble tension politics—the field is divided on whether the tension is real or systematic error, and it's a sensitive ongoing controversy.

controversial_take: Believes the Hubble tension is real and will require new physics to resolve—not systematic error—and that ML-based lensing measurements will be the clinching evidence.
open_problem: Building simulation-based inference that is provably robust to prior misspecification when analyzing real LSST/Euclid data with poorly-known foreground contamination.

name: Bo Li
title: Abbasi Associate Professor
lab: Secure Learning Lab (SL2); Co-Founder, Virtue AI
research_focus: Trustworthy machine learning: robustness against adversarial attacks, privacy (differential privacy, federated learning), fairness, and their mathematical interconnections. Increasingly focused on trustworthiness of LLMs and agentic AI systems. Co-founded Virtue AI to commercialize AI safety.
direction_of_travel: Vanderbilt PhD → UC Berkeley postdoc → UIUC faculty. Clear commercialization arc: co-founded Virtue AI in April 2024, raised $30M by April 2025 (with Dawn Song, Carlos Guestrin, Sanmi Koyejo). IJCAI Computers and Thought Award; Sloan Fellow. Becoming a significant AI safety entrepreneur while maintaining academic role.
key_contributions: IJCAI Computers and Thought Award; Alfred P. Sloan Research Fellowship
55,000+ Google Scholar citations on adversarial ML, security, privacy
Co-founded Virtue AI (April 2024): $30M Seed+Series A by April 2025
Physical-world adversarial attacks research collected by Science Museum London
DecodingTrust: comprehensive trustworthiness evaluation framework for GPT models

recent_arxiv.summary: NeurIPS 2023 Outstanding Paper evaluating GPT-4 across toxicity, adversarial robustness, privacy, fairness, and ethics dimensions.

current_projects: Virtue AI (VirtueRed, VirtueGuard, VirtueAgent products); agentic AI security (prompt injection, tool-chain manipulation); trustworthy federated learning; FM4Science workshop co-organization.
personal_site_summary: SL2 lab site covering adversarial ML, trustworthy federated learning, and LLM security research. Links to Virtue AI.
industry_connections: Virtue AI co-founder ($30M raised); Dawn Song (Oasis founder) co-founder relationship; Carlos Guestrin (Apple acquisition of Turi) network; Information Trust Institute at UIUC.
commercialization_signal: Very high—active founder of funded AI safety company with three commercial products. One of the few academics who has directly commercialized this research.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: The person who showed stop signs could be hacked to fool self-driving cars—and then turned that into a $30M AI safety company with Dawn Song. Now she's applying the same trustworthiness frameworks to foundation models for science.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: For scientific foundation models, are you seeing qualitatively different attack surfaces than general-purpose LLMs—e.g., prompt injections that induce physically implausible predictions that look valid to a non-expert?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: FM4Science trustworthiness dimension: scientific AI models need reliability guarantees beyond accuracy—Virtue AI's framework is becoming the standard for this.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Series B investor introductions—Virtue AI raised $30M and will need follow-on growth capital within 18 months.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Specific Virtue AI client names or pricing—standard startup confidentiality.

controversial_take: Argues that 'AI safety' as practiced by major labs (RLHF, constitutional AI) is fundamentally insufficient for agentic systems—it addresses outputs but ignores the attack surface created by tool use, memory, and multi-agent communication.
open_problem: Proving formal security guarantees for agentic AI systems operating in open environments—the combination of tool use and memory creates attack surfaces that current formal methods cannot handle.

name: Swarat Chaudhuri
title: Associate Professor
lab: Trishul Laboratory, Department of Computer Science, UT Austin
research_focus: Neurosymbolic programming: combining deep learning with program synthesis and formal methods to build AI systems that are reliable, transparent, and can solve reasoning-intensive tasks. Applications in automated mathematical reasoning, scientific code synthesis, and verified AI.
direction_of_travel: From pure formal methods/program verification into neurosymbolic AI as the synthesis field expanded. NSF Expeditions grant (with Solar-Lezama/MIT team) indicates field-defining stature. The FM4Science connection reflects interest in applying program synthesis to scientific AI reasoning.
key_contributions: NSF Expeditions in Computing grant for neurosymbolic program synthesis (with Solar-Lezama, MIT)
Co-authored foundational Foundations and Trends survey 'Neurosymbolic Programming' (2021)
Director of Trishul Laboratory at UT Austin
Pioneered application of neurosymbolic techniques to automated mathematical reasoning

top_papers.title: Neurosymbolic Programming
top_papers.one_liner: Foundations and Trends survey defining neurosymbolic programming as a field—the canonical reference.

top_papers.one_liner: Updated handbook chapter on neurosymbolic synthesis covering LLM-era advances.

current_projects: Neurosymbolic approaches to scientific reasoning; automating mathematical proof synthesis; Trishul lab research on verified AI; FM4Science workshop co-organization.
personal_site_summary: UT Austin CS page describing Trishul Lab research on formal methods meets ML; lists NSF Expeditions grant; neurosymbolic programming as core agenda.
industry_connections: NSF Expeditions consortium (UT Austin, MIT, others); UT Austin CS has strong industry recruiting; Google, Microsoft research collaborations in PL/FM space.
commercialization_signal: Medium—neurosymbolic tools have clear applications in code generation (GitHub Copilot space) and scientific computing verification, but no direct startup activity found.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: The person who invented neurosymbolic programming as a field—where neural networks and logical programs are combined. Now applying this to make AI reason reliably about science, not just pattern-match.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: For scientific AI, you'd want models that can prove their predictions satisfy physical constraints—are you working on lightweight formal verification methods that could be embedded in a foundation model inference pipeline?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: FM4Science's reliability challenge: scientific AI needs to be verified, not just accurate. Chaudhuri's neurosymbolic approach is the most mature framework for building such guarantees.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to investors in AI reasoning/code generation space (e.g., those backing Lean/proof assistants, or scientific computing tools).
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Pure LLM-based code generation as a competitor to program synthesis—he has strong views and can be dismissive of approaches that lack formal guarantees.

controversial_take: Believes LLM-based code generation (Copilot, Claude) is fundamentally unsuitable for safety-critical scientific computing because it cannot provide formal guarantees, and that neurosymbolic program synthesis is the only viable path to verified AI-generated code.
open_problem: Scaling neurosymbolic synthesis to handle the complexity of real scientific codebases (100K+ lines of Fortran climate models) while maintaining formal correctness guarantees.

name: Mahdi Soltanolkotabi
title: Professor; Inaugural Director, USC Center on AI Foundations for Science (AIF4S)
lab: USC Center on AI Foundations for Science (AIF4S); Departments of ECE, CS, and ISE
research_focus: Mathematical foundations of modern AI, from generative AI to deep learning and signal processing. Develops rigorous theory for non-convex optimization, compressed sensing, attention mechanisms, transfer learning, and untrained neural networks. Applies this to science, healthcare, and medicine in collaboration with domain scientists.
direction_of_travel: Stanford PhD (Candès) → UC Berkeley postdoc (Recht/Wainwright) → USC faculty. Rapid rise: Packard Fellow, Sloan Fellow, NSF CAREER, AFOSR YIP. Now founding and directing USC's new AIF4S center—a major institutional investment in AI for science.
key_contributions: Packard Fellowship in Science and Engineering 2018 (one of 18 nationally)
Sloan Research Fellowship; NSF CAREER Award; AFOSR Young Investigator Award
Information Theory Society Best Paper Award
Founded and directs USC Center on AI Foundations for Science (AIF4S)
Theoretical foundations of attention, prompt-tuning, and transfer learning (ICML 2023)

current_projects: AIF4S center launch and research agenda; theoretical foundations of generative AI for scientific discovery; collaboration with domain scientists on AI-accelerated drug design and materials.
personal_site_summary: USC Viterbi page describing mathematical foundations research agenda, AIF4S center directorship, and research collaborations with physicians and domain scientists.
industry_connections: USC-Meta Center associate; AIF4S center with industry partnership potential; USC Viterbi strong industry recruiting; Candès (Stanford) alumni network.
commercialization_signal: Medium-high—AIF4S center explicitly targets applications in healthcare and medicine; foundational work on compressed sensing is deployed in MRI machines (GE, Siemens); new center will attract industry partners.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: A Packard Fellow who proved why certain AI training tricks actually work mathematically, and now runs USC's new center for 'AI foundations for science'—the theory behind making AI useful for physics and medicine. One of the few people who can bridge rigorous math and practical AI for science.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your attention theory work shows prompt-tuning works through a specific mechanism—does the same attention geometry explain why in-context learning in scientific domains is harder than language, or do scientific distributions break those assumptions?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: FM4Science needs rigorous theoretical foundations to know which approaches will generalize—Soltanolkotabi provides exactly that, and AIF4S is institutionalizing the connection.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to pharmaceutical company R&D labs or medical imaging companies (GE Healthcare, Siemens Healthineers) that AIF4S might partner with.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Specific partnerships or funding details about AIF4S before they're public—he'll be cautious about pre-announcing center activities.

controversial_take: Believes 'overparameterization' explanations for deep learning generalization are incomplete—the real story is in the geometry of implicit regularization from gradient flow, which most practitioners don't understand well enough to exploit.
open_problem: Providing end-to-end theoretical guarantees for fine-tuned foundation models in scientific settings where the true data distribution is governed by known physical laws—can we use the physics to tighten generalization bounds?

before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your zero-shot chaotic system forecasting at ICLR 2025—does the foundation model learn actual Lyapunov exponents, or is it learning trajectory statistics that break down for long forecast horizons?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: FM4Science speaker on scientific time series: climate, turbulence, and geophysics all generate time series that need forecasting. AWS Forecast's architecture could become the backbone for scientific time series foundation models.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: AWS partnership discussions for scientific organizations that need enterprise-grade forecasting infrastructure—he's the right door to knock on.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Amazon Forecast pricing or competitive intelligence about AWS vs. Azure/GCP—standard industry sensitivities.

controversial_take: Believes time series foundation models (Chronos, TimesFM) will outperform domain-specific models even for scientific applications within 3 years, because the diversity of training data provides implicit physics knowledge—a claim the physics-informed ML community disputes.
open_problem: Building probabilistic forecasting models for chaotic systems that are calibrated not just in expectation but in tail risk—essential for climate extremes and tipping points.

name: Rebecca Willett
title: Worah Family Professor of Statistics and Computer Science; Faculty Director of AI, Data Science Institute
lab: Data Science Institute, UChicago; NSF-Simons NITMB; NSF-Simons SkAI Institute
research_focus: Mathematical and statistical foundations of machine learning for scientific problems. Develops theory and methods for inverse problems, compressed sensing, and physics-informed learning across astronomy, materials science, microscopy, neuroscience, and atmospheric science. Faculty Director of AI at UChicago's Data Science Institute.
direction_of_travel: Rice PhD → Duke → Wisconsin → UChicago Worah Family Professor. IEEE Fellow (2021), SIAM Fellow (2021), inaugural SIAM AGDS Career Prize (2024). Now Deputy Director of two major NSF-Simons institutes (NITMB and SkAI). Expanding from statistical theory into leading institutional AI+science infrastructure.
key_contributions: Inaugural SIAM Activity Group on Data Science Career Prize 2024
IEEE Fellow and SIAM Fellow (2021)
Deputy Director: NSF-Simons NITMB and NSF-Simons SkAI Institute
Neumann networks: data-driven inverse problem solving outperforming model-free and unrolled methods
Faculty Director of AI, UChicago Data Science Institute

top_papers.title: Neumann Networks for Linear Inverse Problems in Imaging
top_papers.one_liner: Neumann series-inspired end-to-end deep learning for inverse problems, outperforming unrolled iterative methods.

top_papers.title: Learning to Solve Inverse Problems Using Deep Neural Networks
top_papers.one_liner: Framework for jointly training imaging priors and inverse problem solvers.

current_projects: NSF-Simons SkAI Institute (astronomy AI); NITMB (biology-mathematics interface); UChicago Data Science Institute AI programs; precision agriculture ML collaboration.
personal_site_summary: UChicago personal site describing research on ML foundations for scientific inverse problems, NSF-Simons institute roles, and interdisciplinary collaborations across 6+ scientific domains.
industry_connections: Eric and Wendy Schmidt AI in Science Postdoctoral Fellowship director; NSF-Simons institutes connect to Simons Foundation industry network; UChicago proximity to Chicago financial sector.
commercialization_signal: Medium—foundational academic work, but inverse problem methods directly applicable in medical imaging (MRI reconstruction), astronomy instruments (telescopes), and satellite remote sensing. Schmidt AI in Science connections.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: The person who's building the statistical mathematics that makes scientific AI trustworthy—she works on inverse problems (MRI, telescopes, microscopes) and runs AI programs at two NSF-Simons institutes and UChicago's Data Science Institute. An institutional powerhouse in scientific ML.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your Neumann network approach outperforms unrolled methods for imaging inverse problems—do you see a similar pattern emerging in scientific foundation models, where structured architectures beat general transformers for physics-constrained inference?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: FM4Science's statistical foundations: Willett provides the rigorous ML theory that ensures scientific AI models are not just accurate but statistically valid and robust to distribution shifts.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Connections to medical imaging companies (GE Healthcare, iCAD) or satellite analytics firms that need her inverse problem methods deployed at scale.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Pure language model applications—she focuses on scientific domains and may be skeptical of LLM-first approaches to scientific computing.

controversial_take: Believes the rush to apply large language models to scientific data analysis is premature and potentially dangerous—scientific data has physical structure that LLMs don't understand, and the field needs to build physics-aware architectures first.
open_problem: Developing uncertainty quantification for scientific machine learning models that is both calibrated and computationally tractable at the scale needed for large scientific surveys (LSST, next-gen telescopes).

name: Sherry Yang
title: Assistant Professor of Computer Science, NYU Courant; Staff Research Scientist, Google DeepMind
lab: NYU Courant Institute; Google DeepMind
research_focus: World models for robot learning and decision making. UniSim (ICLR 2024 Outstanding Paper) learned an interactive real-world simulator from video. WorldGym evaluates robot policies entirely within learned world models. VideoAgent enables self-improving video generation. Also works on RL for ML engineering agents and generative models for scientific discovery (materials).
direction_of_travel: Offline RL and off-policy evaluation (2019–2022, Google Brain) → multi-game decision transformers + text-guided policy learning (2022–2023) → world models as interactive simulators for robot learning (UniSim, 2023–2024) → self-improving video generation + RL for ML engineering agents (2024–2025) → NYU faculty + DeepMind staff scientist (2025–present).
