notable_quotes: We envision video as the new language for real-world decision making — video generation models provide a universal interface across tasks and embodiments
UniSim demonstrates that an interactive simulator of the real world can be learned from diverse data, enabling agents to evaluate and improve policies without physical interaction
industry_connections: Staff Research Scientist at Google DeepMind concurrent with NYU faculty. Collaborations with Pieter Abbeel (Covariant), Percy Liang (Together AI). UniSim + WorldGym together constitute a platform for robot policy evaluation without hardware — significant commercial value.
commercialization_signal: MEDIUM — academic with DeepMind role. WorldGym + UniSim could spin out as robot policy evaluation platform. Self-improving video generation has commercial potential. Likely to be approached for advisory/founding roles as NYU lab grows.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: She builds AI that can watch video of the world and use it as a virtual test bed for robots — robots learn entirely in simulation generated from real-world video, without touching a physical robot
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: World-Gymnast showed RL in video world models outperforms supervised learning 18x on robot manipulation — do you think this holds at the distribution boundary (novel objects/environments), or does the world model fidelity degrade exactly where you need it most?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: UniSim (world models) + WorldGym (world models for physical AI) + VideoAgent (self-improving generation) + RL for ML engineering (self-improvement) — she's explicitly at the intersection of your three thesis pillars, and is co-organizing RSI which signals she sees self-improvement as the unifying thread.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to robotics companies needing world-model-based policy evaluation; NYU research funding connections; advisory opportunities as she grows her lab.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Do not ask her to compare with Genie/Sora/World Labs — she builds functional decision-making systems, not just generative video, and she distinguishes carefully on this

controversial_take: Video generation models will replace physical robot simulators within 5 years — World-Gymnast already shows 18x data efficiency. The robotics community is sleeping on this.
open_problem: How to close the world model fidelity gap so policies trained entirely in learned simulators (zero physical robot interaction) transfer reliably to the real world — the holy grail of robot learning.

name: Gabriele Steidl
title: Professor of Mathematics
lab: Image Analysis Group, Department of Mathematics, TU Berlin
research_focus: Steidl is a leading applied mathematician working on the mathematical foundations of optimal transport, Wasserstein gradient flows, and their applications to generative modeling and image analysis. Her core focus is on Wasserstein gradient flows of maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) functionals and f-divergences — proving well-posedness, establishing connections to score-based models and flow matching, and designing neural-network-based algorithms to approximate these flows for sampling and inverse problems. She also develops the mathematical theory of flow matching via Markov kernels, stochastic processes, and transport plans.
direction_of_travel: PhD at Rostock in fast Fourier algorithms (1988) → Professor at TU Darmstadt, Mannheim, and TU Kaiserslautern (computational harmonic analysis, image processing, convex optimization, normalizing flows) → Fraunhofer Institute industrial consultant → Professor at TU Berlin (2020–present), pivoting into the deep intersection of optimal transport, Wasserstein geometry, and generative modeling (flow matching, diffusion, score-based models). SIAM Fellow 2022. CNRS Fellow Ambassador 2026.
key_contributions: Neural Wasserstein Gradient Flows for MMD functionals with Riesz kernels: neural-network approximation of Wasserstein GF backward/forward schemes handling singular measures (ICML 2023)
Posterior sampling via conditional MMD gradient flows with negative distance kernel (ICLR 2024)

Mathematical framework for flow matching via Markov kernels, stochastic processes, and transport plans — unifying conditional and unconditional flow matching with Bayesian inverse problem applications (arXiv 2025)
Adapting noise to data in generative flows via learnable 1D quantile-function parameterization for heavy-tailed and compact-support distributions (arXiv Oct 2025)
Neural sampling from Boltzmann densities via Fisher-Rao curves in Wasserstein geometry (ICLR 2025)
PnP-Flow: plug-and-play image restoration with flow matching (ICLR 2025)
Stochastic Normalizing Flows for inverse problems — unified Markov chain framework for SNFs, diffusion normalizing flows, and VAEs (2021)

top_papers.title: Neural Wasserstein Gradient Flows for Maximum Mean Discrepancies with Riesz Kernels
top_papers.one_liner: ICML 2023 — proposes neural-network-based approximation of Wasserstein gradient flows for non-smooth MMD functionals, handling transitions between singular and absolutely continuous measures.

top_papers.title: Posterior Sampling Based on Gradient Flows of the MMD with Negative Distance Kernel
top_papers.one_liner: ICLR 2024 — proves that a conditional particle flow is a Wasserstein gradient flow and uses it for posterior sampling in Bayesian inverse problems with error bounds.

top_papers.title: Stochastic Normalizing Flows for Inverse Problems: a Markov Chains Viewpoint
top_papers.one_liner: Unifies normalizing flows, diffusion NFs, and VAEs as Markov chain pairs, providing a general framework for stochastic generative models applied to inverse problems.

top_papers.title: Neural sampling from Boltzmann densities: Fisher-Rao curves in the Wasserstein geometry
top_papers.one_liner: ICLR 2025 — uses Fisher-Rao geodesics in Wasserstein space to construct sampling paths from Boltzmann densities, connecting information geometry and optimal transport.

top_papers.title: Flow Matching: Markov Kernels, Stochastic Processes and Transport Plans
top_papers.one_liner: arXiv 2025 — mathematical review unifying three characterizations of flow matching velocity fields (transport plans, Markov kernels, stochastic processes) with applications to Bayesian inverse problems.

recent_arxiv.title: Flow Matching: Markov Kernels, Stochastic Processes and Transport Plans
recent_arxiv.summary: Mathematical review of flow matching from three perspectives (couplings, Markov kernels, stochastic processes) in Wasserstein geometry; shows flow matching solves Bayesian inverse problems via conditional Wasserstein distances.

recent_arxiv.title: Adapting Noise to Data: Generative Flows from 1D Processes
recent_arxiv.summary: Introduces learnable 1D quantile-function parameterization for noise distributions in flow-based generative models, adapting latent distributions to heavy-tailed or compactly supported data while maintaining compatibility with flow matching and consistency models.

recent_arxiv.title: Wasserstein gradient flows of MMD functionals with distance kernel and Cauchy problems on quantile functions
recent_arxiv.summary: Studies well-posedness of Wasserstein gradient flows for MMD with positive/negative distance kernels under Sobolev regularization, reformulating as Cauchy problems on quantile functions.

current_projects: Flow matching theory (Markov kernel and stochastic process characterizations); data-adaptive noise distributions for generative flows via learnable quantile functions; neural sampling from Boltzmann densities using Fisher-Rao / Wasserstein geometry; Wasserstein gradient flows for MMD and f-divergence functionals with Sobolev regularization; PnP-Flow for imaging inverse problems; co-authored Oberwolfach Seminars book 'Variational and Information Flows in Machine Learning and Optimal Transport' (Springer, 2025).
personal_site_summary: Institutional TU Berlin math department page with navigation to publications, projects, CV, and team. Research group branding is 'Image Analysis' though the work has expanded well beyond imaging into optimal transport and generative modeling. No social media presence. Strong publication list updated regularly.
notable_quotes: Variational and stochastic flows on measure spaces are ubiquitous in machine learning and generative modeling. Optimal transport and diffeomorphic flows provide powerful frameworks to analyze such trajectories of distributions. (Oberwolfach Seminars book preface, 2025)
industry_connections: Long-term consultant to the Fraunhofer Institute for Industrial Mathematics (while at TU Kaiserslautern, 2011–2020), bridging academic math to industrial applications. Currently on the Scientific Advisory Board of the Helmholtz Imaging Platform. Editor-in-Chief of SIAM Journal on Imaging Sciences. CNRS Fellow Ambassador 2026. No known startup founding, but Fraunhofer and Helmholtz connections represent significant applied/government-industry interface.
commercialization_signal: MEDIUM — Sustained Fraunhofer consulting history and Helmholtz advisory role indicate applied industry engagement over many years. Her work on flow matching and Wasserstein gradient flows is highly relevant to generative model training pipelines used in industry. No direct startup activity, but institutional technology transfer pathways exist through Fraunhofer.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: She builds the mathematical machinery that explains how to move one cloud of probability points to another cloud in the most efficient way possible — and has been showing how this is exactly what diffusion models and flow-based image generators are doing under the hood.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your flow matching review unifies three characterizations via transport plans, Markov kernels, and stochastic processes — do you see one of these as the right 'canonical' framework that will dominate theory, or is the flexibility across all three fundamental to the field's progress?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Steidl's work provides the rigorous mathematical backbone for flow matching and diffusion generative models — exactly the theory that justifies and guides improvements to production generative systems. Her Wasserstein gradient flow results explain training dynamics, and her quantile-noise adaptation work directly targets a practical limitation of current diffusion models (Gaussian latent mismatch for heavy-tailed data).
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to European deep-tech investors or Fraunhofer spin-out programs; context on how her flow matching theory could be translated into open-source tools or benchmarks that applied ML teams would adopt.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Asking only about image processing applications — her work has moved decisively into general generative model theory and she identifies more as a mathematical analyst than an imaging specialist now.

controversial_take: The diffusion / score-matching and flow matching communities have developed largely independently, but Steidl's framework suggests they are both special cases of Wasserstein gradient flows of divergence functionals — implying that the field has been reinventing the same mathematics under different names, and a unified Wasserstein-geometric lens would accelerate progress more than continued empirical scaling.
open_problem: Can Wasserstein gradient flow theory be used to derive principled step-size schedules and noise level sequences for diffusion model training that are optimal in the sense of minimizing the distance between the generated and target distribution, rather than relying on empirically tuned cosine or linear schedules?

name: Qing Qu
title: Assistant Professor
lab: DeepThink Lab, ECE Division
research_focus: Qing Qu studies the mathematical foundations of deep representation learning and diffusion models, exploiting low-dimensional geometric structures in neural network weights and data to develop efficient, controllable generative AI. His work connects phenomena like neural collapse and hidden Gaussian structure in diffusion models to practical tools for model compression, fine-tuning, and watermarking.
direction_of_travel: Rising associate-professor candidate with 2025 Google Research Scholar Award, NSF Career Award, and ONR funding; 6 NeurIPS 2025 papers (3 spotlight) signals a very productive trajectory toward promotion and visibility at major labs.
key_contributions: Neural collapse theory unifying deep representation geometry and transfer learning
Discovered that diffusion models produce identical content from identical noise (hidden Gaussian structure), NeurIPS 2024
Compressible dynamics in deep low-rank learning — ICML 2024 Oral and best poster at MMLS 2024
Low-dimensional subspaces in diffusion models for controllable image editing and watermarking

top_papers.one_liner: Finds low-dimensional semantic subspaces in diffusion latent space enabling interpretable image editing.

top_papers.one_liner: Mathematical analysis of why CFG works so well and what limits it, NeurIPS 2025 Spotlight.

recent_arxiv.summary: Exploits low-dimensional geometric structure to embed invisible, robust watermarks in diffusion-generated images, NeurIPS 2025 Spotlight.

recent_arxiv.title: A Closer Look at Model Collapse
recent_arxiv.summary: Theoretical and empirical study of model collapse in iterative generative model training on synthetic data, NeurIPS 2025 Spotlight.

current_projects: Controllable diffusion models via low-dimensional subspaces; efficient fine-tuning exploiting compressible dynamics; diffusion watermarking.
personal_site_summary: Detailed academic homepage at UMich with lab news, publications sorted by venue, student profiles, and funding acknowledgments including NSF, ONR, and Google.
industry_connections: 2025 Google Research Scholar Award; Amazon AWS AI Award 2023; Microsoft PhD Fellowship alumnus. Strong indirect connections to generative AI industry.
commercialization_signal: Strong signals: his watermarking research (Shallow Diffuse) is directly commercializable; compressible dynamics research is foundational to LoRA and parameter-efficient fine-tuning products.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: He discovered that AI image generators secretly organize their understanding into compact, low-dimensional structures — which lets him control, watermark, and compress them far more efficiently than brute force.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your NeurIPS 2025 work on hidden Gaussian structure in diffusion models — does this mean that model collapse from training on synthetic data is fundamentally tied to the collapse of that Gaussian structure?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Directly on thesis: controllable diffusion models, efficient fine-tuning, watermarking for AI provenance — all are active investment areas in generative AI infrastructure.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Connections to generative AI infrastructure companies (watermarking, model compression, diffusion tooling); intro to IP protection startups building on diffusion model geometry.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Avoid asking about LLM chat or conversational AI — his focus is firmly on generative visual models and mathematical theory.

controversial_take: LoRA and its variants work not because low-rank is a good approximation, but because neural networks actively converge to low-rank dynamics during training — the rank constraint is not a limitation but a description of reality.
open_problem: Providing tight bounds on when diffusion model generalization fails vs memorizes, particularly as a function of dataset size and model capacity.

name: Mengyue Yang
title: Lecturer (Assistant Professor)
lab: School of Engineering Mathematics
research_focus: Mengyue Yang develops causal world models that incorporate causal structure into reinforcement learning, enabling agents to reason about interventions and counterfactuals rather than pure correlations. Her work connects causality, model-based RL, and world model design with applications to decision-making and embodied AI.
direction_of_travel: Fast-moving early-career academic: AAAI 2026 New Faculty Highlight awardee; 6 NeurIPS 2025 papers; organizing multiple workshops (ICLR 2025/2026 World Models, NeurIPS 2025 Embodied World Models) — on track for a tenured position or senior industry role within 3-5 years.
key_contributions: Causal world models for model-based reinforcement learning
Efficient RL with LLM priors via Bayesian inference (ICLR 2025)
Natural language reinforcement learning framework
Co-organizing ICLR 2025 and 2026 World Models workshops

top_papers.one_liner: Incorporates causal structure into world models to improve generalization to interventional distributions.

recent_arxiv.summary: Comprehensive survey of RL methods for LLM agents, covering reward design, policy optimization, and world model integration.

current_projects: Causal world models; LLM-guided RL; embodied world models for robotics; agentic AI.
personal_site_summary: Concise academic homepage listing research on RL and causality, with paper links and open collaboration invitation.
industry_connections: PhD from UCL (Jun Wang lab — strong connections to Microsoft, DeepMind, and Huawei); co-organizing with Google DeepMind researchers (Xidong Feng).
commercialization_signal: No startup. Causal world models for RL are highly relevant to robotics and simulation-based training companies; LLM-guided RL intersects with AI agent product development.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: She builds AI that understands cause-and-effect, not just correlation — so robots and agents can reason about 'what would happen if I did X' rather than just 'what usually happens.'
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your work on causal world models — when you incorporate causal structure, do you see consistent improvements in out-of-distribution generalization, or is the benefit domain-specific?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Directly on the world models and physical AI thesis — causal world models are the next step beyond pure predictive models for enabling truly generalizable AI agents.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to robotics AI startups (Physical Intelligence, Covariant, 1X) actively building world models with causal reasoning; UK AI startup ecosystem contacts.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Avoid asking about pure LLM capabilities without RL grounding — she is focused on learning agents, not language models as standalone systems.

controversial_take: Most world models fail not because they can't predict the future but because they can't distinguish correlation from causation — pure neural architectures will always hit this wall without explicit causal inductive biases.
open_problem: Learning causal structure in world models from purely observational data at scale, without access to interventional experiments.

recent_arxiv.summary: Integrates LLMs as prior distributions in RL via Bayesian inference, enabling sample-efficient agent learning, ICLR 2025.

current_projects: LLM-guided tree search; RL with LLM priors; multi-agent RL; world model research at Google DeepMind Discovery Team.
personal_site_summary: Clean academic site listing research interests in LLMs, single and multi-agent RL, and meta-RL, with links to key papers.
industry_connections: Research Scientist at Google DeepMind Discovery Team — directly inside one of the world's most influential AI research organizations.
commercialization_signal: Google DeepMind is itself the commercialization vehicle. His tree-search + LLM work is directly relevant to o1/reasoning model product development.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: He makes AI reasoning systems smarter by teaching them to plan ahead like a chess player, combining strategic tree-search with language model intuition.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your AlphaZero-like tree-search for LLMs — do you think MCTS-guided decoding will scale to the point where it makes dedicated reasoning models like o1 obsolete, or are they fundamentally different approaches?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: LLM reasoning via tree-search is central to the self-improving AI and world models investment thesis — directly foundational to next-generation AI agent products.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Connections to AI agent startups building reasoning-heavy products; introductions to investors already backing LLM reasoning infrastructure.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Avoid asking about Google's internal product roadmap — he will not discuss confidential information.

controversial_take: AlphaZero-style planning should be the default decoding strategy for all frontier LLMs — the 'next token prediction' paradigm is fundamentally limited for complex reasoning without explicit search.
open_problem: Making tree-search computationally tractable for open-ended reasoning tasks where the action space is unbounded natural language.

His work spans single-task and massively multitask world model learning, hierarchical control for humanoid robots, and generalization in RL.
direction_of_travel: Finishing PhD at UCSD in 2025/2026; already gave a keynote at ICLR 2025 (highly unusual for a PhD student); published at ICLR 2026 (Newt); NVIDIA Graduate Research Fellowship recipient — prime academic job market or senior researcher at top lab.

commercialization_signal: Very strong: his TD-MPC2 codebase is widely used in robot learning research; humanoid and continuous control work directly relevant to physical AI companies (Figure AI, Physical Intelligence, 1X).

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: He builds AI brains that let robots learn from videos and practice in their imagination, then transfer those skills to real-world physical tasks.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: With Newt doing multitask pretraining across 200 tasks — do you see evidence that diverse task coverage creates emergent generalization to held-out tasks, similar to how language model pretraining generalizes?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: World models for continuous control and humanoid robotics is the core physical AI investment thesis — he is one of the top PhD students in this exact space.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to physical AI companies actively hiring PhD-level researchers (Physical Intelligence, Figure AI, Skild AI); VC introductions for potential future entrepreneurial ventures.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Avoid asking about specific papers from his advisors — focus on his own independent contributions.

controversial_take: The key bottleneck for physical AI is not the robot hardware but the lack of diverse, scalable offline data with good task coverage — the 'internet of robots' problem is more important than better architectures.
open_problem: Enabling world models to generalize zero-shot to novel objects and task configurations never seen during training, without requiring privileged state information.

name: Francesco Faccio
title: Senior Research Scientist

research_focus: Francesco Faccio works on world models, reinforcement learning, and meta-learning, building on a PhD under Jürgen Schmidhuber at IDSIA. His research spans learning to learn, universal models of intelligence, and the development of scalable world models that enable agents to plan and adapt in complex environments.
direction_of_travel: Completed PhD at IDSIA (Schmidhuber group) → postdoc at IDSIA/KAUST → joined Google DeepMind Discovery Team in London in 2025. The Discovery Team is Google's AGI-oriented research group — fast track toward AGI research at one of the most resourced labs in the world.

Contributions to scalable world model architectures for general intelligence
Co-organizing ICLR 2025 and 2026 World Models workshops
Research on learning to learn and universal intelligence frameworks

top_papers.one_liner: Estimates value functions in the weight space of policies rather than state space, enabling meta-RL.

top_papers.one_liner: Multi-agent LLM societies that self-organize to solve complex problems through natural language communication.

current_projects: World model research at Google DeepMind Discovery Team; scaling world models for general-purpose agents; society-of-mind LLM architectures.
personal_site_summary: LinkedIn profile confirms Google DeepMind Discovery Team role in London; limited independent web presence.
industry_connections: Research Scientist at Google DeepMind — direct access to Google's most AGI-oriented research agenda and resources.
commercialization_signal: Google DeepMind is the commercialization vehicle. His world model work feeds directly into Google's AI products and potential AGI systems.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: He builds AI systems that can imagine and plan in a mental model of the world — teaching machines to think ahead before acting, like a human strategist.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your position paper argues world model research is distinct from world knowledge injection — practically speaking, how do you see the two converging in systems like Gemini, which needs both?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: World models for general intelligence is the central thesis — he is at the intersection of foundational world model research and the world's most capable AI lab.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Connections to physical AI startups that could benefit from collaborating with DeepMind researchers; potential for academic collaborations or advisory roles post-DeepMind.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Avoid asking about DeepMind's confidential projects or Gemini's internal architecture.

controversial_take: The fundamental challenge in world model research is not prediction accuracy but causal structure learning — a world model that perfectly predicts but cannot reason about interventions is not truly intelligent.
open_problem: Building world models that can represent and reason about the full causal graph of an environment, not just correlational predictive models.

name: Yuntian Deng
title: Assistant Professor

research_focus: Yuntian Deng studies how language models reason, with a focus on internalizing explicit chain-of-thought into implicit computation through knowledge distillation and training. He also builds large-scale datasets of real-world LLM interactions (WildChat) to study model behavior and limitations in deployment.
direction_of_travel: New faculty at Waterloo since 2024, building on postdoc work at AI2 under Yejin Choi; rapidly establishing an independent research agenda at the intersection of implicit reasoning and real-world LLM behavior.
key_contributions: Developed implicit CoT training via knowledge distillation, teaching LLMs to internalize reasoning steps
Built WildChat, a 1M+ conversation dataset enabling study of real-world LLM usage and failure modes
Created OpenNMT, Im2LaTeX, and LaTeX2Im tools widely used in academia and industry

current_projects: Co-organizing LIT workshop at ICLR 2026; serving as Co-CTO for ACL Rolling Review; building Waterloo NLP lab; ongoing implicit CoT and world model research
personal_site_summary: Faculty site at Waterloo covering implicit reasoning research, WildChat dataset, and NLP tools like OpenNMT and Im2LaTeX; recently updated to reflect new Waterloo position and ICLR 2026 activities.
industry_connections: NVIDIA Fellowship; Baidu Fellowship; Harvard SEAS affiliation; Co-CTO of ACL Rolling Review gives him broad academic community influence; University of Chicago Rising Stars in Data Science
commercialization_signal: Moderate—implicit CoT work has direct relevance to inference-efficient LLM products; WildChat dataset has commercial data value for studying user behavior

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Yuntian teaches language models to think without showing their work—he distills the explicit reasoning steps into the model's internal computation, making inference faster and cheaper.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: In your step-by-step CoT internalization work, does the model actually develop qualitatively different internal representations compared to standard training, or is it mostly a compression of the same computation?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Core to inference-time scaling and LLM post-training: implicit CoT internalization is a post-training technique that could dramatically reduce the token cost of reasoning at inference time.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to inference optimization startups; feedback on which aspects of implicit CoT are most commercially tractable in the near term
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Avoid asking about the tension between his Harvard NLP affiliation and his new Waterloo primary role

controversial_take: Most of the benefit from chain-of-thought comes from the training signal it provides, not from the test-time tokens—models that learn to internalize reasoning will match or beat verbose CoT at a fraction of the cost.
open_problem: What is the maximum compression ratio for internalizing CoT steps before accuracy degrades, and does this limit depend on the reasoning task type or model scale?

name: Devvrit Khatri

His work spans scaling reinforcement learning compute for LLMs, elastic inference architectures, and scalable nearest-neighbor search—all with demonstrated deployment at Google, Meta, and Microsoft scale.
direction_of_travel: Completing PhD under Inderjit Dhillon at UT Austin with a strong industry track record; joining Reflection AI as Member of Technical Staff upon graduation, moving directly into frontier AI development.

personal_site_summary: PhD student site at UT Austin showcasing work on scalable ML, elastic inference, and RL for LLMs; notes industry deployments at Google, Meta, and Microsoft.
industry_connections: Student Researcher at Google (2022-2025) working directly with Prateek Jain; Student Researcher at Meta working with Rishabh Agarwal; MSR internship 2019; joining Reflection AI (new frontier lab) as MTS

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Devvrit builds the algorithms that make large-scale ML practical—his DiskANN is used for vector search at billion-point scale, his MatFormer lets one model serve different compute budgets, and his ScaleRL paper is about how to responsibly scale up RL training for LLMs.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: ScaleRL explores principled scaling of RL compute for LLMs—what's the biggest bottleneck you found: reward hacking, KL divergence blowup, or the fundamental sample inefficiency of PPO-style algorithms?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Directly core to RL for LLMs and LLM post-training: ScaleRL addresses the scaling laws and stability of RL-based post-training, the central challenge for building o1/DeepSeek-style reasoning models.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to Reflection AI's leadership or investors; perspective on which post-training efficiency papers the investor community is most excited about
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Avoid asking specifics about his Reflection AI role—he hasn't fully disclosed the scope yet

controversial_take: Most RL post-training failures are optimization failures, not reward failures—the community over-indexes on reward hacking when the real problem is that PPO is fundamentally unstable at LLM scale.
open_problem: What are the correct scaling laws for RL post-training—specifically, how should compute be allocated between rollout generation, reward modeling, and policy updates as model size grows?

personal_site_summary: PhD student site at Meta/UCL covering thinking models, RL for self-improvement, and inference-efficient ML; notable Llama 3 core contributor and high-citation publication record for a PhD student.
industry_connections: Core contributor to Llama 3 (Meta's flagship product); PhD at Meta MSL with direct access to frontier model training pipelines; former Google Research India pre-doctoral researcher under Prateek Jain; 10,000+ Google Scholar citations
commercialization_signal: Very high—Llama 3 is one of the most commercially impactful open-source models; his thinking tokens research directly informs post-training decisions at Meta and across the industry

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Lovish helped build Llama 3 and now studies whether the extra 'thinking tokens' LLMs generate before answering actually help—he's reframing them as iterative improvement operators and asking when they're worth the extra compute cost.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your improvement operator framing of thinking tokens suggests diminishing returns at some depth—have you identified task properties that predict when iterating past a certain number of thinking steps actually hurts performance?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Directly core to LLM post-training and inference-time scaling: his work characterizes when and how much test-time thinking helps, which determines whether scaling inference compute via thinking tokens is efficient or wasteful.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to startups building post-training infrastructure or evaluation tools; perspective on the investor community's view of open vs. closed model strategies
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Avoid asking about Meta's competitive strategy relative to OpenAI on Llama releases—too politically sensitive

controversial_take: Thinking tokens are often glorified self-correction with extra steps—the performance gains come almost entirely from the first few iterations, and the industry is dramatically overestimating the value of very long reasoning chains.
open_problem: Is there a task-specific optimal thinking depth, and can we learn to predict it from the input alone to adaptively allocate inference compute rather than using fixed-length reasoning chains?

commercialization_signal: Direct commercial application at Waymo—formal specification learning and constrained RL for self-driving vehicles is exactly the thesis. ICRA 2026 paper on VLA test-time adaptation is immediately deployable in robotics.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Fresh PhD from Berkeley just started at Waymo building formal verification into self-driving AI. He bridges the gap between abstract temporal logic safety proofs and real robotic systems.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: In your LTL-constrained RL work, how do you handle the case where the formal spec is incomplete or misspecified—does the cycle replay approach degrade gracefully or catastrophically?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Core thesis person: his work is literally LTL-constrained RL + formal specification learning + test-time adaptation for safety-critical robotics.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Investor perspective on formal verification in AV stack; connections to robotics founders building on VLA models who need safety guarantees.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Do not ask about his undergraduate interpretability work—it's very early and he's moved well past it.

controversial_take: Reward machines are a more principled foundation for safe multi-agent RL than RLHF-based alignment—the field is sleeping on formal symbolic task representations.
open_problem: How do you efficiently learn temporal logic specifications from human demonstrations when the spec space is exponentially large and demonstrations are sparse?

Builds full-stack verification tools (Algaroba SMT solver, PVerifier for distributed systems, Eudoxus LLM-to-formal-spec tool, UCLID5 multi-modal verifier) that make formal verification usable by non-expert software engineers.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Formal methods researcher at AWS who builds tools that make SMT solvers and verification accessible to regular software engineers—think 'Copilot for correctness proofs.' Moving to Waterloo as faculty in fall 2026.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Eudoxus uses LLMs to generate formal queries—when the LLM produces an incorrect or incomplete spec, does the downstream verifier catch it, or does it produce false positives that erode engineer trust?

before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Connections to enterprise DevSecOps and cloud infrastructure investors; perspective on whether AWS sees formal verification as a product or internal tool.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Do not ask about pure string solver benchmarks—he's moved toward LLM-assisted verification and distributed systems.

controversial_take: The SMT solving community has spent 20 years optimizing solvers nobody can use—usability is now the binding constraint, not solver performance, and LLMs are the UX layer formal methods never had.
open_problem: How do you compositionally verify a system where some components are neural networks (not amenable to SMT) and others are traditional software—the heterogeneous verification problem is unsolved.

recent_arxiv.summary: Synthesis-guided lifting of tensor computations to high-level tensor abstractions for performance optimization.

current_projects: Reader at Edinburgh; working on LLM-guided synthesis, polyglot verification, tensor program optimization, and co-developing CYANEA portfolio synthesis solver; speaker at VerifAI-2 ICLR 2026.
personal_site_summary: Reader at University of Edinburgh School of Informatics. Research on formal program synthesis, LLM-synthesis hybrids, verification scalability, tensor optimization. CGO 2025 Distinguished Paper Award.
industry_connections: Close collaborations with Sanjit Seshia's group (UC Berkeley), overlaps with AWS and hardware industry via tensor lifting work. UCLID5 used in academic and industrial hardware verification.
commercialization_signal: Tensor lifting (Tensorize, Guided Tensor Lifting) targets compiler optimization—directly relevant to AI chip companies. LLM-guided synthesis reduces the cost of formal verification, a known bottleneck in safety-critical software development.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Edinburgh professor who makes formal program synthesis practical by combining LLMs with classical symbolic search—her tools automatically generate verified, optimized code from specs. Won a best paper award at CGO 2025 for making AI chips faster via synthesis.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: In CYANEA, the multi-armed bandit selects between symbolic solvers and LLM calls—how stable is the bandit policy across problem domains, and have you seen reward hacking where the bandit exploits easy LLM outputs at the cost of correctness?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Directly on thesis: LLM + formal synthesis = the core of what 'AI-assisted formal verification' means in practice, and her CYANEA/CAV 2024 work is the leading empirical demonstration.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Connections to EDA (electronic design automation) investors interested in synthesis-based chip verification; perspective on demand for formal synthesis in safety-critical compiler toolchains.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Do not ask about pure controller synthesis for continuous dynamical systems—that was her PhD focus and she's moved on.

controversial_take: LLMs will not replace formal synthesizers—but they will obsolete every synthesizer that cannot integrate LLM guidance. The future is hybrid, and pure symbolic tools without LLM interfaces are already legacy.
open_problem: How do you provide correctness guarantees when the LLM is in the loop of a synthesis-verification cycle—the LLM introduces an opaque probabilistic component into an otherwise sound verification process.

name: Matej Balog
title: Research Scientist
lab: Google DeepMind (AlphaEvolve / Discovery team)
research_focus: Machine learning for program synthesis and algorithmic discovery, combining neural networks with symbolic search. Key architect of AlphaEvolve—an evolutionary coding agent that improves algorithms through LLM-driven code mutations and automated evaluation, achieving the first improvement on Strassen matrix multiplication in 56 years.
direction_of_travel: Oxford MMathCompSci → Cambridge-Tübingen PhD (Ghahramani, Schölkopf) → Google DeepMind Research Scientist. Career arc: kernel methods + differential privacy → DeepCoder (program synthesis) → AlphaTensor (algorithm discovery) → AlphaEvolve (evolutionary LLM coding agent). Clear progression toward AI-driven mathematical/algorithmic discovery.
key_contributions: AlphaEvolve: evolutionary LLM coding agent achieving first improvement on Strassen matrix multiplication in 56 years (2025)
DeepCoder: first deep learning approach to inductive program synthesis from input-output examples (ICLR 2017)
Neural Program Synthesis with a Differentiable Fixer: iterative program correction via differentiable fixer network (2020)
AlphaTensor: quantum circuit optimization via reinforcement learning (Nature Machine Intelligence)
Differentially Private Database Release via Kernel Mean Embeddings (ICML 2018)

top_papers.title: DeepCoder: Learning to Write Programs
top_papers.one_liner: Trains neural nets to predict program properties from I/O examples, guiding search-based synthesis for order-of-magnitude speedups.

top_papers.title: AlphaEvolve: A coding agent for scientific and algorithmic discovery
top_papers.one_liner: Evolutionary LLM agent that mutates and evaluates code to discover new algorithms—found first improvement over Strassen in 56 years.

top_papers.title: Neural Program Synthesis with a Differentiable Fixer
top_papers.one_liner: Combines encoder-decoder synthesis with differentiable correction network that iteratively fixes incorrect programs.

recent_arxiv.title: AlphaEvolve: A coding agent for scientific and algorithmic discovery
recent_arxiv.summary: Evolutionary pipeline using LLMs to propose code mutations and automated evaluators to score them, discovering novel algorithms for matrix multiplication and data center scheduling.

current_projects: Research Scientist at Google DeepMind working on AlphaEvolve and AI-driven algorithmic discovery; speaker at RSI ICLR 2026 on evolutionary self-improvement of coding agents.
personal_site_summary: Research Scientist at Google DeepMind. Cambridge-Tübingen PhD (Ghahramani, Schölkopf). 3,669 citations. Key contributor to DeepCoder, AlphaEvolve, AlphaTensor. Works on AI-driven algorithmic discovery.
industry_connections: Senior Google DeepMind researcher. AlphaEvolve is actively optimizing Google's internal data center scheduling and hardware accelerator designs—direct commercial deployment at Google scale.
commercialization_signal: AlphaEvolve is already saving Google compute costs by optimizing their data center scheduling. The evolutionary coding agent paradigm is directly applicable to compiler optimization, hardware design, and scientific computing—multi-billion dollar markets.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: DeepMind researcher who built AlphaEvolve—an AI that writes and tests its own code in a loop to discover better algorithms. It just cracked a 56-year-old open problem in matrix multiplication and is already optimizing Google's data centers.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: AlphaEvolve uses automated evaluators to score algorithm candidates—how do you design evaluators that are tight enough to guide search but loose enough to allow genuinely novel solutions that don't fit existing metrics?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Core RSI thesis: AlphaEvolve IS recursive self-improvement of code—an AI that evolves its own programs via mutation and selection. This is the most concrete existing demonstration of RSI in practice.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Investor perspective on commercializing evolutionary algorithm discovery outside Google; connections to semiconductor and HPC companies who would pay for AlphaEvolve-style compiler optimization.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Don't ask about differential privacy or kernel methods—that's his early PhD work, long superseded.

controversial_take: Evolutionary search guided by LLMs will outperform pure LLM code generation for algorithm discovery—evolution doesn't hallucinate, it evaluates. The field overestimates LLMs and underestimates evolutionary algorithms.
open_problem: Can AlphaEvolve-style evolution discover algorithms in domains where automated evaluation is expensive or impractical—the search efficiency degrades rapidly when the evaluator is not a simple correctness check.

name: Marta Skreta
title: Postdoctoral Researcher
lab: Supervised by Kirill Neklyudov, Mila / Université de Montréal
research_focus: Skreta works on generative models for molecular design and AI for science, with particular focus on flow matching and diffusion-based methods for molecular generation, Feynman-Kac correctors for controlled diffusion sampling, and robotic chemistry (self-driving labs). She bridges theoretical diffusion model methodology with direct application to drug discovery and materials science.
direction_of_travel: PhD at University of Toronto (Aspuru-Guzik lab, Matter Lab); Apple Scholar in AIML; now postdoc at Mila with Neklyudov. Clear path: theoretical ML for chemistry → postdoc deepening generative model foundations → likely faculty or senior research scientist role. The ORGANA robotic chemistry paper signals interest in full-stack AI-for-science systems.
key_contributions: Feynman-Kac Correctors in Diffusion — principled SMC-based method for multi-objective molecular generation
ORGANA: Robotic Assistant for Automated Chemistry (Matter 2025)
Spiers Memorial Lecture on impactful AI for chemistry/materials science (Faraday Discussions 2025)

top_papers.title: Feynman-Kac Correctors in Diffusion: Annealing, Guidance, and Product of Experts
top_papers.one_liner: SMC-based correctors for combining pretrained diffusion models for multi-objective molecular generation.

top_papers.title: ORGANA: A Robotic Assistant for Automated Chemistry Experimentation and Characterization
top_papers.one_liner: Full robotic chemistry pipeline with LLM orchestration for self-driving labs; Matter 2025.

top_papers.title: A Sober Look at LLMs for Material Discovery
top_papers.one_liner: Critical evaluation of LLMs for Bayesian optimization over molecular spaces.

recent_arxiv.title: Feynman-Kac Correctors in Diffusion: Annealing, Guidance, and Product of Experts
recent_arxiv.summary: Introduces FKC framework using SMC for sampling from product distributions derived from pretrained diffusion models, demonstrated on molecule generation.

current_projects: Postdoc at Mila developing controlled generation methods for molecular design; research on flow matching for molecules; NeurIPS AI4Mat workshop co-organization.
personal_site_summary: Mila directory page. Research profile emphasizes AI for Science, generative models, and molecular modeling. Active Twitter presence in the AI4Science community.
industry_connections: Apple Scholar in AIML (Apple internship/funding). Deep connections to Aspuru-Guzik Matter Lab ecosystem which has multiple spin-outs. Collaborations with Yoshua Bengio, Alexander Tong, Arnaud Doucet.
commercialization_signal: High. Molecular generation and self-driving labs are directly commercially relevant (drug discovery, materials). Aspuru-Guzik lab has a track record of company formation (Kebotix, etc.). FKC methods are immediately applicable to multi-objective drug design.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Marta works on the methods that let diffusion models generate molecules with specific desired properties—like drug-likeness and binding affinity simultaneously. She also helped build ORGANA, a robot that runs chemistry experiments autonomously.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Feynman-Kac Correctors use SMC resampling to combine pretrained models — how do you handle the weight degeneracy problem in high dimensions where molecule space is huge?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Core thesis — diffusion/flow matching for molecular generation, generative models for science, controlled/guided generation. Very high priority.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to biotech and drug discovery companies using generative models; connections to AI4Science investors (DCVC Bio, Lux Capital); lab automation investment angles.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Don't reduce her work to 'drug discovery' framing — her methods are general and she is invested in the theoretical foundations.

controversial_take: Self-driving labs are not about autonomy for its own sake — the real value is generating interpretable scientific hypotheses, not just optimizing a black-box property. Most current robotic chemistry systems optimize the wrong objective.
open_problem: How to design diffusion/flow guidance that provably generates molecules satisfying hard multi-objective constraints (ADMET properties, synthesizability) without sacrificing chemical diversity.

name: Michael Albergo
title: Junior Fellow, Harvard Society of Fellows; IAIFI Fellow, MIT; Incoming Assistant Professor of Applied Mathematics (Harvard, July 2026)
lab: Harvard Society of Fellows / Kempner Institute for the Study of Natural and Artificial Intelligence
research_focus: Albergo develops the mathematical foundations of flow-based and diffusion generative models, with emphasis on stochastic interpolants as a unifying framework. His work bridges statistical physics, applied mathematics, and machine learning — using physics-inspired stochastic dynamics to construct principled generative models for scientific applications including lattice field theory and probabilistic forecasting. He also works on sampling from unnormalized densities via non-equilibrium transport.
direction_of_travel: Harvard undergrad (Physics/History), Cambridge MPhil (Physics), NYU PhD (2024), Harvard Society of Fellows junior fellow, incoming Harvard Applied Math assistant professor (July 2026). Schmidt Sciences AI2050 Early Career Fellow. Highly unusual physics-to-ML-theory trajectory. One of the most mathematically sophisticated researchers in the flow matching space. ICML 2024 spotlight and NeurIPS 2025 Spotlight signal consistent top-tier venue output.
key_contributions: Stochastic Interpolants: A Unifying Framework for Flows and Diffusions (JMLR) — foundational framework for flow-based generative models
NETS: Non-equilibrium Transport Sampler (ICML 2025) — sampling from unnormalized densities via non-equilibrium dynamics
LEAPS: Discrete neural sampler via locally equivariant networks (ICML 2025, co-author with Holderrieth)

top_papers.title: Stochastic Interpolants: A Unifying Framework for Flows and Diffusions
top_papers.one_liner: Foundational mathematical framework unifying flow and diffusion generative models via stochastic interpolants; JMLR.

top_papers.title: SiT: Exploring Flow and Diffusion-based Generative Models with Scalable Interpolant Transformers
top_papers.one_liner: Vision transformer architecture for flow/diffusion generative models; ECCV 2024.

top_papers.title: Stochastic Interpolants with Data-Dependent Couplings
top_papers.one_liner: Extends stochastic interpolants to arbitrary couplings between source and target; ICML 2024 Spotlight.

recent_arxiv.title: NETS: A Non-Equilibrium Transport Sampler
recent_arxiv.summary: Uses non-equilibrium statistical physics dynamics to sample from unnormalized target densities; ICML 2025.

recent_arxiv.title: Tilt Matching for Scalable Sampling and Fine-Tuning
recent_arxiv.summary: Simple scalable algorithm using stochastic interpolants to sample from unnormalized densities and fine-tune generative models.

current_projects: Building Harvard Applied Math group focused on generative models and physical sciences (starting July 2026); Schmidt AI2050 fellowship research on stochastic interpolants for scientific discovery; multitask learning with stochastic interpolants.
personal_site_summary: Elegant personal site listing all papers with brief descriptions. Clear research identity at physics-ML interface. Actively recruiting graduate students and postdocs for July 2026 Harvard group.
industry_connections: Schmidt Sciences AI2050 Early Career Fellow. Harvard Kempner Institute affiliation (large AI research endowment). IAIFI (NSF-funded MIT-Harvard AI physics institute) network. No direct startup/industry affiliations yet.
commercialization_signal: Moderate-low currently — deeply foundational. But stochastic interpolants and non-equilibrium sampling have direct applications to molecular simulation (pharma) and materials design. Post-lab-formation, expect industry collaborations.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Michael built the mathematical framework (stochastic interpolants) that lets you construct a generative model between ANY two probability distributions, not just Gaussian noise to data. He's starting a Harvard Applied Math group in July 2026 and is looking for students.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Stochastic interpolants give you a framework for building flows between arbitrary distributions — when the source distribution has structure (e.g., a crystal lattice), does the interpolant framework preserve that structure during transport?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Core thesis — flow matching foundations, diffusion models, generative models for physical science. His physics background makes him uniquely positioned on the physical AI angle.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Connections to pharma and materials companies needing non-equilibrium sampling; introductions to deep-tech investors focused on AI-for-science; Schmidt Sciences network connections.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Don't ask about career plans relative to pure math vs. ML — he has a clear identity at the interface and bridges it deliberately.

controversial_take: Most flow matching implementations are solving an easier problem than they think — the choice of coupling is where almost all the information sits, and optimal transport couplings will dominate once they're computationally tractable at scale.
open_problem: How to construct stochastic interpolants for discrete combinatorial spaces (molecules, code) that preserve the mathematical guarantees of the continuous theory.

name: Carles Domingo-Enrich
title: Senior Researcher
lab: Microsoft Research New England, Cambridge MA
research_focus: Domingo-Enrich works at the intersection of diffusion/flow matching generative models and stochastic optimal control (SOC). He has established the theoretical connection between reward fine-tuning of generative models and SOC, introducing Adjoint Matching and Adjoint Sampling as key algorithms. His work provides the principled mathematical scaffolding for reward alignment of diffusion models with applications to chemistry and robotics.
direction_of_travel: Math/Physics BSc at UPC Barcelona, NYU PhD (Joan Bruna), visiting researcher at Meta FAIR Labs, now Senior Researcher at Microsoft Research New England. Fast trajectory — PhD finished May 2024 and already senior researcher at MSR. The Adjoint Matching paper (ICLR 2025 Spotlight) is widely cited in the reward alignment for generative models community.
key_contributions: Adjoint Matching: reward fine-tuning of flow and diffusion models via memoryless SOC (ICLR 2025 Spotlight)
Adjoint Sampling: scalable algorithm for learning diffusion processes from unnormalized energies
A Taxonomy of Loss Functions for Stochastic Optimal Control — unifying framework for SOC losses

top_papers.title: Adjoint Matching: Fine-Tuning Flow and Diffusion Generative Models with Memoryless Stochastic Optimal Control
top_papers.one_liner: Theoretical and practical framework for reward fine-tuning diffusion/flow models via SOC; ICLR 2025 Spotlight.

top_papers.title: Adjoint Sampling: Scalable and Efficient Sampling via Diffusion Processes from Unnormalized Densities
top_papers.one_liner: First on-policy approach allowing more gradient updates than energy evaluations in diffusion-based sampling.

top_papers.title: Stochastic Optimal Control Matching
top_papers.one_liner: Novel IDO technique for SOC via least-squares matching; ICLR 2024.

recent_arxiv.title: Energy-Based Fine-Tuning of Language Models
recent_arxiv.summary: Applies energy-based model fine-tuning to LLMs, outperforming SFT while matching RLVR performance.

recent_arxiv.title: Value Gradient Guidance for Flow Matching Alignment
recent_arxiv.summary: Value-function-based guidance for aligning flow matching models; NeurIPS 2025.

current_projects: Adjoint Sampling extensions to large-scale molecular systems; energy-based LLM fine-tuning; weekly Generative Modeling & Sampling Seminar at MSR New England; reward alignment for robotics and chemistry.
personal_site_summary: Clean academic site listing papers and CV. Note: The workshop listed him as 'MIT' but he is at Microsoft Research New England (Cambridge, MA) — distinct from MIT affiliation.
industry_connections: Senior Researcher at Microsoft Research New England. Prior internships at IBM Research and Meta FAIR Labs. MSR position provides direct pathways into Azure AI and Microsoft Copilot teams.
commercialization_signal: High. Adjoint Matching and Adjoint Sampling are directly applicable to fine-tuning foundation generative models for commercial applications (drug design, content generation, robotics). Microsoft context amplifies commercial reach.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Carles figured out the right mathematical framing for making diffusion models do what you want — treating reward fine-tuning as a stochastic control problem. His Adjoint Matching paper is the theory paper the alignment-of-generative-models community was waiting for.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Adjoint Matching assumes memoryless control — but real reward functions in chemistry are path-dependent (synthesis routes, not just final molecules). How does the SOC framing extend to non-Markovian objectives?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Core thesis — reward alignment for diffusion/flow models, controlled generation, generative models for science. Very high priority.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Connections to drug discovery companies needing principled reward alignment; introductions to robotics companies fine-tuning diffusion policies; Microsoft enterprise AI partnership angles.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Don't confuse his MSR New England position with being an MIT researcher — he's at Microsoft, not MIT. An easy mistake given he's in Cambridge, MA.

controversial_take: Most 'reward alignment' papers for diffusion models are doing something equivalent to Adjoint Matching in disguise but without the theoretical clarity — the field needs to standardize on the SOC framing to make progress on the really hard problems.
open_problem: Computationally tractable reward alignment for diffusion models with long trajectories and high-dimensional reward functions, where backpropagating through the full reverse process is infeasible.

name: Erik Bekkers
title: Associate Professor in Geometric Deep Learning
lab: Amsterdam Machine Learning Lab (AMLab), University of Amsterdam
research_focus: Bekkers leads research on geometry-grounded representation learning — developing group equivariant neural architectures, equivariant neural fields, and flow matching methods on non-Euclidean spaces. His core thesis is that all data is physically grounded and representations must respect the underlying geometry (symmetries, manifold structure) to generalize well. Applications span computational physics, chemistry, robotics, and medical imaging.
direction_of_travel: PhD in Biomedical Engineering at TU/e (cum laude), postdoc in applied differential geometry, then Amsterdam faculty. Two NWO personal grants (Veni 2019, Vidi 2024) indicate Dutch national recognition. ELLIS Scholar (2023). 9 PhD students and 1 postdoc in his group. The Vidi grant title ('neural ideograms and geometry-grounded learning') defines his next 5-year research arc. Teaching load includes a widely adopted equivariant DL course at UvA.
key_contributions: Equivariant Neural Fields: Grounding Continuous Representations in Geometry (ICLR 2025)
Variational Flow Matching on General Geometries — controlled generation with equivariant VFM
NWO Vidi grant on geometry-grounded representation learning (2024)

top_papers.title: Grounding Continuous Representations in Geometry: Equivariant Neural Fields
top_papers.one_liner: Equivariant neural field architecture preserving geometric symmetries for continuous representations; ICLR 2025.

top_papers.title: Group Equivariant Convolutional Networks
top_papers.one_liner: Foundational paper on group equivariant CNNs by Cohen & Welling (Bekkers contributed early related work).

top_papers.title: Towards Variational Flow Matching on General Geometries
top_papers.one_liner: Flow matching generalized to general Riemannian manifolds via variational framework.

recent_arxiv.title: On the Utility of Equivariance and Symmetry Breaking in Deep Learning Architectures on Point Clouds
recent_arxiv.summary: Empirical study showing equivariant models increasingly outperform non-equivariant ones as geometric task complexity increases.

recent_arxiv.title: Controlled Generation with Equivariant Variational Flow Matching
recent_arxiv.summary: Equivariant controlled generation for molecules using variational flow matching framework; ICML 2025.

current_projects: GRaM Workshop at ICLR 2026 co-organization; equivariant flow matching for molecular generation; neural fields with geometric grounding; Vidi-funded research on neural ideograms; 9-student research group at UvA AMLab.
personal_site_summary: Well-structured academic site with research vision, student profiles, and course materials. The equivariant DL course at UvA has become a standard community resource.
industry_connections: MICCAI and medical imaging community connections (via MICCAI Young Scientist Award 2018). Philips Impact Award suggests early Philips connection. ELLIS Scholar network. Amsterdam AI ecosystem (Qualcomm AI Research Amsterdam, ML6).
commercialization_signal: Moderate. Geometric DL methods have direct applications in drug discovery (molecular property prediction), robotics (SE(3) equivariant manipulation), and medical imaging. His group's flow matching + equivariance work is particularly relevant for generative molecular design.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Erik is Amsterdam's geometric deep learning professor. His thesis is that your neural network should know about rotations, translations, and other symmetries because the physical world does. He's now combining this with generative models (flow matching on manifolds) for molecular design.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your variational flow matching on general geometries paper — when the manifold has non-trivial curvature (like SO(3) for molecular orientations), how does the variational objective handle the metric tensor correction at sampling time?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Core thesis — geometric deep learning for physical AI, equivariant generative models, flow matching on manifolds. Very high investment relevance.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Connections to Dutch deep-tech investors (Lux Capital EU, EQT Ventures); introductions to drug discovery companies using geometric DL for molecular design; ELLIS network connections.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Don't try to reduce his research to 'just making CNNs rotation-invariant' — the scope is much broader (manifolds, non-Euclidean geometry, generative models).

controversial_take: The 'scale everything with transformers and hope symmetry emerges' approach will hit a wall — equivariant architectures will win in any domain where the physical symmetry group is known and data is limited. The debate is not 'equivariant or not' but 'which symmetry group.'
open_problem: Efficient equivariant generative models for crystal structures and amorphous materials — current methods either approximate symmetry or are computationally prohibitive at the scale needed for materials discovery.

name: Robin Walters
title: Assistant Professor of Computer Science
lab: Geometric Learning Lab, Khoury College of Computer Sciences, Northeastern University
research_focus: Walters studies symmetry in deep learning from theory to applications — developing equivariant neural networks (ENNs) that incorporate group symmetries as hard mathematical constraints. He works on relaxed equivariance for approximately symmetric domains, equivariant reinforcement learning for robotics, and applications of ENNs to trajectory prediction, ocean forecasting, and robot manipulation. He is a visiting fellow at the Boston Dynamics AI Institute.
direction_of_travel: Math PhD, then Northeastern faculty. NSF CAREER Award 2025 ($600K, 2025-2030) for relaxed symmetry constraints. Boston Dynamics AI Institute visiting fellowship is a strong industry-academia bridge. Multiple NeurIPS posters and ICLR papers on equivariant methods for robotics. Teaching focus on applications of symmetry from theory to perception/control.
key_contributions: Fourier Transporter: Bi-Equivariant Robotic Manipulation in 3D (ICLR)
A Practical Guide for Incorporating Symmetry in Diffusion Policy (NeurIPS 2024)
NSF CAREER Award: Improving Data Efficiency in Deep Learning with Relaxed Symmetry Constraints (2025)

top_papers.one_liner: Shows equivariant models work even when true symmetry is latent/approximate; ICLR.

top_papers.one_liner: SE(3)-equivariant model for 3D robotic grasping and manipulation; ICLR.

top_papers.one_liner: Framework for injecting equivariance into robot learning diffusion policies; NeurIPS 2024.

recent_arxiv.summary: Unifies equivariant GNN and spherical CNN approaches for physical simulation domains; NeurIPS 2024.

current_projects: GRaM Workshop at ICLR 2026; NSF CAREER research on relaxed symmetry constraints; Boston Dynamics AI Institute fellowship on equivariant robot perception/manipulation; equivariant diffusion policies for robotics.
personal_site_summary: Clean professional academic site with research, teaching, and lab information. CV available. Reflects clear research identity at symmetry-in-deep-learning intersection with robotics applications.
industry_connections: Visiting Fellow at Boston Dynamics AI Institute (developing equivariant NNs for robot perception and manipulation). NSF CAREER support. MIT Lincoln Lab collaboration (Rajmonda Caceres). Northeastern Experiential AI (EAI) seed funding.
commercialization_signal: High for robotics. Boston Dynamics AI Institute connection is directly on the physical AI investment thesis. Equivariant diffusion policies for manipulation are immediately applicable to warehouse robotics, surgical robotics, and industrial automation.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Robin is the person figuring out how to make robot learning algorithms use the fact that physics has symmetries. He's working with Boston Dynamics' AI team on making manipulation robots learn faster by encoding rotation and translation symmetry into their neural networks.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Relaxed equivariance introduces a softened symmetry bias — how do you empirically determine what the effective symmetry group of a robot manipulation task is without exhaustively testing all candidate groups?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Direct thesis connection — equivariant neural networks for physical AI/robotics, geometric DL for manipulation and control. High priority for physical AI angle.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to robotics AI investors (Bessemer, Lux Capital, DCVC); connections to physical AI companies hiring geometric DL researchers; Boston Dynamics and adjacent ecosystem connections.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Don't ask him to take a position on equivariance vs. data augmentation — it's a politically charged topic in the community and he has nuanced views on both.

controversial_take: Diffusion policies for robotics will only reach production reliability when they incorporate equivariant structure — pure data-driven policies without symmetry constraints will fail on out-of-distribution grasps in ways that are both surprising and catastrophic.
open_problem: Automatic symmetry discovery — learning the correct symmetry group from data for robotics tasks where the true symmetry is unknown or approximately satisfied across different configurations.

name: Rogerio Schmidt Feris
affiliation: IBM Research / MIT-IBM Watson AI Lab
title: Principal Scientist & Senior Manager
lab: MIT-IBM Watson AI Lab
research_focus: Multimodal perception combining vision, language, and audio with emphasis on data efficiency and model efficiency. Leads large research programs at the industry-academia boundary. Developed commercial AI systems deployed at scale for sports broadcast and law enforcement.
direction_of_travel: From pure computer vision research toward orchestrating large-scale industry-academic AI programs (DARPA, IARPA). Increasingly interested in associative memory as a unifying theoretical lens for modern foundation models.
key_contributions: Over 200 papers and 50+ patents in computer vision and multimodal AI
Led development of AI-curated sports highlight system (US Open, Wimbledon, Masters) winning Technology Emmy award
Principal Investigator for DARPA Learning with Less Labels (IBM-MIT team)
Principal Investigator for IARPA DIVA (IBM-MIT-Purdue)
IBM Master Inventor with 24,000+ Google Scholar citations

current_projects: Associative memory perspectives on foundation models; multimodal learning efficiency; NFAM workshop co-organization.
personal_site_summary: Covers research agenda on data-efficient and model-efficient multimodal AI; biography emphasizing industry impact and Emmy award; lists major research programs.
industry_connections: Senior IBM Research manager; MIT-IBM Watson AI Lab PI; TPAMI Associate Editor; Area Chair at NeurIPS/CVPR/ICLR/ICML.
commercialization_signal: High—has shipped commercial AI systems (sports highlights, visual search for law enforcement) at IBM scale.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: One of IBM's top AI scientists—ran billion-dollar government contracts and built the AI that auto-edits Wimbledon highlights. Now interested in how associative memory theory unifies modern foundation models.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Given your DARPA work on learning with less labels, do you see dense associative memories as offering better sample-efficiency guarantees than contrastive pretraining approaches like CLIP?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Bridge between academic theory (NFAM) and industrial deployment—he's evidence that associative memory can be a commercial organizing principle, not just theory.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: LP-level connections to sports-tech or enterprise AI companies building multimodal products.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Law enforcement applications of visual search—sensitive area given public controversy.

controversial_take: Believes the 'data efficiency' framing will replace raw scale as the dominant paradigm for enterprise AI within 3 years, because Fortune 500 companies cannot afford to label millions of examples.
open_problem: Building associative memory systems that can retrieve across modalities (image + text + audio) with provable capacity bounds.

name: Yongji Wang
title: Research Scientist
lab: Google DeepMind
research_focus: Physics-informed machine learning with applications to geophysics, glaciology, and fluid dynamics. Combines high-resolution remote sensing with physics-informed deep learning to extract governing equations from observational data. Also works on discovery of mathematical singularities using AI.
direction_of_travel: Stanford postdoc (Ching-Yao Lai lab) → Google DeepMind Research Scientist. The Science paper on Antarctic ice (2025) is a landmark publication connecting AI to climate science. Google DeepMind positioning signals serious commercial/applied science interest.
key_contributions: First author on Science 2025 paper: AI + physics-informed deep learning reveals fundamental physics of Antarctic ice sheet anisotropy
Co-author on 'Discovery of Unstable Singularities' (DeepMind, arXiv 2025)
Research on cryosphere modeling and fluid dynamics with ML
Google DeepMind appointment—strong industry signal

current_projects: Physics-informed ML for geophysical systems at DeepMind; cryosphere modeling; AI-driven mathematical discovery.
personal_site_summary: Google Scholar profile listing cryosphere, fluid dynamics, and physics-informed ML publications. No dedicated personal site found.
industry_connections: Google DeepMind—primary employer with enormous resources and industry reach. Stanford Lai lab alumni network.
commercialization_signal: High—DeepMind role suggests active commercialization interest. Science paper on Antarctic ice directly relevant to climate risk modeling used by insurance and financial institutions.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: A DeepMind scientist who published in Science showing AI can read the physics of Antarctic ice from satellite data—with direct climate prediction implications. Also helping AI discover new mathematics (unstable singularities).
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your Antarctic ice work revealed anisotropy that standard models missed—are you now working on embedding this discovered physics back into ice sheet simulation models to improve sea-level rise projections?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: FM4Science core thesis: foundation models can discover new scientific knowledge, not just accelerate known computations. Wang's ice sheet paper is a proof of concept.

name: Aditi Krishnapriyan
affiliation: UC Berkeley / Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory
title: Assistant Professor
lab: Berkeley AI Research (BAIR); Applied Mathematics and Computational Research Division, LBNL
research_focus: Physics-inspired machine learning for scientific problems: developing ML methods that incorporate physical inductive biases (symmetries, conservation laws), solving PDEs with neural operators, and building ML force fields for molecular dynamics and materials design. The 'two-way street': physics improves ML and ML improves physics simulations.
direction_of_travel: Stanford Physics PhD (DOE Computational Science Graduate Fellowship) → Alvarez Postdoc Fellow at LBNL → UC Berkeley assistant professor. Extremely fast career trajectory: 2025 DOE Early Career Award. Active in ML force fields (MLIPs), neural operators, and turbulence simulation.
key_contributions: 2025 DOE Early Career Research Program award for scalable physics-consistent ML
Luis W. Alvarez Fellowship in Computing Sciences (prestigious LBNL postdoc)
EddyFormer: 30x acceleration of 3D turbulence simulation vs. top numerical solvers (NeurIPS 2025)
MLIP Arena: open benchmark platform for machine learning interatomic potentials
Knowledge distillation for ML force fields (ICLR 2025)

top_papers.one_liner: NeurIPS 2021 paper showing PINNs fail on stiff/chaotic PDEs and proposing curriculum/seq2seq fixes.
