recent_arxiv.title: Transformers Discover Molecular Structure Without Graph Priors
recent_arxiv.summary: Shows unmodified Transformers can discover molecular graph structure from raw data without GNN-style graph inductive biases.

current_projects: DOE Early Career project on scalable physically-consistent ML; MLIP Arena benchmark; turbulence simulation with EddyFormer; ML for distribution shift in molecular modeling.
personal_site_summary: UC Berkeley faculty page describing physics-ML research agenda, BAIR membership, LBNL appointment, and recent papers on MLIPs, neural operators, and turbulence.
industry_connections: LBNL (DOE national lab with large HPC resources); Berkeley AI Research network; DOE Early Career award means sustained federal funding; MLIP Arena connects to materials science industry.
commercialization_signal: High—ML force fields (MLIPs) are directly monetizable for drug discovery and materials design. Companies like Isomorphic Labs, Schrodinger, and DP Technology are competing in this space. Her MLIP Arena could become an industry standard.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: A Berkeley professor who made molecular dynamics 30x faster with AI and just got a DOE Early Career Award. Her ML force fields work sits directly in the drug discovery and materials science AI market where Isomorphic Labs and others are competing.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your EddyFormer achieves 30x speedup on turbulence—are you seeing similar speedups for molecular dynamics with MLIPs, or is the simulation accuracy-speed tradeoff fundamentally different for atomistic vs. continuum systems?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: FM4Science core: foundation models for physics simulations. Krishnapriyan is doing this at the frontier—EddyFormer and MLIP distillation are exactly the models FM4Science aims to produce.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to MLIP-focused VCs (Lux Capital, DCVC) or computational drug discovery companies that need faster, accurate force fields.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: PhysicsInformedNeuralNetworks (PINNs) as a method—she published their failure modes, so she may have strong views about papers that still advocate for PINNs uncritically.

controversial_take: Believes graph neural networks for molecular ML will be displaced by pure transformers within 5 years—her 'Transformers Discover Molecular Structure' paper is the opening salvo in that argument.
open_problem: Building ML models that generalize across different chemical spaces (organic, inorganic, periodic, amorphous) without seeing each family at training time—the same generalization challenge as multi-PDE operators.

name: Mahmoud (Mido) Assran
title: Research Scientist
lab: Meta Fundamental AI Research (FAIR), Montreal / New York
research_focus: Self-supervised representation learning for video and images — Joint Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPA); learning world models for video understanding, prediction, and robotic planning; dense visual representations for embodied AI. Core question: how can a model develop common-sense perceptual understanding of the physical world by watching videos, without labels?
direction_of_travel: Distributed optimization / parallel deep RL (PhD) → semi-supervised visual learning (PAWS, MSN) → self-supervised image representation (I-JEPA with LeCun) → video world models (V-JEPA, V-JEPA 2, V-JEPA 2.1) → robotic planning without task-specific training (V-JEPA 2-AC). Arc: image → video → action; global representation → dense spatial representation; passive understanding → active planning. Operationalizing LeCun's JEPA thesis at scale.
key_contributions: V-JEPA 2 (June 2025) — large-scale self-supervised video model; 77.3% on Something-Something v2; zero-shot robotic planning with <62 hours of unlabeled robot video
V-JEPA 2.1 (March 2026) — dense feature learning; hierarchical self-supervision + multi-modal tokenizers; 20-point robotics grasping improvement over V-JEPA 2-AC
V-JEPA (2024) — video joint embedding predictive architecture; trained on internet video, predicts masked spatiotemporal patches in representation space
I-JEPA (2023, with LeCun) — image-based JEPA; learns semantic representations without hand-crafted augmentations; NeurIPS 2023
MVP Benchmark (2025) — Minimal Video Pairs: humans 92.9% vs. best model 40.2% — reveals massive gap in physical world understanding
MSN / Masked Siamese Networks (ECCV 2022) — label-efficient learning through masked Siamese networks
SpidR (December 2025) — self-supervised speech representation learning; outperforms wav2vec 2.0, HuBERT, WavLM

top_papers.title: V-JEPA 2: Self-Supervised Video Models Enable Understanding, Prediction and Planning
top_papers.one_liner: Pretrained on 1M+ hours of video; enables robotic manipulation planning with <62 hours of unlabeled robot data; demonstrates video world models enable zero-shot robotic planning

top_papers.title: V-JEPA 2.1: Unlocking Dense Features in Video Self-Supervised Learning
top_papers.one_liner: Dense predictive loss + hierarchical self-supervision; 20-point improvement on robotic grasping; SOTA on Ego4D and EPIC-KITCHENS action anticipation

top_papers.title: Self-Supervised Learning from Images with a Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (I-JEPA)
top_papers.one_liner: Predicts representations of masked image regions from context — no hand-crafted augmentations; scalable to ViT-Huge; operationalizes LeCun's JEPA thesis. NeurIPS 2023.

top_papers.title: A Shortcut-aware Video-QA Benchmark for Physical Understanding via Minimal Video Pairs (MVP)
top_papers.one_liner: 55K QA pairs with minimal-change pairs to defeat shortcuts; humans 92.9% vs. best model 40.2% — quantifies how little current video models understand physical causality

recent_arxiv.title: V-JEPA 2.1: Unlocking Dense Features in Video Self-Supervised Learning
recent_arxiv.summary: Dense predictive loss, hierarchical self-supervision across encoder layers, multi-modal tokenizers. 20-point improvement on robotic grasping, 77.7 on Something-Something v2. Authors include Assran and Yann LeCun.

recent_arxiv.title: V-JEPA 2: Self-Supervised Video Models Enable Understanding, Prediction and Planning
recent_arxiv.summary: Pretrained on 1M+ hours of video. V-JEPA 2-AC enables zero-shot robotic arm manipulation planning with <62 hours unlabeled robot data. 77.3% on Something-Something v2. 29 co-authors including Yann LeCun.

recent_arxiv.title: A Shortcut-aware Video-QA Benchmark (MVP) for Physical Understanding
recent_arxiv.summary: 55K minimal-change video QA pairs. Human 92.9%, best open-source model 40.2% vs 25% random. Reveals catastrophic gap in physical causality understanding.

recent_arxiv.title: SpidR: Learning Fast and Stable Linguistic Units for Spoken Language Models Without Supervision
recent_arxiv.summary: Self-supervised speech model using masked prediction + self-distillation. Outperforms wav2vec 2.0, HuBERT, WavLM. Shows JEPA-style predictive learning generalizes to audio.

current_projects: At Meta FAIR, leading the V-JEPA line — the video instantiation of Yann LeCun's JEPA thesis. Core claim: predict representations of masked video patches (not pixels) → develop internal world model with better physical common sense than generative models. V-JEPA 2 demonstrated this translates directly to robotic planning. V-JEPA 2.1 pushes toward dense spatial features for fine-grained physical understanding. MVP benchmark is a scientific instrument measuring progress toward physical world understanding.
personal_site_summary: midoassran.ca is marked 'currently out of date' — directs to Google Scholar. Bio emphasizes McGill/Mila PhD (Vanier Scholar). Research goal: 'enable computers to develop common sense perceptual models of our world.' Also founder of YETE, a not-for-profit advancing youth education. Media coverage in Quanta, Reuters, CNBC, Fortune.
notable_quotes: A major challenge for modern AI is to learn to understand the world and learn to act largely by observation. [V-JEPA 2 abstract]

Human performance on MVP is 92.9%, while the best open-source state-of-the-art video-language model achieves 40.2% compared to random performance at 25%. [MVP benchmark — quantifies the physical understanding gap]
Models that solely rely on visual or textual biases would achieve below random performance. [MVP paper — on why current benchmarks are gamed by shortcuts]
industry_connections: Research Scientist at Meta FAIR; works directly with Yann LeCun (Chief AI Scientist, Meta); key collaborators include Nicolas Ballas, Adrien Bardes, Quentin Garrido, Koustuv Sinha (Meta FAIR). JEPA is Meta's core self-supervised learning research bet.
commercialization_signal: MODERATE — no independent startup; all work at Meta FAIR. However, V-JEPA 2's robotics result is a direct commercial signal: a video world model that enables robotic planning with minimal robot data could be foundational infrastructure for every robotics company. Meta may productize or release as infrastructure.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Assran is building the AI that watches YouTube videos and develops an internal physics simulator in its head — then uses that simulator to figure out how to pick up objects with a robot arm it's barely ever seen before. V-JEPA learned from a million hours of internet video and needed less than 62 hours of robot footage to plan manipulation tasks.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: V-JEPA 2-AC achieves zero-shot robot planning without task-specific training — how are you thinking about the scaling laws here? Does the robotics performance keep improving as you add more internet video pretraining, or does it plateau without robot-specific interaction data eventually?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Assran represents the LeCun JEPA approach to world models — non-generative, energy-based, predictive. This is the theoretical alternative to the diffusion/generative world models approach. If JEPA-style video pretraining can bootstrap robotic manipulation with minimal robot data, it's foundational infrastructure for the entire physical AI ecosystem.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Robotics company intros wanting to pilot V-JEPA-based planning, academic collaboration opportunities, speaking engagements bridging FAIR research to robotics industry, connections to companies applying JEPA to proprioception/tactile sensing.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Do not frame JEPA as 'competing with' or 'better than' Dreamer/diffusion world models simplistically — the debate is subtle and he'll have strong technical opinions.

controversial_take: Assran and LeCun are betting that non-generative world models (predict in representation space, not pixel space) are fundamentally superior to generative world models. The MVP benchmark result (best video models at 40.2% vs. human 92.9%) is empirical evidence that current generative/language models don't actually understand physics, regardless of their visual fluency.
open_problem: How to scale V-JEPA's physical understanding to fine-grained, contact-rich manipulation tasks where dense spatial representations need to capture sub-centimeter physics. V-JEPA 2 shows promise on pick-and-place; the hard problems are cloth folding, connector insertion, or manipulating deformable objects.

name: Jürgen Schmidhuber
title: Director KAUST AI Initiative; Scientific Director IDSIA; Professor of AI
lab: IDSIA (Dalle Molle Institute for Artificial Intelligence Research)
research_focus: Theoretical and empirical foundations of artificial general intelligence: recursive self-improvement, Gödel machines, universal learning algorithms, LSTM and recurrent neural networks, formal theories of creativity and curiosity, algorithmic information theory, computable universes, developmental robotics.
direction_of_travel: From LSTM inventor and empirical deep learning pioneer toward formal AGI theory and provably optimal self-improving systems. Huxley-Gödel Machine achieves human-level coding agent performance. Also pivoting toward robotic systems with curiosity-driven exploration and morphology co-design.
key_contributions: LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory) — 1997, with Sepp Hochreiter; most cited AI paper of the 20th century
CTC (Connectionist Temporal Classification) — enabled modern speech recognition
Gödel Machines — first formal framework for provably optimal recursive self-improvement (2003+)
Neural History Compressor / Unsupervised Pre-training (1991) — precursor to modern self-supervised learning
Linear Transformers / ULTRA (1991) — outer-product key-value attention predating 2017 Transformers
Highway Networks (2015) — first very deep feedforward networks; direct precursor to ResNets
Huxley-Gödel Machine (2025) — practical RSI coding agent achieving human-level SWE-bench

top_papers.title: Long Short-Term Memory
top_papers.one_liner: Solved the vanishing gradient problem in RNNs via gated memory cells; enabled modern NLP, speech, and time-series AI

top_papers.title: Annotated History of Modern AI and Deep Learning
top_papers.one_liner: 97-page argument that nearly every major deep learning idea originated in Schmidhuber's lab or pre-2010 non-Anglophone literature

top_papers.title: Gödel Machines: Self-Referential Universal Problem Solvers Making Provably Optimal Self-Improvements
top_papers.one_liner: First formal framework for recursive self-improvement: a machine rewrites its own code iff it can prove the rewrite is beneficial

recent_arxiv.title: Huxley-Gödel Machine: Human-Level Coding Agent Development by an Approximation of the Optimal Self-Improving Machine
recent_arxiv.summary: Practical instantiation of Gödel machine theory applied to coding agents. Introduces 'Cumulative Meta-Productivity' (CMP) metric. Achieves human-level on SWE-bench. Direct bridge from theoretical RSI to a real deployable system.

recent_arxiv.title: RACAS: Controlling Diverse Robots With a Single Agentic System
recent_arxiv.summary: Cooperative agentic architecture enabling single system to control heterogeneous robot types without retraining. Directly relevant to Physical Intelligence / general robot policies.

recent_arxiv.title: Efficient Morphology-Control Co-Design via Stackelberg Proximal Policy Optimization
recent_arxiv.summary: Uses Stackelberg game theory to jointly optimize robot body structure and control policy.

recent_arxiv.title: Multiple Token Divergence: Measuring and Steering In-Context Computation Density
recent_arxiv.summary: Method to measure and direct how much computation LLMs allocate during inference — relevant to efficient reasoning and test-time compute scaling.

current_projects: Leading KAUST AI Initiative. Running RSI workshop at ICLR 2026. Primary theoretical project: formalizing RSI and connecting Gödel machines to modern LLM coding agents. Directing work on robotic systems, multimodal models, and curiosity-driven agents.
personal_site_summary: Extensive site documenting history of AI contributions. Dedicated pages on LSTM, Gödel machines, world models, transformer precursors, GAN precursors, credit attribution. Site serves as research showcase and historical record / priority claims. Actively hiring at KAUST.
notable_quotes: "The most cited AI paper of the 20th century is the LSTM. The most cited paper of the 21st century builds on my Highway Networks."
"Schmidhuber invented everything." — Elon Musk (Schmidhuber cites this approvingly)
"A Gödel Machine rewrites any part of its own code as soon as it has found a proof that the rewrite is useful — the first technical justification of consciousness."
industry_connections: Technologies licensed and deployed by Google (LSTM in Android voice recognition, Google Translate), Apple (Siri), Microsoft, Amazon (Alexa), Meta, Baidu. Co-founded ANTOPTIMA (AI optimization company). KAUST AI Initiative funded by Saudi sovereign wealth.
commercialization_signal: MEDIUM-HIGH. Huxley-Gödel Machine is a near-term deployable RSI coding agent achieving human-level SWE-bench — acquisition/spinout candidate. LSTM already generates enormous commercial value through licensing precedent.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Schmidhuber invented LSTM — the technology behind Siri, Alexa, and Google Translate — and has spent 30+ years building toward AGI through mathematically provable self-improving machines. The most recent work: the Huxley-Gödel Machine, a real system where an AI improves its own code in a loop guided by a proof that each change is beneficial, now matching the best human-engineered coding agents on standard benchmarks.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: The Huxley-Gödel Machine uses Cumulative Meta-Productivity to evaluate self-modifications by descendants' performance rather than immediate scores — how far is that from a Gödel machine actually generating formal proofs of improvement, and what's the gap between the approximation and the full theoretical ideal?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: If RSI produces agents that can autonomously improve coding agents, that's a force-multiplier for every AI company in your portfolio. Schmidhuber's lab is the only group actively building provably-grounded RSI systems with real SWE-bench results.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Portfolio companies that need custom model optimization or embodied AI for industrial robotics in the Gulf region. Offer to amplify the Huxley-Gödel Machine work to a broader tech/VC audience.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Do NOT bring up the 2018 Turing Award (Hinton, LeCun, Bengio) without being prepared for a 30+ minute lecture. Do not say 'Attention Is All You Need was the first Transformer.' Do not attribute GANs solely to Goodfellow.

controversial_take: Schmidhuber genuinely believes he deserves shared credit for GANs, Transformers, ResNets, knowledge distillation, and unsupervised pre-training — with 600+ annotated references to support the argument. The mainstream community largely ignores these claims, but a careful reading reveals he has a legitimate partial case on several points.
open_problem: The Gödel Machine requires formal proofs of improvement — computationally intractable except for narrow domains. Open problem: build a useful approximation that retains the safety guarantee while being fast enough to compete with pure empirical self-improvement methods.

name: Lingpeng Kong
title: Assistant Professor
lab: HKU NLP Lab (co-director)
research_focus: Diffusion language models; structured prediction and probabilistic NLP; inference-time representation manipulation; mechanistic interpretability of DLMs; byte-level and tokenizer-free language modeling; compositional and mathematical reasoning; multimodal understanding
direction_of_travel: Moved from structured prediction and dependency parsing at CMU/DeepMind → representation learning and generative NLP → now leading research on diffusion language models as an autoregressive alternative, with emphasis on interpretability (DLM-Scope), flexible generation (DreamOn), and inference-time steering (PERSONA)
key_contributions: DreamOn (2026): first variable-length code generation with diffusion LMs—removes fixed-canvas limitation of DLMs
DLM-Scope (2026): first SAE-based mechanistic interpretability framework for diffusion language models; reveals DLMs enable more effective steering than autoregressive LLMs
PERSONA (2026): training-free inference-time personality control via orthogonal activation vector algebra—91% win rate on dynamic personality adaptation
Proxy Compression for Language Modeling (2026): joint training on bytes + compressed representations for tokenizer-free scaling
ToolSelf (2026): self-reconfiguring agents via tool-driven intrinsic adaptation—24% average performance improvement
PhD from CMU; former Research Scientist at DeepMind London
Co-director HKU NLP Lab; teaching AI/NLP/ML across UG, MSc, advanced topics in LLMs

current_projects: HKU NLP Lab: diffusion language model interpretability and flexible generation (DLM-Scope, DreamOn), inference-time representation control (PERSONA), byte-level language modeling (Proxy Compression), self-reconfiguring agents (ToolSelf), mathematical reasoning evaluation (PromptCoT), multimodal understanding.
personal_site_summary: Assistant Professor at HKU CS and co-director of HKU NLP Lab. PhD from CMU; former Research Scientist at DeepMind London. Teaching Introduction to AI, NLP at UG/MSc, and Advanced Topics in Language Models. Personal site quotes Stefan Zweig (1933) on difficulty of ethical decisions—suggests values-driven researcher.
notable_quotes: Aspects of LLM personality are mathematically tractable—personality can be treated as a manageable mathematical construct via vector algebra in activation space.
SAE features in DLMs enable more effective diffusion-time interventions, often outperforming LLM steering—suggesting diffusion architectures may have structural advantages for model control.
industry_connections: Former Research Scientist at DeepMind (Google) London office—strong connections to DeepMind/Google research community. HKU location gives ties to Hong Kong and broader Asia-Pacific ML ecosystem. No known current startup founding or advisory roles.
commercialization_signal: MEDIUM — DLM research is early-stage but uniquely positioned: if diffusion LMs become viable alternatives to autoregressive models (as suggested by his work on steering advantages), his lab would be foundational. PERSONA's inference-time behavioral control has clear applications in AI safety/alignment products. DeepMind background gives industry credibility.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: He's building AI that thinks in a completely different way—using diffusion (like image generation) instead of predicting one word at a time, and showing it can actually be easier to control.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: DLM-Scope shows SAEs work fundamentally differently in diffusion LMs—do you think this structural difference makes DLMs better suited for latent reasoning tasks, where you need to manipulate representations mid-generation?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Central to LIT workshop thesis: diffusion language models are a primary candidate architecture for continuous/latent thinking beyond chain-of-thought. His work on DLM interpretability (DLM-Scope) and steering (PERSONA) is building the infrastructure for latent reasoning models.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to companies building on diffusion LMs (Inception, others); connections to AI safety teams interested in inference-time behavioral control; signal on HKU NLP Lab spinout potential.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Being asked primarily about efficient transformers—that survey work is from his past; he's now fully committed to diffusion LMs and inference-time control.

controversial_take: Diffusion language models have a structural advantage over autoregressive LLMs for controllable generation and steering—the bidirectional denoising process makes representation intervention fundamentally more effective than left-to-right interventions in GPT-style models.
open_problem: Can diffusion LMs escape the fixed-canvas limitation across all natural language tasks (not just code), and can the activation-space steering advantages demonstrated in PERSONA be scaled to align model behavior in production systems?

name: Dmitry Krotov
affiliation: Independent Researcher (formerly MIT-IBM Watson AI Lab / IBM Research Cambridge)
title: Independent Researcher
lab: Independent (resigned IBM Research January 6, 2026)
research_focus: Theoretical foundations of associative memory, energy-based models, and brain-inspired AI architectures. Key threads: (1) Dense Associative Memories (modern Hopfield networks with exponential storage capacity); (2) connections between associative memory, transformers, and diffusion models; (3) energy-based language models (NRGPT); (4) biological plausibility and neuroscience connections; (5) analog hardware implementation of associative memories. Physics background drives a Lyapunov/energy-function view of neural architectures.

Research trajectory shows deliberate effort to connect Dense Associative Memory theory to practical architectures. NRGPT (ICLR 2026) directly challenges transformer language models with energy-based alternatives. The thermodynamics paper (Jan 2026) and biological plausibility work deepens physics/neuroscience foundations. Strategically positioned at NFAM as the theorist who can explain why diffusion models work.
key_contributions: Dense Associative Memories / Modern Hopfield Networks (2016 with John Hopfield): polynomial interaction functions enabling exponential storage capacity
Identified connection between Hopfield network update rules and transformer attention mechanisms
Energy Transformer (NeurIPS 2023): Architecture combining attention, energy-based models, and associative memory
Memory in Plain Sight (2023): Survey unifying diffusion models and associative memories through energy-based language

NRGPT (ICLR 2026): Energy-based alternative to GPT — inference becomes gradient descent on an energy landscape; competitive on language tasks
Stochastic Thermodynamics of Associative Memory (2026): Thermodynamic costs and tradeoffs in Dense Associative Memory

Co-organizing ICLR 2026 NFAM workshop.
personal_site_summary: dmitrykrotov.com confirmed active. Describes himself as independent researcher at intersection of physics, neuroscience, and AI. Confirms resignation from IBM Research effective January 6, 2026. No startup or consulting disclosed, but timing of independence immediately before ICLR 2026 suggests deliberate career repositioning.
notable_quotes: IBM resignation date of January 6, 2026 — weeks before ICLR 2026 — is a strong signal of a deliberate transition; no announcement of next position as of March 2026
industry_connections: Recently departed IBM Research (resigned January 2026). Previously: MIT-IBM Watson AI Lab, IBM Research Cambridge, Institute for Advanced Study Princeton. No disclosed startup, advisory, or consulting arrangements as of March 2026. Analog circuit work (crossbar arrays for constant-time associative memory inference) has natural hardware commercialization angle — relevant to edge AI and neuromorphic computing players (Mythic, Rain Neuromorphics).
commercialization_signal: Low-to-moderate currently, high potential given recent independence. No current startup. Analog hardware implementation paper points toward neuromorphic/edge AI commercialization. NRGPT at ICLR 2026 is the clearest commercial test — if energy-based LLMs prove more inference-efficient than transformers, that's a major commercial angle. IBM resignation creates maximum ambiguity and maximum opportunity for VC engagement.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Dmitry proved mathematically why transformer attention works (it's basically a memory retrieval system), just left IBM to go independent, and is now trying to replace GPT with an energy-based model that treats inference as physics.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: NRGPT shows inference can become gradient descent on an energy landscape — what would it take for that to be more inference-efficient than standard transformer attention at production scale, and are there architecture choices that could get you there?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: His theoretical framework is the most rigorous mathematical account of why both transformers and diffusion models work — and it predicts failure modes that practitioners discover empirically. For a VC building conviction on new AI architectures, he's the canonical theorist to understand. If NRGPT proves viable at scale, that's potentially a new architecture company.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Help thinking through the transition from IBM to independent — whether academic faculty, new industry lab, or startup is the right next step; introductions to neuromorphic hardware companies (Mythic, Rain AI) who would fund his analog circuit work; architecture-focused AI investors if considering commercializing NRGPT.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Whether his work has immediate practical applications — he's a theorist by identity and will push back hard if pressed for near-term product pitches.

controversial_take: The transformer architecture's dominance is an accident of engineering convenience, not theoretical optimality. The true mathematical structure of attention is associative memory retrieval, and NRGPT recovers that structure in a way that could enable dramatically more interpretable and physically principled language models. Scaling laws for transformers may not transfer to energy-based alternatives.
open_problem: Whether the gradient-descent-as-inference property of NRGPT can be exploited to enable fundamentally more efficient test-time compute scaling than chain-of-thought — if 'thinking' in an energy-based model corresponds to energy minimization, you get a physical theory of reasoning rather than a heuristic.

name: Sewon Min

Primary academic organizer of SPOT ICLR 2026.

Continuing OLMo/Ai2 open model post-training. Building nonparametric LM architectures that attribute outputs to training data.

notable_quotes: We're introducing a new way to train AI in a more collective, collaborative way. [On FlexOLMo — enabling data owners to contribute private expertise without sharing data]
industry_connections: Research Scientist at Allen Institute for AI (Ai2) — co-contributor to OLMo, OLMoE, Tulu, OLMoTrace. UC Berkeley EECS faculty (BAIR). Former Meta AI visiting researcher. J.P. Morgan PhD Fellowship alumna. ACL 2025 Dissertation Award winner.
commercialization_signal: LOW-MEDIUM. No current startup. FlexOLMo has strong commercial potential (federated training for enterprises with sensitive data). OLMoTrace could be a compliance/legal tool. Primary value at ICLR: connector to all SPOT speakers/attendees.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: She studies why AI models sometimes make up facts and sometimes get things right, and built tools to trace exactly which training data caused the AI to say what it said. She's running the SPOT workshop.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: OLMoTrace can now attribute any LLM output to specific training documents — do you think this kind of transparency will become a regulatory requirement, and can it be applied to closed models or only fully open ones like OLMo?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Her spurious rewards in RLVR work is directly relevant to post-training quality — if reward signals in RL post-training are unreliable, the scaling thesis for post-training needs more nuance. She's doing the foundational science to determine when/why post-training actually works.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to enterprise customers needing training data attribution for compliance/IP protection (OLMoTrace), connections to federated learning companies, research funding through Berkeley fellowship programs.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Whether open-source models will ever match closed frontier models — deeply committed to the open ecosystem.

controversial_take: Most claimed reasoning capabilities in current LLMs are sophisticated retrieval from training data, not genuine reasoning — benchmark improvements from RLVR may be measuring pattern-matching on verifiable formats rather than actual reasoning improvement.
open_problem: Whether federated/modular LLM training (FlexOLMo) can produce models matching centrally-trained quality, and whether training data attribution can be precise enough for copyright and privacy compliance at scale.

name: Nouha Dziri
title: Research Scientist
lab: Allen Institute for AI (Ai2)
research_focus: Fundamental limits of LLM reasoning: (1) diagnosing when/why LLMs fail on compositional and OOD reasoning (Faith and Fate, OMEGA); (2) building better reasoning through post-training and RL (Tulu 3, RL Grokking Recipe); (3) studying homogenization and creativity collapse (Artificial Hivemind, NeurIPS 2025 Best Paper). Co-led Ai2's OLMo and Tulu 3 safety and post-training efforts.
direction_of_travel: University of Alberta PhD → postdoc with Yejin Choi at UW → Mila/McGill → Microsoft Research Montreal → Google DeepMind → Allen Institute for AI (2023-present). Co-led OLMo 2 and Tulu 3 post-training/safety. NeurIPS 2025 Best Paper.
key_contributions: Faith and Fate: Limits of Transformers on Compositionality (NeurIPS 2023 Spotlight): showed transformers solve compositional tasks via shortcut heuristics, not genuine algorithmic computation

Co-led post-training and safety for OLMo 2 and Tulu 3 at Ai2

current_projects: Speaking at SPOT ICLR 2026 on post-training and reasoning limits. Continuing AI2 work on OLMo post-training. Research on RL Grokking. Investigating diversity/homogenization dynamics. Building automated red-teaming and safety evaluation at Ai2.
personal_site_summary: Research: studying how/why LLMs succeed or fail on OOD reasoning, advancing reasoning through post-training, ensuring safety. Key works: Faith and Fate, Generative AI Paradox, OMEGA, Artificial Hivemind, Tulu 3, OLMo 2. Invited lectures at Stanford, Oxford, Edinburgh, Princeton, CMU.
notable_quotes: We won BEST PAPER AWARD at NeurIPS 2025! [On Artificial Hivemind]
The homogenization we find is not a bug — it's a fundamental consequence of training on shared human-generated data. The AI hivemind is already here.
Even DeepSeek-R1 fails dramatically when math problems require strategies outside its training distribution. Long chains of thought are not sufficient for true mathematical reasoning.
industry_connections: Research Scientist at Allen Institute for AI (Ai2). Co-led OLMo 2 and Tulu 3 post-training/safety. Former Google DeepMind, Microsoft Research Montreal, Mila. Work featured in The Economist, TechCrunch, Science, Quanta Magazine.
commercialization_signal: LOW. Pure academic/research scientist. No current startup signals. High relevance for thesis calibration — OMEGA/Faith-and-Fate work empirically tests limits of the post-training scaling thesis.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: She runs tests that catch AI models faking understanding — they produce the right answer using a completely wrong shortcut, and she's figured out how to tell the difference. Her NeurIPS 2025 Best Paper found that 70+ AI models are increasingly giving the same answers to everything.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your OMEGA paper shows frontier reasoning models fail on math problems requiring novel strategies — is this fundamentally a post-training data coverage problem that more RL can solve, or something structural about how transformers generalize?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Her work sets empirical boundary conditions for the post-training scaling thesis: if OMEGA shows RL post-training fails to generalize beyond training distributions, the 'reasoning will keep improving with more RL compute' thesis needs more nuance. Her RL Grokking Recipe identifies when RL actually works for genuine algorithm discovery.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to AI safety-focused enterprise customers, connections to academic labs doing complementary reasoning evaluation research.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Whether LLMs will ever 'truly reason' — she focuses on specific failure modes rather than general capability claims.

controversial_take: The homogenization of AI outputs — where 70+ different models increasingly give the same answers — is a greater long-term threat to human intellectual diversity than AI replacing jobs, and the AI industry is ignoring it.
open_problem: Whether RL training can ever produce genuine out-of-distribution algorithmic generalization in LLMs, or whether all apparent reasoning improvements are fundamentally interpolation within the training distribution expressed through longer chains of thought.

name: Zahra Kadkhodaie
affiliation: Flatiron Institute, Simons Foundation (Center for Computational Neuroscience + Center for Computational Mathematics)
title: Flatiron Research Fellow
lab: Computational Vision group (CCN)
research_focus: Understanding the mathematical structure of high-dimensional image densities and what diffusion/denoising models actually learn: generalization vs. memorization, geometry-adaptive harmonic representations, sparse semantic representations, blind denoising diffusion, normalized energy model learning.
direction_of_travel: PhD in solid-state physics → computational neuroscience at NYU Courant (Simoncelli + Mallat) → wavelet-based image statistics → diffusion models theory (ICLR 2024 Outstanding Paper) → now at Flatiron probing theoretical limits: blind denoising, normalized density estimation, sparse semantic representations.
key_contributions: Generalization in diffusion models arises from geometry-adaptive harmonic representations (ICLR 2024 Outstanding Paper)
Blind denoising diffusion models and the blessings of dimensionality (2026) — schedule-free diffusion models adaptively track implicit schedule, convergence polynomial in intrinsic dimension
Learning normalized image densities via dual score matching (2025) — first true log-probability extraction from diffusion models
Unconditional CNN denoisers contain sparse semantic representations of images (2025)
Feature-guided score diffusion for conditional sampling (2024)
Generalized compressed sensing with diffusion priors (TMLR 2025)

personal_site_summary: Simons Foundation page confirms Flatiron Research Fellow position (CCN + CCM joint). Focus: 'learning and interpreting high-dimensional image density models.' Mentor: Eero Simoncelli (NYU). No blog/social links.
industry_connections: Flatiron Institute (non-profit research). Advisor Eero Simoncelli has deep computational vision/neuroscience connections. Stéphane Mallat (École Polytechnique) is scientific advisor to Kyutai. No startup connections.
commercialization_signal: Medium. ICLR 2024 Outstanding Paper directly relevant to IP/copyright risk assessment for diffusion model companies. Compressed sensing work has medical imaging applications. Pre-commercial but foundational.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: She is figuring out exactly what an AI image generator has 'understood' about the visual world versus what it has memorized — and proving mathematically why it generalizes to new images instead of copying training data.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your work shows diffusion models learn geometry-adaptive harmonic bases as an inductive bias — does that mean the architecture (UNet/transformer) encodes this bias, or does it emerge from the denoising objective itself regardless of architecture?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Her generalization theory is directly relevant to copyright/IP risk in diffusion model products — courts and companies both need to know when a model memorizes vs. generalizes. Her normalized density estimation work could enable new auditing tools.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Connections to companies facing generalization/copyright questions who need technical advisors; introductions to medical imaging companies who could apply her compressed sensing work.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Whether her work 'supports' or 'undermines' copyright claims — she's doing basic science and will be put off by legal framing.

controversial_take: Diffusion models are not interpolating in pixel space — they have learned a genuine low-dimensional geometric representation of image manifolds that is architecture-independent and dataset-convergent. The 'stochastic parrots for images' framing is empirically and theoretically wrong.
open_problem: Characterizing the intrinsic dimensionality of image manifolds precisely enough to prove polynomial-time convergence for diffusion models in general (not just the blind/schedule-free case).

name: Sitan Chen

personal_site_summary: sitanchen.com — full publication list through 2026. PhD students with Von Neumann, PD Soros, NDSEG, NSF GRFP fellowships. NSF CAREER funded. Clear dual focus: diffusion theory + quantum learning theory.
industry_connections: Pure academic (Harvard CS faculty). NSF-funded. Collaborates with Jerry Li (Microsoft Research), Pang Wei Koh (UW). No startup ties.
commercialization_signal: Low-direct, high-indirect. No commercial activity. But CFG analysis, sampling complexity results, and masked diffusion scheduling work are directly actionable for production teams. VCs can use his papers to diligence technical claims by startups.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: He is a theoretical computer scientist who rigorously proves how fast AI image generators can run and why the guidance tricks everyone uses actually don't do what people think they do — he sets the mathematical speed limits and corrects the field's myths.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: You proved classifier-free guidance doesn't sample from the tilted distribution it's supposed to — given that, should product teams be worried about building on it, or does this not matter for quality in practice?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: His CFG analysis has immediate implications for guidance-dependent products. His sampling complexity results bound achievable inference efficiency. His masked diffusion scheduling and solver optimization translates into engineering improvements that reduce costs.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Real-world data on which theoretical bottlenecks product teams actually hit; introductions to startups who need technical advisors on diffusion theory; signal on which open problems are most commercially urgent.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Quantum computing investment — his quantum learning theory work is long-horizon academic research, not near-term commercial.

controversial_take: Classifier-free guidance — the most widely deployed technique in text-to-image generation — does not sample from the intended conditional distribution; it samples from the boundary of the support. The field has been building on a mathematical mistake, and quality degradation at high guidance is precisely predicted by the correct theory.
open_problem: Closing the gap between dimension-free high-accuracy sampling theory and what is achievable with actual neural score functions in practice — current theory requires smoothness assumptions that real score networks do not satisfy.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: He figured out how to train AI image generators on blurry, incomplete, or noisy data — which is most real-world scientific data — without needing expensive clean labeled datasets.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your ambient diffusion approach is compelling for medical imaging — have you benchmarked against deep unfolding on clinical MRI datasets, and does the model generalize across scanner vendors?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Ambient Diffusion solves a fundamental data acquisition problem in medical imaging and protein structure determination — wherever clean training data is prohibitively expensive or physically impossible, his framework is the enabling technology.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to medical imaging companies (radiology AI, MRI vendors) interested in ambient training; potential seed investment for a spinout targeting medical imaging reconstruction; connections to faculty at strong CS departments.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Do not over-focus on his past — he is focused on faculty search and next steps.

controversial_take: Most diffusion model 'memorization' research is solving the wrong problem — the real issue is that models trained on clean internet data cannot generalize to scientific domains where clean data doesn't exist; ambient diffusion is the right solution.
open_problem: Whether the data scaling laws for ambient diffusion have the same exponents across corruption types, or whether certain corruptions (like phase retrieval in crystallography) follow fundamentally different sample complexity curves.

industry_connections: Core NVIDIA Research team working directly with Vahdat and Kreis. Weather forecasting work positions him in AI for climate sector (NVIDIA Earth-2). BlobGEN connects to NVIDIA content creation pipeline.
commercialization_signal: No startup. Diffusion-for-inverse-problems work directly applicable to medical imaging and climate AI — both commercial NVIDIA product lines (Earth-2, BioNeMo).

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: He works on using AI image generators as smarter tools for reconstructing blurry or incomplete images — like improving MRI quality or predicting weather — by treating the AI as a physics-informed prior.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your test-time scaling paper proposes searching over noise trajectories — is there a natural way to incorporate hard physical constraints (like mass conservation in weather) into the trajectory search, or does that require retraining?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Diffusion models as learned physical priors for inverse problems is a generalization applicable to medical imaging, seismic inversion, and atmospheric modeling — all large commercial markets.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to medical imaging companies or climate tech startups exploring generative emulators; connections to NVIDIA Earth-2 commercial team.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Do not conflate his work with image generation for art/content — his focus is scientific inverse problems.

controversial_take: Test-time scaling (searching over noise trajectories or reward-guided sampling) will prove more impactful for scientific applications of diffusion than further training improvements — the priors are already good enough; we need smarter inference.
open_problem: Whether diffusion-based weather emulators can achieve reliable uncertainty quantification at sub-hourly timescales needed for operational forecasting, or whether implicit assumptions break down at convective scales.

name: Julia Kempe
affiliation: NYU Courant Institute / Meta FAIR Paris (transitioning to Ellison Institute Oxford)
title: Silver Professor of CS, Mathematics and Data Science, NYU; Senior Researcher, Meta FAIR Paris
lab: NYU Center for Data Science + Meta FAIR Paris Foundations of Reasoning Team
research_focus: Theoretical foundations of reasoning and learning in large AI models — what they learn, how they generalize, and where they fail. Leads Meta FAIR Paris's Foundations of Reasoning team: chain-of-thought theory, mechanistic interpretability, RLHF theory, model collapse. Earlier career: quantum computing, random walks, graph algorithms.
direction_of_travel: From quantum computing and graph algorithms (Tel Aviv, Paris 7) → ML theory (generalization, sample complexity) → deep learning foundations at Meta FAIR (reasoning, CoT, robustness) → NFAM workshop (associative memory ↔ LLM reasoning) → incoming Ellison Institute Oxford.
key_contributions: Strong Model Collapse (ICLR 2025) — quantifies when training on AI-generated data causes irreversible quality degradation
Beyond Model Collapse: Scaling Up with Synthesized Data Requires Verification (ICLR 2025)
Mission Impossible: A Statistical Perspective on Jailbreaking LLMs (NeurIPS 2024) — impossibility results for perfect alignment
Soft Tokens, Hard Truths (ICLR 2026) — CoT reasoning with soft continuous token representations
Teaching Models to Teach Themselves: Reasoning at the Edge of Learnability (2026)
How RL after Next-Token Prediction Facilitates Learning (ICLR 2026)

top_papers.title: Strong Model Collapse
top_papers.one_liner: Proves that training on AI-generated data creates a feedback loop irreversibly degrading model quality — formal bounds on model collapse

top_papers.title: Mission Impossible: A Statistical Perspective on Jailbreaking LLMs
top_papers.one_liner: Statistical impossibility results: any sufficiently capable model has adversarial examples — perfect alignment is unachievable

top_papers.title: Beyond Model Collapse: Scaling Up with Synthesized Data Requires Verification
top_papers.one_liner: Shows synthetic data can scale safely if verified against ground truth — the key to preventing collapse

top_papers.title: Teaching Models to Teach Themselves: Reasoning at the Edge of Learnability
top_papers.one_liner: Identifies regime of problems at edge of learnability where self-improvement loops are most effective — a theory of when RLHF works

recent_arxiv.title: Teaching Models to Teach Themselves: Reasoning at the Edge of Learnability
recent_arxiv.summary: Formal theory of self-improvement loops — identifies the learnability frontier where bootstrapped reasoning is most effective.

recent_arxiv.title: Soft Tokens, Hard Truths
recent_arxiv.summary: ICLR 2026. Training CoT with soft token embeddings enables better generalization than discrete text supervision.

recent_arxiv.title: How Reinforcement Learning after Next-Token Prediction Facilitates Learning
recent_arxiv.summary: ICLR 2026. Theoretical analysis of why RLHF on top of next-token prediction enables qualitatively better reasoning than pretraining alone.

current_projects: Foundations of Reasoning at Meta FAIR Paris; NFAM ICLR 2026 workshop organization; theoretical analysis of self-improvement and bootstrapped reasoning; mechanistic interpretability; AI safety robustness lower bounds. Incoming Ellison Institute of Technology Oxford.
personal_site_summary: NYU Courant/CDS faculty page. Silver Professor of CS, Mathematics and Data Science. Meta FAIR Paris Foundations of Reasoning team lead. Incoming Ellison Institute Oxford.
industry_connections: Senior Researcher at Meta FAIR Paris — direct connection to Meta's LLM/AI product teams. NYU 2023 Julius Silver Professorship. Incoming Ellison Institute of Technology Oxford (Larry Ellison's network). Fellow of Asia-Pacific Artificial Intelligence Association.
commercialization_signal: No startup — strongly academic and Meta-affiliated. The Ellison Institute Oxford move positions her at the intersection of AI and science rather than industry commercialization.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: She builds rigorous mathematical proofs for questions about AI reasoning — like why training AI on its own outputs eventually makes it worse, and whether it's theoretically possible to make an LLM completely safe from adversarial attacks (answer: no).
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your model collapse work suggests verification is key to safe synthetic data scaling — are there natural domains like mathematics (where proofs are verifiable) where collapse is fully avoidable, versus language tasks where it seems unavoidable?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Her model collapse and synthetic data scaling work is foundational for any company building AI training pipelines — understanding when synthetic data is safe is a critical infrastructure question for the next wave of AI development.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to AI safety organizations interested in her impossibility results; connections to the Ellison Institute founding team; advisory opportunities at AI infrastructure companies grappling with synthetic data pipelines.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Do not ask about quantum computing background — she has completely transitioned and finds those questions unproductive.

controversial_take: Perfect alignment is statistically impossible — any sufficiently capable model has adversarial examples, and the AI safety community is chasing an unachievable goal if they want provable safety without capability tradeoffs.
open_problem: Whether chain-of-thought reasoning trained with soft tokens can generalize to problems qualitatively harder than the training distribution — the 'reasoning at the edge of learnability' question.

current_projects: Recurrent-depth / latent reasoning architectures for test-time compute scaling; AI safety evaluation benchmarks (NESSiE); AI co-scientist systems with RL rubric rewards; LLM watermarking and AI oversight
personal_site_summary: ML researcher at ELLIS/MPI Tübingen focused on safety, efficiency, and reasoning. Site highlights recurrent-depth latent reasoning, LLM watermarking, adversarial attacks, and efficient training. H-index ~34, 8000+ citations.
notable_quotes: Can we reason well without sacrificing safety, and do computational constraints affect safety guarantees?
Great models think alike — and this undermines AI oversight (paper title, 2025)
industry_connections: Close ties to Tom Goldstein's group (UMD); collaborates with LLNL (Bartoldson, Kailkhura); academic-only so far, no disclosed startup or advisory roles
commercialization_signal: MEDIUM — Binoculars AI detection has clear product path (EU AI Act compliance). Watermarking becoming regulatory requirement. Latent reasoning positions him for inference optimization startups.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: He figures out how to make AI reason smarter at runtime without using more training data, and also how to catch AI-generated content
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your recurrent-depth latent reasoning work sidesteps chain-of-thought tokens entirely — do you see this converging with diffusion-based reasoning approaches, or are they solving fundamentally different problems?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Test-time compute scaling is a direct lever on inference infrastructure investment; his watermarking work is foundational for AI content provenance products
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to inference infrastructure companies (Together AI, Modal, Baseten) who want to implement recurrent-depth serving; potential co-investment in any spinout around LLM watermarking or safety eval
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Don't ask him only about data poisoning — he's moved on and is focused on test-time reasoning; he may find it reductive

controversial_take: Model similarity across frontier LLMs creates correlated failures that fundamentally break current AI oversight approaches — you can't use one frontier model to oversee another
open_problem: Can latent recurrent reasoning be made as interpretable and steerable as chain-of-thought while maintaining its efficiency advantages?

commercialization_signal: MEDIUM — Amazon Scholar role is the clearest signal; symmetry discovery (AtlasD) and equation discovery (KeplerAgent) have direct drug discovery / materials science applications; no disclosed startup

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: She teaches AI to understand and obey the laws of physics so that predictions about weather, molecules, and physical systems are actually trustworthy
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your AtlasD work on automatic symmetry discovery is striking — do you think symmetry is the key abstraction that will let physics-informed models generalize out of distribution, or is it just one piece?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Core to the science AI / physical simulation thesis: her work on physics-grounded LLMs, formal proofs, and agentic scientific reasoning is exactly what frontier science AI companies need
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to life sciences and climate tech companies building AI-accelerated discovery pipelines; potential advisory role for portfolio companies in scientific ML
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Don't reduce her work to 'better weather forecasting' — she's moved into foundational questions about how AI can do real science, not just prediction

controversial_take: LLMs without physical grounding are fundamentally unreliable for scientific applications — scale alone will not solve the physics consistency problem
open_problem: Can AI agents autonomously discover novel physical laws from raw observational data the way Kepler did — without being given the functional form of the law?

name: Tao Yu
title: Assistant Professor of Computer Science
lab: XLANG Lab, HKU NLP Group
research_focus: Embodied AI agents interacting with digital and physical environments through natural language. Defining work: Spider family of text-to-SQL benchmarks and OSWorld (computer-use agent benchmark used internally by OpenAI and Anthropic). Current focus: computer-use agents that perceive screens and take actions, enterprise data science coding agents (Spider 2.0), and grounding LLMs in robotics.
direction_of_travel: Yale PhD (Dragomir Radev) in semantic parsing → UW postdoc (Noah Smith, Zettlemoyer) → HKU Assistant Professor (2022). Text-to-SQL (Spider 2018) → language agents with code execution (Lemur, OpenAgents) → computer use and GUI agents (OSWorld, OpenCUA) → multimodal agentic systems for data science and robotics.
key_contributions: Spider (EMNLP 2018): large-scale text-to-SQL benchmark across 200 databases — became the standard NL-to-SQL dataset (2500+ citations)
OSWorld (NeurIPS 2024 Spotlight): benchmark for multimodal computer-use agents on 369 tasks — used by OpenAI and Anthropic internally
Spider 2.0 (ICLR 2025 Oral): enterprise text-to-SQL at the complexity of real data science workflows

OpenCUA (NeurIPS 2025 Spotlight): open foundations for computer-use agents

notable_quotes: Democratize access to data science capabilities and make complex digital tools more accessible through natural language interfaces.
industry_connections: Google Research Scholar Award 2023; Amazon Research Award 2022. OSWorld used internally by OpenAI and Anthropic. OpenAgents ~4k GitHub stars. Key benchmark provider for the industry.
commercialization_signal: LOW — no startup. But XLANG Lab output consistently becomes industry infrastructure. Text2Reward applicable to robotics RL pipelines. Computer-use agent space rapidly commercializing.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: He builds the tests that measure whether AI can use a computer like a human — clicking buttons, writing SQL queries, and completing real work tasks.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: OSWorld shows the best AI at only 12% vs humans at 72% — do you think the bottleneck is perception (seeing the screen correctly) or reasoning (deciding what to click)?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Text2Reward is the direct connection to the formal verification/RL thesis — automatically generating formally grounded reward functions for RL. OSWorld and Spider 2.0 represent infrastructure for testing AI agents in verifiable environments.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to enterprise software companies as early customers for data science AI agents; bridge to US-based VC ecosystem (HKU is somewhat isolated from Silicon Valley deal flow).
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Don't lead with the original Spider benchmark — he's moved well past text-to-SQL.

controversial_take: Most computer-use AI demos are cherry-picked — OSWorld numbers (12% success rate) represent the actual state of the art, and the gap to human performance is fundamental, not incremental.
open_problem: Can a unified agent handle both precise digital-world tasks (SQL, coding) and physical-world tasks (robotics) through the same language grounding, or do they require fundamentally different representations?

name: Nisha Chandramoorthy
title: Assistant Professor
lab: Committee on Computational and Applied Mathematics (CAM), Department of Statistics
research_focus: Chandramoorthy works at the intersection of dynamical systems theory, ergodic theory, and machine learning, with a focus on rigorous computational methods for high-dimensional complex systems. Her recent work analyzes generative models (score-based and flow-matching) as dynamical systems, using Lyapunov analysis and linear perturbation theory to derive robustness guarantees and dimension-reduction principles. She also develops score-based transport methods for Bayesian inference and sampling from non-Gaussian posteriors.
direction_of_travel: PhD at MIT on sensitivity analysis of chaotic fluid dynamics (with Qiqi Wang) → postdoc at MIT IDSS bridging dynamical systems with ML theory (with Jegelka and Marzouk) → Georgia Tech as Edenfield Early Career Professor → UChicago (2024) where she pivoted toward the mathematics of generative modeling, applying Lyapunov/perturbation tools from chaos theory to score-based diffusion and flow-matching models.
key_contributions: Space-Split Sensitivity (S3) algorithm: a well-conditioned, ergodic-averaging realization of Ruelle's formula for computing linear response in chaotic dynamical systems
Score Operator Newton Transport: an infinite-dimensional Newton method using score functions to construct transport maps for Bayesian inference, with convergence guarantees

Robustness-of-support theory for generative models: dynamical systems analysis showing that top Lyapunov vector alignment with the data manifold tangent space causes learned generative models to preserve the support of the data distribution despite score-function errors
Generalization analysis for non-converging learning algorithms (NeurIPS 2022, with Jegelka et al.)

top_papers.title: A computable realization of Ruelle's formula for linear response of statistics in chaotic systems
top_papers.one_liner: Introduces the S3 algorithm for efficiently computing parametric sensitivities of time-averaged statistics in chaotic systems, making linear response tractable.

top_papers.title: On the generalization of learning algorithms that do not converge
top_papers.one_liner: NeurIPS 2022 — provides generalization bounds for learning algorithms that oscillate rather than converge, connecting ergodic theory to ML generalization.

top_papers.title: Score Operator Newton transport
top_papers.one_liner: AISTATS 2024 — constructs transport maps via an infinite-dimensional Newton method using score functions, enabling fast convergence sampling without mode collapse.

top_papers.title: When and how can inexact generative models still sample from the data manifold?
top_papers.one_liner: Proves that Lyapunov vector alignment with the data manifold tangent bundle explains robustness of generative model support despite score-function learning errors.

top_papers.title: Efficient computation of linear response of chaotic attractors with one-dimensional unstable manifolds
top_papers.one_liner: SIAM Journal on Applied Dynamical Systems — practical algorithm for sensitivity of ergodic averages in chaotic systems with low-dimensional unstable manifolds.

recent_arxiv.title: When and how can inexact generative models still sample from the data manifold?
recent_arxiv.summary: Uses dynamical systems / Lyapunov analysis to show that imperfect score functions cause density errors confined to the data manifold, providing robustness guarantees for flow-matching and score-based models.

recent_arxiv.title: When are dynamical systems learned from time series data statistically accurate?
recent_arxiv.summary: Shows that standard test-error generalization fails to guarantee physical accuracy (correct Lyapunov exponents, statistics) for learned Neural ODEs on chaotic systems; proves Jacobian-informed training improves statistical fidelity.

current_projects: Mathematics of generative modeling and sampling (robustness, dimension reduction, and rare-event sampling in score/flow models); computing with chaotic dynamics (S3 sensitivity algorithm, Bayesian filtering for chaotic systems); computational measure transport for Bayesian posteriors using score-based methods; dynamics of learning (neural network training as a dynamical system).
personal_site_summary: Clean academic site listing four research themes (generative modeling math, chaotic dynamics computing, measure transport, dynamics of learning) with links to papers and GitHub. Affiliations: CAM and Statistics at UChicago. No dedicated lab branding.
notable_quotes: In my most nihilistic moments, I wonder why one should have an online presence. (2013 tweet — suggesting a preference for depth over visibility)
We take a dynamical systems approach... the alignment of the top Lyapunov vectors with the tangent spaces along the boundary of the data manifold leads to robustness. (2025 paper abstract)
industry_connections: No known startup founding or direct industry partnerships. Prior postdoc at MIT IDSS (Youssef Marzouk's group) has adjacency to scientific computing industry. Climate science applications of her chaos/sensitivity work could attract national lab or energy-sector interest. Primarily a theory-focused academic at this stage.
commercialization_signal: LOW — Deep theory focus on mathematical foundations; no disclosed industry affiliations, consulting, or spinout activity. Applications in climate/geoscience and Bayesian inference could attract long-horizon interest but no near-term commercialization pathway evident.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: She figures out why AI image generators still produce realistic-looking images even when they've learned the rules imperfectly — using tools borrowed from the mathematics of chaotic weather systems.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your Lyapunov-vector robustness result essentially says diffusion models are 'forgiving' of score errors in a structured way — do you think that same structure could be exploited to intentionally guide generation toward rare or out-of-distribution data points?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Her robustness-of-support theory provides a mathematical foundation for understanding when and why diffusion and flow-matching models are reliable, which directly informs the trustworthiness of any generative model in production. Her Score Operator Newton Transport also offers a faster alternative sampling path that could reduce inference compute costs.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to applied ML teams at climate-tech or scientific computing companies (national labs, climate startups) that need principled uncertainty quantification; context on how her theory work could map to evaluation benchmarks for generative model reliability.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Asking about immediate productization — her work is foundational math and she is early-career; pushing commercial framing too hard will read as tone-deaf.

controversial_take: Most practitioners dismiss chaos theory as irrelevant to deep learning — but Chandramoorthy's Lyapunov-vector analysis suggests chaotic-system intuitions may be exactly the right framework for understanding why generative models fail or succeed, flipping the usual narrative that 'dynamics don't matter once you have enough data.'
open_problem: Can the Lyapunov alignment condition for generative model robustness be made computationally tractable for high-dimensional image distributions, turning it from a theoretical sufficient condition into a practical diagnostic or training objective?

name: Atsushi Nitanda
title: Principal Scientist (A*STAR CFAR); Associate Professor (NTU CCDS)
lab: Centre for Frontier AI Research (CFAR), A*STAR Singapore
research_focus: Nitanda works on the mathematical foundations of machine learning, focusing on why and how deep/mean-field neural networks converge during training. His primary contribution is the rigorous analysis of mean-field Langevin dynamics (MFLD) — showing global convergence via convex analysis in the space of probability measures, and characterizing the finite-particle approximation error via propagation-of-chaos (PoC) bounds. More recently he connects MFLD convergence theory to sampling methods and generative model training.
direction_of_travel: Industry researcher at NTT DATA-MSI (Japan) → PhD at University of Tokyo (advisor: Taiji Suzuki, 2018) → Assistant Professor at University of Tokyo → Associate Professor at Kyushu Institute of Technology → Principal Scientist at A*STAR CFAR + Associate Professor at NTU (Singapore, ~2022–present). Career arc is Japan-based industry/academia → theoretical ML foundations → Singapore research hub bridging theory and applications.
key_contributions: Particle Dual Averaging (PDA): first polynomial-time convergence guarantee for mean-field two-layer neural network optimization (NeurIPS 2021)
Convex analysis framework for Mean-Field Langevin Dynamics (AISTATS 2022): proved global convergence using proximal Gibbs distribution and entropy-regularized convex functionals in measure space
Uniform-in-time propagation of chaos for MFLD: showed finite-particle + discrete-time + stochastic-gradient errors remain bounded uniformly in time (NeurIPS 2023 Spotlight, ICLR 2023)
Optimal SGD rates under Neural Tangent Kernel regime (ICLR 2021 Outstanding Paper Award)
Improved PoC bound removing exponential dependence on regularization coefficient via refined log-Sobolev inequality (ICML 2025)

top_papers.title: Optimal Rates for Averaged Stochastic Gradient Descent under Neural Tangent Kernel Regime
top_papers.one_liner: ICLR 2021 Outstanding Paper — proves minimax optimal convergence rates for averaged SGD on neural networks in the NTK regime.

top_papers.title: Particle Dual Averaging: Optimization of Mean Field Neural Networks with Global Convergence Rate Analysis
top_papers.one_liner: NeurIPS 2021 — introduces PDA algorithm and provides the first polynomial-time global convergence guarantee for mean-field (two-layer) neural network optimization.

top_papers.title: Convex Analysis of the Mean Field Langevin Dynamics
top_papers.one_liner: AISTATS 2022 — establishes that MFLD minimizes an entropy-regularized convex functional on measure space, providing a clean convex-optimization interpretation of noisy gradient descent for two-layer networks.

top_papers.title: Convergence of mean-field Langevin dynamics: Time and space discretization, stochastic gradient, and variance reduction
top_papers.one_liner: NeurIPS 2023 Spotlight — unified framework proving uniform-in-time PoC for MFLD under finite particles, time discretization, and stochastic gradients simultaneously.

top_papers.title: Propagation of Chaos for Mean-Field Langevin Dynamics and its Application to Model Ensemble
top_papers.one_liner: ICML 2025 — removes exponential dependence on regularization coefficient in PoC bounds for MFLD via refined log-Sobolev inequality; proposes theoretically grounded model ensemble strategy.

recent_arxiv.title: Propagation of Chaos for Mean-Field Langevin Dynamics and its Application to Model Ensemble
recent_arxiv.summary: Refines the defective log-Sobolev inequality for MFLD under neural network training, yielding improved propagation-of-chaos bounds that remove exponential scaling in the regularization coefficient, and proposes a model ensemble strategy with theoretical guarantees.

recent_arxiv.title: Improved Particle Approximation Error for Mean Field Neural Networks
recent_arxiv.summary: Improves dependence on log-Sobolev inequality constants in particle approximation errors for MFLD, addressing the exponential deterioration problem at large regularization coefficients.

current_projects: Propagation of chaos for mean-field Langevin dynamics with tighter log-Sobolev constants; uniform-in-N log-Sobolev inequalities for MFLD with convex energy (SIMA 2026 with Chewi and Zhang); connections between MFLD convergence theory and diffusion/generative model training; sampling methods grounded in mean-field optimization theory; machine learning foundations at A*STAR CFAR with 14 NeurIPS 2025 and 18 ICLR 2025 papers from the group.
personal_site_summary: Minimalist Google Sites page listing current positions at A*STAR CFAR and NTU, research interests, publications organized by venue, student/intern openings, and contact info. No social media links. Maintained regularly with recent NeurIPS/ICLR/ICML papers.
notable_quotes: Our aim is to understand why machine and deep learning work well through the analysis of optimisation dynamics. (A*STAR CFAR profile)
industry_connections: Joint appointment at A*STAR (Singapore's national applied research agency) provides an explicit bridge to industry and government-funded applied AI. NTU CCDS is a strongly industry-linked institution. No known startup founding, but A*STAR's mission is technology transfer; Nitanda supervises research scientists and engineers (not just PhD students), suggesting applied project activity. Prior role at NTT DATA-MSI as an industry researcher.
commercialization_signal: MEDIUM — A*STAR affiliation means applied research with government/industry co-funding is part of the job. His theory on MFLD convergence is directly relevant to training stability and efficiency for large generative models, which has clear enterprise value, but he has not publicly founded or advised any startup.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: He proves mathematically why training neural networks with added noise actually works — specifically why the noisy particles representing the network's weights all converge to the right answer even when you can only track a finite number of them.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your propagation-of-chaos results characterize when finite-particle MFLD approximates the mean-field limit — do you think those same PoC bounds could be used to set principled hyperparameter schedules (noise level, number of particles/ensemble size) for diffusion model training in practice?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Mean-field Langevin dynamics is the theoretical backbone of why noisy-gradient descent trains generative models successfully. Nitanda's convergence guarantees and PoC bounds are foundational to understanding training stability and sample efficiency for diffusion models — directly relevant to the efficiency and reliability of next-generation generative AI systems.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Context on how A*STAR's technology transfer framework could connect his theory to generative AI product teams in Singapore's AI ecosystem; introductions to ML infrastructure companies that would benefit from principled training-stability analysis.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Superficial 'does this make diffusion models faster' questions — his work is rigorous theory; ask about the math first, applications second.

controversial_take: The ML community mostly treats mean-field theory as a curiosity, but Nitanda's work suggests that the convergence behavior of the entire generative AI training stack is best understood through Wasserstein gradient flows on measure space — implying that standard Adam/SGD intuitions may be fundamentally incomplete for large overparameterized networks.
open_problem: Can the uniform-in-time PoC results for MFLD be extended to the multilayer (deep network) case, or is the two-layer structure essential? Closing this gap would connect the mean-field theory to practical architectures used in production diffusion models.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: She figures out how to reconstruct clear 3D images and medical scans from incomplete or noisy measurements — using AI models as intelligent guesses.

before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Avoid asking about video games or LLMs — her work is firmly in the imaging/sensing domain.

controversial_take: NeRF and Gaussian splatting are just fancy signal processing — the right priors from classical compressed sensing theory beat learned priors in most practical low-data medical settings.
open_problem: How to train diffusion-based inverse problem solvers that remain reliable under distribution shift between training and deployment (e.g., different scanner manufacturers).

controversial_take: Weak-to-strong generalization is not magic—it works because the strong model already knows the right answers latently, and alignment is really a retrieval problem, not a value-learning problem.
