commercialization_signal: Moderate-high—Virtue AI commercializes trustworthy AI research; evaluation frameworks have direct commercial application for model developers needing compliance and safety verification; AI policy influence creates regulatory moat

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Sanmi is Stanford's AI evaluation expert—he's shown that most benchmark improvements are statistical noise, built a company around trustworthy AI, and has been advising the White House on AI capability measurement.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your benchmark variance work shows that most reported model improvements are within noise—how should the post-training community adapt its evaluation methodology to distinguish genuine capability improvements from overfitting to evaluation sets?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Relevant to LLM post-training: his evaluation science work is critical for understanding whether post-training actually improves capabilities or just improves benchmark scores; Virtue AI sits at the commercial intersection of evaluation and safety.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to AI governance investors or regulatory-focused LPs; perspective on the commercial market for AI evaluation tools; Virtue AI funding or partnership discussions
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Avoid touching on any criticism of Black in AI's organizational decisions—he's very protective of that community

controversial_take: The entire LLM leaderboard ecosystem is scientifically invalid—benchmark contamination and variance are so severe that we cannot currently make reliable claims about which post-training methods actually work, and the field needs to start over with proper evaluation methodology.
open_problem: Can we design evaluation protocols for LLM post-training that are simultaneously contamination-resistant, low-variance, and practically usable at the speed of modern research iterations?

name: Deyao Zhu
title: Research Scientist
lab: ByteDance Seed Research
research_focus: Multimodal large language models and reinforcement learning for decision-making, with emphasis on vision-language understanding, planning via LLMs, and AI systems that accumulate knowledge continuously. Created MiniGPT-4 and its successors bridging vision encoders with advanced LLMs.
direction_of_travel: Tongji University (Mechatronics) → Leibniz Hannover MSc (EE) + MPI for Intelligent Systems → KAUST PhD (Mohamed Elhoseiny, vision-language) → ByteDance Seed Research Scientist. Clear trajectory: representation learning → multimodal LLMs → RL for decision-making and self-improving agents.
key_contributions: MiniGPT-4: pioneered aligning frozen visual encoders with Vicuna LLM via single projection layer (2023, 5900+ citations)
MiniGPT-v2: unified interface for vision-language multi-task learning
Goldfish + MiniGPT4-Video: long video understanding with retrieval-augmented video description
Value Memory Graph: graph-structured world model for offline RL (ICLR 2023)
HalentNet: multimodal trajectory forecasting with hallucinative intents (ICLR 2021 Oral)

top_papers.title: MiniGPT-4: Enhancing Vision-Language Understanding with Advanced Large Language Models
top_papers.one_liner: Showed that aligning a frozen visual encoder to Vicuna via a single projection layer reproduces GPT-4-level vision-language capabilities at minimal cost.

top_papers.title: Value Memory Graph: A Graph-Structured World Model for Offline Reinforcement Learning
top_papers.one_liner: Graph world model enabling offline RL to generalize across states by storing and retrieving value estimates via graph structure.

top_papers.title: HalentNet: Multimodal Trajectory Forecasting with Hallucinative Intents
top_papers.one_liner: ICLR 2021 oral presenting multimodal intent generation for diverse and realistic trajectory prediction.

recent_arxiv.title: MiniGPT4-Video: Advancing Multimodal LLMs for Video Understanding
recent_arxiv.summary: Extends MiniGPT-4 to video via clip-level description generation and retrieval, enabling long video comprehension.

current_projects: Research Scientist at ByteDance Seed focusing on RL and vision-language models; co-organizer of RSI ICLR 2026 workshop; working on AI systems for decision-making and self-improvement.
personal_site_summary: Research Scientist at ByteDance Seed. KAUST PhD alumni. Created MiniGPT-4 (5900+ citations). Interests in multimodal LLMs, RL for decision-making, and AI self-improvement. At ByteDance working on next-generation multimodal systems.
industry_connections: ByteDance Seed (major Chinese tech company with global AI research arm). MiniGPT-4's open-source release and citations suggest widespread adoption in both research and industry.
commercialization_signal: MiniGPT-4's lightweight architecture is directly deployable in commercial vision-language applications. ByteDance connection means direct path to integration in TikTok/Douyin product ecosystem. RL for decision-making maps to recommendation and autonomous agent products.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: ByteDance researcher who built MiniGPT-4—one of the most cited papers showing you can get GPT-4-level vision-language understanding cheaply by connecting a frozen image encoder to an open-source LLM. Now working on AI decision-making and self-improvement.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: MiniGPT-4's projection-layer approach was elegant but limited in compositional reasoning—in your current work at ByteDance on RL and self-improvement, are you finding that better visual grounding fundamentally changes the sample efficiency of the RL loop?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: RSI connection: his interest in AI systems that 'accumulate knowledge continuously' is the operational definition of recursive self-improvement in the multimodal domain.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Perspective on Western enterprise demand for multimodal decision-making systems; connections to robotics and autonomous agents investors building on open-source vision-language stacks.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Do not ask about MiniGPT-Med or medical applications—that's a side project from KAUST days.

controversial_take: The race to build larger vision encoders is misguided—MiniGPT-4 proved that alignment between modalities matters more than encoder scale. The field will relearn this lesson when 100B parameter vision models still can't count objects.
open_problem: How do you design RL curricula for multimodal agents that prevent the policy from exploiting language shortcuts instead of learning genuine visual reasoning?

name: Francesco Croce
title: Assistant Professor (Tenure-Track) / PS Fellow / PI
lab: ELLIS Institute Finland / Aalto CS
research_focus: Adversarial robustness of vision and multimodal foundation models, including jailbreaks, backdoors, and systematic evaluation. Creator of AutoAttack (most reliable adversarial attack ensemble) and RobustBench (standardized robustness benchmark). Expanding from image classifiers to CLIP, LLaVA, and safety-aligned LLMs.
direction_of_travel: Torino BSc/MSc Mathematics → Tübingen PhD (Matthias Hein) → EPFL postdoc (Flammarion) → DeepMind internship → Aalto Assistant Professor + ELLIS PI. DAGM dissertation award 2024. Moving from image classifier robustness → multimodal model robustness → LLM jailbreaking and safety.
key_contributions: AutoAttack: parameter-free adversarial attack ensemble that is the standard for reliable robustness evaluation (ICML 2020, 5th most influential paper)
RobustBench: standardized adversarial robustness leaderboard with 120+ models (NeurIPS 2021 Benchmarks Track)
FAB Attack: minimally distorted adversarial examples via adaptive boundary (ICML 2020)
1st place: Find the Trojan backdoor detection competition at SaTML 2024
DAGM MVTec Dissertation Award 2024 + Wilhelm Schickard Dissertation Award 2024

top_papers.title: Reliable Evaluation of Adversarial Robustness with an Ensemble of Diverse and Parameter-Free Attacks (AutoAttack)
top_papers.one_liner: Combines four complementary attacks (APGD-CE, APGD-T, FAB, Square) into a parameter-free ensemble—the de facto standard for robust evaluation.

top_papers.title: RobustBench: A Standardized Adversarial Robustness Benchmark
top_papers.one_liner: Standardized leaderboard tracking progress on adversarial robustness using AutoAttack across 120+ models in ℓ∞ and ℓ2 threat models.

top_papers.title: Minimally Distorted Adversarial Examples with a Fast Adaptive Boundary Attack (FAB)
top_papers.one_liner: Finds adversarial examples closest to the decision boundary by adapting the attack direction via first-order methods.

recent_arxiv.title: Jailbreaking Safety-Aligned LLMs via Adversarial Prompt Optimization
recent_arxiv.summary: ICLR 2025: applies AutoAttack-style systematic adversarial evaluation methodology to safety-aligned LLMs, exposing systematic jailbreak vulnerabilities.

recent_arxiv.title: On the Adversarial Robustness of Instruction-Following Models
recent_arxiv.summary: ICLR 2025: systematic evaluation of adversarial robustness in instruction-tuned models, revealing consistent failure modes.

current_projects: Assistant Professor at Aalto + ELLIS PI; extending AutoAttack/RobustBench methodology to multimodal and instruction-following models; co-organizer TTU ICLR 2026; working on robustness of test-time adaptation methods.
personal_site_summary: Assistant Professor at Aalto, PI at ELLIS Finland. Tübingen PhD (Hein), EPFL postdoc (Flammarion). Creator of AutoAttack and RobustBench. DAGM + Schickard dissertation awards 2024. Now extending robustness evaluation to multimodal models and LLMs.
industry_connections: DeepMind internship (Sven Gowal's team). ELLIS Institute connects to major European AI industry ecosystem. AutoAttack/RobustBench are used by every major ML company for safety evaluations.
commercialization_signal: AutoAttack and RobustBench are de facto industry standards for adversarial robustness evaluation—every AI safety company and large ML lab uses them. Extension to LLM jailbreaking maps to growing AI red-teaming market. His backdoor detection work won at SaTML 2024—directly relevant to enterprise AI security.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Aalto professor who built the most widely used tools for testing whether AI vision models are truly robust—AutoAttack and RobustBench. Now applying that same rigorous evaluation methodology to multimodal models and language models, finding systematic jailbreak vulnerabilities.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: RobustBench was so influential partly because it prevented evaluation hacking—how do you port that standardization to LLM jailbreaking where the attack and defense landscape is much more heterogeneous and the success metric is ambiguous?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Connects to TTU workshop: test-time updates can be adversarially manipulated—his ICML 2022 paper with Shelhamer specifically evaluated adversarial robustness of TTA methods, making him the natural bridge between robustness and adaptation.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Connections to AI security startups building on AutoAttack methodology; perspective on enterprise demand for LLM red-teaming products.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Don't ask about pure image robustness benchmarks—he's moved to multimodal and LLM robustness and is eager to establish himself in that space.

controversial_take: The AI safety community has largely ignored adversarial robustness in favor of alignment—but an aligned model that can be jailbroken in 3 gradient steps is not safe, and the two problems must be solved jointly.
open_problem: Is there a fundamental tension between test-time adaptation (which requires the model to change at deployment) and adversarial robustness (which requires the model to be stable under adversarial perturbations)? Can both properties be certified simultaneously?

name: Paris Giampouras
title: Assistant Professor of Machine Learning and AI; Part-time Research Scientist, FAIR
lab: Foundations of AI and Machine Learning (FAM), University of Warwick
research_focus: Giampouras works at the intersection of optimization, probabilistic inference, and representation learning, with a focus on building robust and efficient learning systems. Recent work spans adaptive optimizers for foundation models, federated learning via generalized variational inference, overparameterized recovery methods, and generative modeling for inverse problems. He has increasing interest in AI safety, aligned diffusion models, and agentic AI systems.
direction_of_travel: Moved from pure optimization theory and signal processing (Johns Hopkins) toward ML foundations with a generative modeling and alignment angle. The Meta FAIR part-time role signals growing industry interest; the ReALM-GEN co-organization marks a deliberate pivot into constrained and preference-aligned generative modeling.
key_contributions: Federated Generalized Variational Inference framework (ICML 2025 Spotlight)
Implicit bias of projected subgradient for robust subspace recovery (ICLR 2022)
Ideal Continual Learner — catastrophic-forgetting-free agent (ICML 2023)

top_papers.title: Federated Generalised Variational Inference: A Robust Probabilistic Federated Learning Framework
top_papers.one_liner: Principled probabilistic framework for federated learning robust to data heterogeneity; ICML 2025 Spotlight.

top_papers.title: The Ideal Continual Learner: An Agent That Never Forgets
top_papers.one_liner: Continual learning formulation with provable no-forgetting guarantees; ICML 2023.

top_papers.title: Implicit Bias of Projected Subgradient Method Gives Provable Robust Recovery of Subspaces of Unknown Codimension
top_papers.one_liner: Shows implicit bias in optimization leads to robust subspace recovery; ICLR 2022.

recent_arxiv.title: Guarantees of a Preconditioned Subgradient Algorithm for Overparameterized Asymmetric Low-Rank Matrix Recovery
recent_arxiv.summary: Provable convergence guarantees for preconditioned subgradient methods in overparameterized asymmetric matrix recovery; ICML 2025.

current_projects: Co-organizing ReALM-GEN at ICLR 2026; area chair for ICML 2026 and ICLR 2026; research on constrained and preference-aligned diffusion/flow models; co-running Foundations of AI Seminar (FAIS) series at Warwick.
personal_site_summary: Clean academic site listing papers, talks, and teaching. Highlights optimization-to-generative-modeling research arc and the dual Warwick/Meta FAIR position. Open to PhD students and collaborators.
industry_connections: Part-time Research Scientist at Meta AI (FAIR); Affiliated Researcher at Archimedes AI Research Center (Greece). Dual academic/industry footprint is notable for someone at assistant professor level.
commercialization_signal: Low-to-moderate. Research is mostly theoretical/methodological but the Meta FAIR affiliation and AI-alignment interest suggest readiness to transfer methods into product-facing settings.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Paris is an assistant professor at Warwick who moonlights at Meta's fundamental AI research lab. He co-organized this workshop on making diffusion models respect real-world constraints—think safety guardrails and user-preference alignment for generative AI.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your federated GVI paper treats local posteriors as components of a mixture—do you see that probabilistic scaffolding carrying over to multi-agent or distributed reward alignment for diffusion models?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Direct connection to preference-aligned and constrained diffusion/flow models; the workshop he co-organized is precisely on that thesis pillar.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to applied AI-alignment companies building on top of diffusion foundations; access to portfolio companies exploring federated ML for sensitive data domains.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Don't ask him to compare the Meta FAIR role vs. pure academic work — it can read as probing a sensitive dual-employment arrangement.

controversial_take: Federated learning's probabilistic framing will outlast current privacy-focused narratives and become the default paradigm for fine-tuning foundation models in regulated industries — but only once the community stops treating it as a communication-efficiency problem.
open_problem: How do you impose hard constraint satisfaction (not just soft penalization) in continuous diffusion reverse processes without collapsing sample diversity?

name: Hilde Kuehne
affiliation: University of Tübingen / MIT-IBM Watson AI Lab
title: Professor for Multimodal Learning
lab: CVML Lab, Tübingen AI Center
research_focus: Video understanding and multimodal learning, including self-supervised and weakly supervised learning from large untrimmed video corpora. Developing large-scale multimodal foundation models integrating text, image, video, and audio. Known for creating influential benchmark datasets.
direction_of_travel: Promoted to full professor at Tübingen AI Center in August 2024. Transitioning from action recognition benchmarking toward building multimodal foundation models. Increasingly involved in connecting memory architectures with video understanding.
key_contributions: HMDB51 dataset (Human Motion Database): most-cited large-scale action recognition benchmark, won ICCV 2021 Helmholtz Prize and PAMI Mark Everingham Prize 2022
Pioneering work on learning video representations without labels
General Chair for ICCV 2025
Board member, Women in Computer Vision Initiative
9,800+ Google Scholar citations

top_papers.title: HMDB: A large video database for human motion recognition
top_papers.one_liner: Created HMDB51, the canonical action recognition benchmark, winner of ICCV 2021 Helmholtz (Test-of-Time) Prize.

top_papers.title: Weakly Supervised Action Learning with RNN Based Fine-to-Coarse Modeling
top_papers.one_liner: RNN-based method for learning action ordering without frame-level labels.

current_projects: Large-scale multimodal foundation models; token compression for video-language models; VLM explainability; audio-video understanding; NFAM workshop co-organization.
personal_site_summary: Lists research agenda on weakly supervised and multimodal video understanding; highlights HMDB51; lists full academic lineage and community service.
industry_connections: MIT-IBM Watson AI Lab affiliate professor; Tübingen AI Center (Bosch-funded); frequent collaborations with industry via IBM and German AI ecosystem.
commercialization_signal: Medium—Tübingen AI Center has strong industry ties (Bosch, Zeiss); HMDB51 is widely used in commercial CV pipelines.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Built the dataset that most video AI systems were trained on for a decade. Now running a major lab at Tübingen building multimodal foundation models—sitting at the crossroads of industry (IBM, Bosch) and top academic AI.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: With HMDB51's success, what's your view on whether current video foundation models are actually learning temporal memory or just exploiting static frame statistics?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Connecting associative memory theory to video understanding—temporal retrieval in video models is directly analogous to pattern completion in Hopfield networks.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to European deeptech investors active in computer vision (e.g., Earlybird, Lakestar) given her Tübingen/Germany base.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: The police/surveillance applications of action recognition—she focuses on positive uses and may be uncomfortable with security angle.

controversial_take: Believes most video foundation models are fundamentally broken for temporal understanding and will need architectural redesigns, not just more data, to handle long-range temporal dependencies.
open_problem: How to build multimodal memory architectures that efficiently store and retrieve temporal context across hours-long video.

name: Meisam Razaviyayn
title: Andrew and Erna Viterbi Early Career Chair; Associate Professor
lab: ODDS (Optimization for Data-Driven Science) Research Group; USC-Meta Center for Research and Education in AI and Learning
research_focus: Design and analysis of fundamental optimization algorithms for modern AI. Specializes in non-convex min-max optimization (robust learning, GANs), differentially private optimization, fairness-constrained learning, and bilevel optimization for AutoML.
direction_of_travel: From classical convex optimization (Stanford postdoc, Minnesota PhD) into the heart of modern AI training dynamics. The USC-Meta center appointment signals strong industry engagement. Associate director role suggests institutional leadership trajectory.
key_contributions: 2022 NSF CAREER Award; 2021 AFOSR Young Investigator Award; 2021 3M Nontenured Faculty Award
2020 ICCM Best Paper Award in Mathematics
Foundational work on non-convex min-max optimization for adversarial ML
Associate Director, USC-Meta Center for AI Research and Learning
National Academy of Engineering Frontiers of Engineering 2023

top_papers.title: On the Convergence of Gradient Descent for Finding the Riemannian Center of Mass
top_papers.one_liner: Early foundational paper on Riemannian optimization convergence—widely cited in manifold optimization.

top_papers.title: Unified Convergence Analysis of Block Successive Minimization Methods for Nonsmooth Optimization
top_papers.one_liner: Unifies ADMM-style block coordinate methods under a single convergence framework.

current_projects: Non-convex min-max optimization for robust learning; bilevel optimization for AutoML; differentially private training; theoretical understanding of in-context learning.
personal_site_summary: USC faculty page listing ODDS research group, teaching ISE 633 (large-scale optimization for ML), and research on nonconvex, min-max, fair, and private optimization.
industry_connections: USC-Meta Center associate director; Stanford and Google Research faculty visitor; ISE department at USC has strong industry recruiting pipeline.
commercialization_signal: Medium—Meta partnership for AI research; optimization algorithms underlie many deployed systems; no direct startup.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: An optimization expert who's cracking open the math behind training AI that's both powerful and safe. Runs USC's Meta-funded AI center—sits at the intersection of theoretical guarantees and industry-scale deployment.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Associative memory retrieval is essentially a min-max game between the query and stored patterns—are there connections to your non-convex min-max optimization work that could tighten convergence rates for Hopfield dynamics?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Optimization dynamics of associative memory: the energy minimization in Hopfield networks is a non-convex problem—Razaviyayn's tools apply directly.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Meta connection—if interested in partnership or funding, his center is the gateway. Also USC's strong industry-faculty pipeline.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Chinese government AI policy—USC has a large Chinese student base and this can be politically charged in that context.

controversial_take: Believes differential privacy as currently practiced adds so much noise that it's destroying model utility at realistic epsilon values—the field needs fundamentally new privacy definitions, not just better mechanisms.
open_problem: Finding tight iteration complexity bounds for non-convex min-max problems that appear in GAN training and adversarial robustness.

name: Wuyang Chen
title: Assistant Professor (Tenure-Track)
lab: DeLTA Lab, School of Computing Science, SFU
research_focus: Automated, interpretable, and scalable scientific machine learning. Develops foundation models for multiphysics problems, language models for scientific reasoning, and AutoML approaches for neural architecture design. Applications in fluid dynamics, materials science, and PDEs.
direction_of_travel: UT Austin PhD (Atlas Wang) → UC Berkeley postdoc (Mahoney) → SFU faculty. Rapid rise: INNS and iSchools Doctoral Dissertation Awards 2024. Organizes NeurIPS 2024 and ICLR 2026 FM4Science workshops. Building DeLTA Lab as a center for scientific AI.
key_contributions: INNS Doctoral Dissertation Award 2024 and iSchools Doctoral Dissertation Award 2024
Training-free neural architecture search featured as NSF 'Featured Advance in AI' 2022
Organizer of NeurIPS 2024 and ICLR 2026 FM4Science workshops
Nvidia Academic Grant Award; AAAI New Faculty Highlights

top_papers.title: Neural Architecture Search without Training
top_papers.one_liner: Proposes NASWOT—ranking neural architectures without any training using Jacobian kernel properties.

top_papers.title: Automated, Interpretable, and Scalable Scientific Machine Learning
top_papers.one_liner: AAAI 2025 paper framing scientific ML as requiring automation, interpretability, and scalability together.

current_projects: DeLTA Lab research on foundation models for multiphysics; scientific reasoning with language models; FM4Science ICLR 2026 workshop organization.
personal_site_summary: DeLTA Lab site describing research agenda on scientific ML, AutoML, and foundation models for science. Lists dissertation awards and workshop organization roles.
industry_connections: UC Berkeley Mahoney lab alumni network (ICSI, LBNL); NERSC compute resources; Nvidia grant recipient.
commercialization_signal: Medium—training-free NAS has clear commercialization path (reducing AI training costs); foundation models for physics simulations are investable if accurate.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: A new SFU professor building AI that can reason about physics without needing labeled data. He runs the FM4Science workshop—the community hub for AI for science—and just won two major dissertation awards.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your multiphysics foundation model work—are you encoding conservation laws as hard constraints or learning them from data, and have you tested whether the model extrapolates outside the training distribution of physical regimes?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: FM4Science is his workshop—he defines what 'foundation models for science' means as a research agenda. Core to the ICLR 2026 science track.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to Canadian deep tech investors (BDC Capital, MaRS) or NERSC/national lab partnership opportunities.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Comparing SFU to UBC or top US universities—early in his SFU appointment and building identity there.

controversial_take: Believes most current 'AI for science' papers are just applying standard ML to scientific data, and that true scientific AI requires building in domain structure (symmetries, conservation laws) at the architecture level, not as a loss term.
open_problem: Building a single foundation model that generalizes across multiple PDE families (elliptic, parabolic, hyperbolic) without seeing each family at training time.

name: N. Benjamin Erichson
affiliation: Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory / ICSI (UC Berkeley)
title: Senior Research Scientist; Research Group Leader
lab: Robust Deep Learning Group, ICSI; Applied Mathematics and Computational Research Division, LBNL
research_focus: Robust neural network architectures for scientific computing and vision, spanning deep learning for dynamical systems, physics-aware recurrent networks, state space models, and randomized numerical linear algebra. Studies implicit biases in SSMs and spectral methods for time series.
direction_of_travel: UW Applied Math postdoc (Kutz/Brunton) → UC Berkeley Statistics postdoc (Mahoney) → Pitt assistant professor → LBNL/ICSI. Clear trajectory toward bridging randomized linear algebra and scientific ML. Co-organizing FM4Science 2026 reflects his community leadership role.
key_contributions: Powerformer: causal attention reweighting for time series Transformers
Analysis of implicit bias of State Space Models toward low-frequency components
Learning continuous models for continuous physics
Work on untrained neural networks for compressed sensing
Organizer, Berkeley ML and Science Forum (biweekly, with Boehm/Nachman)

top_papers.title: Compressive Sensing with Deep Image Prior and Learned Regularization
top_papers.one_liner: Compressed sensing recovery using untrained deep generative models (Deep Image Prior).

top_papers.title: Randomized Dynamic Mode Decomposition
top_papers.one_liner: Fast DMD algorithm using randomized linear algebra—bridges data-driven dynamics and scientific computing.

current_projects: FM4Science ICLR 2026 workshop; state space models for scientific time series; robust deep learning for physics applications at LBNL.
personal_site_summary: Personal site emphasizing dynamical systems meets deep learning; LBNL and ICSI affiliations; list of randomized algorithms and physics-aware ML papers.
industry_connections: LBNL (DOE national lab); ICSI Berkeley (industry-adjacent); Kutz/Brunton UW network (strong DOE/DARPA funding base); Mahoney Berkeley network.
commercialization_signal: Medium—LBNL-based research with DOE applications (energy, climate simulations). No startup signals currently.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: A LBNL scientist who proves that neural networks have deep mathematical biases (SSMs prefer low frequencies, untrained nets encode image priors) and uses these insights to build better scientific AI systems.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your SSM low-frequency bias finding—does this mean SSMs are structurally better suited for slow climate dynamics than for fast turbulence? And is this why Mamba outperforms on some scientific tasks but not others?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Foundation models for physical science need the right inductive biases—Erichson is identifying what those biases are mathematically, not just empirically.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: DOE LBNL partnership introductions if interested in national lab compute or collaboration on energy/climate ML.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: State space models vs. transformers as a binary choice—he sees them as complementary with different inductive biases.

controversial_take: Believes the SSM renaissance (Mamba, S4) will stall because their low-frequency bias makes them fundamentally ill-suited for turbulence and fast-timescale physics, and transformers with physics-informed attention will win.
open_problem: Characterizing the full class of functions that untrained convolutional networks can efficiently represent as implicit regularization priors.

name: Rui-Jie Zhu
title: PhD Student
lab: Neuromorphic Computing Group (NCG Lab), UC Santa Cruz
research_focus: Rui-Jie Zhu works at the intersection of efficient language models and neuromorphic computing, developing RNN-based and spiking neural network architectures that challenge the dominance of transformers. His research on looped language models (Ouro) and matmul-free language models explores how recurrent computation in latent space enables implicit multi-step reasoning with dramatically lower compute.
direction_of_travel: Rising PhD student whose hardware-efficient architecture work (RWKV, matmul-free LMs, looped LMs) has attracted strong community attention; on track for high-impact faculty or research scientist role after graduation in 2026-2027.
key_contributions: Co-authored RWKV, reinventing RNNs with linear attention for transformer-scale efficiency
Developed matmul-free language models eliminating matrix multiplication for extreme hardware efficiency
Built Ouro, a family of pre-trained Looped Language Models that match 12B-parameter models at 1.4B-2.6B scale

top_papers.title: RWKV: Reinventing RNNs for the Transformer Era
top_papers.one_liner: Hybrid RNN-transformer architecture with linear attention that matches transformer quality at lower inference cost

top_papers.title: Gated Attention Coding for Training High-performance and Efficient Spiking Neural Networks
top_papers.one_liner: Applies gated attention to spiking neural networks, bridging neuromorphic and deep learning efficiency

recent_arxiv.title: A Survey on Latent Reasoning
recent_arxiv.summary: Comprehensive survey of latent reasoning approaches, examining how neural network layers serve as computational substrate for implicit multi-step inference

recent_arxiv.title: Latent Implicit Visual Reasoning
recent_arxiv.summary: Extends implicit reasoning concepts to visual modalities through latent computation

current_projects: Co-organizing LIT workshop at ICLR 2026; developing Ouro looped language model family; survey on latent reasoning; matmul-free model scaling
personal_site_summary: PhD student site at UCSC showcasing hardware-efficient ML architectures, neuromorphic computing, and looped/recurrent language model research with strong open-source community presence via RWKV.
industry_connections: Strong open-source community network through RWKV project (open-source ecosystem); advisor Jason Eshraghian has neuromorphic computing industry ties; NeurIPS and ICLR publication record creates academic network
commercialization_signal: High potential—matmul-free and looped LM work targets edge deployment and inference cost reduction, areas of intense commercial interest; RWKV has active open-source adoption

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Rui-Jie builds language models that don't use standard matrix multiplication or explicit attention, making them much cheaper to run—especially relevant for edge devices and long-context reasoning.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: With Ouro matching 12B-parameter models at 2.6B scale through looping—how does the compute-optimal training point shift compared to standard transformers, and do you think this creates a new scaling law for looped architectures?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Directly relevant to inference-time scaling and self-improvement: looped and matmul-free architectures could reshape the inference cost curve, enabling more test-time compute at lower cost.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to hardware accelerator startups or edge AI companies that need efficient architecture partners; VC perspective on open-source commercialization paths
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Avoid comparing RWKV unfavorably to Mamba—there's community tension between the two architectural camps

controversial_take: The transformer's quadratic attention is not a fundamental limit—it's a historical accident, and recurrent architectures with looped computation will eventually dominate inference-critical deployments.
open_problem: What is the right training curriculum for looped LMs to prevent representation collapse across loops, and how does this interact with the choice of number of loops at test time?

name: Mingchen Zhuge
affiliation: King Abdullah University of Science and Technology (KAUST)
title: PhD Candidate
lab: KAUST AI Initiative (advised by Jürgen Schmidhuber)
research_focus: Recursive self-improvement of AI agents, spanning multi-agent collaborative frameworks, code-generating agents, optimizable agent graphs, and agent evaluation methodologies. Builds systems where agents optimize their own workflows and evaluate each other, targeting human-level software engineering via self-improvement loops.
direction_of_travel: MSc at China University of Geosciences → KAUST PhD (Schmidhuber lab) → strong trajectory toward founding or joining an AI agent startup (runs metauto.ai). Work progresses from MetaGPT (multi-agent SOP) → GPTSwarm (graph optimization) → HGM (recursive self-improvement oral at ICLR 2026).
key_contributions: MetaGPT: multi-agent framework encoding human SOPs for collaborative software development (ICLR 2024 Oral)
GPTSwarm: language agents as optimizable computational graphs enabling swarm-level self-improvement (ICML 2024 Oral)
Agent-as-a-Judge: framework for agents to evaluate other agents, cited in Google Agent Whitebook (ICML 2025)
HGM: recursive self-improvement approach for AI agents (ICLR 2026 Oral)
OpenHands: open-source AI software developer platform (ICLR 2025)

top_papers.title: MetaGPT: Meta Programming for A Multi-Agent Collaborative Framework
top_papers.one_liner: Encodes human SOPs into multi-agent LLM pipelines, enabling collaborative software engineering with role-specialized agents.

top_papers.title: GPTSwarm: Language Agents as Optimizable Graphs
top_papers.one_liner: Represents agent workflows as differentiable graphs and optimizes them end-to-end for improved task performance.

top_papers.title: Agent-as-a-Judge: Evaluate Agents with Agents
top_papers.one_liner: Proposes using LLM agents as evaluators of other agents, cited in Google's foundational agent research documents.

recent_arxiv.title: HGM: Human-Level Generalist Machine via Recursive Self-Improvement
recent_arxiv.summary: ICLR 2026 Oral presenting a recursive self-improvement architecture for achieving human-level generalist AI capabilities.

recent_arxiv.title: VideoAuto-R1: Multimodal Reasoning for Video Understanding
recent_arxiv.summary: CVPR 2026 paper on reinforcement-learning-based multimodal reasoning for video (accepted as CVPR 2026).

current_projects: PhD at KAUST (Schmidhuber); runs metauto.ai exploring autonomous agent systems; organizer of RSI ICLR 2026 workshop; ICLR 2026 and CVPR 2026 papers under review/accepted.
personal_site_summary: KAUST PhD candidate under Jürgen Schmidhuber. 5,000+ citations, 20+ top-tier publications. Two Oral papers (ICLR 2024, ICML 2024). Runs metauto.ai. NeurIPS Ro-FoMo Best Paper. WAIC Future Star nominee.
industry_connections: Runs metauto.ai (early-stage venture signals); Schmidhuber lab has strong connections to IDSIA and European AI ecosystem; prior internships at Alibaba Group, Microsoft, IIAI.
commercialization_signal: metauto.ai is effectively a proto-startup. MetaGPT's 2500+ citations make it foundational to the agent framework ecosystem (competitors: AutoGen, CrewAI). Agent-as-a-Judge cited by Google signals direct industry relevance.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: KAUST PhD student who built MetaGPT (the most cited multi-agent LLM framework) and is now working on AI systems that recursively improve themselves. Has a startup in the space (metauto.ai).
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: In GPTSwarm, agent graphs are optimized end-to-end—what prevents the optimization from collapsing to a degenerate solution where one agent does everything, and how does this connect to your recursive self-improvement framework in HGM?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Central RSI thesis: MetaGPT → GPTSwarm → HGM is a clear progression toward agents that redesign their own architecture—the core of recursive self-improvement.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Pre-seed/seed investor perspective on agent framework startups; introductions to enterprise customers evaluating agentic coding tools (GitHub Copilot Workspace competitors).
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Avoid asking about safety of RSI systems—he's explicitly on the 'accelerate exploration' side of that debate and views safety-first arguments as premature constraints.

controversial_take: The agent framework wars (AutoGen vs. CrewAI vs. MetaGPT) are irrelevant—within 2 years, agents will self-design their own coordination protocols, making current handcrafted frameworks obsolete.
open_problem: How do you prevent recursive self-improvement from optimizing for easily measurable proxies rather than true capability—the inner alignment problem in self-modifying agent systems.

name: Peter Holderrieth
title: PhD Student
lab: CSAIL, MIT, supervised by Tommi Jaakkola
research_focus: Holderrieth works on the mathematical foundations of generative modeling, focusing on flow matching, diffusion models, and their unification through generator-based frameworks. He developed Generator Matching (ICLR 2025 Oral), a modality-agnostic framework that unifies diffusion, flow matching, and discrete diffusion via Markov process generators. He also created the leading tutorial resource for flow matching and diffusion models (MIT 6.S184).
direction_of_travel: Math BSc (Bonn), two Oxford MScs (Statistics + Neuroscience) as Rhodes Scholar, now PhD at MIT CSAIL with Jaakkola. Internship at MetaAI with Yaron Lipman and Ricky Chen (Flow Matching Guide co-author). Fast-rising: ICLR 2025 Oral and ICLR 2026 Oral (GLASS Flows, top 1%) within 2 years of PhD. Strong candidate for junior faculty or senior research scientist at a top lab.
key_contributions: Generator Matching: unifying diffusion, flow matching, and discrete diffusion (ICLR 2025 Oral)
GLASS Flows: inference-time reward alignment for diffusion/flow models (ICLR 2026 Oral, top 1%)
Flow Matching Guide and Code (MetaAI, 2024) — definitive field reference co-authored with Yaron Lipman

top_papers.title: Generator Matching: Generative Modeling with Arbitrary Markov Processes
top_papers.one_liner: Modality-agnostic generative modeling framework unifying diffusion and flow matching via Markov generators; ICLR 2025 Oral.

top_papers.title: GLASS Flows: Transition Sampling for Alignment of Flow and Diffusion Models
top_papers.one_liner: Efficient inference-time reward alignment for flow/diffusion models; ICLR 2026 Oral (top 1%).

top_papers.title: Flow Matching Guide and Code
top_papers.one_liner: Comprehensive review and PyTorch implementation guide for flow matching; MetaAI.

recent_arxiv.title: An Introduction to Flow Matching and Diffusion Models
recent_arxiv.summary: Self-contained textbook-style introduction to flow matching and diffusion models from mathematical first principles, developed from MIT 6.S184.

current_projects: GLASS Flows (ICLR 2026); MIT 6.S184 course on generative AI with SDEs; research on inference-time alignment and reward-guided sampling for flow/diffusion models; discrete generative modeling.
personal_site_summary: Clean personal academic site emphasizing research on generative models and teaching (MIT 6.S184). Strong signal of a researcher who invests in community education.
industry_connections: MetaAI internship (Yaron Lipman, Ricky Chen — key flow matching researchers). Prior internships at Cellarity (biotech) and Genomics plc. BCG consulting background before Oxford. Multi-sector exposure unusual for a PhD student.
commercialization_signal: Moderate. Strong methodology researcher. Industry interest likely post-PhD via research scientist roles. The reward alignment direction (GLASS Flows) has direct application to RLHF-style fine-tuning of generative models.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Peter is the PhD student who wrote the tutorial that most ML researchers use to understand flow matching. He's also made a fundamental contribution proving that diffusion, flow matching, and discrete diffusion are all special cases of a single framework.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Generator Matching covers continuous and discrete Markov processes — is there a natural extension to jump processes with heavy-tailed jump distributions, and what would that buy us for molecule generation over bonds?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Direct core thesis — flow matching foundations, reward alignment for generative models, unification of diffusion paradigms.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Early introductions to research scientist roles at frontier labs post-PhD; connections to AI infrastructure companies building on flow matching.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Don't ask about his non-ML background (politics/German Parliament) in a professional context — it's interesting but can distract from the research conversation.

controversial_take: Discrete diffusion models will displace autoregressive models for structured discrete data (molecules, code) not because they're theoretically superior but because their parallel generation makes fine-tuning and reward alignment dramatically more tractable.
open_problem: Computationally efficient exact inference in Generator Matching for non-Gaussian generators — current approximations break for processes with complex transition kernels.

name: Sékou-Oumar Kaba
title: PhD Candidate
lab: Mila – Quebec AI Institute; McGill University, supervised by Siamak Ravanbakhsh
research_focus: Kaba works on geometric deep learning and generative modeling for molecular and materials science. His core contributions include equivariance via learned canonicalization (an alternative to hard-coded symmetry), symmetry-aware crystal generation with diffusion models, and equivariant adaptation of large pretrained models. He bridges geometric ML theory with AI for materials discovery.
direction_of_travel: Physics background (ULaval, Sherbrooke), then ML pivot to Mila/McGill. Microsoft Research Amsterdam internship, Yoshua Bengio mentorship. PhD with strong publication record at ICML, NeurIPS, ICLR. 610+ citations despite being a PhD student signals high impact. GRaM workshop area chair. Likely to pursue faculty or top-lab research position post-PhD.
key_contributions: Equivariance with Learned Canonicalization Functions (ICML 2023) — flexible alternative to hard-coded equivariance
SymmCD: Symmetry-Preserving Crystal Generation with Diffusion Models (ICLR 2025)
EquiAdapt: library for equivariant adaptation of pretrained neural networks

top_papers.title: Equivariance with Learned Canonicalization Functions
top_papers.one_liner: Learn canonicalization functions to achieve equivariance without hard-coded symmetry architectures; ICML 2023.

top_papers.title: SymmCD: Symmetry-Preserving Crystal Generation with Diffusion Models
top_papers.one_liner: Diffusion model for crystal structure generation that preserves space group symmetries; ICLR 2025.

top_papers.title: Equivariant Adaptation of Large Pre-trained Models
top_papers.one_liner: Adapts pretrained non-equivariant models to be equivariant via canonicalization; NeurIPS 2023.

recent_arxiv.title: Symmetry-Aware Generative Modeling through Learned Canonicalization
recent_arxiv.summary: Extends canonicalization approach to generative modeling, enabling symmetry-aware generation without hard-coded group actions.

recent_arxiv.title: Energy Loss Functions for Physical Systems
recent_arxiv.summary: Loss functions respecting conservation laws for physical system learning; NeurIPS 2025.

current_projects: GRaM Workshop at ICLR 2026; symmetry-aware generative modeling; equivariant adaptation of large models for materials science; Geometric Deep Learning Reading Group at Mila.
personal_site_summary: Clear academic site with publications and research description. Reflects a researcher with strong theoretical foundations in physics and machine learning.
industry_connections: Microsoft Research Amsterdam internship (ML for electronic structure). Mila/Bengio network gives access to Quebec AI ecosystem and ML startups. EquiAdapt library shows open-source tooling interest.
commercialization_signal: Moderate-high for materials and pharma. Crystal generation with symmetry preservation is directly relevant to materials discovery companies (Schrodinger, Recursion, Orbital Materials). Equivariant adaptation of large models could unlock broad industrial applications.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Oumar figured out how to give a neural network equivariance without building it into the architecture — you just learn a canonical representation. He's applying this to crystal structure generation, which is a huge materials discovery problem.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Learned canonicalization avoids hard-coded symmetry but introduces ambiguity when multiple canonical forms are equally valid — how do you handle this multiplicity in the context of crystal generation where the unit cell isn't unique?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Core thesis — geometric DL for physical AI, equivariant generative models for materials. High relevance for AI-for-science investment angle.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to materials discovery companies (Orbital Materials, DeepMind Materials, Recursion); connections to physical AI investors; Mila ecosystem introductions.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Don't treat him as only a 'molecules/materials' researcher — his canonicalization work is a general geometric ML contribution.

controversial_take: The materials discovery field is overselling diffusion models for crystal generation — the real bottleneck is not generative modeling quality but synthesizability prediction and experimental validation. We need tighter ML-experiment loops, not better generators.
open_problem: How to generate crystal structures that are not just geometrically valid but also thermodynamically stable and experimentally synthesizable — current metrics don't capture this.

name: Theo X. Olausson
title: PhD Student, MIT CSAIL
lab: Computer-Assisted Programming Group, MIT CSAIL (Solar-Lezama lab)
research_focus: Neurosymbolic AI and language models — code generation correctness, self-repair limitations, logical reasoning via LLM+prover combinations, and diffusion language models. Bridges the Solar-Lezama program synthesis tradition with modern LLMs. Recent pivot toward diffusion LMs and learned unmasking policies (2025–2026) with Apple Research co-authors.
direction_of_travel: MIT CSAIL → code generation and library learning (LINC, LILO, self-repair) → EMNLP 2023 Outstanding Paper (LINC) → diffusion language models and learned unmasking policies (2025) → MIPS and broader generative model research with Apple/Google co-authors.
key_contributions: LINC: Neurosymbolic logical reasoning combining LLMs with first-order logic provers — EMNLP 2023 Outstanding Paper
Is Self-Repair a Silver Bullet: rigorous analysis showing code LLM self-repair gains are modest (ICLR 2024)
LILO: Learning Interpretable Libraries by Compressing and Documenting Code (ICLR 2024)
The Counterfeit Conundrum: code LLMs fail to detect their own subtle errors (ACL Findings 2024)
Learning Unmasking Policies for Diffusion Language Models (2025)
Tri-Modal Masked Diffusion Models (2026, with Apple/Google co-authors)

top_papers.one_liner: EMNLP 2023 Outstanding Paper: LLM parses language to FOL, prover does the reasoning — beats GPT-4 CoT

top_papers.one_liner: Systematic study showing self-repair gains are often marginal and feedback-quality-dependent — ICLR 2024

top_papers.one_liner: Neurosymbolic library learning with LLM-generated documentation — ICLR 2024

recent_arxiv.summary: Multimodal discrete diffusion on text, image, audio; pretrains 3B-parameter model

current_projects: Pivoting from code-AI/neurosymbolic toward diffusion language models and learned search/retrieval. Apple Research co-authorship signals possible industry transition. Still co-organizing VerifAI 2026.
personal_site_summary: PhD student in Solar-Lezama's group at MIT CSAIL. Recent paper affiliations suggest Apple Research collaboration.
industry_connections: Recent papers co-authored with Apple Research and Google DeepMind researchers. Solar-Lezama lab has strong MIT-industry pipeline.
commercialization_signal: LOW — PhD student. Diffusion LM + RL sampling work foundational for large tech labs.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: He tests whether AI coding and reasoning tools actually work as advertised — and usually finds the gaps the marketing doesn't mention.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your LINC paper showed LLM+FOL-prover beats GPT-4 on logical reasoning — has that held up as models got bigger, or do large models close the gap on formal reasoning without the symbolic component?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: LINC is a direct implementation of the LLM+formal-verifier thesis. His self-repair and counterfeit papers are the rigorous empirical foundation for why formal verification (not just LLM confidence) is needed.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to Apple/Google Research contacts for post-PhD options.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Don't ask about his thesis timeline.

controversial_take: Self-repair in code LLMs is largely theater — performance gains vanish when you account for compute cost and the failure modes are systematic, not random.
open_problem: Can diffusion LMs with learned unmasking policies verify code correctness as part of generation, rather than as a post-hoc step?

name: Emily McMilin
title: Research Scientist
lab: Meta AI Research (Language Modeling / Applied RL team)
research_focus: Causal inference applied to large language models, with emphasis on detecting and exploiting selection bias phenomena (selection collider bias, underspecification) for hallucination detection and reliability evaluation. Also works on offline RL and applied language modeling.
direction_of_travel: Hardware engineer at Meta (2015-18) → Software engineer at Meta (2018-23) → Stanford PhD (2012-16, non-linear path) → Research Scientist at Meta (2023-present). Pivoted from hardware/software engineering to NLP research with a causal inference lens. Speaking at VerifAI suggests growing interest in LLM reliability and verification.
key_contributions: Selection Bias Induced Spurious Correlations in Large Language Models: identified systematic selection biases in LLM inference (ICML 2022 workshop)
Selection Collider Bias in Large Language Models: used causal graph analysis to explain LLM failures (UAI 2022 workshop)
Exploiting Selection Bias for Hallucination Detection: 84% accuracy detecting LLM pronoun hallucinations without labels (NeurIPS 2022 workshop)
Underspecification in Language Modeling Tasks: causality-informed study of gendered pronoun resolution (AAAI 2024)

top_papers.title: Underspecification in Language Modeling Tasks: A Causality-Informed Study of Gendered Pronoun Resolution
top_papers.one_liner: Uses causal framework to show how task underspecification induces latent selection bias causing LLM hallucinations.

top_papers.title: Selection Bias Induced Spurious Correlations in Large Language Models
top_papers.one_liner: Demonstrates that selection bias in LLM training creates spurious correlations exploitable for reliability detection.

recent_arxiv.title: Exploiting Selection Bias on Underspecified Tasks in Large Language Models
recent_arxiv.summary: Shows that selection-bias-induced patterns can be leveraged to detect when LLMs are hallucinating with high accuracy.

current_projects: Research Scientist at Meta working on language modeling, causal inference, and applied RL; speaker at VerifAI-2 ICLR 2026 on LLM reliability and verification.
personal_site_summary: Research Scientist at Meta. Causal inference + NLP researcher focused on understanding and detecting LLM hallucinations via selection bias analysis. Prior career in hardware and software engineering at Meta.
industry_connections: Senior industry position at Meta. Direct access to production LLM systems and scale. Overlap with Meta's LLaMA and applied AI safety teams.
commercialization_signal: Work on hallucination detection without labels is immediately commercializable—any enterprise deploying LLMs needs reliability guarantees. Her causal framework could underpin verification layers in LLM pipelines.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Research scientist at Meta who uses causal inference to detect when language models are hallucinating—without needing labeled examples. She's speaking at the verification workshop because her causal approach gives formal handles on LLM unreliability.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your selection-collider-bias approach detects hallucinations without labels—how does this scale when the task distribution shifts (e.g., from pronouns to factual claims) and the causal graph structure changes?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Connection to formal verification: causal graphical models provide a formal framework for specifying and detecting LLM failure modes, bridging statistics and verification.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Perspective on enterprise demand for hallucination detection; connections to AI reliability startups building on causal approaches.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Avoid dwelling on her hardware engineering background—she's fully pivoted to research.

controversial_take: Hallucination is not a model failure—it's a specification failure. The model is doing exactly what the training distribution implies; we just haven't formally specified what we want.
open_problem: Can causal graphical models be automatically inferred from LLM behavior rather than manually designed, enabling scalable unsupervised reliability audits?

name: Ailing Zeng
title: Technical Staff / Research Lead
lab: Anuttacon (formerly Shanghai AI Laboratory)
research_focus: Human-centric interactive multimodal video generation and whole-body human motion generation. Research spans 3D pose estimation, text/speech/music-driven motion synthesis, and building AI systems that perceive and interact with humans in real-time for long-horizon video generation.
direction_of_travel: CUHK research → Shanghai AI Lab → Anuttacon (interactive video generation startup). Moved from time series analysis (influential AAAI 2023 paper) → 3D human pose/motion generation → interactive multimodal video generation. Joining RSI workshop suggests interest in how generative models improve themselves via world modeling.
key_contributions: Are Transformers Effective for Time Series Forecasting? (AAAI 2023 Most Influential Paper)
SMPLer-X: large-scale expressive whole-body human pose and shape estimation
Motion-X / Motion-X++: large-scale multimodal 3D whole-body human motion dataset
HumanTOMATO: text-aligned whole-body motion generation with hierarchical VQ-VAE
OmniMotion-X: versatile multimodal whole-body human motion generation via autoregressive diffusion transformer

top_papers.title: Are Transformers Effective for Time Series Forecasting?

top_papers.one_liner: Large-scale foundational model for expressive whole-body human pose estimation covering face, hands, and body.

recent_arxiv.summary: ICLR 2026 paper addressing data, architecture, and evaluation challenges for building generalizable human motion generation systems.

recent_arxiv.title: OmniMotion-X: Versatile Multimodal Whole-Body Motion Generation
recent_arxiv.summary: Autoregressive diffusion transformer enabling text-to-motion, music-to-dance, speech-to-gesture in a single framework.

current_projects: Technical staff at Anuttacon building human-centric interactive multimodal video generation; co-organizer of RSI ICLR 2026 workshop; KAUST AI Rising Star 2025; Area Chair CVPR 2025.
personal_site_summary: Technical staff at Anuttacon. Former Shanghai AI Lab researcher. 11,800+ citations. KAUST AI Rising Star 2025. AAAI 2023 Most Influential Paper. Expert in human motion generation and whole-body pose estimation.
industry_connections: At Anuttacon (startup). Former collaborations with Shanghai AI Lab (major Chinese government AI institute). CVPR 2025 Area Chair indicates strong academic-industry bridge.
commercialization_signal: Anuttacon is building commercial interactive video generation products. Motion generation has immediate applications in game development, VR/AR, digital humans, and film production.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Researcher at an interactive video generation startup who wrote the paper that proved simple linear models beat Transformers for time series (major impact). Now builds AI systems that generate realistic human motion and video in real time.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: In the RSI context, do you see motion generation models improving via self-play or synthetic data loops—i.e., can a motion generator critique and retrain itself without human-labeled motion capture data?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: RSI connection: interactive video generation requires models that continuously adapt to human behavior—a form of online recursive improvement. Her Anuttacon work is essentially building a world model for human interaction.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Connections to investors in digital human and interactive video companies (Runway, Pika, ElevenLabs adjacents); perspective on enterprise demand for avatar and motion generation APIs.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Do not lead with the time series paper—it was a side project that happened to be impactful; her core identity is human-centric AI.

controversial_take: Transformers are overengineered for many temporal tasks—the field wasted years on attention mechanisms where linear extrapolation was sufficient. The same overcomplexity is happening now in motion generation architectures.
open_problem: How do you build a generalizable motion prior that transfers across body types, cultural movement styles, and action categories without needing separate training datasets for each domain?

name: Benjamin Hoover
title: Research Engineer / PhD Candidate
lab: PoloClub @ Georgia Tech; Visual AI Lab @ IBM Research (departed)
research_focus: Interprets and visualizes the internal workings of foundation models through the lens of associative memory and Hopfield networks. Builds interactive open-source tools (ExBERT, WizMap, Transformer Explainer) used by 125,000+ users. Studies diffusion models as memory retrieval systems.
direction_of_travel: Moving from IBM to independent/academic work after 7.5 years. Likely transitioning toward a faculty or senior research role combining interpretability and associative memory theory. Recently departed IBM.

Transformer Explainer: live GPT-2 visualization tool with 125,000+ users (AAAI 2025)

First work showing diffusion models perform memory retrieval in denoising dynamics
NeurIPS 2025 Spotlight on Dense Associative Memory with Epanechnikov Energy
