top_papers.one_liner: AAAI 2025 tool running live GPT-2 in the browser used by 125K+ non-expert learners.

recent_arxiv.title: Dense Associative Memory with Epanechnikov Energy
recent_arxiv.summary: NeurIPS 2025 Spotlight: LSR energy function for dense associative memory with exponential capacity and emergent local minima.

current_projects: Post-IBM transition; NFAM 2026 workshop co-organization; ongoing Hopfield network interpretability research.
personal_site_summary: Personal site emphasizes open-source interpretability tools and the associative memory lens on foundation models; calls himself a bridge between visualization and dynamical systems.
industry_connections: IBM Research (departed); Georgia Tech PoloClub academic lab; tools widely used across industry practitioners.
commercialization_signal: Medium—tools have large practitioner adoption but no direct startup activity. Post-IBM trajectory could lead to a startup.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: The person who makes neural networks visually understandable—his Transformer Explainer has been used by 125K people. Now applying that lens to show that diffusion models and Hopfield networks are basically the same thing.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your diffusion-as-memory-retrieval work is striking—are you seeing retrieval capacity limits in practice (like hallucinations) that map cleanly to the energy landscape framework from Hopfield theory?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Core NFAM thesis: associative memory as a unifying principle for diffusion models and transformers—Hoover is the practitioner making this concrete through visualization.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Connections to interpretability-focused AI safety investors or labs hiring after his IBM departure.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Asking about IBM internal research directions now that he's departed—may be sensitive.

controversial_take: Argues most 'emergent' capabilities in LLMs are actually predictable from the Hopfield energy landscape—randomness in scaling law papers comes from not thinking about memory capacity correctly.
open_problem: Building a visualization that makes Hopfield energy landscapes interpretable for practitioners without physics backgrounds.

name: Xueyan Niu
title: Principal Engineer
lab: Theory Laboratory, Central Research Institute (2012 Labs), Huawei
research_focus: Theoretical connections between transformer attention and continuous Hopfield networks. Studies how energy-based associative memory explains transformer scaling behavior, memorization capacity, and model size relationships. Also works on generative modeling, semantic communications, and LLM knowledge editing.
direction_of_travel: From operations research PhD (Purdue) and Baidu research into Huawei's theory lab, where she's bridging information theory with deep learning theory. The NFAM invitation reflects growing recognition for connecting Hopfield theory to transformer scaling laws.
key_contributions: EECS Rising Star Award, UC Berkeley 2020
Outstanding Early Career Researcher Paper Award, IEEE ISITA 2020
Paper: 'Beyond Scaling Laws: Understanding Transformer Performance with Associative Memory' (arXiv 2024)
Work on NeuralDB: scaling knowledge editing in LLMs to 100,000 facts
Research on semantic communication theory

top_papers.title: Beyond Scaling Laws: Understanding Transformer Performance with Associative Memory
top_papers.one_liner: Shows transformer attention emerges from continuous Hopfield energy, linking model size to memorization capacity theoretically.

recent_arxiv.title: Beyond Scaling Laws: Understanding Transformer Performance with Associative Memory
recent_arxiv.summary: Derives theoretical links between Hopfield network energy functions and transformer attention, explaining performance beyond empirical scaling laws.

current_projects: Associative memory theory for transformers; knowledge editing in LLMs; semantic communication systems at Huawei Theory Lab.
personal_site_summary: Academic-style site listing research at Huawei Theory Lab; focus on ML theory and information theory; lists publications and awards.
industry_connections: Huawei Principal Engineer—large Chinese tech company with significant AI investment. Former Baidu Research.
commercialization_signal: Medium—works at Huawei deploying these ideas in products; but primarily theory-oriented.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: A Huawei theorist who proved mathematically why transformer attention and Hopfield networks are the same thing—and what that means for understanding why bigger models memorize more. Rising star from Berkeley's EECS program.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your associative memory framework predicts memorization capacity scales with attention head count—have you been able to test this against empirical memorization measurements in Llama-scale models?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Directly validates NFAM's core claim: transformers are associative memories. Her paper provides the theoretical bridge that makes the workshop's thesis concrete.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to Western AI safety or interpretability researchers interested in theoretical memory capacity guarantees—a perspective Huawei's theory lab would value.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Huawei's regulatory situation in the US/EU—politically sensitive and off-topic.

controversial_take: Claims scaling laws are empirical proxies for a deeper Hopfield capacity relationship, and that the 'emergent' abilities at scale are predictable once you understand the associative memory math.
open_problem: Deriving tight bounds on the number of 'facts' a transformer can reliably retrieve without interference, analogous to Hopfield network storage capacity.

name: Paul Liang
title: Assistant Professor
lab: MIT Media Lab (MAS Program) & MIT EECS / Schwarzman College of Computing
research_focus: Theoretical foundations and practical systems for multimodal machine learning, integrating text, vision, audio, and sensors. Develops evaluation frameworks, large-scale benchmarks, and neural architectures for multisensory AI. Applications in mental health, cancer prognosis, and robot control.
direction_of_travel: PhD from CMU (Morency/Salakhutdinov), now MIT faculty. Building out the Media Lab's AI + Human Experience program. Increasingly focused on the social and human dimensions of multimodal AI, not just technical capabilities.
key_contributions: awesome-multimodal-ml GitHub repository: 6,800+ stars, the canonical reading list for the field
MultiBench: multiscale benchmarks for multimodal representation learning (NeurIPS 2021)
HEMM: holistic evaluation of multimodal foundation models
Survey 'Foundations and Recent Trends in Multimodal Machine Learning' (tutorial at ICML/CVPR/NAACL)
Siebel Scholar; Facebook PhD Fellowship; four paper awards

top_papers.title: Foundations and Trends in Multimodal Machine Learning: Principles, Challenges, and Open Questions
top_papers.one_liner: Comprehensive survey covering 6 core challenges and 700+ references; the standard reference for the field.

top_papers.title: MultiBench: Multiscale Benchmarks for Multimodal Representation Learning
top_papers.one_liner: NeurIPS 2021 benchmark unifying evaluation across 15 datasets and 10 modality combinations.

current_projects: AI + Human Experience at MIT Media Lab; multimodal reasoning under distribution shift; applying multimodal systems to mental health and education.
personal_site_summary: Lists research agenda on multimodal ML foundations, applications to healthcare and education, MIT Media Lab role, and CMU course materials.
industry_connections: DeepMind, Facebook, Nvidia, Google internship experience; MIT Media Lab cross-industry collaborations; rising profile in industry-used benchmarks.
commercialization_signal: Medium-high—MultiBench and HEMM are used across industry; applications deployed in mental health and cancer prognosis; MIT Media Lab has strong spinout culture.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: The person who built the field of multimodal AI's curriculum (the GitHub repo with 6,800 stars) and is now an MIT professor connecting associative memory theory to how models fuse information across senses.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: In your HEMM evaluation framework, are multimodal models showing systematic failure modes that look like capacity limits—like they can remember two modalities but forget a third?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Multimodal associative memory: how do transformers store and retrieve associations across modalities? Paul's work makes this empirically testable.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Connections to healthcare AI investors—his cancer prognosis and mental health applications are deployment-ready and fundable.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: The corporate politics of CMU vs. MIT—he's recently transitioned and may not want to relitigate allegiances.

controversial_take: Believes most 'multimodal' models today are just text models with a vision encoder bolted on—true multimodal reasoning that exploits cross-modal memory hasn't been solved yet.
open_problem: Formally characterizing what information is lost when modalities are fused versus kept separate—the fundamental theory of multimodal information integration.

name: Parikshit Ram
title: Principal Research Staff Member
lab: IBM Research – Thomas J. Watson Research Center
research_focus: Similarity search, computational geometry, density estimation, and AutoML/AutoAI systems. Studies neuro-inspired sparse representations for learning. Works on bilevel optimization for meta-learning and transformer in-context learning theory. Co-authored key paper on Epanechnikov energy for dense associative memory.
direction_of_travel: From Skytree (ML startup, acquired by Infosys) to IBM Research. Growing focus on theoretical understanding of modern transformers through bilevel optimization and associative memory lenses. The Epanechnikov paper marks his move into the Hopfield network research community.
key_contributions: NeurIPS 2025 Spotlight: Dense Associative Memory with Epanechnikov Energy (with Hoover, Shi, Krotov)
Developed Lale: gradual AutoML framework at IBM
Foundational work on fast similarity search (thesis at Georgia Tech)
Multi-objective bilevel optimization for AutoML
3,472+ Google Scholar citations in ML, computational geometry, nearest neighbor search

top_papers.title: Tree-Independent Dual-Tree Algorithms
top_papers.one_liner: General framework for dual-tree algorithms enabling fast all-pairs computation across many problem types.

top_papers.title: Dense Associative Memory with Epanechnikov Energy
top_papers.one_liner: NeurIPS 2025 Spotlight connecting density estimation kernels to Hopfield network energy design.

recent_arxiv.title: Dense Associative Memory with Epanechnikov Energy
recent_arxiv.summary: NeurIPS 2025 Spotlight: proposes LSR energy for dense associative memory with exponential capacity and rich attractor structure.

current_projects: Epanechnikov energy associative memory; AutoML bilevel optimization (Lale); theoretical analysis of in-context learning and compositional generalization in transformers.
personal_site_summary: Lists research on similarity search, density estimation, AutoML, and neuro-inspired sparse representations at IBM Research.
industry_connections: IBM Research principal staff; Lale AutoML framework (open source, IBM); former Skytree (Infosys acquisition) experience.
commercialization_signal: Medium—IBM products built on his AutoML work; nearest neighbor search algorithms underpin industrial recommendation systems.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: An IBM researcher who built fast nearest-neighbor algorithms, then pivoted to AutoML, and now is connecting density estimation theory to the math of how Hopfield networks store memories. The Epanechnikov paper was a key unifying move.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: The Epanechnikov kernel's compact support creates bounded energy wells—did you explore other compactly supported kernels (Uniform, Biweight), and do they give qualitatively different attractor structures?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Co-author on the NeurIPS 2025 Spotlight that gives NFAM workshop its theoretical centerpiece. His density estimation background is what enabled the Epanechnikov insight.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: IBM Research partnership contacts for applied AutoML or nearest-neighbor acceleration projects.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: IBM's strategic direction under current leadership—company politics are sensitive.

controversial_take: Believes the current FAISS/Annoy-style approximate nearest neighbor search algorithms will need a complete rethink as query distributions shift in RAG systems—sparse codes from neuro-inspired models will outperform dense vector search.
open_problem: Understanding when neuro-inspired sparse representations are provably better than dense embeddings for similarity search—the theoretical gap between practice and theory is large.

name: Liu Yang
title: PhD Candidate
lab: CS Department, University of Wisconsin-Madison
research_focus: Liu Yang studies in-context learning in transformers, focusing on how task vectors emerge and how looped transformer architectures improve learning algorithm induction. Her research spans optimization, latent reasoning, and the theoretical underpinnings of how transformers implement learning algorithms within their forward pass.
direction_of_travel: Late-stage PhD student with strong publication record at ICLR, COLM, and TMLR; Google Research internship in 2025 positions her for a top industry research role or academic faculty position upon graduation.
key_contributions: Showed that looped transformers are better at learning learning algorithms, connecting recurrence to in-context learning
Characterized the emergence and formation of task vectors in transformer in-context learning
Explored the power of future prediction in partially observable environments

top_papers.title: Looped Transformers are Better at Learning Learning Algorithms
top_papers.one_liner: Demonstrates that weight-tied looped transformers outperform standard transformers on meta-learning and algorithm induction tasks

top_papers.title: Task Vectors in In-Context Learning: Emergence, Formation, and Benefits
top_papers.one_liner: Mechanistic study of how transformers form and use task vectors during in-context learning

recent_arxiv.title: An Empirical Study on the Power of Future Prediction in Partially Observable Environments
recent_arxiv.summary: Studies whether future prediction objectives improve representation quality in POMDPs

current_projects: Co-organizing LIT workshop at ICLR 2026; completing dissertation on looped transformers and in-context learning; Google Research internship follow-on work
personal_site_summary: PhD student site at UW-Madison listing research on looped transformers, task vectors, and in-context learning, with photography and rowing hobbies; advised by Nowak, Papailiopoulos, and Lee.
industry_connections: Google Research internship (2025); Meta Ads Ranking internships (2023, 2024); advisors Papailiopoulos and Lee have strong industry connections
commercialization_signal: Low direct signal; looped transformer research has indirect relevance to efficient inference products

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Liu studies how transformers learn to learn—specifically how looping the same weights multiple times makes models much better at picking up new tasks from just a few examples.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your task vectors paper suggests that ICL representations are more structured than we thought—do you see a path from that to training models that can explicitly manipulate their own task representations at inference time?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Relevant to LLM post-training and self-improvement: understanding how task vectors form and how looping enables better algorithm learning could inform next-generation fine-tuning and meta-learning approaches.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to ML research teams at major labs post-graduation; perspective on which of her directions are most product-relevant
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Avoid asking about dissertation timeline pressure—she's in a sensitive completion phase

controversial_take: In-context learning is not a separate capability from fine-tuning—they are the same mechanism operating at different timescales, and models that understand this distinction will be more efficiently trained.
open_problem: Is there a fundamental limit to how many 'loops' improve in-context learning before diminishing returns kick in, and can we predict this limit from the task structure?

name: Rishabh Tiwari
title: PhD Student
lab: Berkeley AI Research (BAIR) Lab
research_focus: Rishabh Tiwari works on efficient deep learning, focusing on network pruning, simplicity bias mitigation, and inference efficiency. His recent work includes speculative decoding with quantized KV caches and analysis of multi-agent LLM systems, bridging model compression and practical LLM deployment.
direction_of_travel: PhD student at Berkeley advised by Kurt Keutzer; strong early track record from pre-doctoral work at Google DeepMind; pivoting from model compression toward LLM inference efficiency and multi-agent systems.
key_contributions: QuantSpec: self-speculative decoding with hierarchical quantized KV cache for inference acceleration
Overcoming simplicity bias in deep networks without bias labels using Feature Sieve
ChipNet: budget-aware network pruning with continuous Heaviside approximations

top_papers.title: Overcoming Simplicity Bias in Deep Networks using a Feature Sieve
top_papers.one_liner: Addresses spurious correlation learning in neural networks without requiring bias annotations

top_papers.title: ChipNet: Budget-Aware Pruning with Heaviside Continuous Approximations
top_papers.one_liner: Differentiable pruning approach that finds optimal subnetworks within strict budget constraints

top_papers.title: QuantSpec: Self-Speculative Decoding with Hierarchical Quantized KV Cache
top_papers.one_liner: Uses quantized KV caches hierarchically to enable efficient speculative decoding without a separate draft model

recent_arxiv.title: QuantSpec: Self-Speculative Decoding with Hierarchical Quantized KV Cache
recent_arxiv.summary: Self-speculative decoding using hierarchically quantized KV caches as a draft mechanism, improving inference throughput

recent_arxiv.title: Why Do Multi-Agent LLM Systems Fail?
recent_arxiv.summary: Systematic analysis of failure modes in multi-agent LLM systems under review

current_projects: Co-organizing SPOT workshop at ICLR 2026; QuantSpec paper under review; multi-agent LLM failure analysis; PhD research on efficient inference at Berkeley
personal_site_summary: Berkeley PhD student site covering efficient deep learning research, with publications spanning pruning, bias mitigation, and LLM inference efficiency; founding member of Transmute AI Labs nonprofit.
industry_connections: Pre-doctoral researcher at Google DeepMind (with Pradeep Shenoy); founding member Transmute AI Labs; Berkeley BAIR affiliation connects to broad industry network through Keutzer
commercialization_signal: Moderate—QuantSpec and speculative decoding work is directly relevant to LLM inference optimization products; simplicity bias work has fairness/robustness applications

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Rishabh works on making neural networks faster and more robust—his recent work uses quantized key-value caches for self-speculative decoding, which speeds up LLM inference without a separate smaller draft model.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: In QuantSpec, the quantized KV cache acts as the draft model—what's the accuracy-speed trade-off frontier, and do you think this approach scales to longer contexts where the KV cache itself becomes the bottleneck?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Relevant to inference-time scaling: self-speculative decoding with quantized caches is a practical approach to accelerating test-time compute without additional model complexity.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to inference optimization startups; perspective on which efficient inference papers are gaining investor attention
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Avoid asking about his Kaggle background—he's moved well past that phase and prefers to be known for research

controversial_take: Speculative decoding's practical impact is overestimated in benchmarks because it assumes IID token distributions—in real-world diverse queries, the acceptance rate drops enough to undermine most claimed speedups.
open_problem: Is there a principled way to select the optimal quantization level for speculative decoding drafts that adapts dynamically to input difficulty rather than using a fixed compression ratio?

name: Celine Lee
title: PhD Candidate
lab: Cornell NLP (advised by Alexander Rush)
research_focus: Language model reasoning with code, focusing on code generation, transpilation, and understanding what kinds of complexity govern optimal reasoning length. Work spans LM-guided program analysis, system-2 reasoning in latent space, and evaluation of code language model failures.
direction_of_travel: Undergraduate researcher in semantic role labeling (UPenn/Dan Roth) → structured language and code generation (Cornell/Rush) → reasoning length and complexity governance in LLMs → now co-organizing VerifAI workshop bridging code LMs with formal verification. Trajectory points toward neuro-symbolic verification and proof-generating agents.
key_contributions: Commit0: first benchmark for library-level code generation from scratch (ICLR 2025)
Guess & Sketch: LM-guided binary transpilation using sketching + LLM fill-in (ICLR 2024)
Critical Thinking: empirical study on complexity types governing optimal reasoning chain length (ACL Findings 2025)
Counterfeit Conundrum: analysis of code LM failures to understand their own incorrect generations (ACL Findings 2024)
Sampling Language from Latent System-2 Reasoning: structured reasoning in latent space (NeurIPS 2024 workshop)

top_papers.title: Commit0: Library Generation from Scratch
top_papers.one_liner: Benchmark requiring agents to synthesize entire software libraries from specifications, testing end-to-end code generation.

top_papers.title: Guess & Sketch: Language Model Guided Transpilation
top_papers.one_liner: Uses LLM to produce program sketches from assembly, then fills in gaps, enabling decompilation of real binary code.

top_papers.title: Critical Thinking: Which Kinds of Complexity Govern Optimal Reasoning Length?
top_papers.one_liner: Finds that problem type—not just difficulty—determines whether longer chain-of-thought helps or hurts.

recent_arxiv.title: Critical Thinking: Which Kinds of Complexity Govern Optimal Reasoning Length?
recent_arxiv.summary: Empirical study showing that complexity type (structural vs. surface) determines the ideal reasoning chain length for LLMs.

recent_arxiv.title: Sampling Language from Latent System-2 Reasoning
recent_arxiv.summary: Method to generate explicit language descriptions from learned latent reasoning representations to improve interpretability.

current_projects: PhD research on code reasoning and generation at Cornell; AI research intern at Figma; co-organizer of VerifAI-2 ICLR 2026 workshop on verification and foundation models.
personal_site_summary: PhD candidate at Cornell advised by Sasha Rush. Research on code reasoning and generation with LLMs. Prior experience at Google DeepMind, IBM TJ Watson, Intel Labs. Organized VerifAI workshop at ICLR 2026.
industry_connections: Research internships at Figma, Google DeepMind, IBM TJ Watson, Intel Labs. Advisor Sasha Rush has strong industry ties.
commercialization_signal: Work on transpilation (decompilation of binary code) has direct applications in legacy code modernization and cybersecurity; Commit0 benchmark targets agentic software development. Figma internship suggests interest in applying LLMs to developer tooling.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: PhD student at Cornell studying how LLMs reason about code—she builds benchmarks to test whether AI can write entire software libraries and understand its own mistakes. She just co-organized the VerifAI ICLR workshop.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your Commit0 benchmark requires generating whole libraries—have you seen agents succeed by top-down planning vs. bottom-up iterative synthesis, and does that correlate with formal spec adherence?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Directly relevant to formal verification: her transpilation work and reasoning-length research underpin the question of when and how LLMs can produce verifiable code artifacts.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to founders building AI-assisted developer tooling (decompilation, code migration). Access to practitioner feedback on Commit0-style agentic coding benchmarks.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Do not ask about NLP-style pronoun disambiguation or traditional semantic role labeling—that was her undergrad work.

controversial_take: Longer chain-of-thought reasoning actively hurts performance on structurally simple problems—the field's obsession with 'think more' is misguided for a large class of real tasks.
open_problem: How do you verify that an LLM-generated library actually satisfies its specification without full formal proof? The gap between test-passing and provably-correct is the central unsolved problem in agentic coding.

name: Sharvaree Vadgama
title: Postdoctoral Researcher
lab: UC San Diego (postdoc); formerly AMLab, University of Amsterdam (PhD with Erik Bekkers)
research_focus: Vadgama works on geometric deep learning and equivariant generative models. Her PhD research (with Bekkers) produced foundational results on equivariant neural fields and utility of equivariance for point cloud architectures. Her most recent work develops equivariant variational flow matching for controlled molecular generation and the Platonic Transformer — a symmetry-grounded attention mechanism using Platonic solid symmetry groups.
direction_of_travel: PhD at University of Amsterdam (AMLab, Bekkers), now postdoc at UC San Diego. Strong publication record for a postdoc — ICLR 2025 (equivariant neural fields), ICML 2025 (equivariant VFM). Active GRaM workshop organizer. Trajectory toward assistant professor or research scientist position at a lab with geometric DL focus.
key_contributions: Grounding Continuous Representations in Geometry: Equivariant Neural Fields (ICLR 2025)
Controlled Generation with Equivariant Variational Flow Matching (ICML 2025)
The Platonic Transformer — Platonic solid-grounded symmetry-equivariant attention

top_papers.title: Grounding Continuous Representations in Geometry: Equivariant Neural Fields
top_papers.one_liner: Neural fields with explicit geometric equivariance for continuous spatial representations; ICLR 2025.

top_papers.title: Controlled Generation with Equivariant Variational Flow Matching
top_papers.one_liner: Equivariant controlled generation for molecules using variational flow matching; ICML 2025.

top_papers.title: On the Utility of Equivariance and Symmetry Breaking in Deep Learning Architectures on Point Clouds
top_papers.one_liner: Empirical analysis of equivariance benefits vs. symmetry breaking costs across geometric tasks.

recent_arxiv.title: The Platonic Transformer
recent_arxiv.summary: Transformer architecture with Platonic solid symmetry-grounded attention, achieving combined equivariance to translations and Platonic symmetries at no extra cost.

recent_arxiv.title: On the Importance of Embedding Norms in Self-Supervised Learning
recent_arxiv.summary: Theoretical and empirical analysis of embedding norm behavior in self-supervised learning frameworks.

current_projects: GRaM Workshop at ICLR 2026; equivariant variational flow matching for molecular generation; Platonic Transformer development; postdoc research at UC San Diego.
personal_site_summary: UCSD profiles page. Research listed under geometric deep learning and generative models. Active researcher with multiple 2025 publications.
industry_connections: Collaboration with Max Welling (UvA / CuspAI), David Romero (NVIDIA Research). Amsterdam ML ecosystem connections (Qualcomm AI Research Amsterdam, ML6). No direct startup/industry role currently.
commercialization_signal: Moderate. Equivariant flow matching for molecular generation has direct drug discovery applications. The Platonic Transformer could have broader CV and 3D understanding applications. Early stage but technically strong.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Sharvaree combines equivariant deep learning with flow matching to generate molecules that respect physical symmetries like rotational invariance. Her Platonic Transformer is a clever architecture hack that gives you free symmetry invariance in any standard transformer.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: The Platonic Transformer induces equivariance to discrete Platonic symmetry groups — but molecular symmetries are continuous (SO(3)). Is there a systematic way to approximate continuous group equivariance with Platonic discretization?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Strong — geometric DL, equivariant generative models, flow matching for molecules. Directly on physical AI and generative AI thesis pillars.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to molecular generation startups and structural biology AI companies; connections to NVIDIA Research (through her Romero collaboration); academic job market advice for UCSD-to-faculty transition.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Don't ask about her transition from Amsterdam to UCSD — international academic moves can involve personal complexities.

controversial_take: The debate between equivariant and non-equivariant models for point clouds will be settled empirically — but current benchmarks don't capture the tail distribution of geometric configurations where equivariance matters most, so published results understate the gap.
open_problem: How to efficiently combine SE(3) equivariance with the full expressivity of modern transformer architectures — current equivariant transformers either sacrifice expressivity or computational efficiency.

name: Krishnakumar Balasubramanian
title: Associate Professor
lab: Department of Statistics; CeDAR; TETRAPODS Institute of Data Science
research_focus: Works at the interface of statistics, optimization, and machine learning, with emphasis on stochastic sampling, non-log-concave and heavy-tailed distributions, and deep learning theory. Develops algorithms for Riemannian optimization and geometric/topological statistics. Also exploring how transformers handle econometric problems (endogeneity) in-context.
direction_of_travel: Starting from pure statistical theory (Riemannian optimization, sampling), increasingly bridging toward modern ML—especially analyzing transformers theoretically and connecting sampling methods to associative memory energy landscapes.
key_contributions: 2024 INFORMS Computing Society Prize for path-breaking work in Riemannian optimization
Theoretical analysis of heavy-tailed and non-log-concave sampling via discretized Itô diffusions (JMLR 2024)
Stein Variational Gradient Descent convergence rates (ICLR 2025)
Co-authored NeurIPS 2025 Spotlight on Dense Associative Memory with Epanechnikov Energy

top_papers.title: Mean-Square Analysis of Discretized Itô Diffusions for Heavy-tailed Sampling
top_papers.one_liner: Establishes iteration complexity for sampling from heavy-tailed targets using Langevin-style discretizations.

top_papers.title: Improved Finite-Particle Convergence Rates for Stein Variational Gradient Descent
top_papers.one_liner: Tightens finite-particle convergence guarantees for SVGD, a key approximate inference method.

top_papers.title: Dense Associative Memory with Epanechnikov Energy
top_papers.one_liner: NeurIPS 2025 Spotlight proposing log-sum-ReLU energy enabling exponential memory capacity without exponential separation.

recent_arxiv.title: Transformers Handle Endogeneity in In-Context Linear Regression
recent_arxiv.summary: Shows transformers can implicitly handle endogeneity (correlated errors) in in-context learning, published at ICLR 2025.

current_projects: Theoretical foundations of associative memories with Epanechnikov kernels; non-log-concave sampling theory; statistical aspects of in-context learning in transformers.
personal_site_summary: UC Davis Statistics associate professor listing research in stochastic optimization, sampling, geometric/topological statistics, and deep learning theory. Lists mentoring and teaching activities.
industry_connections: Amazon Research (AWS) affiliate; Simons Institute visiting scientist; NSF-funded.
commercialization_signal: Low—primarily academic theory. Some indirect relevance through sampling algorithms used in generative models.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: A theorist who proves that AI sampling algorithms actually work—especially for messy, heavy-tailed distributions. He recently connected these ideas to Hopfield/associative memory networks.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your Epanechnikov energy paper shows exponential capacity without exponential separation—do you see a path to leveraging the finite-particle SVGD analysis to tighten retrieval dynamics bounds in modern Hopfield networks?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Direct: co-authored the NeurIPS 2025 Spotlight on Dense Associative Memory; organizes NFAM workshop connecting statistical theory to Hopfield networks.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to applied labs seeking rigorous sampling theory for generative models or Bayesian inference at scale.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Asking him to speculate on product applications—he is a pure theorist and will redirect to math.

controversial_take: Believes modern diffusion model success is partly explained by the same energy-landscape intuition behind Hopfield networks—a claim most practitioners dismiss as too abstract to be actionable.
open_problem: Proving finite-time retrieval guarantees for dense associative memories with realistic (non-Gaussian) noise distributions.

name: Jay McClelland
title: Lucie Stern Professor; Director, Center for Mind, Brain, Computation and Technology
lab: Center for Mind, Brain, Computation and Technology, Stanford
research_focus: Cognitive neuroscience through the lens of parallel distributed processing (PDP) and connectionist models. Studies learning, memory, language, perception, and mathematical cognition as emergent properties of neural network dynamics. Consulting Research Scientist at DeepMind.
direction_of_travel: Foundational figure who essentially invented modern deep learning's conceptual framework (PDP, 1986). Now providing intellectual legitimacy for the cognitive-neuroscience grounding of associative memory research. Bridge between 1980s neural net theory and today's transformers.
key_contributions: Co-founded Parallel Distributed Processing (PDP) research group with Rumelhart; 1986 PDP volumes are among most cited works in cognitive science
Largely responsible for the 1980s resurgence of scientific interest in connectionism
2001 Grawemeyer Prize in Psychology; 2002 IEEE Neural Networks Pioneer Award; NAS member
Ongoing consulting research scientist at DeepMind
Work on complementary learning systems theory of hippocampal-neocortical interaction

top_papers.title: Parallel Distributed Processing: Explorations in the Microstructure of Cognition (Vol 1 & 2)
top_papers.one_liner: The foundational text of connectionism—essentially invented the modern conception of neural networks as cognitive models.

top_papers.title: Why There Are Complementary Learning Systems in the Hippocampus and Neocortex
top_papers.one_liner: Theoretical framework for why memory consolidation requires two systems—hugely influential in neuroscience and AI.

current_projects: Comparing statistical learning in humans vs. large language models; consulting at DeepMind on cognitive foundations of AI; panelist at NFAM 2026.
personal_site_summary: Stanford home page listing PDP framework, ongoing research on cognition as emergent from PDP, course syllabi, and consulting work at DeepMind.
industry_connections: Consulting Research Scientist at DeepMind; founding intellectual influence across the entire AI industry.
commercialization_signal: Low directly—but his work is the intellectual foundation of the entire deep learning industry.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: One of the three or four people who invented modern deep learning (with Rumelhart and Hinton). His 1986 PDP books are why neural networks exist as a field. Now connecting his 1980s cognitive theory to today's LLMs at DeepMind.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Modern Hopfield networks look like a formal realization of your complementary learning systems theory—do you think fast associative binding in transformers is the 'hippocampus' of LLMs?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: NFAM's intellectual anchor: the cognitive neuroscience origins of associative memory. McClelland's PDP framework is what modern Hopfield theory is returning to.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Connections to neuroscience-AI crossover investors (e.g., Andreessen, a16z bio) who'd value his perspective on brain-inspired AI.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Debates about whether LLMs are 'truly' intelligent—he has nuanced views and dislikes reductive takes from either camp.

controversial_take: Believes GPT-style models have rediscovered the PDP insight that statistical regularities in language implicitly encode world knowledge—but are missing the complementary memory system that humans use for episodic learning.
open_problem: How to build AI systems that can learn new facts from single examples (one-shot) without catastrophically forgetting, as humans do via hippocampal-neocortical complementary learning.

name: Noor Sajid
title: Research Fellow, Kempner Institute
lab: Kempner Institute for the Study of Natural and Artificial Intelligence, Harvard
research_focus: Generative models emulating biological decision-making under uncertainty. Primary framework: active inference (Karl Friston's Free Energy Principle) applied to build AI agents that are inherently adaptive, uncertainty-aware, and capable of flexible generalization with minimal data. Cross-modal alignment via conditional flow matching.
direction_of_travel: From pure active inference theory (UCL with Friston) → biological decision-making generative models → AI alignment and multi-modal model alignment (Harvard/MPI) → dissociating model architectures from inference computations.
key_contributions: Active Inference as a Model of Agency (2024): normative framework for AI agency beyond reward maximization
Model alignment using inter-modal bridges (2025): semi-supervised cross-modal alignment using conditional flow matching
Dissociating model architectures from inference computations (2025): how autoregressive models mimic deep temporal computations
Microsoft Research PhD Fellowship (2021) recipient

top_papers.title: Active Inference as a Model of Agency
top_papers.one_liner: Active inference as normative AI agency framework — minimizing risk and ambiguity rather than maximizing reward

top_papers.title: Model alignment using inter-modal bridges
top_papers.one_liner: Semi-supervised flow matching approach to align foundation models across vision and text modalities

top_papers.title: Dissociating model architectures from inference computations
top_papers.one_liner: Distinguishes how prediction distributions are structured from how inference is computed

recent_arxiv.summary: Demonstrates that autoregressive models mimic deep temporal computations by structuring context access

recent_arxiv.title: Model alignment using inter-modal bridges
recent_arxiv.summary: Conditional flow matching for semi-supervised cross-modal alignment of text and vision foundation models, matching end-to-end performance with limited data

recent_arxiv.title: Active Inference as a Model of Agency
recent_arxiv.summary: Frames active inference as normative framework for agency — agents minimize expected free energy (risk + ambiguity) rather than maximize reward

current_projects: Kempner Fellowship research: expanding generative models of biological decision-making, studying flexible knowledge generalization across tasks with limited data, bridging neuroscience-inspired models with modern AI architectures. Guest scientist collaboration with Max Planck Institute (Peter Dayan group).
personal_site_summary: Lists Harvard Kempner Fellow, MPI guest scientist, prior internships at Microsoft Research Cambridge, Huawei, Zebra Technologies, and PhD under Karl Friston at UCL.
notable_quotes: Biological systems demonstrate remarkable flexibility — they apply learned knowledge to new situations with minimal data. Understanding this in terms of generative models is the key to making AI systems genuinely adaptive.
industry_connections: Research internships at Microsoft Research Cambridge, Huawei Technologies, and Zebra Technologies. No startup involvement currently. Active inference community has growing interest from AI safety and alignment investors.
commercialization_signal: LOW currently — primarily fundamental researcher. Active inference has potential in AI safety, healthcare AI decision-making, and adaptive robotics.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: She studies how the brain makes decisions under uncertainty and uses those principles to build AI that's genuinely flexible — able to apply what it learned in one situation to completely new situations with very little training.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Active inference frames agency as minimizing expected free energy rather than maximizing reward — do you see a path where this provides a practical alternative to RLHF for aligning AI agents to human preferences, given how brittle reward specification is?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Tangential to core thesis but relevant to AI safety: active inference provides a principled framework for agents that model uncertainty and generalize adaptively — properties needed for reliable physical AI deployment.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to AI safety investors (Open Philanthropy, ARC) funding neuroscience-AI bridge research; healthcare AI companies needing adaptive decision-making models.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Avoid reducing her work to 'just another Bayesian agent' — active inference is a broader normative theory of mind.

controversial_take: Reward maximization is the wrong objective for intelligent agents. Biological intelligence works by minimizing surprise (free energy minimization), not maximizing reward — and AI should too.
open_problem: How to scale active inference generative models to match the capability of modern large neural networks while retaining principled uncertainty quantification and flexible generalization.

name: Mingyuan Bai
title: Postdoctoral Researcher
lab: Tensor Learning Team, RIKEN AIP
research_focus: Mingyuan Bai works on generative models for adversarial machine learning, tensor learning, and graph neural networks. Her research bridges high-dimensional structured data representation with generative modeling, studying robustness and efficiency of deep generative frameworks.
direction_of_travel: Early-career postdoc (PhD from University of Sydney, 2023) actively publishing at ICML and NeurIPS; organizing a major ICLR workshop suggests growing leadership ambitions — likely targeting a faculty or senior industry role by 2026-2027.
key_contributions: Generative models for adversarial machine learning and robustness
Tensor learning methods for high-dimensional data
Graph neural network theory and applications
Workshop co-organizer bridging DGM theory and practice at ICLR 2025 and 2026

top_papers.title: Publications in ICML and NeurIPS on generative models and tensor learning
top_papers.one_liner: Research on tensor-structured generative models for high-dimensional data.

current_projects: Generative models under adversarial conditions; tensor decomposition for efficient deep learning; ICLR 2026 workshop organization.
personal_site_summary: Google Scholar profile lists research areas including high-dimensional data, tensor methods, dimensionality reduction, and deep learning, with publications at ICML and NeurIPS.
industry_connections: RIKEN AIP postdoc; connected to Qibin Zhao (Tensor Learning Team), an internationally recognized tensor computing researcher with industry ties.
commercialization_signal: No startup signals detected. Research on robust generative models has potential relevance to adversarial AI defense products.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: She develops AI methods that can learn from complex multi-dimensional data structures and remain robust when someone tries to fool them.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: How do you see tensor-structured generative models scaling to the size of modern foundation models — is tensor factorization a viable approach to parameter-efficient fine-tuning?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Robust generative models connect to the physical AI thesis; tensor methods are increasingly relevant to efficient foundation model training.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to efficient AI/model compression startups; exposure to industry use-cases for robust generative models.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Avoid asking about her future career plans in too direct a manner — she is in a transitional postdoc stage.

controversial_take: Tensor decomposition methods, despite being decades old, may outperform many modern PEFT approaches for specific structured data modalities.
open_problem: Scaling tensor-based generative models to billions of parameters without losing the theoretical tractability that makes them useful.

name: Johann Wenckstern
title: PhD Student
lab: AIMM Lab (AI in Molecular Medicine), EPFL, supervised by Charlotte Bunne
research_focus: Wenckstern works at the intersection of machine learning and computational biomedicine, focusing on generative and multimodal models for spatial proteomics, digital pathology, and personalized medicine. His work develops vision transformers and diffusion models to learn from highly multiplex pathology data and predict diagnostic properties of cancer patients.
direction_of_travel: Mathematics BSc/MSc at ETH Zurich, then PhD at EPFL's AIMM Lab. Early career but fast-moving — a Best Paper Award at ICLR MLGenX Workshop 2025 signals strong trajectory. The ReALM-GEN co-organization alongside much more senior colleagues is an early career differentiator.
key_contributions: AI-powered virtual tissues from spatial proteomics (ICLR MLGenX Best Paper 2025)
Test-Time View Selection for multi-modal biomedical decision making (ICLR Genomics Workshop 2025)
Generative AI methods for therapy planning at AIMM Lab

top_papers.one_liner: Vision-transformer-based virtual tissue model from spatial proteomics; Best Paper at ICLR MLGenX 2025.

top_papers.title: Test-Time View Selection for Multi-Modal Decision Making
top_papers.one_liner: Multi-modal view selection at inference time for biomedical decision support.

recent_arxiv.title: AI-powered virtual tissues from spatial proteomics for clinical diagnostics and biomedical discovery

current_projects: PhD research at EPFL AIMM Lab on generative AI for personalized medicine; co-organizing ReALM-GEN at ICLR 2026; supervising semester projects at AIMM on generative AI for therapy planning.
personal_site_summary: Early-stage academic page associated with AIMM Lab and EPFL. Research focused on biomedical AI.
industry_connections: Through AIMM Lab's close ties to Genentech and the broader Bunne lab network (Charlotte Bunne's prior Stanford/Genentech postdoc). Indirect pharma/biotech connections.
commercialization_signal: Moderate-high for biotech. Spatial proteomics + AI for diagnostics is a hot commercial area (Akoya, 10x Genomics). Early stage but in the right domain.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: Johann is a PhD student at EPFL working on applying diffusion and transformer models to spatial biology and cancer diagnostics. He's an early career researcher being co-mentored into the generative modeling space by Charlotte Bunne.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: In your virtual tissue work, how do you handle the distribution shift between patients when the spatial proteomics data varies so much in density and marker panel?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Tangential — generative models for science (biomedical domain). Not core to diffusion/flow matching methods but relevant to AI-for-science investment thesis.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to biotech companies doing spatial proteomics or digital pathology; connections to CZI or biomedical AI funds.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Don't lead with pure ML theory questions — his strength is the biomedical application layer.

controversial_take: Digital pathology AI companies are building on the wrong foundation — proprietary slide scanners and closed annotation pipelines will lose to open spatial proteomics + foundation models within five years.
open_problem: How to build generative models of spatial tissue that are interpretable enough to inform therapeutic hypotheses, not just diagnostic labels.

name: David Clark
title: Research Fellow
lab: Kempner Institute for the Study of Natural and Artificial Intelligence; Pehlevan Group
research_focus: Theoretical neuroscience of large neural circuits using tools from statistical physics and machine learning. Studies how recurrent circuit features (nonlinear dynamics, synaptic plasticity) enable computation and memory storage. Works on transient dynamics of associative memory models and continuous attractor networks.
direction_of_travel: Columbia PhD (Abbott lab) → Kempner Research Fellow. Classic theoretical neuroscience-to-AI bridge trajectory. 2025 Kempner Fellow signals Harvard is positioning him for a faculty role. His associative memory dynamics work is increasingly cited in the Hopfield/modern ML literature.
key_contributions: 2025 Kempner Institute Research Fellow (competitive)
Transient dynamics of associative memory models (arXiv 2025)
Associative synaptic plasticity creates dynamic persistent activity (with Abbott, bioRxiv 2025)
Structure of activity in multiregion recurrent neural networks (PNAS 2025)
PhD under Larry Abbott at Columbia (premier computational neuroscience group)

top_papers.title: Structure of activity in multiregion recurrent neural networks
top_papers.one_liner: PNAS 2025 analysis of how inter-region connectivity structures neural activity across brain areas.

top_papers.title: Transient dynamics of associative memory models
top_papers.one_liner: Analyzes how neural systems navigate Hopfield energy landscapes during memory retrieval—bridging neuroscience and ML.

recent_arxiv.title: Transient dynamics of associative memory models
recent_arxiv.summary: Studies transient retrieval dynamics in Hopfield networks, showing how energy landscape geometry determines retrieval speed and error rates.

recent_arxiv.title: Associative synaptic plasticity creates dynamic persistent activity
recent_arxiv.summary: With Abbott: Hebbian plasticity rules create dynamic memory states rather than static attractors, reconciling neural observations with attractor network theory.

current_projects: Transient associative memory dynamics; continuous attractor theory; multiregion circuit models; NFAM 2026 invited talk.
personal_site_summary: Personal site listing research on neural circuit theory, associative memory dynamics, and statistical physics approaches to neuroscience.
industry_connections: Kempner Institute (Harvard with industry partnerships); Abbott lab alumni network (Columbia); Pehlevan Group at Harvard SEAS.
commercialization_signal: Low—early career academic. But Kempner Institute connections could lead to brain-inspired AI startup roles.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: A theoretical neuroscientist at Harvard who's working out the exact mathematical dynamics of how Hopfield-style memories are retrieved in neural circuits—bridging the biological reality to the AI models.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: Your transient dynamics work shows retrieval is a trajectory, not a jump—how does this change our understanding of 'spurious attractors' in large transformer-based associative memories?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Provides the biological validity for NFAM's claims: real neural circuits actually do use the energy landscape dynamics that modern Hopfield networks abstract.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Introductions to brain-inspired computing investors or neurotech companies that need theoretical grounding.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Asking him to make strong engineering claims—he's a theorist and will be cautious about over-selling applications.

controversial_take: Argues that the brain doesn't actually use Hopfield-style energy minimization for most memory—the dynamic, transient picture is fundamentally different from the static attractor view that modern Hopfield networks assume.
open_problem: Deriving the retrieval capacity of a network with realistic synaptic dynamics (Hebbian plasticity, short-term depression) versus idealized fixed weights.

name: Zhaoyang Shi
affiliation: Harvard University (Postdoc, formerly UC Davis PhD)
title: Postdoctoral Fellow
lab: Department of Statistics, Harvard University
research_focus: Statistical theory at the intersection of machine learning, nonparametric statistics, and applied probability. Works on Gaussian approximations for matching estimators, fractional Laplacian eigenmaps for nonparametric regression, and random forest theory. Co-authored NeurIPS 2025 Spotlight connecting kernel density estimation (Epanechnikov kernel) to dense associative memory.
direction_of_travel: Recent UC Davis PhD (co-advised by Balasubramanian and Polonik) now at Harvard as postdoc. Bridging from pure statistics into ML theory, particularly associative memory and its statistical foundations.
key_contributions: NeurIPS 2025 Spotlight: Dense Associative Memory with Epanechnikov Energy (with Hoover, Krotov, Ram)
Minimax optimal nonparametric regression via fractional Laplacian eigenmaps (UAI 2025)
Gaussian approximation bounds for matching-based ATE estimators
Flexible normal approximation for geometric/topological statistics (Bernoulli 2024)

top_papers.one_liner: NeurIPS 2025 Spotlight connecting optimal kernel density estimation to Hopfield network energy functions.

top_papers.one_liner: General CLT-type results for persistent homology and geometric statistics.

current_projects: Statistical foundations of associative memory; causal inference with matching estimators; NFAM 2026 workshop co-organization from Harvard base.
personal_site_summary: Personal page showing PhD background at UC Davis with Balasubramanian/Polonik, research focus on statistical theory and ML, now postdoc at Harvard under Zheng (Tracy) Ke.
industry_connections: Harvard Statistics postdoc; IBM Research collaborator (through Krotov and Ram); PhD network at UC Davis.
commercialization_signal: Low—early career academic statistician. Trajectory toward faculty position.

before_you_walk_up.plain_english: A talented young statistician who made the key mathematical connection between kernel density estimation and Hopfield network energy functions—the insight behind the NeurIPS 2025 Spotlight paper.
before_you_walk_up.informed_question: The Epanechnikov kernel's compact support is what gives you the bounded energy landscape—are there other kernels from nonparametric statistics that could yield qualitatively different memory retrieval behaviors?
before_you_walk_up.thesis_connection: Statistical foundations of associative memory—bridges the abstract Hopfield theory to concrete density estimation tools that practitioners understand.
before_you_walk_up.what_you_can_offer: Connections to statistician-friendly ML labs or faculty hiring committees—she's likely on the academic job market soon.
before_you_walk_up.avoid_topic: Asking about post-postdoc plans if she hasn't announced—sensitive for junior researchers.

controversial_take: Thinks the 'modern Hopfield network' framing by Ramsauer et al. obscures the deeper statistical interpretation, and that the density estimation lens is more mathematically natural and productive.
open_problem: Deriving finite-sample guarantees for Epanechnikov associative memory retrieval under misspecified priors.
